topics.title: PN Junction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** PN Junction # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 🔬 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **PN Junction** is the fundamental semiconductor device structure formed when a **p-type** semiconductor region (heavily doped with acceptor impurities, creating holes as majority carriers) is brought into intimate atomic contact with an **n-type** semiconductor region (doped with donor impurities, creating electrons as majority carriers). At the metallurgical interface, a **Space Charge Region (SCR)** or **Depletion Region** is formed due to carrier diffusion and recombination, establishing a **built-in electric field** that opposes further diffusion. This self-consistent electro-static structure is the *elementary building block* of virtually all VLSI devices — diodes, BJTs, MOSFETs (source/drain to body junctions), and CMOS well ties. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-1 Highlight:** The PN junction is the *mother structure* of all silicon VLSI. The MOSFET channel is essentially a pair of back-to-back PN junctions (source-body and drain-body) whose depletion regions are *modulated* by the gate electric field. **Mastering PN junction physics = mastering MOSFET operation.** --- ## 🌊 Real-World Analogy — The "One-Way Floodgate" Imagine two large reservoirs connected by a thin porous wall: - The **left reservoir (P-side)** is filled with buoyant, light wooden balls (holes — positive charge carriers that *float* and diffuse). - The **right reservoir (N-side)** is filled with heavy underwater bubbles (electrons — negative carriers that *sink* and diffuse). - When the wall is removed, the wooden balls float *rightward* and bubbles rise *leftward*. They meet in the middle, collide, and cancel (recombination). A clear, neutral, empty zone forms at the center — this is your **depletion region**. - Because balls have left the left side, that wall develops a **negative** charge (uncovered acceptor ions). The right wall develops a **positive** charge (uncovered donor ions). This sets up an internal pressure difference (built-in potential $V_{bi}$) that *stops further mixing* — equilibrium is reached. - Now, if you push water from the left (forward bias), the balls flood through easily — **current flows**. If you push from the right (reverse bias), the empty zone just *widens* — **negligible current flows**. That's the **rectifying diode action**. --- ## 📏 Standard Constants & Metrics (at **T = 300 K**) | Symbol | Quantity | Value | Units | |---|---|---|---| | $q$ | Electronic charge | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | C | | $k$ | Boltzmann constant | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K | | $V_T = kT/q$ | Thermal voltage | $\approx \mathbf{25.85}$ | mV | | $n_i$ (Si) | Intrinsic carrier conc. | $1.5 \times 10^{10}$ | cm$^{-3}$ | | $\varepsilon_0$ | Vacuum permittivity | $8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ | F/cm | | $\varepsilon_{r,Si}$ | Si relative permittivity | **11.7** | — | | $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ | Si permittivity | $1.036 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/cm | > [!NOTE] > The frequently used approximation **$V_T \approx 26$ mV at 300 K** is what you will see in every KTU numerical problem unless the problem explicitly states otherwise. Always carry this value to 2–3 significant figures. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🧠 The Formation Process — Step-by-Step Physics 1. **Initial State:** P-side has $\boxed{\text{high hole density } p_{p0} \approx N_A}$ and very few electrons. N-side has $\boxed{\text{high electron density } n_{n0} \approx N_D}$ and very few holes. 2. **Diffusion Drive:** A steep concentration gradient exists across the junction. Holes diffuse *from P → N*; electrons diffuse *from N → P*. 3. **Recombination:** Near the interface, diffusing carriers recombine with the local majority carriers — leaving behind a region *devoid of mobile carriers* but full of **immobile ionized dopants** ($\text{N}_{\text{A}}^{-}$ on the P-side, $\text{N}_{\text{D}}^{+}$ on the N-side). 4. **Space-Charge Build-up:** These uncovered ions create a **dipole layer** of net negative charge (P-side) and net positive charge (N-side). 5. **Internal Electric Field:** $\vec{E}$ points from N → P (from + to − ions). This field *drifts* minority carriers back across the junction (holes from N → P, electrons from P → N). 6. **Dynamic Equilibrium:** At equilibrium, the **diffusion current (majority) = drift current (minority)**. Net current = 0. A self-consistent **built-in potential barrier** $V_{bi}$ exists across the depletion region. 7. **Charge Neutrality:** Total negative charge on P-side = Total positive charge on N-side: $$q N_A x_p = q N_D x_n \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_A x_p = N_D x_n$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Charge neutrality** is the single most important constraint used in *every* PN junction derivation. Memorize it. It tells you that on the **lighter-doped side, the depletion width is larger**. --- ## ⚖️ Energy Band Picture (Equilibrium) - Fermi level $E_F$ is **constant** throughout the structure at equilibrium (single Fermi level = no net current). - Conduction band $E_C$ and valence band $E_V$ **bend** near the junction by an amount $qV_{bi}$. - Built-in potential is the difference between the intrinsic Fermi levels on the two sides: $$V_{bi} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$$ ## 🔋 Bias Configurations | Mode | Applied Voltage | Depletion Width | Barrier Height | Current | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Equilibrium** | 0 V | $W_0$ | $qV_{bi}$ | 0 | | **Forward Bias** (P +, N −) | $V > 0$ | $W < W_0$ | $q(V_{bi}-V)$ | **Large (exponential)** | | **Reverse Bias** (P −, N +) | $V < 0$ | $W > W_0$ | $q(V_{bi}+ \vert V \vert)$ | **Tiny (≈ − $I_S$)** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Killer Fact:** In **reverse bias**, current is *not* exactly zero. A small **reverse saturation current** $I_S$ flows due to thermally generated minority carriers being swept across the junction by the built-in field. This $I_S$ is **independent of reverse voltage** but *strongly dependent on temperature* (doubles every ~10 °C). --- ## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** The KTU paper setters for PECST415 (VLSI Design) almost always test **derivation of $V_{bi}$, $W$, and $C_j$** as 7-mark sub-questions. Commit the boxed formulas to memory. | # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Built-in Potential | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$ | $V_T = 26$ mV at 300 K | | 2 | Total Depletion Width | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \dfrac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}}$ | cm | | 3 | One-sided Width (e.g., $N_A \gg N_D$) | $x_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}}$ | Width lies mostly in lightly doped side | | 4 | Side-by-Side Widths | $x_n = \dfrac{N_A}{N_A+N_D} W$, $\quad x_p = \dfrac{N_D}{N_A+N_D} W$ | From charge neutrality | | 5 | Maximum Electric Field | $\vert E_{max} \vert = \dfrac{q N_D x_n}{\varepsilon_s} = \dfrac{2(V_{bi}-V)}{W}$ | V/cm | | 6 | Junction Capacitance (per area) | $C_j' = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s}{W} = \sqrt{\dfrac{q \varepsilon_s N_A N_D}{2(N_A+N_D)(V_{bi}-V)}}$ | F/cm² | | 7 | Diode Equation (Shockley) | $I = I_S \left( e^{V/V_T} - 1 \right)$ | $V$ is **applied** voltage (forward = +) | | 8 | Reverse Saturation Current | $I_S = q A \left( \dfrac{D_p n_{i}^{2}}{L_p N_D} + \dfrac{D_n n_{i}^{2}}{L_n N_A} \right)$ | A (area-dependent) | | 9 | Breakdown Voltage (avalanche) | $V_{BR} \propto \dfrac{1}{N_B}$ | Lighter doping → higher $V_{BR}$ | | 10 | Depletion Approximation | $\rho = q N_D$ for $0 < x < x_n$; $\rho = -q N_A$ for $-x_p < x < 0$ | Charges on ionized dopants only | > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - The **junction capacitance $C_j$** is the parasitic "MOSFET source-bulk" capacitance that limits high-frequency / digital switching speed in CMOS. Designers *minimize* it by reducing doping (SOI, retrograde wells) or by using **Schottky** or **hetero-junction** contacts. > - The **built-in potential $V_{bi}$** sets the threshold voltage of a MOSFET ($V_{th} \approx V_{th0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$ — where $2\phi_F$ depends on $V_{bi}$). > - **Breakdown voltage** dictates the **drain-extended MOS (DEMOS)** and **LDMOS** device designs used in power-management ICs for automotive/industrial applications. # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > Every line below is **algebraically explicit**. No step is skipped. This is the level of detail KTU external examiners expect in a 7-mark derivation answer. --- ## 🧮 Derivation 1 — Built-in Potential $V_{bi}$ **Setup:** We equate the **drift** and **diffusion** components of electron current at equilibrium. Alternatively, we use the fact that $E_F$ is constant, so the separation of $E_F$ from $E_i$ on each side gives $V_{bi}$. **Step 1:** On the P-side, the intrinsic Fermi level $E_i$ lies *below* the Fermi level $E_F$ by an amount $q\phi_p$ where hole concentration is $p_{p0} = N_A$. $$p_{p0} = n_i \, e^{(E_i - E_F)/kT} = N_A$$ Solving: $$E_F - E_i = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = q\phi_p$$ **Step 2:** On the N-side, $E_i$ lies *above* $E_F$ by $q\phi_n$ where electron concentration is $n_{n0} = N_D$. $$n_{n0} = n_i \, e^{(E_F - E_i)/kT} = N_D$$ Solving: $$E_F - E_i = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right) = -q\phi_n$$ **Step 3:** The total band-bending $qV_{bi}$ equals the sum of these two separations: $$qV_{bi} = (E_F - E_i)_N + (E_i - E_F)_P = kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right) + kT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Combine the logarithms: $$V_{bi} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$$ > **KTU Board-Valuation Style:** For full marks, state *clearly* the assumption that $n_i \ll N_A, N_D$ (i.e., non-degenerate doping). Then plug in numbers and report in **Volts** (not eV!). --- ## 🧮 Derivation 2 — Depletion Width $W$ **Step 1 — Poisson's Equation in Depletion Region (1D, P-side):** For $-x_p \le x \le 0$, charge density $\rho = -qN_A$. So: $$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = \frac{-qN_A}{\varepsilon_s}$$ **Step 2 — Integrate once** (electric field): $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -E(x) = -\frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_s}(x + x_p) + C_1$$ Boundary: at $x = -x_p$, $\vec{E} = 0$ (edge of depletion region). So $C_1 = 0$. **Step 3 — Integrate again** (potential): $$V(x) = -\frac{qN_A}{2\varepsilon_s}(x + x_p)^2 + C_2$$ Similarly on the N-side, $0 \le x \le x_n$: $$V(x) = -\frac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s}(x_n - x)^2 + C_3$$ **Step 4 — Match potentials at $x = 0$** and set $V(x_p) = 0$, $V(x_n) = V_{bi}$ (or $V_{bi} - V$ for applied bias $V$): $$V_{bi} - V = \frac{qN_A x_p^{2}}{2\varepsilon_s} + \frac{qN_D x_n^{2}}{2\varepsilon_s}$$ **Step 5 — Apply Charge Neutrality** $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$: $$x_p = \frac{N_D}{N_A} x_n$$ Substitute: $$V_{bi} - V = \frac{q}{2\varepsilon_s}\left(N_A \cdot \frac{N_D^{2}}{N_A^{2}} x_n^{2} + N_D x_n^{2}\right) = \frac{q N_D x_n^{2}}{2\varepsilon_s}\left(1 + \frac{N_D}{N_A}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $x_n$:** $$x_n = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q N_D} \cdot \frac{N_A}{N_A + N_D}}$$ **Step 7 — Total width** $W = x_n + x_p$: $$W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q} \cdot \frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}}$$ **Step 8 — Numerical evaluation pattern (KTU 7-mark):** If $N_A = 10^{18}$ cm$^{-3}$, $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$, $V_{bi} = 0.75$ V, $V_{reverse} = 5$ V: $$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 5.75}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \cdot \frac{1.01 \times 10^{18}}{10^{34}}}$$ After careful arithmetic (deferred to part-2(b) numerical): $$W \approx 0.86 \; \mu\text{m}$$ --- ## 🧮 Derivation 3 — Junction Capacitance (per unit area) **Step 1:** Capacitance per area is defined as $C_j' = \dfrac{dQ'}{dV}$ where $Q'$ is the magnitude of stored charge per area on the depletion side. **Step 2:** Charge on N-side: $Q' = q N_D x_n$. Differentiate with respect to applied voltage $V$ (note $V$ is forward bias, $V_{bi}-V$ in formula). $$x_n = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q N_D} \cdot \frac{N_A}{N_A + N_D}}$$ $$\frac{dx_n}{dV} = -\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s}{q N_D} \cdot \frac{N_A}{N_A + N_D}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{V_{bi} - V}}$$ **Step 3:** $$\frac{dQ'}{dV} = qN_D \frac{dx_n}{dV} = -\sqrt{\frac{q \varepsilon_s N_A N_D}{2(N_A+N_D)(V_{bi}-V)}}$$ **Step 4 — Take magnitude, identify $C_j' = \vert dQ'/dV \vert$:** $$C_j' = \sqrt{\frac{q \varepsilon_s N_A N_D}{2(N_A+N_D)(V_{bi}-V)}} = \frac{\varepsilon_s}{W}$$ **Step 5 — Total capacitance** for an area $A$: $$C_j = \frac{\varepsilon_s A}{W} = A \sqrt{\frac{q \varepsilon_s N_A N_D}{2(N_A+N_D)(V_{bi}-V)}}$$ > **One-sided junction simplification** (e.g., $N^+P$ with $N_A \gg N_D$, $\frac{N_A N_D}{N_A+N_D} \approx N_D$): $$C_j' = \sqrt{\frac{q \varepsilon_s N_B}{2(V_{bi}-V)}}$$ This is the form used in **CV profiling** of doping profiles in process control. # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > Mermaid diagrams use the `flowchart` and `graph` directives which require **alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters**. All labels are plain uppercase text — no markdown bold/italics inside the quoted label strings. --- ## 🗺️ Diagram 1 — PN Junction Physical Structure & Depletion Region ```mermaid flowchart LR A["P TYPE REGION
NA ACCEPTORS
NA = 1E18 CM3"]:::pSide B["DEPLETION REGION
XP ON P SIDE
XN ON N SIDE
NO FREE CARRIERS"]:::dep C["N TYPE REGION
ND DONORS
ND = 1E16 CM3"]:::nSide D["NEGATIVE ION
NA MINUS FIXED"]:::neg E["POSITIVE ION
ND PLUS FIXED"]:::pos F["BUILT IN FIELD E
POINTS N TO P
MAGNITUDE qNDxN EPS"]:::field G["Vbi BARRIER
APPROX 0.75 V"]:::barrier A --- B B --- C D --- B E --- B F --- B G --- B classDef pSide fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef nSide fill:#E0F2FF,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dep fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#556B2F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef neg fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef pos fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef field fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 classDef barrier fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 ``` --- ## 🗺️ Diagram 2 — Equilibrium Energy Band Diagram Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["P SIDE BAND EDGE
EC P AND EV P
BAND BENT DOWN"]:::left S2["METALLURGIC JUNCTION
X EQUALS ZERO
EF CONTINUOUS"]:::mid S3["N SIDE BAND EDGE
EC N AND EV N
BAND BENT UP"]:::right S4["EF POSITION P SIDE
EF NEAR EV
DISTANCE QPHIP"]:::fermiP S5["EF POSITION N SIDE
EF NEAR EC
DISTANCE QPHIN"]:::fermiN S6["TOTAL BAND BENDING
Q Vbi EQUALS Q PHI P + Q PHI N"]:::total S7["BARRIER FOR ELECTRONS
FROM N TO P EQUALS Q Vbi"]:::barrierE S8["BARRIER FOR HOLES
FROM P TO N EQUALS Q Vbi"]:::barrierH S1 --> S2 --> S3 S4 --> S2 S5 --> S2 S4 --> S6 S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7 S6 --> S8 classDef left fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#556B2F,color:#000 classDef right fill:#E0F2FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef fermiP fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef fermiN fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef total fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 classDef barrierE fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 classDef barrierH fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 ``` --- ## 🗺️ Diagram 3 — Bias Mode Comparison Flow (Forward vs Reverse) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["APPLY VOLTAGE V TO PN JUNCTION"]:::start A --> B{"SIGN OF V"} B -- "V GREATER THAN 0" --> C["FORWARD BIAS MODE
REDUCES BARRIER TO Q Vbi MINUS Q V
DEPLETION WIDTH DECREASES"]:::forward B -- "V LESS THAN 0" --> D["REVERSE BIAS MODE
INCREASES BARRIER TO Q Vbi PLUS Q ABS V
DEPLETION WIDTH INCREASES"]:::reverse C --> E["MAJORITY DIFFUSION CURRENT
EXPONENTIAL WITH V
I EQUALS Is EXP V OVER Vt MINUS 1"]:::fcur C --> F["ON STATE FOR DIODE
RECTIFIER ON
VF APPROX 0.7 V FOR SI"]:::fon D --> G["MINORITY DRIFT CURRENT
CONSTANT REVERSE SATURATION Is
NANO AMP TO MICRO AMP"]:::rcur D --> H["OFF STATE FOR DIODE
RECTIFIER BLOCKS
UNTIL BREAKDOWN VBR"]:::roff classDef start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef forward fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef reverse fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef fcur fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 classDef rcur fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 classDef fon fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef roff fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 ``` --- ## 🗺️ Diagram 4 — Functional Block Architecture of a PN Junction as a VLSI Building Block ```mermaid flowchart LR IN["INPUT TERMINAL
P SIDE CONTACT
ALUMINUM METAL"]:::in DEV["CORE PN JUNCTION
DEPLETION REGION
BUILT IN Vbi"]:::core OUT["OUTPUT TERMINAL
N SIDE CONTACT
ALUMINUM METAL"]:::out F1["FUNCTION 1 RECTIFICATION
AC TO DC CONVERSION"]:::f1 F2["FUNCTION 2 VOLTAGE REFERENCE
ZENER OR BANDGAP"]:::f2 F3["FUNCTION 3 CHARGE STORAGE
Cj EQUALS EPS A OVER W"]:::f3 F4["FUNCTION 4 JUNCTION ISOLATION
CMOS WELL TIE"]:::f4 IN --> DEV --> OUT DEV --> F1 DEV --> F2 DEV --> F3 DEV --> F4 F4 --> M["MOSFET BODY DIODE
USED IN POWER CMOS"]:::m F3 --> CAP["VARACTOR DIODE
VCO TUNING"]:::cap classDef in fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef core fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#556B2F,color:#000 classDef out fill:#E0F2FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef f1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 classDef f2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 classDef f3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef f4 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef m fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef cap fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 ``` # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### ❓ Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define built-in potential of a PN junction. Why is it independent of the applied reverse bias voltage?** **✅ Model Answer (3 marks):** The **built-in potential ($V_{bi}$)** of a PN junction is the internal electrostatic potential barrier that arises at thermal equilibrium due to the diffusion of majority carriers across the metallurgical junction and the resulting exposure of ionized dopant atoms. Mathematically: $$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$$ It depends only on the **doping concentrations** $N_A, N_D$ and the **intrinsic carrier density** $n_i$ (i.e., on material and doping, not on external bias). It is independent of the applied reverse bias because $V_{bi}$ is a property of the *equilibrium* junction; the applied bias only modifies the *net* barrier to $V_{bi} - V$, not the contact potential itself. **(3 marks)** --- ### ❓ Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Distinguish between avalanche breakdown and Zener breakdown in a PN junction.** **✅ Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Zener Breakdown | Avalanche Breakdown | |---|---|---| | Mechanism | **Quantum tunneling** of electrons from valence band of P to conduction band of N across a thin barrier | **Impact ionization** — carriers gain kinetic energy and create e-h pairs by collision | | Doping | **Heavily doped** junction (narrow depletion width $\sim 10$ nm) | **Lightly doped** junction (wide depletion width) | | $V_{BR}$ | **Low**, typically $< 5$ V | **Higher**, typically $> 6$ V | | Temp. coeff. | **Negative** (voltage decreases with T) | **Positive** (voltage increases with T) | | Typical use | Voltage reference in bandgap circuits | High-voltage clamping, ESD protection | **(3 marks)** --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Every Module question in the End Semester Exam (ESE) carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice**. You must answer *one* of the two full 14-mark questions per module. Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts: usually **(a)** a conceptual/derivation part and **(b)** a numerical/analytical part. --- ### ❓ Question B.A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]` **Question B.A (14 marks):** **(a)** With the help of necessary diagrams, explain the formation of the depletion region in a PN junction diode at equilibrium. Derive the expression for the **built-in potential** $V_{bi}$. (7 marks) **(b)** A silicon PN junction at $T = 300$ K has $N_A = 2 \times 10^{18}$ cm$^{-3}$ and $N_D = 5 \times 10^{15}$ cm$^{-3}$. Calculate: - (i) The built-in potential $V_{bi}$ - (ii) The depletion widths $x_n$ and $x_p$ on each side at equilibrium - (iii) The maximum electric field $\vert E_{max} \vert$ Given: $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$, $\varepsilon_s = 1.036 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm, $V_T = 0.0259$ V. (7 marks) #### ✅ Model Solution to B.A(a) — Conceptual + Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Diagram & Physical Picture (2 marks):** At the instant of contact, the large concentration gradient of holes (P→N) and electrons (N→P) drives diffusion. As carriers cross and recombine, they leave behind a region near the junction that is depleted of mobile carriers but contains a space charge of ionized dopants: $N_A^{-}$ on the P-side, $N_D^{+}$ on the N-side. A built-in electric field $\vec{E}$ directed from N to P opposes further diffusion. **Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition (1 mark):** At thermal equilibrium, drift current = diffusion current, net current = 0, and the Fermi level $E_F$ is constant across the device. **Step 3 — Derivation of $V_{bi}$ (4 marks):** Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3** of these notes. The band picture shows $E_i$ on the P-side lies *below* $E_F$ by $q\phi_p$ where $p_{p0} = N_A = n_i e^{q\phi_p/kT}$, giving $\phi_p = V_T \ln(N_A/n_i)$. Similarly on the N-side, $\phi_n = V_T \ln(N_D/n_i)$. The total band-bending is: $$V_{bi} = \phi_p + \phi_n = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$$ State the result and physical interpretation. **(7 marks total)** #### ✅ Model Solution to B.A(b) — Numerical (7 marks) **(i) Built-in potential:** $$V_{bi} = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{2\times 10^{18} \times 5\times 10^{15}}{(1.5\times 10^{10})^{2}}\right)$$ $$= 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{34}}{2.25 \times 10^{20}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(4.44 \times 10^{13})$$ $$= 0.0259 \times 31.72 = 0.821 \text{ V} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ **(ii) Depletion widths:** Since $N_A \gg N_D$, the depletion region lies almost entirely in the N-side (one-sided $N^+P$ junction approximation is *not* perfect here, $N_A/N_D = 400$, but for illustration use full formula): $$W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \cdot \frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A N_D}}$$ $$\frac{N_A+N_D}{N_A N_D} = \frac{2 \times 10^{18} + 5\times 10^{15}}{(2\times 10^{18})(5\times 10^{15})} = \frac{2.005 \times 10^{18}}{10^{34}} = 2.005 \times 10^{-16} \text{ cm}^3$$ $$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.821}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \times 2.005 \times 10^{-16}}$$ $$= \sqrt{1.063 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.005 \times 10^{-16}} = \sqrt{2.13 \times 10^{-24}} = 1.46 \times 10^{-12} \text{ cm}$$ $$W \approx 0.0146 \; \mu\text{m} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$ Side widths (charge neutrality $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$): $$x_n = \frac{N_A}{N_A+N_D} W \approx \frac{2 \times 10^{18}}{2.005 \times 10^{18}} \times 1.46 \times 10^{-12} \approx 1.46 \times 10^{-12} \text{ cm}$$ $$x_p = \frac{N_D}{N_A+N_D} W \approx \frac{5 \times 10^{15}}{2.005 \times 10^{18}} \times 1.46 \times 10^{-12} \approx 3.6 \times 10^{-15} \text{ cm}$$ Note that **$x_n \gg x_p$** because $N_D \ll N_A$. **(2 marks)** **(iii) Maximum electric field:** $$|E_{max}| = \frac{q N_D x_n}{\varepsilon_s} = \frac{1.6\times 10^{-19} \times 5\times 10^{15} \times 1.46\times 10^{-12}}{1.036\times 10^{-12}}$$ $$= \frac{1.168 \times 10^{-15}}{1.036 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.13 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V/cm} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Units trap:** $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$ but the formula uses $n_i^{2}$ in cm$^{-6}$. Watch the exponents — a single missing factor of 10 will cost 2 marks. > 2. **Mistaking $W$ for $x_n$ or $x_p$:** Always report $W$ as the *total* depletion width and *separately* give $x_n, x_p$. Many students write only $x_n$ and lose 1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting $V_{bi} - V$:** In *reverse* bias, replace $V_{bi}$ with $V_{bi} + \vert V_{rev} \vert$. In *forward* bias, use $V_{bi} - V_f$. > 4. **No units in final answer:** Always report with units (V, cm, V/cm). Answers without units lose 0.5–1 mark per sub-part. --- ### ❓ Question B.B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]` **Question B.B (14 marks):** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **depletion capacitance per unit area** of a PN junction. Show that the capacitance varies as $(V_{bi} - V)^{-1/2}$ for an abrupt junction. (7 marks) **(b)** A silicon PN junction diode has $N_A = 10^{17}$ cm$^{-3}$, $N_D = 10^{15}$ cm$^{-3}$, area $A = 100 \times 100 \; \mu\text{m}^2$, $V_{bi} = 0.75$ V. Calculate: - (i) The junction capacitance at zero bias - (ii) The capacitance when reverse-biased at 5 V - (iii) The cut-off frequency if the diode has a series resistance $R_S = 10 \; \Omega$ at $V_R = 5$ V Given: $\varepsilon_s = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm. (7 marks) #### ✅ Model Solution to B.B(a) — Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Define depletion capacitance** as the rate of change of depletion charge with applied voltage (1 mark): $$C_j = \left\vert \frac{dQ}{dV} \right\vert$$ **Step 2 — Express charge on N-side** (per unit area) using depletion width $x_n$ (1 mark): $$Q' = q N_D x_n$$ **Step 3 — Use the depletion width result** derived earlier (1 mark): $$x_n = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi}-V)}{q N_D} \cdot \frac{N_A}{N_A+N_D}}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate** with respect to $V$ (2 marks): $$\frac{dx_n}{dV} = -\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s}{q N_D} \cdot \frac{N_A}{N_A+N_D}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{V_{bi}-V}}$$ $$\frac{dQ'}{dV} = q N_D \frac{dx_n}{dV} = -\sqrt{\frac{q\varepsilon_s N_A N_D}{2(N_A+N_D)(V_{bi}-V)}}$$ **Step 5 — Magnitude gives $C_j'$** and identify the $(V_{bi}-V)^{-1/2}$ dependence (2 marks): $$C_j' = \frac{\varepsilon_s}{W} = \sqrt{\frac{q\varepsilon_s N_A N_D}{2(N_A+N_D)(V_{bi}-V)}} \propto (V_{bi}-V)^{-1/2}$$ > This is the famous "$1/\sqrt{V}$" law used in **CV profiling** of doping concentration in semiconductor process control. #### ✅ Model Solution to B.B(b) — Numerical (7 marks) **(i) Capacitance at zero bias:** Total depletion width: $$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.75}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \times \frac{1.01 \times 10^{17}}{10^{32}}}$$ $$= \sqrt{9.75 \times 10^{6} \times 1.01 \times 10^{-15}} = \sqrt{9.85 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 9.92 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}$$ Area: $A = (100 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 10^{-6}$ cm$^2$. $$C_j = \frac{\varepsilon_s A}{W} = \frac{1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-6}}{9.92 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-14} \text{ F} = 10.5 \text{ fF} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ **(ii) Capacitance at $V_R = 5$ V:** Using the $(V_{bi} + V_R)^{-1/2}$ scaling: $$\frac{C_j(V_R=5)}{C_j(0)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_{bi}}{V_{bi} + V_R}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.75}{5.75}} = 0.361$$ $$C_j(5\text{V}) = 0.361 \times 10.5 \text{ fF} = 3.79 \text{ fF} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ **(iii) Cut-off frequency** $f_c = 1/(2\pi R_S C_j)$: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 3.79 \times 10^{-15}} = \frac{1}{2.38 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 4.2 \text{ GHz} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for B.B:** > 1. **Use the correct bias in $V_{bi} - V$:** For reverse bias, $V$ is *negative*, so $V_{bi} - V = V_{bi} + \vert V_R \vert$. Students often write $V_{bi} - V_R$ which is *wrong* for reverse bias. > 2. **Don't confuse $\varepsilon_s$ with $\varepsilon_0$:** $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} = 1.036 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm. Mixing up gives an answer off by **11.7×**. > 3. **Area unit conversion:** $100 \; \mu\text{m} = 100 \times 10^{-4}$ cm. Forgetting the $10^{-4}$ factor costs 1 mark easily. > 4. **Cut-off frequency unit:** Report in Hz, kHz, MHz, or GHz — whichever is appropriate. Always state units in the final answer. --- ## 🎯 KTU Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam.** - 🔑 **Built-in potential:** $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^{2})$. **Always** depends on doping and temperature via $n_i(T)$. Doubling the doping of one side increases $V_{bi}$ by **only ~18 mV** at 300 K — not much! - 🔑 **Charge neutrality:** $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$. The depletion region is wider on the **lighter-doped** side. In a one-sided $N^+P$ diode, almost all of $W$ is on the **P-side**. - 🔑 **Total depletion width:** $W = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V) / q \cdot (N_A+N_D)/(N_A N_D)}$ — memorize this. - 🔑 **Junction capacitance:** $C_j = \varepsilon_s A / W \propto (V_{bi} - V)^{-1/2}$. **Smaller** in reverse bias, **larger** in forward bias. - 🔑 **Electric field profile:** Triangular — peaks at the metallurgical junction, falls linearly to zero at the depletion edges. Peak value $|E_{max}| = q N_D x_n / \varepsilon_s = 2(V_{bi}-V)/W$. - 🔑 **Diode equation:** $I = I_S(e^{V/V_T}-1)$. For $V \gg V_T$ (forward), $I \approx I_S e^{V/V_T}$ — every additional **60 mV** of forward bias **decouples** the current by a factor of 10. - 🔑 **Reverse saturation current** $I_S$ is *temperature-sensitive* (doubles per ~10 °C) but *voltage-insensitive*. It is *area-dependent*. - 🔑 **Breakdown:** Zener (tunneling, low $V_{BR}$, negative temp. coeff.) vs Avalanche (impact ionization, high $V_{BR}$, positive temp. coeff.). Cross-over ~5–6 V for Si. - 🔑 **VLSI Connection:** The PN junction appears in *every* MOSFET as source-bulk and drain-bulk diodes. Their **junction capacitance** is the dominant high-frequency / switching-speed limiter in deep-submicron CMOS. - 🔑 **Units Sanity Check:** Always carry V, cm, F/cm, V/cm. A numerical answer without units = automatic half-mark deduction. - 🔑 **KTU Hot Topics:** "Derive $V_{bi}$" and "Derive $C_j$" appear in nearly every VLSI Design paper. Practice the algebra until it's muscle memory. > 💡 **Pro Tip for KTU 2024:** When a question says "with a neat diagram", always include the **energy band diagram** *and* the **depletion region cross-section**. Examiners give **0.5–1 extra mark** for a clear, labeled band diagram showing $E_C, E_V, E_F$ and the band-bending $qV_{bi}$.

topics.title: MOS Transistor Theory and Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MOS Transistor Theory and Operation**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MOS Transistor (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor / MOSFET):**
> A four-terminal, voltage-controlled, unipolar semiconductor device in which the current flow between two terminals (Source and Drain) is regulated by an electric field generated by the voltage applied to a third terminal (Gate), which is insulated from the conducting channel by a thin layer of silicon dioxide (SiO₂).

A standard **n-channel MOS (NMOS)** transistor consists of:
- **n⁺ Source** and **n⁺ Drain** regions formed in a **p-type silicon substrate** (Body/Bulk).
- A **polysilicon (or metal) Gate** electrode positioned above the channel region.
- A thin **gate oxide (SiO₂)** layer of thickness **t_ox** (typically **2 nm – 100 nm**) insulating the gate from the channel.
- A **Body (Substrate/Bulk)** terminal that is typically tied to ground (0 V) for NMOS and to V_DD for PMOS.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic is the **foundational pillar** of Module 1. Mastery of the threshold voltage equation, the quadratic I-V relationship, and the body effect is mandatory, as direct 14-mark derivation questions appear frequently in End Semester Examinations (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Faucet" Model

Imagine the MOSFET as a **pressurized water tap (faucet)** in your kitchen:
- The **Gate** voltage is your hand turning the knob — you don't touch the water; you only control the *flow indirectly*.
- The **Drain** is the high-pressure outlet pipe, and the **Source** is the water exit point.
- The **Gate Oxide** is the sealed rubber gasket between your hand and the water — it prevents any current from flowing into the gate, ensuring **infinite DC input resistance**.
- The **Body (Substrate)** is the supporting base of the pipe; if the surrounding water pressure (body bias) changes, the effort required to turn the knob (threshold voltage) also changes — this is the **Body Effect**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** A MOSFET never draws steady-state gate current. It is a **voltage-controlled current source** that consumes negligible static power, which is precisely why billions of MOSFETs can be packed onto a single modern chip.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gate oxide thickness | $t_{ox}$ | **2 nm – 50 nm** |
| Gate capacitance per unit area | $C_{ox}$ | **~1 – 10 fF/μm²** |
| Silicon permittivity (relative) | $\varepsilon_{si}$ | **11.7** |
| SiO₂ permittivity (relative) | $\varepsilon_{ox}$ | **3.9** |
| Free electron charge magnitude | $q$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** |
| Thermal voltage (at 300 K) | $V_{T} = \frac{kT}{q}$ | **~25.85 mV** |
| Room temperature | $T$ | **300 K** |
| Intrinsic carrier concentration (Si) | $n_i$ | **~1.5 × 10¹⁰ cm⁻³** |
| Substrate doping (typical p-type) | $N_A$ | **10¹⁵ – 10¹⁷ cm⁻³** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Notation:** In this module, $V_{T}$ denotes the **threshold voltage** of the MOSFET. The thermal voltage $\frac{kT}{q}$ is conventionally written as $v_t$ (lowercase) to avoid confusion. We will adhere strictly to this convention.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The MOS Capacitor — The Heart of the Device

Before analyzing a transistor, we must understand the **MOS Capacitor**, which is the gate region viewed in isolation. It is a parallel-plate capacitor where:
- **Top plate** = Gate (polysilicon/metal)
- **Dielectric** = SiO₂ (thickness $t_{ox}$, permittivity $\varepsilon_{ox}$)
- **Bottom plate** = Silicon substrate (the "channel" surface)

The gate oxide capacitance per unit area is:

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_{r,ox}}{t_{ox}}$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the vacuum permittivity.

### 2.2 The Three Operating Regimes of the MOS Surface

When a voltage $V_{GB}$ (gate-to-body) is applied, the semiconductor surface responds in three distinct ways:

**Step 1 — Flat-Band Condition ($V_{GB} = V_{FB}$)**
- No band bending. The applied voltage exactly cancels the work-function difference $\phi_{MS}$ plus any fixed oxide charge.
- $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \frac{Q_{fc}}{C_{ox}}$

**Step 2 — Accumulation ($V_{GB} < V_{FB}$, for p-substrate)**
- Holes are *attracted* to the oxide-silicon interface.
- Surface is enriched with majority carriers.
- Behaves like a slightly larger oxide capacitor.

**Step 3 — Depletion ($V_{FB} < V_{GB} < V_{TN}$)**
- Holes are *repelled* from the interface, leaving behind a region of uncovered negative acceptor ions.
- A depletion region of width $W_{dep}$ forms:

$$W_{dep} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_{si} \phi_s}{q N_A}}$$

**Step 4 — Inversion ($V_{GB} > V_{TN}$)**
- The surface potential $\phi_s$ exceeds **$2\phi_F$** (where $\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\frac{N_A}{n_i}$).
- Minority carrier electrons form a thin conducting layer at the surface — this **IS the channel** of the MOSFET.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Voltage (V_TN) Definition:** The minimum gate-to-source voltage required to create an inversion layer (surface electron density equals bulk hole density). It is the single most important parameter of a MOSFET.

### 2.3 The Threshold Voltage Equation (Full Form)

$$V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\, q\, N_A\, (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

Breaking this down:
- $V_{FB}$ — the **flat-band voltage** (process-dependent offset).
- $2\phi_F$ — the **surface potential** required for strong inversion.
- $\frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\, q\, N_A\, (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$ — the **voltage drop across the depletion region** at the onset of strong inversion.

### 2.4 The Body Effect — When the Substrate Isn't Grounded

When the source and body are *not* at the same potential, the threshold voltage shifts. For an NMOS with source-bulk voltage $V_{SB}$:

$$V_{TN}(V_{SB}) = V_{TN0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

where:
- $V_{TN0}$ = threshold voltage at $V_{SB} = 0$.
- $\gamma$ = **body-effect coefficient**, given by:

$$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\, q\, N_A}}{C_{ox}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in KTU exams?** Body effect is critical in **source-coupled logic, cascode amplifiers, and dynamic logic**, and frequently appears as a 7-mark sub-question.

### 2.5 I–V Characteristics — The Three Regions of Operation

For a long-channel NMOS in **saturation**, the drain current follows the famous **square-law**:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TN})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

For a long-channel NMOS in the **linear (triode)** region:

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TN}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The transition from triode to saturation occurs at the **saturation voltage** $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}$.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Region | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | F/m² |
| 2 | $\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | Fermi potential of p-substrate | V |
| 3 | $V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$ | Threshold voltage (NMOS) | V |
| 4 | $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | Body-effect coefficient | V$^{1/2}$ |
| 5 | $V_{TN}(V_{SB}) = V_{TN0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F+V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | Threshold voltage with body bias | V |
| 6 | $I_{D,lin} = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TN})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | Triode-region current | A |
| 7 | $I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TN})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | Saturation-region current | A |
| 8 | $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}$ | Saturation drain voltage | V |
| 9 | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TN})$ | Transconductance (triode) | S |
| 10 | $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L} I_D}$ | Transconductance (saturation) | S |
| 11 | $r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | Output resistance (channel-length modulation) | Ω |
| 12 | $I_{sub} = I_0\, e^{\frac{V_{GS}-V_{TN}}{n\, v_t}}\!\left(1 - e^{-\frac{V_{DS}}{v_t}}\right)$ | Subthreshold current | A |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical bar caution in tables:** All absolute-value and conditional notations use `\vert` or `\mid` — never the bare pipe `|` — to prevent breaking the markdown table parser.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Logic (CMOS):** The square-law saturation model governs the **drive strength** of an inverter's pull-down network — directly determining **propagation delay** and **dynamic power**.
- **Analog Design (RF PAs, Op-amps):** The transconductance $g_m$ sets the **gain**, while the output resistance $r_o$ sets the **intrinsic gain** ($g_m r_o$).
- **Memory (DRAM, SRAM):** The MOSFET acts as an access transistor whose subthreshold leakage dictates the **data retention time**.
- **Power Electronics:** Short-channel MOSFETs handle **kW-level power** in voltage regulators; understanding $R_{on}$ vs. $V_{GS}$ is essential.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Threshold Voltage $V_{TN}$

We start from Gauss's law applied at the oxide-semiconductor interface. The total charge induced in the silicon must equal the charge on the gate plate:

$$Q_G = -Q_S$$

At the verge of strong inversion, the depletion region reaches its **maximum** width, and the depletion charge is:

$$Q_{B,max} = -q N_A W_{T,max}$$

with

$$W_{T,max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_{si} (2\phi_F)}{q N_A}}$$

Substituting and applying $Q_G = C_{ox}(V_{GB} - \phi_{MS} - 2\phi_F)$:

$$C_{ox}\,(V_{GB} - \phi_{MS} - 2\phi_F) = q N_A \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}(2\phi_F)}{q N_A}}$$

$$C_{ox}\,(V_{GB} - \phi_{MS} - 2\phi_F) = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}$$

Solving for $V_{GB}$ at the threshold point and noting $V_{TN} = V_{GS} = V_{GB}$ when $V_S = 0$:

$$V_{TN} = \phi_{MS} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

Incorporating fixed oxide charge $Q_{fc}$, the flat-band voltage is $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \frac{Q_{fc}}{C_{ox}}$, yielding:

$$\boxed{V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Body Effect on Threshold Voltage

With source at potential $V_S$ above the grounded bulk, the surface must invert at a potential $2\phi_F + V_{SB}$. Re-evaluating the depletion charge with this enhanced surface potential:

$$Q_B(V_{SB}) = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$

The new threshold voltage is:

$$V_{TN}(V_{SB}) = V_{FB} + (2\phi_F + V_{SB}) + \frac{Q_B(V_{SB})}{C_{ox}}$$

Subtracting the zero-bias expression $V_{TN0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$:

$$V_{TN}(V_{SB}) - V_{TN0} = V_{SB} + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}}{C_{ox}}\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F+V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

$$V_{TN}(V_{SB}) = V_{TN0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F+V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

where $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}}{C_{ox}}$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drain Current in the Triode (Linear) Region

The channel charge density (per unit area) at any point $y$ along the channel is:

$$Q_n(y) = -C_{ox}\!\left[V_{GS} - V_{TN} - V(y)\right]$$

where $V(y)$ is the local channel potential (varying from $0$ at source to $V_{DS}$ at drain). The drift current at position $y$ is:

$$I_D = -W \mu_n Q_n(y) \frac{dV(y)}{dy}$$

Treating $I_D$ as constant along the channel (no gate current):

$$I_D \, dy = W \mu_n C_{ox}\!\left[V_{GS} - V_{TN} - V(y)\right] dV(y)$$

Integrating from $y=0$ to $y=L$ and $V=0$ to $V=V_{DS}$:

$$I_D \int_0^L dy = W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_0^{V_{DS}} \!\left[V_{GS} - V_{TN} - V\right] dV$$

$$I_D L = W \mu_n C_{ox}\!\left[(V_{GS} - V_{TN})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

$$\boxed{I_{D,lin} = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TN})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Saturation Current via Pinch-Off

As $V_{DS}$ increases, the channel narrows at the drain end. Pinch-off occurs when $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}$. Substituting $V_{DS,sat}$ into the triode expression:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TN})^2 - \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{TN})^2}{2}\right]$$

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\cdot\frac{(V_{GS}-V_{TN})^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TN})^2}$$

Including **channel-length modulation** (the effective channel shortens as $V_{DS}$ grows beyond saturation):

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TN})^2(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** An NMOS transistor is fabricated with $t_{ox} = 10$ nm, $N_A = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, $\phi_{MS} = -0.9$ V, $Q_{fc} = 0$, and $V_{SB} = 2$ V. Find $V_{TN}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $C_{ox}$:**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 3.9}{10 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.45 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{F/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\phi_F$:**

$$\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.0259 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{16}}{1.5\times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.0259 \cdot \ln(6.67\times 10^{5})$$

$$\phi_F = 0.0259 \cdot 13.41 = 0.347\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{FB}$:**

$$V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \frac{Q_{fc}}{C_{ox}} = -0.9 - 0 = -0.9\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $V_{TN0}$ (at $V_{SB}=0$):**

$$V_{TN0} = -0.9 + 2(0.347) + \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot 11.7 \cdot 8.854\times10^{-12} \cdot 1.6\times10^{-19} \cdot 10^{22} \cdot 2(0.347)}}{3.45\times 10^{-3}}$$

$$V_{TN0} = -0.9 + 0.694 + \frac{\sqrt{2.30 \times 10^{-8}}}{3.45\times 10^{-3}} = -0.206 + 1.39 = \mathbf{0.484\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply body effect with $V_{SB}=2$ V:**

First, $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}}{C_{ox}} = \frac{\sqrt{2.30 \times 10^{-8}}}{3.45 \times 10^{-3}} = \mathbf{1.39\ \text{V}^{1/2}}$

$$V_{TN}(V_{SB}=2) = 0.484 + 1.39\!\left(\sqrt{0.694 + 2} - \sqrt{0.694}\right)$$

$$V_{TN} = 0.484 + 1.39\!\left(\sqrt{2.694} - \sqrt{0.694}\right) = 0.484 + 1.39(1.641 - 0.833)$$

$$V_{TN} = 0.484 + 1.124 = \mathbf{1.608\ \text{V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice how $V_{TN}$ increased by **~3.3×** due to body effect. This is why **source-follower stages and cascode transistors** see significant threshold shifts if $V_{SB} \neq 0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Structure of an NMOS Transistor (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph crossSection[NMOS Cross Section - Side View]
        node1[Source Terminal - n+ region] --> node2[Channel - Inversion Layer]
        node3[Gate Terminal - Polysilicon] --> node4[Gate Oxide - SiO2]
        node2 --> node5[Drain Terminal - n+ region]
        node4 --> node2
        node6[Body Bulk - p-type Substrate] -.-> node2
        node7[Source] -. Contact .- node1
        node8[Drain] -. Contact .- node5
        node9[Gate Contact] -. Metal .- node3
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted connections ($-.$) represent the **non-conducting** paths: the gate oxide blocks DC, while the body tie provides a reverse-biased p-n junction isolation from source/drain.

### 4.2 MOS Capacitor Operating Regimes — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[VGB applied to gate] --> nodeB{VGB vs Flatband VFB}
    nodeB -- VGB less than VFB --> nodeC[Accumulation - Holes at surface]
    nodeB -- VFB less than VGB less than VTN --> nodeD[Depletion - Ionized acceptors]
    nodeB -- VGB greater than VTN --> nodeE[Inversion - Electron channel formed]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Capacitance C equal to Cox]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Capacitance C drops - Series depletion]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Capacitance returns to Cox - Strong inversion]
```

### 4.3 I-V Characteristics — Operating Region Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Apply VGS greater than VTN and sweep VDS] --> cut{VDS compared to VGS minus VTN}
    cut -- VDS less than VGS minus VTN --> triode[Triode Linear Region - Channel intact, I_D rises with V_DS]
    cut -- VDS equal to VGS minus VTN --> pinch[Pinch off - Channel pinched at drain end]
    cut -- VDS greater than VGS minus VTN --> sat[Saturation Region - I_D approximately constant - channel length modulation via lambda]
    pinch --> sat
    triode --> region1[Slope controlled by mobility and Cox]
    sat --> region2[Current scales with VGS minus VTN squared]
```

### 4.4 Threshold Voltage Composition (Functional Decomposition)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeV[V Total Threshold Voltage VTN] --> nodeA[Flatband Voltage VFB]
    nodeV --> nodeB[2 times Fermi Potential - 2 phiF]
    nodeV --> nodeC[Depletion Region Voltage Drop]
    nodeA --> nodeA1[Workfunction difference phiMS]
    nodeA --> nodeA2[Fixed oxide charge contribution]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Depends on NA doping]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Depends on Cox - inverse of t_ox]
```

### 4.5 Subthreshold Conduction — Leakage Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS[Source] --> nodeCh[Weak Inversion Channel - VGS less than VTN]
    nodeCh --> nodeD[Drain]
    nodeTh[Thermal Energy kT] --> nodeCh
    nodeDif[Diffusion dominated transport] --> nodeCh
    nodeCh --> nodeLeak[Exponential leakage current I_sub]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define threshold voltage of a MOSFET. List the three factors that influence its value.*

**Model Answer:**
The threshold voltage ($V_{TN}$) of an NMOS transistor is defined as the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to create a conducting inversion layer (channel) between the source and drain at the oxide-silicon interface. Below $V_{TN}$, the transistor is in cutoff; above $V_{TN}$, significant current flows.

**Three influencing factors:**
1. **Gate oxide thickness ($t_{ox}$):** Thinner oxide ⇒ higher $C_{ox}$ ⇒ lower $V_{TN}$.
2. **Substrate doping ($N_A$):** Higher doping ⇒ higher depletion charge ⇒ higher $V_{TN}$.
3. **Source-to-body voltage ($V_{SB}$):** Larger $V_{SB}$ ⇒ stronger body effect ⇒ higher $V_{TN}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between the triode and saturation regions of operation of an NMOS transistor.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Triode (Linear) Region | Saturation Region |
|---|---|---|
| Condition | $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TN}$ | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TN}$ |
| Channel | Continuous, intact | Pinched off at drain end |
| $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ | Approximately linear (resistor-like) | Nearly constant (current source) |
| Governing equation | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TN})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | $I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TN})^2$ |
| Primary use | Switches, pass transistors, analog resistors | Amplifiers, current sources, active loads |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the threshold voltage of an NMOS transistor, clearly explaining each term in the final equation. State the typical values for silicon at 300 K. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an NMOS device, $N_A = 5 \times 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, $t_{ox} = 20$ nm, $\phi_{MS} = -0.85$ V, $Q_{fc} = 2 \times 10^{-8}$ C/cm², and $V_{SB} = 1.5$ V. Compute $V_{TN}$ at $V_{SB} = 0$ and $V_{SB} = 1.5$ V. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Define the threshold voltage.**
The threshold voltage is the gate voltage at which the surface electron concentration equals the bulk hole concentration, marking the onset of strong inversion.

**Step 2 — Apply charge neutrality.**
Charge on the gate = charge in the silicon (depletion + inversion). At threshold, the inversion charge is just starting, so we equate gate charge to the maximum depletion charge.

**Step 3 — Express the depletion charge at strong inversion:**

$$Q_{B,max} = -q N_A W_{T,max} = -\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}$$

**Step 4 — Express the gate voltage as a sum of drops:**

$$V_{TN} = V_{FB} + \phi_s + \frac{\vert Q_{B,max}\vert}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ and $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \frac{Q_{fc}}{C_{ox}}$:**

$$\boxed{V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}}$$

**Step 6 — Typical values at 300 K:**
- $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \cdot \varepsilon_0$
- $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \cdot \varepsilon_0$
- $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³
- $\frac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85$ mV

**Step 7 — Each term explained:**
- $V_{FB}$: process-dependent offset from workfunction difference and oxide charge.
- $2\phi_F$: the surface potential needed to make $n_s = p_{bulk}$.
- $\frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$: voltage drop across the depletion region at strong inversion.

---

**Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $C_{ox}$:**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{3.9 \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{20 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.726 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{F/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\phi_F$:**

$$\phi_F = 0.0259 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.0259 \cdot 15.02 = 0.389\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{FB}$:**

$$V_{FB} = -0.85 - \frac{2 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 10^{-4}}{1.726 \times 10^{-3}} = -0.85 - 1.16 \times 10^{-9} \approx -0.85\ \text{V}$$

(For $Q_{fc}$ in C/cm², conversion to C/m²: multiply by $10^4$.)

**Step 4 — Compute $V_{TN0}$ (at $V_{SB}=0$):**

$$V_{TN0} = -0.85 + 2(0.389) + \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot 11.7 \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 5 \times 10^{22} \cdot 2(0.389)}}{1.726 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$V_{TN0} = -0.85 + 0.778 + \frac{\sqrt{1.295 \times 10^{-7}}}{1.726 \times 10^{-3}} = -0.072 + 0.660 = \mathbf{0.588\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute body-effect coefficient $\gamma$:**

$$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{1.295 \times 10^{-7}}}{1.726 \times 10^{-3}} = \mathbf{0.660\ \text{V}^{1/2}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $V_{TN}$ at $V_{SB} = 1.5$ V:**

$$V_{TN}(1.5) = 0.588 + 0.660\!\left(\sqrt{0.778 + 1.5} - \sqrt{0.778}\right)$$

$$V_{TN}(1.5) = 0.588 + 0.660(\sqrt{2.278} - \sqrt{0.778}) = 0.588 + 0.660(1.509 - 0.882)$$

$$V_{TN}(1.5) = 0.588 + 0.414 = \mathbf{1.002\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 7 — Comment:**
The threshold voltage increased from **0.588 V to 1.002 V** — a rise of about **0.414 V (~70%)** — confirming the strong influence of body effect. This is highly significant in analog design where $V_{SB} \neq 0$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the drain current expression for an NMOS transistor operating in **(i)** the triode region, and **(ii)** the saturation region. State clearly the boundary condition between the two regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An NMOS transistor with $W/L = 10$, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 200\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $V_{TN} = 0.5$ V is biased at $V_{GS} = 1.5$ V. Determine:
   (i) The drain current in saturation.
   (ii) The drain-source saturation voltage.
   (iii) The on-resistance ($R_{on}$) in the linear region for $V_{DS} = 0.1$ V. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 — Channel charge derivation:**
The inversion-layer charge per unit area at position $y$ along the channel is:

$$Q_n(y) = -C_{ox}\!\left[V_{GS} - V_{TN} - V(y)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Drift current expression:**

$$I_D = -W \mu_n Q_n(y) \frac{dV}{dy}$$

**Step 3 — Separation of variables and integration from source ($y=0$, $V=0$) to drain ($y=L$, $V=V_{DS}$):**

$$I_D L = W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_0^{V_{DS}}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TN}) - V\right] dV$$

$$I_D L = W \mu_n C_{ox}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TN})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

**(i) Triode Region Expression:**

$$\boxed{I_{D,lin} = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TN})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]}$$

**Step 4 — Boundary condition for saturation:**
As $V_{DS}$ increases, the channel at the drain end becomes thinner. **Pinch-off** occurs when the inversion charge at the drain becomes zero:

$$Q_n(L) = -C_{ox}[V_{GS}-V_{TN}-V_{DS}] = 0$$

This gives the boundary:

$$\boxed{V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}}$$

**Step 5 — For $V_{DS} > V_{DS,sat}$,** the channel is pinched off, and excess voltage drops across the pinch-off region. The current becomes (ideally) independent of $V_{DS}$:

**(ii) Saturation Region Expression:**

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TN})^2}$$

(With channel-length modulation, multiplied by $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$.)

---

**Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Given:** $W/L = 10$, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 200\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ A/V², $V_{TN} = 0.5$ V, $V_{GS} = 1.5$ V.

**(i) Drain current in saturation:**

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10 \cdot (1.5 - 0.5)^2$$

$$I_{D,sat} = 1000 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1 = 1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A} = \mathbf{1\ \text{mA}}$$

**(ii) Drain-source saturation voltage:**

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TN} = 1.5 - 0.5 = \mathbf{1.0\ \text{V}}$$

**(iii) On-resistance $R_{on}$ in linear region for $V_{DS} = 0.1$ V:**

For small $V_{DS}$, neglect the $\frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}$ term:

$$I_{D,lin} \approx \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TN})V_{DS} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.1 = 200 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}$$

$$R_{on} = \frac{V_{DS}}{I_{D,lin}} = \frac{0.1}{200 \times 10^{-6}} = \mathbf{500\ \Omega}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfalls — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Sign errors in flat-band voltage:** Forgetting the negative contribution of $Q_{fc}$ in $V_{FB}$ when oxide charge is positive.
> 2. **Unit mismatch on $N_A$:** Using cm⁻³ in one equation and m⁻³ in another. Convert consistently (1 cm⁻³ = 10⁶ m⁻³).
> 3. **Confusing $V_T$ with $v_t$:** $V_T$ = threshold voltage; $v_t$ = thermal voltage. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Forgetting channel-length modulation:** In the saturation region, write $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ if the question mentions a finite $\lambda$; otherwise state the assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Neglecting $\frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}$:** For $V_{DS} \ll 2(V_{GS}-V_{TN})$, dropping this term is justified; otherwise the full quadratic must be used.

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **MOS = Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor.** A 4-terminal, voltage-controlled device (Gate, Drain, Source, Body).
- ✅ **Gate is insulated** from the channel by SiO₂ → infinite DC input resistance.
- ✅ **Three surface regimes:** Accumulation, Depletion, Inversion.
- ✅ **Threshold voltage** has three components: $V_{FB}$, $2\phi_F$, and depletion-region drop.
- ✅ **Strong inversion** begins when surface potential reaches $2\phi_F$.
- ✅ **Body effect** raises $V_{TN}$ when $V_{SB} > 0$; coefficient is $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}}{C_{ox}}$.
- ✅ **Triode region:** $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TN}$; current has linear + quadratic $V_{DS}$ terms.
- ✅ **Saturation region:** $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TN}$; current follows the **square law** $I_D \propto (V_{GS}-V_{TN})^2$.
- ✅ **Channel-length modulation** $\lambda$ causes $I_D$ to increase linearly with $V_{DS}$ in saturation.
- ✅ **Transconductance** $g_m$ in saturation = $\sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L} I_D}$ — a critical analog design parameter.
- ✅ **Subthreshold conduction** is exponential in $V_{GS}$ with slope factor $n$ — key for low-power design.
- ✅ **Substrate doping** $N_A$ appears in $V_{TN}$, $\phi_F$, and $\gamma$ — changing it affects multiple parameters simultaneously.
- ✅ **Always convert units consistently** — CGS-mks mismatch is the #1 source of numerical errors in KTU answer sheets.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PMOS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PMOS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of PMOS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **PMOS (P-channel Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor)** is a four-terminal field-effect device in which the conducting channel is formed by **holes (positive charge carriers)** flowing through a p-type inversion layer induced at the surface of an **n-type semiconductor substrate (body/bulk)**.

The four terminals are:
- **Source (S)** – the terminal from which majority carriers (holes) enter the channel. For PMOS, the source is biased at the **higher potential**.
- **Drain (D)** – the terminal through which carriers exit the channel; biased at the **lower potential**.
- **Gate (G)** – the control terminal, electrically isolated from the channel by a thin **SiO₂ (or high-k) gate oxide** layer of physical thickness $t_{ox}$ (or equivalent oxide thickness **EOT**).
- **Body/Substrate (B)** – the n-type bulk, typically tied to the most positive supply $V_{DD}$ to keep the source–body and drain–body p-n junctions reverse-biased.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST415 Module 1):** PMOS is studied as the electrical dual of the NMOS device. Its **threshold voltage is negative** ($V_{TP} < 0$), the device turns **ON** when $V_{GS} \le V_{TP}$ (i.e., $V_{GS}$ sufficiently negative), and the conventional drain current $I_D$ flows **from Source to Drain** (opposite to the electron flow).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" View

Imagine a **horizontal water pipe** carrying water (analogous to holes/positive charge) from a **high-altitude reservoir (Source)** down to a **low-altitude sink (Drain)**.

- The pipe is buried in **n-type earth (Body)**. The water can only flow when a *valve* (the **Gate**) opens.
- In an **NMOS** device, the water (electrons) flows only when the valve is **pulled open by positive pressure** on the gate.
- In a **PMOS** device, the same pipe exists, but the water only flows when the valve is **pushed shut by negative pressure** (negative gate voltage). The pipe (channel) is "depletion-formed" by *pushing holes into* the n-type body.
- A **constant pressure difference** between source and drain is what drives the flow — this is the **$V_{DS}$** for PMOS, defined as $V_{DS} = V_D - V_S$ (which is **negative** for normal operation).

This is why PMOS is often called a "normally-on when properly biased" device: it conducts when the gate is *low*, and stops conducting when the gate is *high*. This inversion of logic is what gives **CMOS** its name (**C**omplementary **MOS**).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Elementary charge: **$q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C**
- Thermal voltage at 300 K: **$V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV** (often rounded to **26 mV**)
- Intrinsic carrier concentration in Si: $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$ at 300 K
- Permittivity of SiO₂: $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \varepsilon_0 = 3.45 \times 10^{-13}$ F/cm
- Permittivity of Silicon: $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \varepsilon_0 = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm
- Typical supply: $V_{DD} = 0.5$ V to **3.3 V** (depending on technology node: 180 nm, 90 nm, 65 nm, 45 nm, 28 nm, etc.)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** In all derivations below, magnitudes are taken with care. The PMOS threshold is denoted $V_{TP}$ (a **negative** quantity). When writing saturation-region drain current, we conventionally use the **magnitude** form with $V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$ to keep expressions positive.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Device Structure (Cross-Sectional Anatomy)

The PMOS is fabricated on an **n-type well (or n-type substrate)** into which two heavily doped **p⁺ regions** are diffused to form the **Source** and **Drain**. A thin **gate oxide** layer is grown over the region between source and drain, and a **polysilicon** (or metal) gate electrode is deposited on top. The four terminals are then connected via ohmic contacts.

Key structural parameters:
- **Channel length $L$** – the mask dimension between source and drain metallurgical junctions (typically in nm for modern nodes).
- **Channel width $W$** – the perpendicular dimension controlling current drive capability.
- **Gate oxide thickness $t_{ox}$** – now replaced by **EOT** in advanced nodes where high-k dielectrics are used.
- **Junction depth $x_j$** – depth of the p⁺ source/drain diffusions.
- **Body doping $N_D$** (donor concentration, since body is n-type).

## 2.2 Operating Regimes — Step-by-Step Logic

The PMOS operates in three regimes, controlled by the **source-to-gate voltage** $V_{SG}$ relative to $\vert V_{TP} \vert$ and the **drain-to-source voltage** $V_{SD}$:

### Step 1: Cut-off Region
- **Condition:** $V_{SG} < \vert V_{TP} \vert$
- **Channel status:** No inversion layer exists. The surface of the n-type body is in **accumulation** (holes pile up at the oxide–silicon interface but the surface is not yet inverted).
- **Current:** $I_D = 0$ (only sub-threshold leakage, $I_{D0} \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{SG}-\vert V_{TP}\vert}{nV_T}\right)$).

### Step 2: Triode (Linear) Region
- **Condition:** $V_{SG} > \vert V_{TP} \vert$ **AND** $V_{SD} < V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$
- **Channel status:** A continuous p-type inversion layer connects source to drain. The channel acts as a **voltage-controlled resistor**.
- **Current behavior:** $I_D$ is approximately proportional to $V_{SD}$ for small $V_{SD}$.

### Step 3: Saturation (Active) Region
- **Condition:** $V_{SG} > \vert V_{TP} \vert$ **AND** $V_{SD} \ge V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$
- **Channel status:** At the drain end, the induced channel charge drops to **zero** — this point is called the **pinch-off point**. Beyond pinch-off, carriers are swept into the drain by the high lateral field.
- **Current behavior:** $I_D$ becomes (ideally) independent of $V_{SD}$ — the device acts as a **voltage-controlled current source**.

## 2.3 Why PMOS has Lower Current Drive than NMOS

For identical $W/L$ and $\vert V_{GS} - V_T \vert$:

- Hole mobility $\mu_p \approx 100\text{–}200$ cm²/V·s
- Electron mobility $\mu_n \approx 500\text{–}1350$ cm²/V·s

Since $I_D \propto \mu$, and $\mu_p \approx \mu_n / 2$ to $\mu_n / 3$, **PMOS is inherently 2×–3× weaker than NMOS**. To compensate, PMOS transistors in CMOS digital gates are sized with $W_p \approx 2W_n$ to $3W_n$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Expression | Region | Notes |
|---|----------------------|-----------|--------|-------|
| 1 | Threshold voltage (long-channel, with body effect) | $V_{TP} = V_{TP0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right)$ | All | $V_{SB} \ge 0$ for PMOS; $V_{TP0} < 0$ |
| 2 | Surface potential at strong inversion | $\phi_F = \pm V_T \ln(N_{A,D}/n_i)$ | All | Negative for n-type body (PMOS) |
| 3 | Body-effect coefficient | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_D}}{C_{ox}}$ | All | For PMOS use donor $N_D$ |
| 4 | Oxide capacitance per unit area | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | All | Use EOT for high-k stacks |
| 5 | Transconductance parameter | $k_p = \mu_p \, C_{ox}$ | All | Half of typical $k_n$ |
| 6 | Drain current — Triode (PMOS magnitude form) | $I_D = k_p \dfrac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{SG}-\vert V_{TP}\vert)V_{SD} - \dfrac{V_{SD}^2}{2}\right]$ | Triode | Valid for $0 \le V_{SD} \le V_{SG}-\vert V_{TP}\vert$ |
| 7 | Drain current — Saturation (PMOS, no CLM) | $I_D = \dfrac{k_p}{2} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{SG}-\vert V_{TP}\vert)^2$ | Saturation | Ideal long-channel form |
| 8 | Drain current — Saturation with Channel Length Modulation | $I_D = \dfrac{k_p}{2} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{SG}-\vert V_{TP}\vert)^2 \left(1 + \lambda V_{SD}\right)$ | Saturation | $\lambda$ = CLM parameter, $1/V$ |
| 9 | Output resistance (saturation) | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | Saturation | Finite due to CLM |
| 10 | Saturation voltage (overdrive) | $V_{OV} = V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$ | — | Also called $V_{DS,sat}$ magnitude |
| 11 | Drain-source saturation voltage | $V_{DS,sat} = V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$ | — | Same as overdrive magnitude |
| 12 | Sub-threshold swing | $S = n V_T \ln 10$ | Sub-$V_T$ | $S \ge 60$ mV/dec at 300 K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala Board Valuation Tip:** Examiners **deduct marks** if students write $V_{GS}$ instead of $V_{SG}$ for PMOS derivations. Always use **$V_{SG}$ (positive magnitude)** in PMOS current equations to keep signs consistent.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

PMOS transistors are the cornerstone of:
- **CMOS logic gates** (NOT, NAND, NOR) — the PMOS pull-up network complements the NMOS pull-down.
- **Transmission gates / pass transistors** — used for analog switches and memory cells.
- **Source-followers and level shifters** — exploiting the body-effect to shift DC levels.
- **Active loads in analog ICs** — current-mirror loads in op-amp input stages (e.g., the classic 741 op-amp input).
- **Charge pumps and bootstrap drivers** — high-voltage generation circuits.

In modern **FinFET** technology (Intel 22 nm, TSMC 16 nm and below), PMOS and NMOS are realized as vertical fins, but the underlying $I$–$V$ equations retain the same form with $\mu_p / \mu_n$ ratio.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PMOS Threshold Voltage with Body Effect

**Starting point (Poisson's equation in the depletion region under the gate oxide):**

For an n-type body with donor doping $N_D$, applying depletion approximation:

$$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_{si}}$$

**Step 1 — Integrate twice across the depletion width $x_d$:**

Boundary conditions: $\phi(0) = \phi_s$ (surface potential), $\phi(x_d) = 0$, and electric field $\mathcal{E}(x_d) = 0$ at the edge of depletion.

$$\phi(x) = \phi_s - \frac{q N_D}{2\varepsilon_{si}}(x - x_d)^2$$

**Step 2 — Maximum depletion width at the onset of strong inversion:**

The surface potential required to invert the surface is $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ (for n-type body, $\phi_F < 0$, so $\vert 2\phi_F \vert$ is used). Solving for $x_{d,max}$:

$$x_{d,max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_{si} \vert 2\phi_F \vert}{q N_D}}$$

**Step 3 — Gate voltage equation (charge balance on the gate):**

The gate voltage is supported by the flat-band voltage, the oxide drop, and the surface potential:

$$V_{GB} = V_{FB} + \phi_s - \frac{Q_{total}}{C_{ox}}$$

At threshold, the depletion charge is $Q_{D} = -q N_D x_{d,max}$ and we define $V_{GB} = V_{TN}$:

$$V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F - \frac{Q_{D,max}}{C_{ox}} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_D \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce body bias and the body-effect coefficient $\gamma$:**

For a source-to-body bias $V_{SB} \ge 0$ in PMOS, the threshold shifts:

$$V_{TN} = V_{TN0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Final PMOS form (with $\phi_F$ replaced by its magnitude for clarity):**

$$\boxed{\,V_{TP} = V_{TP0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right)\,}$$

> **Why this matters (KTU context):** This equation explains **why threshold voltage is not constant** in modern VLSI — when the body of a PMOS is biased above $V_S$ (e.g., in stacked transistors or source-followers), $V_{SB} > 0$ and $\vert V_{TP} \vert$ *increases*, weakening the device — this is the **body effect**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PMOS Drain Current in Triode Region

**Step 1 — Inversion charge per unit area at any point $y$ in the channel:**

The voltage at point $y$ is $V(y)$ measured from source. The local gate-to-channel voltage is $V_{SG} - V(y)$. The hole charge per unit area is:

$$Q_p(y) = -C_{ox}\!\left[V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert - V(y)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Drift current at point $y$:**

The drain current (conventional, from D to S inside the device) is:

$$I_D = -\mu_p W Q_p(y) \frac{dV(y)}{dy}$$

The negative sign accounts for the fact that holes flow from S to D, opposite to the conventional $I_D$ direction. Substituting:

$$I_D \, dy = \mu_p W C_{ox}\!\left[V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert - V(y)\right] dV(y)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $y=0$ (source, $V=0$) to $y=L$ (drain, $V=V_{SD}$):**

$$I_D \int_0^L dy = \mu_p W C_{ox} \int_0^{V_{SD}} \!\left[V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert - V(y)\right] dV(y)$$

$$I_D \cdot L = \mu_p W C_{ox} \left[\left(V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert\right)V_{SD} - \frac{V_{SD}^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Final triode-region expression:**

$$\boxed{\,I_D = \mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}\left[\left(V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert\right)V_{SD} - \frac{V_{SD}^2}{2}\right]\,}$$

**Step 5 — Validity condition:** $0 \le V_{SD} \le V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PMOS Drain Current in Saturation Region

**Step 1 — Identify the pinch-off condition:**

At saturation onset, $V_{SD} = V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert = V_{OV}$ (overdrive). Substituting into the triode equation:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}\!\left[V_{OV} \cdot V_{OV} - \frac{V_{OV}^2}{2}\right] = \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2$$

**Step 2 — Define the transconductance parameter $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox}$:**

$$\boxed{\,I_{D,sat} = \frac{k_p}{2} \frac{W}{L}\!\left(V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert\right)^2\,}$$

**Step 3 — Include channel length modulation (short-channel correction):**

For real short-channel devices, $I_D$ increases slightly with $V_{SD}$ due to effective channel-length reduction. Empirically:

$$L_{eff} \approx L - \Delta L, \quad \Delta L \propto V_{SD}$$

Modeling this as $L_{eff} = L/(1 + \lambda V_{SD})$:

$$\boxed{\,I_{D,sat} = \frac{k_p}{2} \frac{W}{L}\!\left(V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert\right)^2 \left(1 + \lambda V_{SD}\right)\,}$$

where $\lambda$ is the **channel-length modulation parameter** (units: V$^{-1}$), typically $0.01$ to $0.1$ V$^{-1}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Transconductance $g_m$ of a PMOS in Saturation

**Step 1 — Differentiate $I_{D,sat}$ w.r.t. $V_{SG}$ (with $V_{SD}$ held constant):**

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_{D,sat}}{\partial V_{SG}} = \frac{k_p}{2} \frac{W}{L} \cdot 2(V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert) \cdot (1 + \lambda V_{SD})$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{\,g_m = k_p \frac{W}{L} (V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert)(1 + \lambda V_{SD}) = \mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}\,(1+\lambda V_{SD})\,}$$

For long-channel ($\lambda \to 0$): $g_m = \mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}$.

> **Physical meaning:** $g_m$ tells us how effectively the gate voltage modulates the drain current — the higher the $g_m$, the more useful the device as an analog amplifier.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A PMOS transistor has $W/L = 10/0.5$, $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $V_{TP} = -0.7$ V, $\lambda = 0.05$ V$^{-1}$. Compute $I_D$ for $V_{SG} = 1.5$ V, $V_{SD} = 1.0$ V.

**Step 1 — Compute overdrive:**

$$V_{OV} = V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert = 1.5 - 0.7 = 0.8\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Check the region:**

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{OV} = 0.8\ \text{V}, \quad V_{SD} = 1.0\ \text{V} > 0.8\ \text{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{SATURATION}$$

**Step 3 — Apply saturation formula:**

$$I_D = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \times \frac{10}{0.5} \times (0.8)^2 \times (1 + 0.05 \times 1.0)$$

$$I_D = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 0.64 \times 1.05$$

$$I_D = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 0.672 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 13.44 = \mathbf{3.36 \times 10^{-4}\ A = 336\ \mu A}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $g_m$:**

$$g_m = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{10}{0.5} \times 0.8 \times 1.05 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 0.84 = \mathbf{8.4 \times 10^{-4}\ S = 0.84\ mS}$$

## 4.2 PMOS Operating-Regime State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Accumulation
    Accumulation --> Depletion : V_SG increases past flat-band
    Depletion --> Triode : V_SG exceeds V_TP magnitude - channel fully formed
    Triode --> Saturation : V_SD reaches V_OV - pinch-off at drain
    Saturation --> Triode : V_SD decreases below V_OV
    Triode --> Cutoff : V_SG falls below V_TP magnitude
    Saturation --> Cutoff : V_SG falls below V_TP magnitude
    Cutoff --> [*] : OFF state - I_D zero
    Accumulation --> Cutoff : V_SG much less than V_TP magnitude

    note right of Accumulation : Holes pile up at oxide interface
    note right of Triode : Channel acts as VCR
    note right of Saturation : Channel pinched off - VCCS
```
