# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"With a neat diagram, explain the construction of a p-channel MOSFET and identify the carriers responsible for conduction."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A PMOS is fabricated on an **n-type silicon substrate (body)** in which two heavily doped **p⁺ regions** (source and drain) are created using diffusion or ion-implantation.
- A thin layer of **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)** — the gate oxide — is grown thermally between the source and drain, and a **polysilicon (or metal) gate electrode** is deposited on top.
- The **body** is connected to a separate terminal (usually tied to $V_{DD}$) to keep the source–body and drain–body p-n junctions **reverse-biased**.
- **Conduction is by holes** (majority carriers of p-type inversion layer), which flow from **source (higher potential) to drain (lower potential)**.
- **Marks split:** (1) Substrate + diffusions, (1) Oxide + gate, (1) Hole conduction.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Define the threshold voltage of a PMOS transistor. Why is it negative?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **threshold voltage $V_{TP}$** of a PMOS is defined as the gate-to-source voltage at which a **p-type inversion layer** (conducting channel of holes) is just formed at the surface of the n-type body.
- It is **negative** because, in an n-type body, the surface must be driven into strong inversion by **accumulating holes**, which requires the **gate to be biased negative with respect to the body (and the source)**.
- Mathematically, $V_{TP} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F - \frac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_D \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$ and for an n-type body, $V_{FB} + 2\phi_F < 0$ plus the depletion-charge term, yielding $V_{TP} < 0$.
- **Marks split:** (1) Definition, (1) Why negative physically, (1) Mathematical support.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the drain current $I_D$ of a PMOS transistor operating in the **saturation region**, clearly stating the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a PMOS with $W = 20\ \mu\text{m}$, $L = 0.5\ \mu\text{m}$, $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $V_{TP} = -0.6$ V, $\lambda = 0.04$ V$^{-1}$, calculate the drain current and the transconductance when $V_{SG} = 1.2$ V and $V_{SD} = 1.5$ V. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Gradual-channel approximation: electric field along the channel ($\partial V/\partial y$) is much smaller than the field perpendicular to the channel ($\partial V/\partial x$).
2. Drift current only (no diffusion); constant mobility along the channel.
3. Threshold voltage is constant along the channel (no body-effect variation — i.e., source and body tied).
4. Channel-length modulation is initially ignored (ideal long-channel).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The hole inversion charge at any point $y$ along the channel (voltage $V(y)$ w.r.t. source):

$$Q_p(y) = -C_{ox}\!\left[V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP}\vert - V(y)\right]$$

Drift current (positive from source to drain):

$$I_D = \mu_p W \left(-Q_p(y)\right) \frac{dV(y)}{dy} = \mu_p C_{ox} W \left[V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP}\vert - V(y)\right]\frac{dV(y)}{dy}$$

Integrate from $y=0$, $V=0$ to $y=L$, $V=V_{SD}$:

$$I_D L = \mu_p C_{ox} W \left[\left(V_{SG}-\vert V_{TP}\vert\right)V_{SD} - \frac{V_{SD}^2}{2}\right]$$

This is the **triode-region** expression. At the **boundary of saturation**, the channel pinches off at the drain, so $V_{SD} = V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP}\vert \equiv V_{OV}$. Substituting:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{\mu_p C_{ox}}{2} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2 = \frac{k_p}{2} \frac{W}{L} \left(V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP}\vert\right)^2$$

Adding channel-length modulation:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{k_p}{2} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2 \left(1 + \lambda V_{SD}\right)$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute overdrive (1 mark):**

$$V_{OV} = V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP}\vert = 1.2 - 0.6 = 0.6\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Check region of operation (1 mark):**

$V_{DS,sat} = V_{OV} = 0.6$ V. Since $V_{SD} = 1.5$ V $> 0.6$ V $\Rightarrow$ **SATURATION**.

**Step 3 — Substitute into saturation formula (2 marks):**

$$I_D = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \times \frac{20}{0.5} \times (0.6)^2 \times \left(1 + 0.04 \times 1.5\right)$$

$$I_D = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 \times 0.36 \times (1 + 0.06)$$

$$I_D = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 \times 0.36 \times 1.06$$

$$I_D = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 15.264 = \mathbf{3.816 \times 10^{-4}\ A \approx 381.6\ \mu A}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $g_m$ (2 marks):**

$$g_m = k_p \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}(1 + \lambda V_{SD}) = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 \times 0.6 \times 1.06$$

$$g_m = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 \times 0.636 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 25.44 = \mathbf{1.272 \times 10^{-3}\ S = 1.272\ mS}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $r_o$ (1 mark):**

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.04 \times 3.816 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1}{1.5264 \times 10^{-5}} \approx \mathbf{65.5\ k\Omega}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** With the help of a labeled cross-sectional diagram, explain the **body effect** in a PMOS transistor and derive the body-effect-incorporated threshold voltage equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PMOS device has the following parameters: $V_{TP0} = -0.5$ V, $\gamma = 0.4$ V$^{1/2}$, $\vert 2\phi_F \vert = 0.6$ V. Calculate the new threshold voltage when the source-to-body voltage $V_{SB} = 1.0$ V. If the supply $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, what is the maximum allowed $V_{SG}$ to keep the device OFF? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Cross-sectional diagram and explanation (3 marks):**

A PMOS is built in an n-type body tied to the highest potential. When $V_{SB} > 0$ (body at higher potential than source), the source–body p-n junction is **more strongly reverse-biased**, widening the depletion region under the gate. A larger negative gate voltage is then required to attract enough holes to invert the surface — so $\vert V_{TP}\vert$ **increases**.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

At threshold, the depletion charge per unit area is $Q_D = -q N_D x_{d,max}$, where

$$x_{d,max} = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si} (\vert 2\phi_F\vert + V_{SB})}{q N_D}}$$

Gate-voltage equation at threshold:

$$V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F - \frac{Q_{D,max}}{C_{ox}} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_D (\vert 2\phi_F\vert + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

Recognize $\gamma = \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_D}/C_{ox}$ and define $V_{TN0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F\vert}$:

$$\boxed{\,V_{TP} = V_{TP0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F\vert + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F\vert}\right)\,}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute new threshold (3 marks):**

$$V_{TP} = -0.5 + 0.4 \left(\sqrt{0.6 + 1.0} - \sqrt{0.6}\right)$$

$$\sqrt{1.6} = 1.2649, \quad \sqrt{0.6} = 0.7746$$

$$V_{TP} = -0.5 + 0.4 \times (1.2649 - 0.7746) = -0.5 + 0.4 \times 0.4903 = -0.5 + 0.1961 = \mathbf{-0.3039\ V}$$

**Step 2 — Device OFF condition (2 marks):**

The PMOS is OFF when $V_{SG} < \vert V_{TP}\vert$. The maximum $V_{SG}$ that still keeps the device OFF (in the limiting case) is:

$$V_{SG,max(OFF)} = \vert V_{TP}\vert = 0.3039\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check with $V_{DD}$ (2 marks):**

For the device to be reliably OFF at $V_{SG} = V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, we need $\vert V_{TP}\vert \ge 1.8$ V. The current $V_{TP} = -0.304$ V is far too small in magnitude; the device would be strongly ON. To use this PMOS in a 1.8 V digital circuit, the body-bias condition must be redesigned (e.g., $V_{SB} \approx 0$ or a higher $\gamma$ technology required). This illustrates a real **low-power design challenge** in nanometer CMOS.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on PMOS Problems:**
> 1. **Sign confusion:** Writing $V_{GS}$ instead of $V_{SG}$ is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss. Always use $V_{SG}$ for PMOS to keep equations in the positive-magnitude form.
> 2. **Region-of-operation check:** Students often blindly plug into the saturation formula without verifying $V_{SD} \ge V_{OV}$. This loses **1 full mark** in numerical problems.
> 3. **Channel-length modulation forgotten:** At 65 nm and below, $\lambda$ matters. Omitting the $(1 + \lambda V_{SD})$ factor when explicitly mentioned in the question costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Body effect omitted:** When $V_{SB} > 0$ (always true in PMOS by default), the threshold shifts. Many students forget this in sub-parts that ask for "new $V_{TP}$".
> 5. **Using $V_{DS}$ instead of $V_{SD}$:** In PMOS, $V_{DS} = V_D - V_S$ is *negative*; the more convenient positive quantity is $V_{SD} = V_S - V_D$. Examiners will read both, but consistent use of $V_{SD}$ in derivations is preferred.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **PMOS = p-channel MOSFET** with **holes** as majority carriers in an **n-type body**.
- **Threshold voltage is negative:** $V_{TP} < 0$, with typical magnitudes **0.3 V to 0.9 V** depending on technology.
- **Device is ON when $V_{SG} > \vert V_{TP}\vert$** (i.e., the gate is *low* w.r.t. source). The PMOS conducts when its input is logic 0.
- **Conventional current flows from Source (higher V) to Drain (lower V)** — opposite to NMOS.
- **Three operating regions:** Cut-off ($V_{SG} < \vert V_{TP}\vert$), Triode ($V_{SD} < V_{OV}$), Saturation ($V_{SD} \ge V_{OV}$).
- **Triode-region current equation:** $I_D = k_p \frac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{SG}-\vert V_{TP}\vert)V_{SD} - \frac{V_{SD}^2}{2}\right]$
- **Saturation-region current equation:** $I_D = \frac{k_p}{2}\frac{W}{L}(V_{SG}-\vert V_{TP}\vert)^2(1+\lambda V_{SD})$
- **Transconductance:** $g_m = k_p \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}(1+\lambda V_{SD}) = \mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}$ (long channel).
- **Output resistance in saturation:** $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$.
- **Body effect formula:** $V_{TP} = V_{TP0} + \gamma(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F\vert + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F\vert})$ — threshold magnitude **increases** with reverse body bias.
- **PMOS mobility disadvantage:** $\mu_p \approx \mu_n / 3$, so PMOS is **2×–3× weaker** than NMOS of the same size.
- **PMOS in CMOS:** Acts as the **pull-up network** in static CMOS logic; OFF when input is high, ON when input is low.
- **Always remember to use $V_{SG}$ and $V_{SD}$** (not $V_{GS}$, $V_{DS}$) in PMOS derivations for sign consistency.
- **KCL/KVL caution:** In a CMOS inverter with input = $V_{DD}$, the PMOS is OFF ($V_{SG}=0$); the NMOS is ON and pulls output to GND.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NMOS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** NMOS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **NMOS (N-channel Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor)** is a unipolar, voltage-controlled, three-terminal (Source, Gate, Drain) plus a fourth body/substrate terminal semiconductor device in which the conducting channel is formed by negatively charged electrons in an n-type inversion layer created at the surface of a p-type silicon substrate beneath the gate oxide. The current conduction between the Drain (D) and Source (S) terminals is modulated by the electric field generated by the voltage applied to the Gate (G) electrode, which is electrically isolated from the channel by a thin silicon dioxide (SiO₂) insulating layer.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the NMOS is classified as an **enhancement-mode n-channel MOSFET** (the most widely manufactured variant in modern CMOS VLSI technology nodes), characterized by the requirement of a positive gate-to-source voltage (V_GS) exceeding the threshold voltage (V_TH) to form an inversion layer and initiate channel conduction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The NMOS device physics, I-V characteristics, second-order effects (body effect and channel length modulation), and small-signal parameters form the foundational basis for the analysis of CMOS logic gates, analog building blocks, and digital VLSI subsystems covered in subsequent modules.

## 1.2 Physical Structure

A modern NMOS transistor consists of the following layers (from bottom to top):

| Layer / Region | Material | Doping Type | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Substrate (Body) | Silicon (Si) | p-type (Boron) | Mechanical support & back-gate terminal |
| Source / Drain | Silicon (Si) | n⁺ heavy (Arsenic/Phosphorus) | Charge carrier reservoirs |
| Channel | Inversion layer (electrons) | Induced n-type | Conduction path between S and D |
| Gate Oxide | SiO₂ (or High-κ dielectric) | Insulator | Electric field coupling medium |
| Gate Electrode | Heavily doped poly-Si or metal | Conductor | Field plate controlling channel |

**Critical physical parameters:**
- **Gate oxide thickness (t_ox):** typically **1.2 nm to 5 nm** in modern nodes (45 nm down to 7 nm FinFET).
- **Channel length (L):** minimum drawn length, e.g., **L = 7 nm** at 7 nm technology node.
- **Channel width (W):** designer-controlled parameter; larger W = more drive current.
- **Body doping (N_A):** typically **10¹⁵ to 10¹⁷ cm⁻³** for bulk CMOS.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the NMOS as a "smart water tap" controlled by an invisible hand:**
>
> - The **Gate (G)** is the **handle of the tap**. The harder you turn it (higher V_GS), the wider the channel opens and the more current flows.
> - The **Drain (D)** is the **high-pressure inlet**, and the **Source (S)** is the **outlet**. The pressure difference between them (V_DS) pushes the water (electrons) through the opened channel.
> - The **threshold voltage (V_TH)** is the **minimum force you must apply to the handle** before any water flows at all. Below this, the tap stays "stuck shut" (cutoff region).
> - The **Body (B)** terminal is the **wall the tap is mounted on**. If the wall exerts a back-pressure (V_SB > 0, the body effect), it becomes slightly harder to turn the handle, requiring a slightly greater force (higher V_TH) to open the tap.
> - The **gate oxide** is the **insulated rubber grip** on the handle — no water (current) actually flows through the handle, only through the pipe.

This analogy immediately conveys that the NMOS is a **voltage-controlled current source** (VCCS) where the gate draws **zero DC current** (ideal infinite input resistance), but the drain current I_D is a strong nonlinear function of V_GS.

## 1.4 Symbol and Terminal Conventions

The four-terminal NMOS symbol used in KTU textbook diagrams:

- **Arrow on source points OUTWARD (away from gate)** for NMOS, indicating n-channel current flow.
- **Bulk/Body arrow points TOWARD the channel** (p-type substrate → n-channel).
- **Standard reference directions:** All voltages are measured with respect to the Source (V_GS, V_DS, V_BS) unless otherwise stated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Convention:** Always assume **Source is grounded (V_S = 0)** and **Body is grounded (V_B = 0)** unless the problem explicitly specifies a non-zero body bias (V_SB ≠ 0). This is the most common assumption in KTU numerical problems.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — The Three Regions of Operation

The NMOS transistor operates in three distinct regions depending on the relative values of V_GS, V_TH, and V_DS. The graduate-level understanding requires recognizing that the channel charge density is **non-uniform along the channel** (from source to drain) once V_DS is applied.

### Region 1: Cutoff (Subthreshold) Region
- **Condition:** V_GS < V_TH
- **Mechanism:** The gate voltage is insufficient to invert the p-type surface; only a weak depletion region exists beneath the oxide. The drain current is **exponentially small** (subthreshold leakage, typically in nanoamperes).
- **Equation:** I_D ≈ I_off (off-state leakage current, typically < 1 nA/μm in modern processes).
- **Engineering Utility:** This is the OFF state of an NMOS switch in digital CMOS logic.

### Region 2: Triode (Linear / Ohmic) Region
- **Condition:** V_GS > V_TH **AND** V_DS < (V_GS − V_TH) ≡ V_DS,sat
- **Mechanism:** A continuous inversion layer (channel) is formed. The channel behaves as a **voltage-controlled resistor** whose resistance R_DS is modulated by V_GS.
- **Why "Triode"?** Historically, this I-V curve resembles the triode vacuum tube characteristic.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used in pass-transistor logic, analog resistive loads, and triode-biased amplifiers.

### Region 3: Saturation (Active) Region
- **Condition:** V_GS > V_TH **AND** V_DS ≥ (V_GS − V_TH)
- **Mechanism:** The channel is **pinched off** near the drain end. Electrons are injected into the depletion region and swept to the drain by the high lateral field. I_D becomes (ideally) **independent of V_DS** — the transistor acts as a **voltage-controlled current source**.
- **Why "Saturation"?** Because the current saturates to a value set solely by V_GS and the geometry.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used in CMOS digital logic (saturated-load) and analog amplifiers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition (pinch-off / saturation edge):** V_DS,sat = V_GS − V_TH = V_OV (overdrive voltage / effective voltage / V_DSAT). This V_OV is one of the most frequently tested parameters in KTU board examinations.

## 2.2 Threshold Voltage and the Body Effect

The threshold voltage of an NMOS is **not a constant** — it depends on the source-to-body bias V_SB. This is the **Body Effect**, a critical second-order effect.

The complete threshold voltage equation (with body bias):

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left[ \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right]$$

where:
- V_TH0 = zero-bias threshold voltage (V_SB = 0, typically 0.3 V to 0.7 V for NMOS).
- γ = body-effect coefficient (typically **0.3 V^{1/2} to 0.7 V^{1/2}**).
- 2φ_F = surface potential at strong inversion (typically 0.6 V to 0.9 V).
- V_SB = source-to-body voltage (≥ 0 for NMOS in normal operation).

**Physical interpretation:** When V_SB > 0, the source junction becomes reverse-biased, widening the depletion region and **reducing the surface potential** available for inversion, thereby **increasing V_TH**. This is why stacked NMOS transistors in pass-transistor logic suffer from degraded drive strength.

## 2.3 Channel Length Modulation (CLM)

In saturation, the pinch-off point actually moves slightly toward the source as V_DS increases, **shortening the effective channel length** from L to L − ΔL. This causes I_D to increase linearly with V_DS, modeled by the parameter λ (channel length modulation coefficient).

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

For long-channel devices, λ ≈ 0 (often neglected); for short-channel devices, λ becomes significant and V_DS-dependent. λ is typically in the range **0.01 V⁻¹ to 0.1 V⁻¹** for L = 0.5 μm to L = 0.1 μm technologies respectively.

## 2.4 Small-Signal Parameters (for Analog VLSI Analysis)

These are the AC parameters essential for amplifier design:

- **Transconductance:** g_m measures the sensitivity of I_D to V_GS.
- **Body transconductance:** g_mb measures the sensitivity of I_D to V_BS.
- **Output resistance:** r_o models finite output impedance due to CLM.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield NMOS Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery of this table is essential for KTU ESE questions on NMOS device physics.** All equations assume the standard convention V_S = V_B = 0 unless otherwise noted.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Equation | Region / Condition | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Threshold Voltage (with body effect) | $V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma\!\left[\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right]$ | All regions | V | Min V_GS to form inversion layer |
| 2 | Overdrive Voltage (V_OV) | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | V_GS > V_TH | V | Effective gate drive above threshold |
| 3 | Drain Current (Triode) | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TH})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | 0 < V_DS < V_OV | A | Linear-region current |
| 4 | On-Resistance (deep triode) | $R_{DS,on} \approx \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})}$ | V_DS → 0 | Ω | Channel resistance (pass transistor) |
| 5 | Drain Current (Saturation) | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | V_DS ≥ V_OV | A | Active-region current |
| 6 | Saturation Drain Current (ideal, λ=0) | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | Ideal saturation | A | Ideal VCCS current |
| 7 | Transconductance (saturation) | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH}) = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} I_D}$ | Saturation | A/V (S) | V-to-I gain |
| 8 | Body Transconductance | $g_{mb} = g_m \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}}$ | Saturation | A/V | Back-gate transconductance |
| 9 | Output Resistance | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D} \approx \dfrac{V_{A}}{I_D}$ | Saturation | Ω | Finite output impedance |
| 10 | Intrinsic Gain | $A_{int} = g_m \cdot r_o = \dfrac{2(V_{GS}-V_{TH})}{\lambda V_{DS}}$ | Saturation | Dimensionless | Max single-stage gain |
| 11 | Body-Effect Coefficient | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}} = \dfrac{t_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{ox}}\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}$ | Constant | V^{1/2} | Back-gate coupling strength |
| 12 | Oxide Capacitance per unit area | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.9 \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$ | Constant | F/m² | Gate-oxide specific capacitance |
| 13 | Process Transconductance (k_n') | $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ | Constant | A/V² | Technology-dependent gain factor |
| 14 | Conduction Parameter (k_n) | $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} = k_n' \cdot \dfrac{W}{L}$ | Geometry + tech | A/V² | Full device gain factor |
| 15 | Charge Density in Channel | $Q(x) = -C_{ox}\!\left[V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x)\right]$ | Triode | C/m² | Local mobile charge per area |

**Standard physical constants (required for numerical problems):**
- Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- Permittivity of SiO₂: **ε_ox = 3.9 × ε₀ = 3.45 × 10⁻¹¹ F/m**
- Permittivity of silicon: **ε_si = 11.7 × ε₀ = 1.04 × 10⁻¹⁰ F/m**
- Elementary charge: **q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- Intrinsic carrier concentration in Si at 300 K: **n_i = 1.45 × 10¹⁰ cm⁻³**

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of NMOS Device Models

The NMOS equations above are not just academic — they are the **foundation of every VLSI design decision**:

1. **Digital CMOS logic:** Sizing the W/L ratio of NMOS pull-down transistors directly determines the **propagation delay** and **rise/fall times** of an inverter. Larger W → faster switching, but more silicon area and higher dynamic power dissipation.
2. **Analog VLSI (CMOS amplifiers):** The intrinsic gain g_m·r_o sets the maximum achievable gain of a single-stage amplifier. Designers trade off g_m (via W/L) against r_o (via L) to maximize gain.
3. **Memory cells (SRAM/DRAM):** The threshold voltage V_TH sets the read/write margins and retention characteristics. Body effect degrades drive in stacked pull-down networks.
4. **I/O pads and ESD protection:** NMOS in the triode region acts as a clamping element; its on-resistance R_DS,on determines the clamping voltage.
5. **Power management (LDO pass transistors):** Large NMOS devices operating in saturation provide load current with high efficiency.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Drain Current in the Triode Region (Gradual Channel Approximation)

This is the most heavily tested derivation in KTU board exams. We derive the I-V relationship using the **Gradual Channel Approximation (GCA)**, which assumes the lateral electric field along the channel is much smaller than the transverse field from the gate.

### Starting Point: Charge Density at a Point x Along the Channel

At a distance x from the source, the local gate-to-channel potential is V(x). The induced mobile electron charge per unit area at that point is given by:

$$Q_n(x) = -C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x) \right]$$

> **Logic:** At the source end, V(0) = 0, so Q_n(0) = −C_ox(V_GS − V_TH) = full inversion charge. At the drain end where V(L) = V_DS, the charge is Q_n(L) = −C_ox(V_GS − V_TH − V_DS) = −C_ox·V_OV (when V_DS < V_OV, this is still negative, channel exists).

### Step 2: Drift Current Density

The electron drift current density at point x is:

$$J_n(x) = -Q_n(x) \cdot v_{drift} = Q_n(x) \cdot \mu_n \cdot \mathcal{E}_x = -Q_n(x) \cdot \mu_n \cdot \frac{dV}{dx}$$

> **Logic:** The drift velocity is v = μ_n·E, where E = −dV/dx. The current density is charge times velocity. Substituting Q_n(x) gives a positive current in the direction of decreasing V (i.e., from drain to source in electron-flow direction).

### Step 3: Drain Current Integration

The total drain current I_D is the integral of the current density over the channel cross-section (width W, inversion layer depth → integrated into Q_n):

$$I_D = -W \cdot Q_n(x) \cdot \mu_n \cdot \frac{dV}{dx} = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x) \right] \frac{dV}{dx}$$

> **Logic:** Multiply by W to get total current through the channel width. Since I_D is constant along the channel (charge conservation), we can separate variables.

### Step 4: Separation of Variables and Boundary Integration

Rearranging to separate V and x:

$$I_D \, dx = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x) \right] dV$$

Integrating from x = 0 (V = 0) to x = L (V = V_DS):

$$\int_{0}^{L} I_D \, dx = W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_{0}^{V_{DS}} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V \right] dV$$

$$I_D \cdot L = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

### Step 5: Final Triode Equation

$$\boxed{\,I_{D,triode} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]\,}$$

> **Logic:** The result is a **quadratic polynomial in V_DS** with a maximum at the boundary V_DS = V_GS − V_TH. This is the fundamental current-voltage relationship for an NMOS in the linear (triode) region.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Saturation Current and Overdrive Voltage

### Step 1: Identify the Saturation Boundary

As V_DS increases, the channel charge at the drain end Q_n(L) = −C_ox(V_GS − V_TH − V_DS) decreases in magnitude. When V_DS reaches V_GS − V_TH, the charge at the drain end becomes **zero** — the channel is "pinched off."

$$\text{Pinch-off condition: } V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} \equiv V_{OV}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Saturation Boundary into the Triode Equation

Setting V_DS = V_OV in the triode equation:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{OV} - \frac{V_{OV}^2}{2} \right]$$

Since V_OV = V_GS − V_TH:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_{OV}^2 - \frac{V_{OV}^2}{2} \right] = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{V_{OV}^2}{2}$$

### Step 3: Final Saturation Equation (Ideal, λ = 0)

$$\boxed{\,I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2\,}$$

> **Logic:** Beyond pinch-off, the channel is detached from the drain, and the additional V_DS drops across the depletion region. The voltage across the inversion layer remains V_OV, so the current saturates at this value. This is the classic **square-law** characteristic of a long-channel MOSFET.

### Step 4: Include Channel Length Modulation

For non-ideal devices in saturation:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

> **Logic:** The factor (1 + λV_DS) empirically accounts for the shortening of the effective channel as the depletion region expands toward the source. Empirically, λ is often estimated as λ ≈ ΔL/(L·V_DS) and is inversely proportional to L.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Transconductance g_m in Saturation

The transconductance g_m is defined as the partial derivative of I_D with respect to V_GS, holding V_DS constant:

### Step 1: Start with the Saturation Equation

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

### Step 2: Apply the Chain Rule

$$g_m \equiv \left. \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{V_{DS} = \text{const}}$$

$$g_m = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot 2(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) \cdot 1$$

### Step 3: Final Expression for g_m

$$\boxed{\,g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = k_n' \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 k_n' \frac{W}{L} I_D}\,}$$

> **Logic:** g_m is **linearly proportional to V_OV** in one form, and **linearly proportional to W/L** in another. Increasing the overdrive voltage or the aspect ratio (W/L) linearly increases the transconductance. The third form (square root of I_D) is useful for bias-current-dependent design.

### Step 4: Body Transconductance g_mb

When V_SB ≠ 0, V_TH becomes a function of V_SB. Differentiating I_D with respect to V_BS (with V_GS fixed) gives:

$$g_{mb} = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{BS}} = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{TH}} \cdot \frac{\partial V_{TH}}{\partial V_{BS}} = (-g_m) \cdot \left( -\frac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\,g_{mb} = g_m \cdot \frac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}} = \eta \cdot g_m\,}$$

where η = g_mb / g_m is the body-effect factor, typically **0.1 to 0.3** in modern technologies.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** An NMOS transistor has μ_n C_ox = 50 μA/V², W/L = 10, V_TH0 = 0.6 V, V_GS = 1.5 V, V_DS = 2.0 V, V_SB = 0 V, γ = 0.4 V^{1/2}, 2φ_F = 0.6 V, λ = 0.02 V⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Compute V_TH (no body effect since V_SB = 0):**

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left[ \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right] = 0.6 + 0$$

$$V_{TH} = 0.6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute overdrive voltage:**

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 1.5 - 0.6 = 0.9 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Check region of operation:**

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{OV} = 0.9 \text{ V}; \quad V_{DS} = 2.0 \text{ V} > 0.9 \text{ V} \Rightarrow \text{SATURATION}$$

**Step 4 — Compute I_D (with λ):**

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 50\,\mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 10 \cdot (0.9)^2 \cdot (1 + 0.02 \cdot 2.0)$$

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 50 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.81 \cdot 1.04$$

$$I_D = 250 \cdot 0.81 \cdot 1.04 = 210.6\,\mu\text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Compute g_m:**

$$g_m = 50\,\mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.9 = 450\,\mu\text{A/V} = 0.45 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 6 — Compute r_o:**

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \cdot 210.6\,\mu\text{A}} = \frac{1}{4.212\,\mu\text{A/V}} \approx 237.4 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 7 — Compute intrinsic gain:**

$$A_{int} = g_m \cdot r_o = 0.45\,\text{mA/V} \cdot 237.4\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 106.8 \text{ V/V}$$

> **Key takeaway for KTU exams:** Always follow this 7-step structured approach — (1) compute V_TH, (2) compute V_OV, (3) determine region, (4) compute I_D, (5) compute g_m, (6) compute r_o, (7) compute gain. This exact sequence is what KTU board examiners look for.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NMOS Physical Cross-Section (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the physical cross-section of an NMOS transistor, showing the functional regions and their interactions. (Adapted for clarity since true cross-section drawings require specialized CAD tools.)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["GATE TERMINAL: Poly-Si or Metal Electrode"] -->|"Applied V_GS creates vertical electric field"| B["GATE OXIDE SiO2: Thin Insulator t_ox 1 to 5 nm"]
    B -->|"Field-induced electron inversion at SiO2-Si interface"| C["CHANNEL: Induced n-type inversion layer electrons"]
    C -->|"Electrons drift from D to S, conventional I_D flows D to S"| D["DRAIN n+ Region: Heavily doped n+ source of electrons"]
    C -->|"Electron reservoir and reference node V_S"| E["SOURCE n+ Region: Heavily doped n+"]
    F["BODY SUBSTRATE: p-type Si, V_B reference"] -->|"Reverse-biased p-n junctions isolate S and D"| D
    F -->|"Reverse-biased p-n junctions isolate S and D"| E
    G["DEPLETION REGIONS: Space-charge regions at p-n junctions"] -->|"Widen with increasing V_DS and V_SB body effect"| C
    H["Pinch-off Point: Location where Q_n = 0 in saturation"] -->|"Moves toward source as V_DS increases causes CLM"| C
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **gate voltage V_GS** controls the vertical field (top-down arrows), which creates the inversion layer.
- The **drain voltage V_DS** controls the lateral field (left-to-right), which drifts electrons from drain to source.
- The **body terminal** is the reference; reverse-biased p-n junctions at source-body and drain-body isolate the device.
- In **saturation**, a pinch-off point forms near the drain, beyond which carriers travel through the depletion region.

## 4.2 NMOS Operation Regions — State Machine Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start(["NMOS Power-On V_DD applied"]) --> CheckVGS{"V_GS greater than V_TH?"}
    CheckVGS -->|"NO V_GS less than V_TH"| Cutoff["CUTOFF REGION: I_D = 0, no inversion layer, transistor OFF"]
    CheckVGS -->|"YES V_GS greater than V_TH"| FormChannel["Inversion layer forms beneath gate oxide"]
    FormChannel --> CheckVDS{"V_DS less than V_OV?"}
    CheckVDS -->|"YES V_DS less than V_OV"| Triode["TRIODE REGION: Continuous channel from S to D, behaves as VCR, I_D quadratic in V_DS"]
    CheckVDS -->|"NO V_DS greater than or equal V_OV"| Pinchoff["Pinch-off at drain end: channel detached"]
    Pinchoff --> Saturation["SATURATION REGION: I_D = 0.5 k_n W by L V_OV squared, current source mode, CLM via lambda"]
    Triode -->|"Increase V_DS past V_OV"| Pinchoff
    Saturation -->|"Increase V_GS raises I_D proportionally to V_OV squared"| Saturation
```

**Interpretation of the state machine:**
- This flowchart precisely captures the **decision logic** an NMOS follows at every operating point.
- The boundary **V_DS = V_OV = V_GS − V_TH** is the **triode-to-saturation transition** — one of the most heavily tested concepts.
- Once in saturation, the current is **saturated** with respect to V_DS (ideally), but still **quadratically controlled** by V_OV.

## 4.3 I_D vs V_DS Characteristic Family — Functional Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Input_Variables["Input Variables"]
        VGS1["V_GS = 1.0 V"]
        VGS2["V_GS = 1.5 V"]
        VGS3["V_GS = 2.0 V"]
        VGS4["V_GS = 2.5 V"]
    end
    subgraph Transistor_NMOS["NMOS Device"]
        Device["Square-law device: I_D = 0.5 k_n W/L V_OV squared"]
    end
    subgraph Output_Curves["Output I_D vs V_DS Curves"]
        Curve1["Curve 1: V_GS = 1.0 V, V_OV = 0.4 V, I_Dsat low"]
        Curve2["Curve 2: V_GS = 1.5 V, V_OV = 0.9 V, I_Dsat medium"]
        Curve3["Curve 3: V_GS = 2.0 V, V_OV = 1.4 V, I_Dsat high"]
        Curve4["Curve 4: V_GS = 2.5 V, V_OV = 1.9 V, I_Dsat very high"]
    end
    VGS1 --> Device
    VGS2 --> Device
    VGS3 --> Device
    VGS4 --> Device
    Device --> Curve1
    Device --> Curve2
    Device --> Curve3
    Device --> Curve4
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each curve rises linearly at first (triode), then flattens (saturation) at I_D = 0.5·k_n·(W/L)·V_OV².
- The **knee** of each curve occurs at V_DS = V_OV.
- For V_GS < V_TH, the curve is essentially flat at I_D = 0 (cutoff).
- Real curves tilt slightly upward in saturation due to channel length modulation (λ ≠ 0).

## 4.4 Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit of NMOS in Saturation

```mermaid
graph TD
    G["Gate Node G"] -->|"No current flows into gate infinite R_in"| Gint["g_m times V_gs: Voltage-controlled current source"]
    S["Source Node S reference"] --> Gint
    Gint --> D["Drain Node D"]
    D --> ro["r_o = 1 by lambda I_D: Output resistance due to CLM"]
    ro --> S
    B["Body Node B"] --> gmb["g_mb times V_bs: Back-gate controlled current source"]
    gmb --> D
    S --> gmb
```

**Interpretation:**
- This is the **hybrid-π small-signal model** used for AC analysis of NMOS amplifiers.
- The **gate draws no current** (only the oxide capacitance C_ox matters at high frequency).
- The **current source g_m·V_gs** is the primary amplification mechanism.
- The **g_mb·V_bs source** accounts for body effect — important when source is not at AC ground.
- The **r_o** in parallel with the current source models finite output resistance due to channel length modulation.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define the threshold voltage of an NMOS transistor. Explain the body effect with a neat sketch showing how V_TH varies with source-to-body voltage V_SB.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The threshold voltage V_TH of an NMOS transistor is defined as the minimum gate-to-source voltage V_GS required to create a strong inversion layer (n-type channel) at the surface of the p-type substrate beneath the gate oxide, such that a measurable drain current begins to flow (conventionally defined at I_D = 0).

**Body Effect Explanation (1.5 marks):** The body effect is the phenomenon by which the threshold voltage V_TH of an NMOS increases as the source-to-body voltage V_SB becomes more positive. Mathematically:

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left[ \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right]$$

where γ is the body-effect coefficient. Physically, a positive V_SB reverse-biases the source-body p-n junction, widening the depletion region and reducing the surface potential available for inversion, thereby requiring a higher V_GS to achieve the same inversion charge density.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**With the help of the I_D–V_DS characteristic curves, explain the three regions of operation of an NMOS transistor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Cutoff Region (1 mark):** When V_GS < V_TH, no inversion layer forms, and the drain current I_D is essentially zero (only subthreshold leakage flows). The transistor is in the OFF state.

2. **Triode / Linear Region (1 mark):** When V_GS > V_TH and 0 < V_DS < (V_GS − V_TH), a continuous conducting channel exists from source to drain. The I_D versus V_DS curve rises approximately linearly for small V_DS, with the slope controlled by V_GS. Mathematically:

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

3. **Saturation / Active Region (1 mark):** When V_GS > V_TH and V_DS ≥ (V_GS − V_TH), the channel is pinched off near the drain. The drain current saturates to a value set only by V_GS and device geometry:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

The transistor behaves as a voltage-controlled current source in this region.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B — Option (A)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]

**Derive the I-V characteristics of an NMOS transistor in the triode and saturation regions using the gradual channel approximation. Clearly state the assumptions made.**

#### Part (a) — Triode Region Derivation [7 marks]

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The gradual channel approximation (GCA) holds: lateral electric field along the channel is much smaller than the transverse field from the gate.
2. The gate oxide is perfect — no gate leakage current.
3. The threshold voltage is constant along the channel (no body effect along the channel).
4. Carrier mobility μ_n is constant (no velocity saturation — valid for long-channel devices).
5. The p-n junctions between source/drain and body are reverse-biased (no substrate current).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Step 1:** At a point x along the channel, the local potential is V(x). The induced mobile electron charge per unit area is:

$$Q_n(x) = -C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x)\right]$$

**Step 2:** The drift current density is:

$$J_n(x) = -Q_n(x) \cdot \mu_n \cdot \frac{dV}{dx} = \mu_n C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x)\right] \frac{dV}{dx}$$

**Step 3:** The drain current (constant along the channel by KCL) is:

$$I_D = W \mu_n C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x)\right] \frac{dV}{dx}$$

**Step 4:** Separating variables and integrating from x = 0 (V = 0) to x = L (V = V_DS):

$$I_D \int_0^L dx = W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_0^{V_{DS}} \left[V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V\right] dV$$

**Step 5:** Performing the integration:

$$I_D \cdot L = W \mu_n C_{ox}\left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 6:** Final triode-region equation:

$$\boxed{\,I_{D,triode} = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]\,}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Saturation Region Derivation [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the pinch-off condition. The channel charge at the drain end becomes zero when:

$$V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} \equiv V_{OV}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute V_DS = V_OV into the triode equation:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{OV} - \frac{V_{OV}^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Simplify using V_OV = V_GS − V_TH:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\left[V_{OV}^2 - \frac{V_{OV}^2}{2}\right] = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}V_{OV}^2$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final ideal saturation equation:

$$\boxed{\,I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2\,}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Including channel length modulation (CLM) — physical explanation and modified equation:

When V_DS > V_OV, the pinch-off point moves toward the source by ΔL, shortening the effective channel to (L − ΔL). This gives:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

where λ is the channel-length modulation coefficient (≈ 0.01–0.1 V⁻¹). The factor (1 + λV_DS) shows that I_D has a weak linear dependence on V_DS in saturation, making the output resistance r_o = 1/(λI_D) finite.

---

### **Question B — Option (B)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]

**An NMOS transistor has the following parameters: μ_n C_ox = 100 μA/V², W = 10 μm, L = 1 μm, V_TH0 = 0.7 V, γ = 0.5 V^{1/2}, 2φ_F = 0.6 V, λ = 0.01 V⁻¹. The transistor is biased at V_GS = 1.5 V, V_DS = 2.0 V, V_SB = 1.0 V.**

**Compute:**
**(a)** The threshold voltage V_TH, the overdrive voltage V_OV, and the region of operation.
**(b)** The drain current I_D, transconductance g_m, body transconductance g_mb, output resistance r_o, and the intrinsic gain A_int = g_m·r_o.

#### Part (a) — V_TH, V_OV, and Region Identification [7 marks]

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute V_TH with body effect (V_SB = 1.0 V):

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma\left[\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right]$$

$$V_{TH} = 0.7 + 0.5\left[\sqrt{\vert 0.6 + 1.0 \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 0.6 \vert}\right]$$

$$V_{TH} = 0.7 + 0.5\left[\sqrt{1.6} - \sqrt{0.6}\right] = 0.7 + 0.5\left[1.2649 - 0.7746\right]$$

$$V_{TH} = 0.7 + 0.5 \cdot 0.4903 = 0.7 + 0.2452 = 0.9452 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute V_OV:

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 1.5 - 0.9452 = 0.5548 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute W/L ratio:

$$\frac{W}{L} = \frac{10\,\mu\text{m}}{1\,\mu\text{m}} = 10$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Determine region of operation:

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{OV} = 0.5548 \text{ V}; \quad V_{DS} = 2.0 \text{ V}$$

Since V_DS (2.0 V) > V_OV (0.5548 V), the transistor is in **SATURATION**.

> **Commentary (1 mark):** Note how the body effect increased V_TH from 0.7 V to 0.945 V, reducing the effective overdrive voltage by ~34% compared to the V_SB = 0 case. This is the degradation that body effect causes in stacked NMOS circuits.

---

#### Part (b) — I_D, g_m, g_mb, r_o, and A_int [7 marks]

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute I_D in saturation (with CLM):

$$k_n' \frac{W}{L} = 100\,\mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 10 = 1000\,\mu\text{A/V}^2 = 1 \text{ mA/V}^2$$

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1\,\text{mA/V}^2 \cdot (0.5548)^2 \cdot (1 + 0.01 \cdot 2.0)$$

$$I_D = 0.5 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.3078 \cdot 1.02 = 0.5 \cdot 0.3078 \cdot 1.02$$

$$I_D = 0.1570 \text{ mA} = 157.0\,\mu\text{A}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Compute g_m:

$$g_m = k_n' \frac{W}{L} \cdot V_{OV} = 1\,\text{mA/V}^2 \cdot 0.5548\,\text{V} = 0.5548 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute g_mb:

$$g_{mb} = g_m \cdot \frac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}} = 0.5548 \cdot \frac{0.5}{2\sqrt{1.6}}$$

$$g_{mb} = 0.5548 \cdot \frac{0.5}{2 \cdot 1.2649} = 0.5548 \cdot \frac{0.5}{2.5298} = 0.5548 \cdot 0.1976$$

$$g_{mb} = 0.1097 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute r_o:

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.01 \cdot 0.157\,\text{mA}} = \frac{1}{1.57\,\mu\text{A/V}} \approx 636.9 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute intrinsic gain:

$$A_{int} = g_m \cdot r_o = 0.5548\,\text{mA/V} \cdot 636.9\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$A_{int} \approx 353.4 \text{ V/V} \approx 50.96 \text{ dB}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks on NMOS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting body effect (2-3 marks lost):** Many KTU problems include a non-zero V_SB. Students who plug V_SB = 0 silently into the V_TH formula and use V_TH0 directly lose full marks. **Always check** if V_SB is given; if it is non-zero, **you must apply the body-effect correction.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the (1 + λV_DS) CLM factor (1-2 marks lost):** When λ ≠ 0 is specified in the problem, the saturation current **must** include this factor. Students who compute the "ideal" saturation current lose marks for incomplete treatment.
>
> 3. **Region misidentification (1-2 marks lost):** Computing I_D using the **triode equation** when V_DS > V_OV, or vice versa, is a fatal error. **Always** compute V_OV first, compare to V_DS, then select the correct region.
>
> 4. **Sign convention error in V_TH equation (1 mark lost):** The body-effect equation uses **square roots of absolute values**. Writing $\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ without the absolute-value bars is technically wrong when 2φ_F + V_SB < 0 (which doesn't occur in normal NMOS operation, but KTU strict graders may still mark down).
>
> 5. **Units inconsistency (1 mark lost):** Mixing μA with mA, or mA/V with μA/V, without unit conversion leads to a wrong numerical answer. **Always convert** to a consistent unit system (SI: A, V, m) before computing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NMOS Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 1 — High-Yield):**

- ☐ **NMOS = n-channel enhancement-mode MOSFET** — voltage-controlled, current flows via electrons, gate isolated by SiO₂.
- ☐ **Three regions:** Cutoff (V_GS < V_TH), Triode (V_GS > V_TH, V_DS < V_OV), Saturation (V_GS > V_TH, V_DS ≥ V_OV).
- ☐ **Overdrive voltage** V_OV = V_GS − V_TH is the **single most important parameter** for NMOS analysis — memorize it.
- ☐ **Triode I_D equation** is **quadratic in V_DS** with a maximum at V_DS = V_OV.
- ☐ **Saturation I_D equation** (ideal) is **quadratic in V_OV** and **independent of V_DS** (without CLM).
- ☐ **Body effect:** V_TH increases with V_SB due to widening of the source-body depletion region. Use the full square-root equation.
- ☐ **Channel length modulation (CLM):** Add factor (1 + λV_DS) to saturation I_D for finite output resistance r_o = 1/(λI_D).
- ☐ **Transconductance g_m** in saturation is **linear in V_OV** and **linear in (W/L)** — design levers for analog gain.
- ☐ **Body transconductance g_mb = η·g_m** where η = γ/(2√(2φ_F + V_SB)) is the body-effect factor (typically 0.1–0.3).
- ☐ **Intrinsic gain** A_int = g_m·r_o = 2·V_OV/(λ·V_DS) — sets the maximum gain of a single-stage CMOS amplifier.
- ☐ **On-resistance** R_DS,on = 1/[μ_n C_ox (W/L)(V_GS − V_TH)] — used when NMOS acts as a switch in deep triode.
- ☐ **Physical constants to memorize:** ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m, ε_ox = 3.45 × 10⁻¹¹ F/m, ε_si = 1.04 × 10⁻¹⁰ F/m, q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.
- ☐ **Process transconductance k_n' = μ_n C_ox** — technology-dependent gain factor (units A/V²).
- ☐ **Conduction parameter k_n = k_n' · (W/L)** — full device gain factor.
- ☐ **Gradual Channel Approximation (GCA)** is the foundation of all NMOS I-V derivations.
- ☐ **NMOS as a switch:** ON → deep triode (low R_DS,on), OFF → cutoff (no current).
- ☐ **NMOS passes a strong '0' but a weak '1'** in pass-transistor logic — due to threshold drop (an important KTU Module 1 concept).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CMOS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of CMOS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CMOS** stands for **Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor**. It is a dominant VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration) fabrication technology that uses **symmetrically paired p-type and n-type Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors (MOSFETs)** to implement logic gates, digital circuits, and analog subsystems on a single silicon die.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** CMOS technology is the process of constructing integrated circuits by combining **NMOS (n-channel MOS)** and **PMOS (p-channel MOS)** transistors on the same silicon substrate in a **complementary, pull-up (PMOS) and pull-down (NMOS)** configuration, enabling **ultra-low static power dissipation** and high noise immunity.

The fundamental building block is the **CMOS Inverter**, which is constructed by:
- A **PMOS transistor** connected between **$V_{DD}$** (positive supply) and the **Output Node**.
- An **NMOS transistor** connected between the **Output Node** and **$V_{SS}$** (ground, **0 V**).

The gates of both transistors are tied together to form the **Input Node**, and the drains converge to form the **Output Node**. This is the textbook **"complementary pair"** topology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of CMOS as a **two-handed water-pump system** working in perfect opposition:
- Imagine a mechanical water pump with **two handles** (one for the left hand, one for the right hand).
- The **PMOS handle** (right hand) is the "Pull-Up" — it lifts water (current) **UP** from the ground bucket into the output pipe.
- The **NMOS handle** (left hand) is the "Pull-Down" — it pulls water (current) **DOWN** from the output pipe to the ground bucket.
- You **never push both handles at the same time**. One is always firmly locked while the other moves.
- When the **input is HIGH (Logic 1)**: The NMOS pushes down (conducts), PMOS is locked → output goes LOW.
- When the **input is LOW (Logic 0)**: The PMOS pulls up (conducts), NMOS is locked → output goes HIGH.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Freshers:** The "complementary" magic is that in steady state, exactly **one transistor is ON** and the **other is OFF**. There is no direct current path from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{SS}$, which is why CMOS consumes almost **zero static power**.

## 1.3 Standard CMOS Physical Constants & Metrics

The following are **standard physical parameters** and **device metrics** for CMOS that every KTU student must memorize:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard CMOS Value | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Electron Mobility** | $\mu_n$ | **$\approx 1350 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V}\cdot\text{s}$** (bulk silicon) | Determines NMOS drive current |
| **Hole Mobility** | $\mu_p$ | **$\approx 480 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V}\cdot\text{s}$** | Determines PMOS drive current |
| **Mobility Ratio** | $r$ | $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.5$ to $3$ | Explains PMOS sizing requirement |
| **Gate Oxide Thickness** | $t_{ox}$ | **$\approx 2 \text{ nm}$ to $10 \text{ nm}$** (modern nodes) | Controls threshold voltage |
| **Supply Voltage** | $V_{DD}$ | **$0.7 \text{ V}$ to $3.3 \text{ V}$** (technology dependent) | Power/performance trade-off |
| **Threshold Voltage (NMOS)** | $V_{tn}$ | **$\approx 0.3 \text{ V}$ to $0.7 \text{ V}$** | Turn-on voltage of NMOS |
| **Threshold Voltage (PMOS)** | $V_{tp}$ | **$\approx -0.3 \text{ V}$ to $-0.7 \text{ V}$** | Turn-on voltage of PMOS |
| **Oxide Capacitance per unit area** | $C_{ox}$ | **$\varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$** | Process transconductance parameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mobility Asymmetry — The Hidden Reason for PMOS Upsizing:** Since holes ($\mu_p$) are **2.5×–3× slower** than electrons ($\mu_n$), to make a CMOS inverter **symmetrically balanced** (equal rise and fall times), the PMOS transistor's **Width-to-Length (W/L) ratio must be made 2× to 3× larger** than that of the NMOS. This is the famous **"PMOS Wp/Wn ≈ 2 to 3"** design rule every KTU examiner expects.

## 1.4 Why CMOS is the Backbone of VLSI

| Metric | NMOS-only / Bipolar | **CMOS** | Why CMOS Wins |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Static Power** | High (always conducting) | **Near Zero (nW range)** | No DC path in steady state |
| **Noise Margin** | Low | **High (~0.4 Vdd)** | Sharp VTC transition |
| **Power per Gate** | mW | **µW / nW** | Suitable for billion-gate chips |
| **Scalability** | Poor | **Excellent (Moore's Law)** | Dennard scaling friendly |
| **Integration Density** | Low | **Ultra High** | Small cell area, no resistors |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CMOS Inverter — Structural Anatomy

A standard **CMOS Inverter** is the parent cell of every CMOS gate. It consists of:

1. **Pull-Up Network (PUN):** Built using **PMOS** transistors, connected to **$V_{DD}$**.
2. **Pull-Down Network (PDN):** Built using **NMOS** transistors, connected to **$V_{SS}$** (GND).
3. **Input Node (Vin):** Connected to gates of all transistors.
4. **Output Node (Vout):** The shared drain terminal of the complementary pair.

### The Two Cardinal Rules of CMOS Logic Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 1 (Pull-Up):** The PUN uses **PMOS** devices, which conduct when the input is **LOW (Logic 0)**. The PUN must be a **dual of the PDN**.
>
> **Rule 2 (Pull-Down):** The PDN uses **NMOS** devices, which conduct when the input is **HIGH (Logic 1)**. The PDN structure must implement the **Boolean function F** (for the output's "pull to 0" condition).
>
> **Rule 3 (Inversion):** CMOS logic gates are **naturally inverting**. NAND, NOR, and AOI functions are direct. **Non-inverting gates (AND, OR) require an additional inverter stage.**

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Switching Operation

| Input State (Vin) | NMOS (PDN) State | PMOS (PUN) State | Output Node State | Logic Operation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Vin = 0 (LOW)** | **OFF** (Vgs < Vtn) | **ON** (Vsg < $\vert V_{tp}\vert$) | Vout pulled UP to **$V_{DD}$** | Output = **1** |
| **Vin = $V_{DD}$ (HIGH)** | **ON** (Vgs > Vtn) | **OFF** (Vsg > $\vert V_{tp}\vert$) | Vout pulled DOWN to **0 V** | Output = **0** |
| **Vin = $V_{DD}/2$ (Midpoint)** | Partially ON (Saturation) | Partially ON (Saturation) | Both conduct → **High shoot-through current** | Transition region |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Note:** The **switching threshold $V_M$** (or $V_{th,inv}$) is the point where **$V_{in} = V_{out}$**. This is the most important DC parameter. KTU questions on **symmetric inverter design** always revolve around calculating this point.

## 2.3 Voltage Transfer Characteristics (VTC) — The Heart of CMOS

The **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** is the plot of **$V_{out}$ versus $V_{in}$** as $V_{in}$ ramps from 0 to $V_{DD}$. The VTC is divided into **5 distinct regions**:

| Region | Vin Range | NMOS State | PMOS State | Vout Expression | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Region I** | $0 \le V_{in} < V_{tn}$ | Cutoff | Triode (Linear) | $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ | Output HIGH |
| **Region II** | $V_{tn} \le V_{in} < V_{out} + V_{tp}$ | Saturation | Triode | Decreasing | NMOS saturates, PMOS linear |
| **Region III** | $V_{in} = V_M$ | Saturation | Saturation | $V_{in} = V_{out}$ | Switching threshold |
| **Region IV** | $V_{out} + V_{tp} < V_{in} \le V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$ | Triode | Saturation | Decreasing | NMOS linear, PMOS saturates |
| **Region V** | $V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$ | Triode | Cutoff | $V_{out} = 0$ | Output LOW |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are board-exam gold. Memorize them in the form given.**

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Process Transconductance (NMOS)** | $k_n = \mu_n \, C_{ox} \dfrac{W_n}{L_n}$ | A/$V^2$ |
| 2 | **Process Transconductance (PMOS)** | $k_p = \mu_p \, C_{ox} \dfrac{W_p}{L_p}$ | A/$V^2$ |
| 3 | **Transconductance Ratio** | $k_r = \dfrac{k_n}{k_p} = \dfrac{\mu_n (W_n/L_n)}{\mu_p (W_p/L_p)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **Switching Threshold** | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{tn} + \sqrt{k_r}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$ | Volts |
| 5 | **Symmetric Inverter Condition** | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ | Balanced noise margins |
| 6 | **PMOS/NMOS Width Ratio for symmetry** | $\dfrac{W_p}{W_n} = \dfrac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot r_{design}$ | $r_{design} \approx 2$ to $3$ |
| 7 | **Noise Margin High** | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Volts |
| 8 | **Noise Margin Low** | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Volts |
| 9 | **Static Power Dissipation** | $P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD} \approx 0$ | Watts (ideal CMOS) |
| 10 | **Dynamic Power (Switching)** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f$ | Watts (dominant) |
| 11 | **Short-Circuit Power** | $P_{sc} = I_{peak} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot t_{sc} \cdot f$ | Watts |
| 12 | **Propagation Delay (approx.)** | $t_p = \dfrac{0.69 \, C_L}{2 \, k_n \, (V_{DD} - V_t)}$ | Seconds |
| 13 | **Oxide Capacitance** | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.9 \, \varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$ | F/m² |
| 14 | **Energy per Transition** | $E_{sw} = C_L \, V_{DD}^2$ | Joules |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners will often give you $\mu_n, \mu_p, V_{tn}, V_{tp}, V_{DD}$ and ask for the **PMOS/NMOS width ratio** to achieve a symmetric inverter. Plug into formula 6 directly.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CMOS inverter is the **fundamental computational primitive** in:
- **Microprocessors** (Intel, AMD, ARM cores) — billions of CMOS inverters.
- **DRAM & SRAM Memory Cells** — 6T SRAM uses two cross-coupled CMOS inverters.
- **ASICs and SoCs** — every NAND, NOR, XOR, and latch is built from CMOS pairs.
- **Analog/Mixed-Signal ICs** — Op-amps, ADCs, and PLLs use CMOS differential pairs.
- **IoT and Edge AI** — Ultra-low-power CMOS enables battery-operated AI chips.

The **energy per bit operation** in modern CMOS has dropped below **10 fJ (femto-Joules)**, making it indispensable for **AI accelerators** and **edge inference devices**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CMOS Inverter Switching Threshold ($V_M$)

The **switching threshold $V_M$** is defined as the value of $V_{in}$ at which **$V_{in} = V_{out}$**. At this point, both NMOS and PMOS are in **saturation**. The K-N and K-P currents must be equal (Kirchhoff's Current Law — no DC path to ground in steady state).

### Step 1: Write the saturation current equations for both transistors.

For **NMOS in saturation**:
$$
I_{DN} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{tn})^2 = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{tn})^2
$$

For **PMOS in saturation** (note: $V_{SG} = V_{DD} - V_M$):
$$
I_{DP} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{SG} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2
$$

### Step 2: Apply the KCL condition at the output node.

In the static transition point, the current flowing into the output from the PMOS must equal the current flowing out through the NMOS:
$$
I_{DN} = I_{DP}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the equations.

$$
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2
$$

### Step 4: Cancel the common factor ½ on both sides.

$$
k_n(V_M - V_{tn})^2 = k_p(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2
$$

### Step 5: Take the square root of both sides (taking the positive root, since both terms are positive at this point).

$$
\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_{tn}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{tp}\vert)
$$

### Step 6: Substitute the transconductance ratio $k_r = k_n / k_p$, so $\sqrt{k_n/k_p} = \sqrt{k_r}$.

$$
\sqrt{k_r}\,(V_M - V_{tn}) = (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{tp}\vert)
$$

### Step 7: Expand both sides.

$$
\sqrt{k_r}\,V_M - \sqrt{k_r}\,V_{tn} = V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{tp}\vert
$$

### Step 8: Collect all $V_M$ terms on the left side.

$$
\sqrt{k_r}\,V_M + V_M = V_{DD} + \sqrt{k_r}\,V_{tn} - \vert V_{tp}\vert
$$

### Step 9: Factor $V_M$ on the left.

$$
V_M(1 + \sqrt{k_r}) = V_{DD} + \sqrt{k_r}\,V_{tn} - \vert V_{tp}\vert
$$

### Step 10: Solve for $V_M$.

$$
V_M = \frac{V_{tn} + \sqrt{\frac{1}{k_r}}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\frac{1}{k_r}}}
$$

Or equivalently, the **standard KTU form**:
$$
V_M = \frac{V_{tn} + \sqrt{k_r}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}
$$

where $k_r = \dfrac{k_n}{k_p}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The result above is the most-asked CMOS derivation in KTU University Exams. Practice writing it 5 times from memory.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation of Symmetric Inverter PMOS/NMOS Ratio

For a **symmetric (balanced) CMOS inverter**, we require **$V_M = V_{DD}/2$**. This guarantees **equal rise and fall times** and **equal noise margins**.

### Step 1: Set $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ in the switching threshold equation.

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{V_{tn} + \sqrt{k_r}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}
$$

### Step 2: Cross-multiply.

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2}(1 + \sqrt{k_r}) = V_{tn} + \sqrt{k_r}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)
$$

### Step 3: Expand the left side.

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2} + \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\sqrt{k_r} = V_{tn} + \sqrt{k_r}\,V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_r}\,\vert V_{tp}\vert
$$

### Step 4: Collect terms containing $\sqrt{k_r}$ on one side.

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2}\sqrt{k_r} - \sqrt{k_r}\,V_{DD} + \sqrt{k_r}\,\vert V_{tp}\vert = V_{tn} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}
$$

### Step 5: Factor out $\sqrt{k_r}$.

$$
\sqrt{k_r}\left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{DD} + \vert V_{tp}\vert\right) = V_{tn} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}
$$

### Step 6: Simplify the bracket.

$$
\sqrt{k_r}\left(\vert V_{tp}\vert - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right) = V_{tn} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}
$$

### Step 7: Multiply by $-1$ on both sides to flip the signs (note: $\vert V_{tp}\vert - V_{DD}/2$ is negative in typical designs).

$$
\sqrt{k_r}\left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - \vert V_{tp}\vert\right) = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{tn}
$$

### Step 8: Solve for $\sqrt{k_r}$.

$$
\sqrt{k_r} = \frac{\dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{tn}}{\dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} - \vert V_{tp}\vert}
$$

### Step 9: Substitute $k_r = k_n/k_p$ and recall $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} (W_n/L_n)$, $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} (W_p/L_p)$.

Assuming equal channel lengths $L_n = L_p = L$ (a standard layout choice), then:
$$
\sqrt{k_r} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_n W_n}{\mu_p W_p}}
$$

### Step 10: Equate and solve for $W_p/W_n$.

$$
\frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \left(\frac{\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - \vert V_{tp}\vert}{\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{tn}}\right)^2
$$

If we further assume $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert$ (matched process), this reduces to the **famous KTU result**:
$$
\frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \approx 2.5 \text{ to } 3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This ratio $W_p/W_n \approx 2.5$–$3$ is the most common numerical answer in KTU CMOS problems. Memorize it.**

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Question Style)

**Problem (KTU University Exam Pattern):**
A CMOS inverter is fabricated in a **$0.18 \, \mu m$** process with **$V_{DD} = 1.8 \, V$**, $V_{tn} = 0.4 \, V$, $\vert V_{tp}\vert = 0.4 \, V$, $\mu_n = 600 \, cm^2/V\cdot s$, $\mu_p = 200 \, cm^2/V\cdot s$. The NMOS has $W_n/L_n = 2$. Find the **PMOS W/L ratio** required for a symmetric inverter and the **switching threshold** $V_M$.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert = 0.4 \, V$
- $\mu_n / \mu_p = 600 / 200 = 3$
- $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert$ ⟹ symmetric threshold simplifies

**Step 1:** Since $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert$, the symmetric PMOS ratio is:
$$
\frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} = 3
$$

**Step 2:** Given $W_n/L_n = 2$, therefore:
$$
\frac{W_p}{L_p} = 3 \times 2 = 6
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the switching threshold using the simplified form:
$$
V_M = \frac{V_{tn} + \sqrt{k_r}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}
$$

Since $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert$, $\sqrt{k_r} = \sqrt{\mu_n W_n / (\mu_p W_p)} = \sqrt{(600 \cdot 2)/(200 \cdot 6)} = \sqrt{1} = 1$:
$$
V_M = \frac{0.4 + 1 \times (1.8 - 0.4)}{1 + 1} = \frac{0.4 + 1.4}{2} = \frac{1.8}{2} = 0.9 \, V
$$

**Final Answer:** $V_M = 0.9 \, V = V_{DD}/2$. The design is perfectly symmetric. ✔
