# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define CMOS technology. Why is it preferred over NMOS-only logic in modern VLSI design?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** CMOS stands for **Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor**. It is a VLSI technology that uses **symmetric pairs of NMOS (Pull-Down) and PMOS (Pull-Up)** transistors on the same silicon substrate to implement logic functions.
- **Static Power Advantage (1 Mark):** In steady state, **one transistor is always OFF**, so there is **no direct current path from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{SS}$**, resulting in **near-zero static power dissipation**.
- **Noise Immunity & Density (1 Mark):** CMOS offers **high noise margins (~0.4 $V_{DD}$)**, **high integration density**, and excellent **scalability** (Moore's Law compatibility), making it the universal choice over pure NMOS logic.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the switching threshold ($V_M$) of a CMOS inverter? Write its expression in terms of $V_{tn}, V_{tp}, k_n, k_p$ and $V_{DD}$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** The **switching threshold $V_M$** is the point on the VTC curve where **$V_{in} = V_{out}$**. At this point, both NMOS and PMOS are in **saturation**, and the slope of the VTC is **infinite** (ideal sharp transition).
- **Expression (2 Marks):**
$$
V_M = \frac{V_{tn} + \sqrt{k_r}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}, \quad \text{where } k_r = \frac{k_n}{k_p}
$$
- For a **symmetric (balanced) inverter**, $V_M = V_{DD}/2$, achieved by setting $W_p/W_n = \mu_n/\mu_p$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **ONE full question** out of the two alternatives provided.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **operation of a CMOS inverter**. Identify all **five regions** of its Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) and draw the VTC curve. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS inverter is fabricated in a **$0.25 \, \mu m$** technology with the following parameters: $V_{DD} = 2.5 \, V$, $V_{tn} = 0.5 \, V$, $\vert V_{tp}\vert = 0.5 \, V$, $\mu_n = 460 \, cm^2/V\cdot s$, $\mu_p = 160 \, cm^2/V\cdot s$, $(W/L)_n = 4$, $(W/L)_p = 12$. Calculate: **(i)** the switching threshold $V_M$, and **(ii)** the **PMOS/NMOS width ratio** required for a symmetric inverter. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) CMOS Inverter Operation & VTC (7 Marks):**

A CMOS inverter consists of a **PMOS transistor** as the **Pull-Up Network (PUN)** and an **NMOS transistor** as the **Pull-Down Network (PDN)**, with their gates tied together to form the input.

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
- **When $V_{in} = 0$ (LOW):** NMOS has $V_{GS} = 0 < V_{tn}$, hence **OFF**. PMOS has $V_{SG} = V_{DD} > \vert V_{tp}\vert$, hence **ON**. Output is pulled **UP to $V_{DD}$** (Logic 1).
- **When $V_{in} = V_{DD}$ (HIGH):** NMOS has $V_{GS} = V_{DD} > V_{tn}$, hence **ON**. PMOS has $V_{SG} = 0 < \vert V_{tp}\vert$, hence **OFF**. Output is pulled **DOWN to 0 V** (Logic 0).
- **When $0 < V_{in} < V_{DD}$:** Both transistors conduct partially. The transition from HIGH to LOW occurs in the **switching region** centered at $V_M$.

**Five Regions of VTC (3 Marks):**

| Region | Vin Range | NMOS | PMOS | Vout |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| I | $0 \le V_{in} < V_{tn}$ | Cutoff | Triode | $V_{DD}$ |
| II | $V_{tn} \le V_{in} < V_{out} + V_{tp}$ | Saturation | Triode | Decreasing |
| III | $V_{in} = V_M$ | Saturation | Saturation | $V_M$ |
| IV | $V_{out} + V_{tp} < V_{in} \le V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$ | Triode | Saturation | Decreasing |
| V | $V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$ | Triode | Cutoff | 0 |

**VTC Curve (1 Mark):** A sharp S-shaped curve with five distinct regions, high noise margins, and the switching point at $V_M$.

**Diagram (Refer to Section 4.3 block diagram above for the region-by-region VTC map).**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $V_{DD} = 2.5 \, V$, $V_{tn} = 0.5 \, V$, $\vert V_{tp}\vert = 0.5 \, V$, $\mu_n = 460$, $\mu_p = 160$, $(W/L)_n = 4$, $(W/L)_p = 12$.

**Step 1: Compute the transconductance ratio $k_r$.** (2 Marks)
$$
k_r = \frac{k_n}{k_p} = \frac{\mu_n (W_n/L_n)}{\mu_p (W_p/L_p)} = \frac{460 \times 4}{160 \times 12} = \frac{1840}{1920} = 0.9583
$$

**Step 2: Calculate $\sqrt{k_r}$.** (1 Mark)
$$
\sqrt{k_r} = \sqrt{0.9583} = 0.9789
$$

**Step 3: Compute the switching threshold $V_M$.** (2 Marks)
$$
V_M = \frac{V_{tn} + \sqrt{k_r}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}} = \frac{0.5 + 0.9789 \times (2.5 - 0.5)}{1 + 0.9789}
$$

$$
V_M = \frac{0.5 + 0.9789 \times 2.0}{1.9789} = \frac{0.5 + 1.9578}{1.9789} = \frac{2.4578}{1.9789} = 1.242 \, V
$$

**Step 4: For a symmetric inverter, $V_M = V_{DD}/2 = 1.25 \, V$.** (1 Mark)

**Step 5: Compute the required PMOS/NMOS width ratio for symmetry.** (1 Mark)
$$
\frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} = \frac{460}{160} = 2.875
$$

**Final Answer:** $V_M = 1.242 \, V$ (which is very close to $V_{DD}/2 = 1.25 \, V$, indicating near-symmetric design). Required symmetric ratio $W_p/W_n \approx 2.875 \approx 3$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **Pull-Up Network (PUN)** and **Pull-Down Network (PDN)** design methodology for CMOS logic gates. Construct the **CMOS schematic for a 2-input NAND gate** and a **2-input NOR gate**, and derive the **Boolean output expressions**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **sources of power dissipation in CMOS circuits** with relevant formulas. Compare **static power, dynamic switching power, and short-circuit power**, and explain how the equation $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ drives the design of low-power VLSI systems. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) PUN/PDN Design & 2-Input CMOS NAND/NOR (7 Marks):**

**PUN/PDN Design Rules (3 Marks):**
- The **PUN (Pull-Up Network)** uses **PMOS transistors** and is responsible for **pulling the output to $V_{DD}$** when the Boolean function evaluates to **Logic 1**.
- The **PDN (Pull-Down Network)** uses **NMOS transistors** and is responsible for **pulling the output to $V_{SS}$** when the Boolean function evaluates to **Logic 0**.
- The PUN and PDN are **duals of each other**: series in PUN corresponds to parallel in PDN and vice versa.
- At any input combination, **exactly one network is conducting** and the other is OFF, ensuring no static current path.

**2-Input CMOS NAND Gate (2 Marks):**

A 2-input NAND gate has the output function: $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$.

- **PDN (NMOS):** Two NMOS transistors in **series** (to realize the AND condition $A \cdot B$ for pulling down).
- **PUN (PMOS):** Two PMOS transistors in **parallel** (dual of the series PDN).
- **Operation:** If **both A AND B are HIGH**, both NMOS conduct (output = 0). Otherwise, at least one PMOS conducts (output = 1).

**Boolean Verification:** The output is LOW only when $A = 1$ AND $B = 1$. In all other input combinations, the output is HIGH. Therefore, $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$ (NAND).

**2-Input CMOS NOR Gate (2 Marks):**

A 2-input NOR gate has the output function: $F = \overline{A + B}$.

- **PDN (NMOS):** Two NMOS transistors in **parallel** (to realize the OR condition $A + B$ for pulling down).
- **PUN (PMOS):** Two PMOS transistors in **series** (dual of the parallel PDN).
- **Operation:** If **either A OR B is HIGH**, the corresponding NMOS conducts (output = 0). Only when **both A and B are LOW** do both PMOS conduct (output = 1).

**Boolean Verification:** The output is LOW if $A = 1$ OR $B = 1$. Otherwise, the output is HIGH. Therefore, $F = \overline{A + B}$ (NOR).

---

**(b) Power Dissipation in CMOS (7 Marks):**

**Three Sources of Power Dissipation (3 Marks):**

1. **Static Power (Leakage Power):** Even when the transistor is OFF, sub-threshold leakage and reverse-biased junction leakage flow. Modern CMOS leakage can be in the range of **nA to µA** per gate.
$$
P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}
$$

2. **Dynamic Switching Power:** This is the **dominant** power in CMOS. Power is dissipated when the load capacitor $C_L$ is charged and discharged through the transistors at the clock frequency $f$.
$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f
$$
where $\alpha$ is the **switching activity factor** (0 to 1).

3. **Short-Circuit Power:** During the brief transition period (when both NMOS and PMOS are partially ON), a direct current path exists from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{SS}$, causing **shoot-through current**.
$$
P_{sc} = I_{peak} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot t_{sc} \cdot f
$$

**Design Implications of $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ (4 Marks):**

- **$V_{DD}^2$ Dominance:** Power scales **quadratically** with supply voltage. This is the **most exploited knob** in low-power design — dropping $V_{DD}$ from 1.8 V to 0.9 V gives a **4× power reduction**.
- **Clock Frequency $f$:** Reducing clock frequency linearly reduces power. This is exploited in **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling)** used in mobile processors.
- **Switching Activity $\alpha$:** Power can be reduced by minimizing unnecessary transitions. Techniques include **clock gating, operand isolation, and encoding schemes** (e.g., Gray code addressing).
- **Capacitive Load $C_L$:** Reducing wire and gate capacitance (via shorter interconnects, multi- $V_{th}$ cells) directly reduces power.
- **Total Power Equation:**
$$
P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dyn} + P_{sc} = I_{leak} V_{DD} + \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f + I_{peak} V_{DD} t_{sc} f
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse static and dynamic power.** Many students write "CMOS consumes zero power" — this is only true for **ideal static power**. Real CMOS has **leakage power**, which has become dominant in sub-90 nm technologies.
> 2. **Mobility ratio pitfall:** The ratio $W_p/W_n = \mu_n / \mu_p$ holds **only when $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert$**. If the thresholds are unequal, you must use the full square-root formula or re-derive from scratch. Examiners will deduct marks if you blindly apply the simplified ratio.
> 3. **PMOS current direction:** In saturation equations for PMOS, students often forget the sign convention: $I_{DP} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{SG} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$, where $V_{SG} = V_{DD} - V_{in}$.
> 4. **Switching threshold formula:** Always double-check whether $V_M$ is defined as $V_{in}$ at $V_{in} = V_{out}$. If the question gives a different definition (e.g., $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ for symmetry), derive accordingly.
> 5. **Power units:** Many students forget that the formula $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ gives power **per gate**. For an entire chip, multiply by the **number of switching gates per cycle**.
> 6. **PUN/PDN duality inversion:** For NAND, PDN is series and PUN is parallel. For NOR, PDN is parallel and PUN is series. Mixing this up will cost **2 full marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **CMOS Definition:** Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor — pair of NMOS + PMOS transistors.
- **Complementary Pair:** PMOS Pull-Up (to $V_{DD}$) + NMOS Pull-Down (to $V_{SS}$).
- **Steady-State Property:** One transistor ON, the other OFF → **near-zero static power**.
- **Mobility Asymmetry:** $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.5$ to $3$ → PMOS must be 2×–3× wider than NMOS.
- **Switching Threshold Formula:**
$$
V_M = \frac{V_{tn} + \sqrt{k_r}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}
$$
- **Symmetric Inverter:** $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ achieved by $W_p/W_n = \mu_n / \mu_p$.
- **VTC Five Regions:** Cutoff-Triode → Saturation-Triode → Both Saturation → Triode-Saturation → Triode-Cutoff.
- **Noise Margins:** $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ and $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$.
- **PUN/PDN Duality Rule:** Series in PUN ↔ Parallel in PDN, and vice versa.
- **NAND Gate:** PDN series, PUN parallel. **NOR Gate:** PDN parallel, PUN series.
- **Dynamic Power Equation:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — the **dominant** power term.
- **Short-Circuit Power:** Occurs during switching transitions when both transistors conduct.
- **Static Power:** Due to sub-threshold and junction leakage; dominant in sub-90 nm processes.
- **Propagation Delay:** $t_p \approx 0.69 \, C_L / [2 k_n (V_{DD} - V_t)]$.
- **Energy per Transition:** $E_{sw} = C_L V_{DD}^2$ — independent of frequency.
- **Dennard Scaling:** Power density remains constant as transistors shrink (broken at ~90 nm).
- **KTU High-Yield Numbers:** $V_{tn} \approx 0.4 \, V$, $\vert V_{tp}\vert \approx 0.4 \, V$, $V_{DD}$ typically $0.7$–$3.3 \, V$, $W_p/W_n \approx 2.875$ for $\mu_n/\mu_p = 3$.
- **Always carry the units:** $k_n, k_p$ in **A/V²**, $V_M$ in **Volts**, $C_{ox}$ in **F/m²**, power in **Watts** or **µW**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMOS Inverter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CMOS Inverter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **CMOS Inverter** (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Inverter) is the most fundamental and widely used logic gate in modern Very-Large-Scale Integration (VLSI) digital design. It consists of a **pair of complementary MOSFETs** — one **n-channel MOSFET (NMOS)** as the pull-down network (PDN) and one **p-channel MOSFET (PMOS)** as the pull-up network (PUN) — connected in series between the power supply rail $V_{DD}$ and ground, with their gates tied together to form the input node and their drains tied together to form the output node.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST415 Module 1):**
> "Static CMOS Inverter — DC Characteristics, Switching Threshold, Noise Margins, Static and Dynamic Power Dissipation, Propagation Delay."

The CMOS inverter operates on the principle of **complementary switching**: when the input is logic LOW, the PMOS turns ON and the NMOS turns OFF, pulling the output to $V_{DD}$ (logic HIGH). When the input is logic HIGH, the PMOS turns OFF and the NMOS turns ON, pulling the output to GND (logic LOW). This **rail-to-rail** output swing and the absence of any static current path between $V_{DD}$ and ground are the defining hallmarks of static CMOS logic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dual-Valve Water Tap"

Imagine a clever water tap system connected between a high water tank ($V_{DD}$) and a drainage sink (GND):

- You attach **two valves on the same handle**: one valve opens the pipe from the tank to the tap, and the other valve simultaneously closes the pipe to the drain.
- When you push the handle **down (Input = 1 / HIGH)**: the upper valve snaps shut (PMOS OFF) and the lower valve opens wide (NMOS ON) → water flows straight into the drain, so the tap output is **dry (Logic 0)**.
- When you pull the handle **up (Input = 0 / LOW)**: the upper valve opens (PMOS ON) and the lower valve closes (NMOS OFF) → water gushes out of the tap, so the tap output is **wet at full pressure (Logic 1)**.
- **Critically**: it is *physically impossible* for both valves to be open at the same time. Hence, no water is ever wasted by flowing from tank directly to drain — this corresponds to **zero static power dissipation** in CMOS.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the standard KTU-referenced parameters for CMOS Inverter analysis:

- **$V_{DD}$** — Power supply voltage (typically **1.8 V, 3.3 V, or 5 V** for older nodes; sub-1 V in advanced nanometer nodes).
- **$V_{TN}$** — Threshold voltage of the NMOS transistor (positive, typically **0.4 V to 0.7 V**).
- **$V_{TP}$** — Threshold voltage of the PMOS transistor (negative, typically **−0.4 V to −0.7 V**).
- **$k_n$ and $k_p$** — Transconductance parameters for NMOS and PMOS, where $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n$ and $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p$.
- **$C_L$** — Output load capacitance (typically in the range of **femtofarads to picofarads**).
- **Process mobility ratio** $r = k_p / k_n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two transistors are fabricated to be **perfect complements** on the same silicon die. Since electron mobility $\mu_n$ is approximately **2 to 3 times greater** than hole mobility $\mu_p$, designers must size the PMOS device with a larger $W/L$ ratio (typically $(W/L)_p \approx 2$ to $2.5 \times (W/L)_n$) to make the high-to-low and low-to-high drive strengths **symmetric** and balance the propagation delays.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology

A static CMOS inverter consists of:
1. **PMOS pull-up transistor** ($M_P$): Source tied to $V_{DD}$, Drain tied to **Output Node (OUT)**, Gate tied to **Input Node (IN)**. Bulk tied to $V_{DD}$.
2. **NMOS pull-down transistor** ($M_N$): Source tied to **GND (0 V)**, Drain tied to **Output Node (OUT)**, Gate tied to **Input Node (IN)**. Bulk tied to GND.

Both gates receive the same input voltage $V_{in}$. The output node is labeled $V_{out}$.

## 2.2 Regions of Operation (Voltage Transfer Characteristic Analysis)

As $V_{in}$ sweeps from 0 V to $V_{DD}$, the CMOS inverter traverses **five distinct operating regions**. Understanding these is the most important step in KTU board exam answers.

| Region | $V_{in}$ Range | NMOS State | PMOS State | Output Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Region A** | $0 \le V_{in} < V_{TN}$ | Cutoff (OFF) | Linear (Triode) | $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ (Logic HIGH) |
| **Region B** | $V_{TN} \le V_{in} < V_{in}^{*}$ | Saturation | Saturation | $V_{out}$ begins to drop sharply |
| **Region C** | $V_{in} = V_{in}^{*}$ (Switching Point) | Saturation | Saturation | $V_{out} = V_{M}$ (Critical transition) |
| **Region D** | $V_{in}^{*} < V_{in} \le V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$ | Saturation | Linear | $V_{out}$ continues to drop |
| **Region E** | $V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$ | Linear (Triode) | Cutoff (OFF) | $V_{out} = 0$ (Logic LOW) |

> The notation $V_{in}^{*}$ denotes the switching threshold input voltage where $V_{in} = V_{out}$, i.e., the point on the VTC where the curve crosses the **$V_{out} = V_{in}$ diagonal line**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **high-yield KTU reference** for all numerical problems on CMOS Inverter DC characteristics.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Switching Threshold $V_M$ | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{r}\,(V_{DD} + V_{TP})}{1 + \sqrt{r}}$ | $r = k_p / k_n$ |
| 2 | Simplified Symmetric $V_M$ | $V_M = V_{DD} / 2$ | Valid when $r = 1$ and $\vert V_{TP} \vert = V_{TN}$ |
| 3 | NMOS Saturation Current | $I_{DN,sat} = \dfrac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{TN})^2$ | $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_{TN}$ |
| 4 | PMOS Saturation Current | $I_{DP,sat} = \dfrac{k_p}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{TP})^2$ | Magnitude; $V_{SG} \ge \vert V_{TP} \vert$ |
| 5 | Output High Voltage $V_{OH}$ | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | Ideal CMOS |
| 6 | Output Low Voltage $V_{OL}$ | $V_{OL} = 0$ | Ideal CMOS |
| 7 | Input High Voltage $V_{IH}$ | Slope $= -1$ on VTC | Derived graphically/numerically |
| 8 | Input Low Voltage $V_{IL}$ | Slope $= -1$ on VTC | Derived graphically/numerically |
| 9 | Noise Margin High $NM_H$ | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Ideally $V_{DD}/2$ |
| 10 | Noise Margin Low $NM_L$ | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Ideally $V_{DD}/2$ |
| 11 | Static Power Dissipation $P_{static}$ | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{static} = 0$ | Zero in ideal CMOS |
| 12 | Dynamic Power Dissipation $P_{dynamic}$ | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity factor |
| 13 | Short-Circuit Power $P_{sc}$ | $P_{sc} \approx \dfrac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_T)^{3} \cdot \tau \cdot f$ | During brief transition overlap |
| 14 | Propagation Delay (inverter) $t_p$ | $t_p = \dfrac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2}$ | Average of rise & fall times |
| 15 | $t_{pHL}$ (fall time) | $t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_L}{k_n(V_{DD} - V_{TN})} \left[\dfrac{2 V_{TN}}{V_{DD} - V_{TN}} + \ln\!\left(3 - \dfrac{4V_{TN}}{V_{DD}}\right)\right]^{-1}$ | From 50% input to 50% output |
| 16 | Energy per transition | $E = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ | Fundamental CMOS energy unit |
| 17 | Resistance Ratio $r$ | $r = \dfrac{k_p}{k_n} = \dfrac{\mu_p (W/L)_p}{\mu_n (W/L)_n}$ | For symmetric: $r = 1$ |
| 18 | Mobility Ratio (process) | $r_{process} = \mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2$ to $3$ | PMOS needs larger $W/L$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CMOS inverter is the **atomic building block** of nearly every digital integrated circuit manufactured today:

- **Microprocessors**: Billions of CMOS inverters (and inverter-derived NAND/NOR gates) form the cores, caches, and control logic.
- **DRAM & SRAM Memory Cells**: Each memory cell uses a pair of cross-coupled CMOS inverters as a bistable latch.
- **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs)**: CMOS inverters serve as high-gain comparators in flash and successive-approximation architectures.
- **Ring Oscillators**: An odd number of CMOS inverters in a loop generates a stable clock reference, fundamental to PLLs and on-chip timing.
- **I/O Buffers**: The drivers that connect the chip to the outside world are scaled-up CMOS inverters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Switching Threshold $V_M$ (Most Important KTU Derivation)

The switching threshold $V_M$ is defined as the point on the VTC where the input voltage equals the output voltage:
$$V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$$

**Step 1 — Identify the operating region at $V_M$:**

At the switching point, both transistors are conducting. Since $V_{out}$ is roughly mid-rail (around $V_{DD}/2$), both $V_{DS}$ for NMOS and $\vert V_{SD} \vert$ for PMOS are *larger* than their respective overdrive voltages. Therefore, **both devices operate in the SATURATION region**.

**Step 2 — Apply the saturation current equations:**

The drain current of the NMOS in saturation is:
$$I_{DN} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS,n} - V_{TN})^{2}$$

For the NMOS, $V_{GS,n} = V_{in} = V_M$. Substituting:
$$I_{DN} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{TN})^{2}$$

The drain current of the PMOS in saturation is:
$$I_{DP} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{SG,p} - \vert V_{TP} \vert)^{2}$$

For the PMOS, $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_{in} = V_{DD} - V_M$. Substituting:
$$I_{DP} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP} \vert)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the output node:**

Since the output node is floating (no external load) and the two transistors are in series between $V_{DD}$ and GND, the same current must flow through both. Therefore:
$$I_{DN} = I_{DP}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and simplify:**
$$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{TN})^{2} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP} \vert)^{2}$$

Cancel the common factor $1/2$:
$$k_n(V_M - V_{TN})^{2} = k_p(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP} \vert)^{2}$$

Take the positive square root of both sides (the only physically meaningful branch):
$$\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_{TN}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP} \vert)$$

Define the device ratio $r = k_p / k_n$, so $\sqrt{k_p} = \sqrt{r \cdot k_n}$. Then:
$$\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_{TN}) = \sqrt{r \cdot k_n}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP} \vert)$$

Divide both sides by $\sqrt{k_n}$:
$$V_M - V_{TN} = \sqrt{r}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP} \vert)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_M$ algebraically:**

Expand the right-hand side:
$$V_M - V_{TN} = \sqrt{r}\,V_{DD} - \sqrt{r}\,V_M - \sqrt{r}\,\vert V_{TP} \vert$$

Collect all $V_M$ terms on the left:
$$V_M + \sqrt{r}\,V_M = V_{TN} + \sqrt{r}\,V_{DD} - \sqrt{r}\,\vert V_{TP} \vert$$

Factor out $V_M$:
$$V_M(1 + \sqrt{r}) = V_{TN} + \sqrt{r}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP} \vert)$$

Divide by $(1 + \sqrt{r})$ to obtain the final closed-form expression:

$$
\boxed{\,V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{r}\,(V_{DD} + V_{TP})}{1 + \sqrt{r}}\,}
$$

(Note: $V_{TP}$ is negative, so $V_{DD} + V_{TP} = V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$.)

**Step 6 — Verification using the symmetric design rule:**

If the inverter is designed **symmetrically** (i.e., $r = 1$ and $\vert V_{TP} \vert = V_{TN}$), substitute:
$$V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + 1 \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{TN})}{1 + 1} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

This confirms that a **symmetric CMOS inverter switches exactly at the midpoint** of the supply — the most desirable property for maximum noise immunity. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of Noise Margins

The noise margins are derived from the **critical points** on the VTC where the slope of the transfer curve equals $-1$.

**Step 1 — Recall the saturation-region current equation for the NMOS** (with $V_{DS} = V_{out}$):
$$I_{DN} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{in} - V_{TN})^{2} \quad \text{(valid when } V_{out} \ge V_{in} - V_{TN} \text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Express the output node equation** in the saturation-saturation region:
$$V_{out} = V_{in} - V_{TN} + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}\,\bigl[(V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{TP} \vert) - (V_{in} - V_{TN})\bigr]$$

(Obtained by integrating the small-signal current relation in the transition region.)

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $V_{in}$** and set $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ to find $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$.

After differentiation, simplification, and substitution, the standard closed-form results are:

$$
V_{IH} = \frac{2V_{out,M} + V_{TN}(r-1) - V_{DD}(r+1)}{3r+1} + \text{(higher-order terms)}
$$

$$
V_{IL} = \frac{V_{DD}(3r+1) - 2V_{out,M}(2r+1) - V_{TN}(r+1)}{3r+1}
$$

**Step 4 — The noise margins are then computed as:**
$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}, \qquad NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$$

For the symmetric case ($r = 1$, $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} = 0$):
$$NM_H = NM_L = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_T$$

These are the **maximum theoretical noise margins** of a symmetric CMOS inverter.

## 3.3 Derivation of Dynamic Power Dissipation

**Step 1 — Energy stored on a capacitor charged to voltage $V_{DD}$:**
$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Energy drawn from the supply during charging** (PMOS is in triode, acts as a resistor):
$$E_{supplied} = \int_{0}^{T} V_{DD} \cdot i_{DD}\,dt = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated in the PMOS during one charging cycle:**
$$E_{PMOS} = E_{supplied} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} - \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^{2} = \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Energy dissipated in the NMOS during one discharge cycle** (energy stored in $C_L$ is fully dumped to GND):
$$E_{NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Total energy per switching cycle:**
$$E_{cycle} = E_{PMOS} + E_{NMOS} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by switching frequency** $f$ and activity factor $\alpha$:
$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot f \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

This is the **fundamental CMOS power equation** and is the single most cited formula in low-power VLSI design. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Schematic — CMOS Inverter

The figure below is a **block-level functional architecture flow** of the CMOS inverter. It traces the input signal through the pull-up and pull-down networks to the output node, annotated with the controlling node voltages and current paths.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Voltage VIN] --> B[Gates of M1 and M2]
    B --> C{Region of Operation}
    C -->|VIN is LOW| D[PMOS M1 is ON]
    C -->|VIN is HIGH| E[NMOS M2 is ON]
    D --> F[Current path: VDD to OUT]
    E --> G[Current path: OUT to GND]
    F --> H[Output Node VOUT pulled HIGH]
    G --> I[Output Node VOUT pulled LOW]
    H --> J[Logic Level 1: VOUT equals VDD]
    I --> K[Logic Level 0: VOUT equals 0 V]
    J --> L[Load Capacitance CL charged]
    K --> M[Load Capacitance CL discharged]
    L --> N[Dynamic power: alpha C VDD squared f]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 VTC Operational Regions — Functional Flow

This block diagram maps the **five operating regions** of the CMOS inverter as the input voltage ramps from 0 to $V_{DD}$. It serves as the primary mental model for analyzing the Voltage Transfer Characteristic.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[VIN sweeps from 0 to VDD] --> R1[Region A: VIN less than VTN]
    R1 --> S1[NMOS Cutoff, PMOS Triode]
    S1 --> O1[VOUT equals VDD]
    O1 --> R2[Region B: VTN less than VIN less than Vstar]
    R2 --> S2[Both NMOS and PMOS in Saturation]
    S2 --> O2[VOUT drops steeply through VDD by 2]
    O2 --> R3[Region C: VIN equals Vstar equals Vout equals VM]
    R3 --> S3[Critical Switching Point]
    S3 --> R4[Region D: Vstar less than VIN less than VDD minus VTP]
    R4 --> S4[NMOS Saturation, PMOS Triode]
    S4 --> O3[VOUT continues dropping]
    O3 --> R5[Region E: VIN greater than VDD minus VTP]
    R5 --> S5[NMOS Triode, PMOS Cutoff]
    S5 --> O4[VOUT equals 0]
    O4 --> End[Full VTC traversed]
```

## 4.3 Device-Level Transistor Connection Map

This schematic shows the **structural arrangement** of the two transistors. The notations are standard: $G$ = Gate, $D$ = Drain, $S$ = Source, $B$ = Bulk.

```mermaid
graph LR
    VDD[VDD Power Rail] --- SP[PMOS Source]
    SP --- GP[PMOS Gate]
    GP --- GNDP[GND Reference]
    GP --- IN[Input Node VIN]
    DP[PMOS Drain] --- OUT[Output Node VOUT]
    IN --- GN[NMOS Gate]
    DN[NMOS Drain] --- OUT
    SN[NMOS Source] --- GND[GND Power Rail]
    BP[PMOS Bulk] --- VDD
    BN[NMOS Bulk] --- GND
```

## 4.4 Power Dissipation Flow

The following diagram summarizes the **breakdown of power consumption** in a CMOS inverter, useful for KTU 14-mark analytical questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Total Power Dissipation PT] --> Static[Static Power Pstatic]
    P --> Dynamic[Dynamic Power Pdynamic]
    P --> Short[Short Circuit Power Psc]
    Static --> S1[Leakage current in OFF devices]
    Static --> S2[Ideally zero in static CMOS]
    Dynamic --> D1[Charging load capacitor CL]
    Dynamic --> D2[Equation: alpha CL VDD squared f]
    Dynamic --> D3[Dominant component in modern chips]
    Short --> SC1[Brief current spike during transition]
    Short --> SC2[Both devices ON simultaneously]
    Short --> SC3[Negligible for slow input ramps]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. Define a CMOS inverter. List its two main components and explain the principle of complementary switching.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **CMOS inverter** is a basic digital logic gate that produces the logical complement (NOT) of its input using a pair of complementary MOSFETs.

**Two main components (1 mark):**
1. **PMOS transistor** (Pull-Up Network, PUN) — Source tied to $V_{DD}$.
2. **NMOS transistor** (Pull-Down Network, PDN) — Source tied to GND.

Both gates are tied to the common input; both drains are tied to the common output.

**Principle of complementary switching (2 marks):**
- When $V_{in} = 0$ V (LOW): $V_{GS,n} = 0 < V_{TN}$ → NMOS is **OFF**; $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} > \vert V_{TP} \vert$ → PMOS is **ON**, connecting the output to $V_{DD}$. Hence, $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ (HIGH).
- When $V_{in} = V_{DD}$ (HIGH): $V_{GS,n} = V_{DD} > V_{TN}$ → NMOS is **ON**; $V_{SG,p} = 0 < \vert V_{TP} \vert$ → PMOS is **OFF**, connecting the output to GND. Hence, $V_{out} = 0$ (LOW).
- The two transistors operate in **complement**: one is always ON while the other is OFF, ensuring no static current path between $V_{DD}$ and GND.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Model Paper Style]**

### **Q2. What are noise margins? Define $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ for a CMOS inverter with ideal output levels.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Noise margins (1 mark):** Noise margins are measures of the **tolerance of a logic gate to unwanted voltage disturbances (noise)** at its input terminals without causing the output to flip incorrectly. A larger noise margin implies a more robust digital circuit.

**Definitions (2 marks):** For a CMOS inverter with $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ and $V_{OL} = 0$ V:

$$
NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}
$$

$$
NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}
$$

Where:
- $V_{IH}$ = minimum input voltage recognized as logic HIGH.
- $V_{IL}$ = maximum input voltage recognized as logic LOW.
- $NM_H$ = High Noise Margin.
- $NM_L$ = Low Noise Margin.

For a **symmetric CMOS inverter**, $NM_H = NM_L = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_T$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination — Module 1 Choice Question, Full 14 Marks]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Derive the expression for the switching threshold voltage $V_M$ of a CMOS inverter. (7 marks)**

**(b) For a CMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $V_{TN} = 0.6$ V, $V_{TP} = -0.7$ V, and $(W/L)_n = 1$, determine the required $(W/L)_p$ to make the switching threshold $V_M = 1.5$ V. Assume $\mu_n / \mu_p = 2.5$. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Derivation of $V_M$ (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark)*: State the definition of $V_M$ as the point on the VTC where $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$.

*Step 2 (1 mark)*: Identify that at this point, both NMOS and PMOS operate in the **saturation region** because $V_{DS,n} = V_{out} \approx V_{DD}/2 > V_{GS,n} - V_{TN}$ and similarly for the PMOS.

*Step 3 (1 mark)*: Write the saturation current equations:
$$I_{DN} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{TN})^{2}, \qquad I_{DP} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M + V_{TP})^{2}$$

*Step 4 (1 mark)*: Apply KCL at the output node ($I_{DN} = I_{DP}$):
$$k_n(V_M - V_{TN})^{2} = k_p(V_{DD} - V_M + V_{TP})^{2}$$

*Step 5 (1 mark)*: Take the positive square root, define $r = k_p/k_n$, and rearrange:
$$V_M - V_{TN} = \sqrt{r}\,(V_{DD} - V_M + V_{TP})$$

*Step 6 (2 marks)*: Solve algebraically to obtain the final result:
$$\boxed{\,V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{r}\,(V_{DD} + V_{TP})}{1 + \sqrt{r}}\,}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks)*: Given data: $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $V_{TN} = 0.6$ V, $V_{TP} = -0.7$ V, $V_M = 1.5$ V, $\mu_n / \mu_p = 2.5$, $(W/L)_n = 1$.

*Step 2 (2 marks)*: Express the device ratio $r$ using the mobility and aspect ratios:
$$r = \frac{k_p}{k_n} = \frac{\mu_p (W/L)_p}{\mu_n (W/L)_n} = \frac{(W/L)_p}{2.5 \cdot 1} = \frac{(W/L)_p}{2.5}$$

*Step 3 (2 marks)*: Substitute all known values into the $V_M$ equation:
$$1.5 = \frac{0.6 + \sqrt{r}\,(3.3 - 0.7)}{1 + \sqrt{r}}$$

$$1.5(1 + \sqrt{r}) = 0.6 + 2.6\sqrt{r}$$

$$1.5 + 1.5\sqrt{r} = 0.6 + 2.6\sqrt{r}$$

$$1.5 - 0.6 = 2.6\sqrt{r} - 1.5\sqrt{r}$$

$$0.9 = 1.1\sqrt{r} \implies \sqrt{r} = \frac{0.9}{1.1} = 0.8182$$

*Step 4 (1 mark)*: Solve for $r$ and the PMOS aspect ratio:
$$r = (0.8182)^{2} = 0.6694$$

$$(W/L)_p = 2.5 \times 0.6694 = 1.6735$$

$$\boxed{\,(W/L)_p \approx 1.67\,}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Draw the circuit diagram of a static CMOS inverter. Explain its operation in all five regions of the VTC. (7 marks)**

**(b) A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance $C_L = 50$ fF at a clock frequency $f = 1$ GHz with activity factor $\alpha = 0.2$. If $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, calculate the dynamic power dissipation. Comment on the contribution of static power. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Circuit Diagram & Five-Region Explanation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks)*: Draw the circuit diagram:

- PMOS $M_1$: Source → $V_{DD}$; Gate → Input; Drain → Output; Bulk → $V_{DD}$.
- NMOS $M_2$: Source → GND; Gate → Input; Drain → Output; Bulk → GND.
- Load capacitor $C_L$ between Output and GND.

*Step 2 (1 mark)*: **Region A** ($0 \le V_{in} < V_{TN}$): NMOS in cutoff ($V_{GS,n} = V_{in} < V_{TN}$), PMOS in triode ($V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_{in} \gg \vert V_{TP} \vert$). $V_{out} = V_{DD}$.

*Step 3 (1 mark)*: **Region B** ($V_{TN} \le V_{in} < V_{in}^{*}$): Both NMOS and PMOS in saturation. Output current discharges $C_L$, $V_{out}$ begins to drop.

*Step 4 (1 mark)*: **Region C** ($V_{in} = V_{in}^{*} = V_M$): Both in saturation; $V_{out} = V_M$. This is the unity-gain point and the switching threshold.

*Step 5 (1 mark)*: **Region D** ($V_{in}^{*} < V_{in} \le V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$): NMOS in saturation, PMOS in triode. $V_{out}$ continues to fall.

*Step 6 (1 mark)*: **Region E** ($V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$): NMOS in triode, PMOS in cutoff. $V_{out} = 0$ V (GND).

**Part (b) — Dynamic Power Calculation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark)*: Write the dynamic power equation:
$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$

*Step 2 (1 mark)*: Substitute the given values:
$$P_{dynamic} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.8)^{2} \times (1 \times 10^{9})$$

*Step 3 (2 marks)*: Compute $V_{DD}^{2}$:
$$(1.8)^{2} = 3.24 \text{ V}^{2}$$

*Step 4 (2 marks)*: Multiply all terms:
$$P_{dynamic} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.24 \times 10^{9}$$
$$P_{dynamic} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 3.24 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$P_{dynamic} = 32.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$$
$$\boxed{\,P_{dynamic} = 32.4 \text{ }\mu\text{W}\,}$$

*Step 5 (1 mark)*: **Comment on static power (1 mark):** In an ideal static CMOS inverter, the static power dissipation is **zero** because there is no direct current path between $V_{DD}$ and GND in either stable logic state. The only static power comes from **leakage currents** (sub-threshold, gate-oxide, and reverse-biased junction leakage), which are negligible compared to dynamic power in well-designed modern CMOS circuits.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the KCL step**: Students often write the NMOS and PMOS current equations but forget to equate them using KCL. The examiner's key explicitly requires $I_{DN} = I_{DP}$ as a separate line — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Sign convention for $V_{TP}$**: $V_{TP}$ is **negative**. Many students substitute it as $+0.7$ in the formula, getting a numerically wrong $V_M$. Always use $V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$ or $V_{DD} + V_{TP}$ with the correct sign.
> 3. **Missing region of operation in VTC questions**: A 7-mark question on VTC **always** asks for the five regions. Skipping even one costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $r$ and $1/r$**: The device ratio is $r = k_p / k_n$, not $k_n / k_p$. Getting this inverted changes the final numerical answer drastically.
> 5. **Forgetting units in power calculations**: Always express dynamic power in $\mu$W, mW, or W — units are mandatory.
> 6. **Skipping the "comment" sub-part**: In numerical problems, KTU almost always adds a 1–2 mark "comment" or "justify" sub-part. Students who compute the number but skip the comment lose easy marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CMOS Inverter**

- **Structure**: PMOS (pull-up, source to $V_{DD}$) + NMOS (pull-down, source to GND); gates tied together (input), drains tied together (output).
- **Complementary Operation**: Exactly one transistor is ON in each stable state → **zero static power** in ideal CMOS.
- **Five VTC Regions**:
  - Region A: NMOS OFF, PMOS Triode → $V_{out} = V_{DD}$.
  - Region B: Both Saturation → $V_{out}$ drops sharply.
  - Region C: Switching point at $V_M$ (both saturated).
  - Region D: NMOS Saturation, PMOS Triode.
  - Region E: NMOS Triode, PMOS OFF → $V_{out} = 0$.
- **Switching Threshold**: $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{r}\,(V_{DD} + V_{TP})}{1 + \sqrt{r}}$; symmetric inverter → $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.
- **Noise Margins**: $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$; $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$; symmetric max → $V_{DD}/2 - V_T$.
- **Dynamic Power**: $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ — the **dominant** power term in modern CMOS.
- **Propagation Delay**: $t_p = (t_{pHL} + t_{pLH})/2$, inversely proportional to device drive strength.
- **Sizing Rule**: PMOS $W/L$ must be **2 to 2.5× larger** than NMOS (because $\mu_n \approx 2$ to $3 \times \mu_p$).
- **Energy per transition**: $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ — fundamental CMOS energy unit.
- **Static Power = 0** (ideal); leakage power dominates when dynamic power is suppressed at low $V_{DD}$.
- **Rail-to-Rail Output**: $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} = 0$ — full logic swing, unlike NMOS-only pseudo-NMOS logic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Transfer Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Voltage Transfer Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Voltage Transfer Curve (VTC)** of a logic gate is a graphical representation that plots the steady-state **output voltage ($V_{out}$)** as a function of the **input voltage ($V_{in}$)** applied to the gate. It is the single most important static characterization tool for any digital logic gate, as it encapsulates the gate's switching behavior, noise immunity, and logic levels in one plot.

For the canonical **CMOS Inverter**, the VTC is a smooth, monotonically decreasing S-shaped curve that maps an input logic '0' (0 V) to an output logic '1' ($V_{DD}$) and an input logic '1' ($V_{DD}$) to an output logic '0' (0 V).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The VTC is the foundational concept upon which **Noise Margins**, **Switching Threshold**, and **Static Power Analysis** are built. Mastery of VTC derivations is mandatory for Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap (faucet)** connected to a sensor:
- When the sensor detects "low pressure" (low input voltage), the tap is fully open → water flows out at maximum (HIGH output).
- When the sensor detects "high pressure" (high input voltage), the tap is fully closed → no water flows (LOW output).
- The **VTC is essentially the "tap-closing curve"** — it shows you exactly how much the tap closes for every incremental increase in input pressure.

In VLSI terms: as you slowly raise the input voltage from 0 V to $V_{DD}$, the output voltage smoothly transitions from $V_{DD}$ down to 0 V, with a sharp drop near the middle. This sharp drop is what makes CMOS digital logic robust and noise-tolerant.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Defined on the VTC

| Metric | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Output High Voltage | $V_{OH}$ | The output voltage when the input is at logic '0' |
| Output Low Voltage | $V_{OL}$ | The output voltage when the input is at logic '1' |
| Input Low Voltage | $V_{IL}$ | Maximum input voltage still recognized as logic '0' |
| Input High Voltage | $V_{IH}$ | Minimum input voltage still recognized as logic '1' |
| Switching Threshold | $V_{M}$ or $V_{inv}$ | The point where $V_{in} = V_{out}$ (curve crosses the line $V_{out} = V_{in}$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Values for a Symmetric CMOS Inverter:** $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} = 0$, and $V_{M} = V_{DD}/2$. This assumes perfectly matched NMOS and PMOS transistors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Operating Regions of the CMOS Inverter VTC

The CMOS inverter consists of one **NMOS** (pull-down) and one **PMOS** (pull-up) transistor. As $V_{in}$ sweeps from 0 to $V_{DD}$, the VTC traverses five distinct regions:

- **Region 1 ($0 \leq V_{in} < V_{Tn}$):** NMOS is **OFF** (cutoff), PMOS is in **Triode**. No current flows. Output is tied to $V_{DD}$ through the conducting PMOS. $\Rightarrow V_{out} = V_{OH} = V_{DD}$.

- **Region 2 ($V_{Tn} \leq V_{in} < V_{inv}$):** NMOS enters **Saturation**, PMOS remains in **Triode**. Current begins to flow. $V_{out}$ begins to drop from $V_{DD}$. The slope $|dV_{out}/dV_{in}|$ becomes steep.

- **Region 3 ($V_{in} = V_{inv}$):** Both NMOS and PMOS are in **Saturation**. This is the **switching threshold** point where the curve has its **steepest slope** (maximum gain). Ideally $V_{inv} = V_{DD}/2$.

- **Region 4 ($V_{inv} < V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|$):** NMOS enters **Triode**, PMOS enters **Saturation**. $V_{out}$ continues to fall rapidly toward 0 V.

- **Region 5 ($V_{in} > V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|$):** PMOS is **OFF** (cutoff), NMOS is in **Triode**. Output is pulled to ground. $\Rightarrow V_{out} = V_{OL} = 0$.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | Output High, when PMOS is ON, NMOS is OFF |
| 2 | $V_{OL} = 0$ | Output Low, when NMOS is ON, PMOS is OFF |
| 3 | $V_{inv} = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$ | Switching Threshold (where $V_{in} = V_{out}$) |
| 4 | $V_{inv} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ | Simplified: when $k_n = k_p$ and $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ |
| 5 | $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | NMOS transconductance parameter |
| 6 | $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p$ | PMOS transconductance parameter |
| 7 | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Noise Margin High |
| 8 | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Noise Margin Low |
| 9 | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}$ | Ratio of PMOS-to-NMOS strength (typically 1 for symmetric design) |
| 10 | Region 2 slope: $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -\dfrac{k_n (V_{in} - V_{Tn})^2}{k_p[2(V_{in}-V_{Tn})V_{out} - (V_{in}-V_{Tn})^2]}$ | Steepest transition (negative sign indicates inversion) |

### 2.3 Why This Matters in Real-World Engineering

The VTC directly determines the **robustness of a digital IC** in the presence of electrical noise, voltage droop, and process variations. A steep VTC (high gain near $V_{inv}$) translates to large noise margins, meaning the chip can reliably operate in noisy environments such as automotive ECUs, mobile phones, and aerospace systems. Engineers tune the **W/L ratios** of transistors to shift $V_{inv}$ and tailor the noise margins for specific applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does $V_{inv}$ shift when $k_p \neq k_n$?** If the PMOS is made wider (larger $k_p$) than the NMOS, the PMOS conducts more strongly, pulling the output high for a longer portion of the input range. This shifts $V_{inv}$ **upward**, making the gate favor the HIGH output state — a technique called **ratioed logic**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Switching Threshold $V_{inv}$

The switching threshold is defined as the point where $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_{inv}$. At this point, both transistors are in **saturation**. Equating the NMOS and PMOS saturation currents:

$$
I_{Dn,sat} = I_{Dp,sat}
$$

$$
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_{inv} - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{inv} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2
$$

Taking the square root of both sides:

$$
\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_{inv} - V_{Tn}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_{inv} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)
$$

Dividing both sides by $\sqrt{k_n}$:

$$
(V_{inv} - V_{Tn}) = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} - V_{inv} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)
$$

Expanding the right-hand side:

$$
V_{inv} - V_{Tn} = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}\,V_{DD} - \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}\,V_{inv} - \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}\,\vert V_{Tp}\vert
$$

Grouping $V_{inv}$ terms on the left-hand side:

$$
V_{inv} + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}\,V_{inv} = V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)
$$

Factoring $V_{inv}$:

$$
V_{inv}\left(1 + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}\right) = V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)
$$

Solving for $V_{inv}$ (final boxed result):

$$
\boxed{V_{inv} = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of $V_{IL}$ (in Region 2: NMOS Saturation, PMOS Triode)

By definition, $V_{IL}$ is the input voltage at which the slope of the VTC equals $-1$:

$$
\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1
$$

In Region 2, the current through the inverter (NMOS sat, PMOS triode) is:

$$
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_{in} - V_{Tn})^2 = k_p\left[(V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)(V_{out}) - \frac{V_{out}^2}{2}\right]
$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $V_{in}$ (treating $V_{out}$ as a function of $V_{in}$):

$$
k_n(V_{in} - V_{Tn}) = k_p\left[(-1)(V_{out}) + (V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} - V_{out}\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}}\right]
$$

Setting $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ and substituting into the saturation current equation at $V_{in} = V_{IL}$:

$$
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_{IL} - V_{Tn})^2 = k_p\left[(V_{DD} - V_{IL} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)V_{out} - \frac{V_{out}^2}{2}\right]
$$

Through the standard KTU simplification (expanding and grouping), one obtains the working expression:

$$
V_{IL} = \frac{2V_{out} + V_{Tn} - V_{DD} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert}{\left(\frac{k_n}{k_p}\right)\cdot\frac{(V_{DD} - V_{IL} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{V_{out}} - 1}
$$

In the **symmetric case** ($k_n = k_p$, $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp}\vert$, $V_{inv} = V_{DD}/2$), this simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{V_{IL} \approx \frac{3V_{DD}}{8} \quad \text{(symmetric CMOS)}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of $V_{IH}$ (in Region 4: NMOS Triode, PMOS Saturation)

By symmetry, following the analogous procedure in Region 4 yields:

$$
V_{IH} = \frac{V_{DD} + V_{Tp} + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}\left(\frac{3V_{DD}}{4} - \frac{V_{Tn}}{2}\right)}{\text{... (full expression)}}
$$

In the **symmetric case**:

$$
\boxed{V_{IH} \approx \frac{5V_{DD}}{8} \quad \text{(symmetric CMOS)}}
$$

### 3.4 Noise Margin Computation

With the symmetric approximations:

$$
NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = V_{DD} - \frac{5V_{DD}}{8} = \frac{3V_{DD}}{8}
$$

$$
NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = \frac{3V_{DD}}{8} - 0 = \frac{3V_{DD}}{8}
$$

$$
\boxed{NM_H = NM_L = \frac{3V_{DD}}{8} \approx 0.375\,V_{DD}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example:** For $V_{DD} = 3.3\,\text{V}$, the symmetric noise margins are $NM_H = NM_L = 1.2375\,\text{V}$, comfortably rejecting noise up to ~1.24 V without logic error.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CMOS Inverter Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the transistor-level topology of the CMOS inverter and its connection to the VTC regions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[VDD Power Rail] --> B[PMOS Pull-Up Network]
    B --> C[Output Node Vout]
    D[Input Voltage Vin] --> B
    D --> E[NMOS Pull-Down Network]
    E --> F[Ground GND]
    C --> G[Load Capacitance CL]
    C --> H[Next Stage Input]

    subgraph Region_Map[Five VTC Regions]
        R1[Region 1: PMOS Triode, NMOS OFF]
        R2[Region 2: PMOS Triode, NMOS Saturation]
        R3[Region 3: PMOS Saturation, NMOS Saturation]
        R4[Region 4: PMOS Saturation, NMOS Triode]
        R5[Region 5: PMOS OFF, NMOS Triode]
    end

    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5

    style A fill:#90EE90
    style F fill:#FFB6C1
    style C fill:#FFD700
    style D fill:#87CEEB
    style R3 fill:#FF6347
```

### 4.2 Voltage Transfer Curve (VTC) Block Architecture

The following Mermaid flow depicts the input-to-output mapping and the critical voltage points along the VTC.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Vin = 0 V] --> B[Vout = VOH = VDD]
    C[Vin = VIL] --> D[Vout still HIGH]
    E[Vin = Vinv = VDD/2] --> F[Vout = Vinv]
    G[Vin = VIH] --> H[Vout still LOW]
    I[Vin = VDD] --> J[Vout = VOL = 0 V]

    K[Slope = -1 point 1] --> C
    L[Slope = -1 point 2] --> G
    M[Critical Transition Region] --> E

    style A fill:#90EE90
    style B fill:#90EE90
    style I fill:#FFB6C1
    style J fill:#FFB6C1
    style E fill:#FFD700
    style F fill:#FFD700
    style M fill:#FF6347
```

### 4.3 Operating Region Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Apply Vin] --> Q1{Vin less than VTn?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Reg1[Region 1: NMOS OFF, Vout = VDD]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Vin less than Vinv?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Reg2[Region 2: NMOS Sat, PMOS Triode]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Vin equals Vinv?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Reg3[Region 3: Both in Saturation]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Vin less than VDD minus VTp?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Reg4[Region 4: NMOS Triode, PMOS Sat]
    Q4 -- No --> Reg5[Region 5: PMOS OFF, Vout = 0]

    style Reg1 fill:#90EE90
    style Reg5 fill:#FFB6C1
    style Reg3 fill:#FFD700
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions**

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the Voltage Transfer Curve (VTC) of a CMOS inverter. List the four critical voltages extracted from it.**

**Model Answer:**
The Voltage Transfer Curve is a plot of the DC output voltage $V_{out}$ of a logic gate versus its DC input voltage $V_{in}$, characterizing its static switching behavior.

The four critical voltages are:
1. $V_{OL}$ — Output Low voltage (≈ 0 V)
2. $V_{OH}$ — Output High voltage (≈ $V_{DD}$)
3. $V_{IL}$ — Maximum input voltage recognized as logic '0'
4. $V_{IH}$ — Minimum input voltage recognized as logic '1'

A fifth point, $V_{inv}$ (switching threshold), where $V_{in} = V_{out}$, is also commonly referenced.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**What is the switching threshold $V_{inv}$ of a CMOS inverter? Write its expression for the symmetric case.**

**Model Answer:**
The switching threshold $V_{inv}$ is the point on the VTC where the input voltage equals the output voltage ($V_{in} = V_{out}$). At this point, both NMOS and PMOS transistors are in saturation, and the VTC has its steepest slope.

General expression:

$$
V_{inv} = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}
$$

For the **symmetric case** ($k_n = k_p$ and $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp}\vert$):

$$
\boxed{V_{inv} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}}
$$

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of the Voltage Transfer Curve of a CMOS inverter, explain the **five operating regions** in detail. Identify the transistor operating modes (cutoff / triode / saturation) of NMOS and PMOS in each region. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **switching threshold $V_{inv}$** of a CMOS inverter. Compute $V_{inv}$ for $V_{DD} = 3.3\,\text{V}$, $V_{Tn} = 0.5\,\text{V}$, $\vert V_{Tp}\vert = 0.5\,\text{V}$, and $(W/L)_n = 2$, $(W/L)_p = 4$, assuming $\mu_n = 2\mu_p$ and equal oxide thickness. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a): Five Operating Regions

| Region | Input Range | NMOS Mode | PMOS Mode | Output Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $0 \leq V_{in} < V_{Tn}$ | Cutoff (OFF) | Triode (ON) | $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ |
| 2 | $V_{Tn} \leq V_{in} < V_{inv}$ | Saturation | Triode | $V_{out}$ drops sharply |
| 3 | $V_{in} = V_{inv}$ | Saturation | Saturation | $V_{out} = V_{inv}$ (steepest slope) |
| 4 | $V_{inv} < V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ | Triode | Saturation | $V_{out}$ drops to near 0 |
| 5 | $V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ | Triode (ON) | Cutoff (OFF) | $V_{out} = 0$ |

**Explanation:**
- In **Region 1**, $V_{in}$ is below the NMOS threshold, so the NMOS is completely OFF. The PMOS, with its gate at 0 V relative to its source at $V_{DD}$, has $V_{SG} = V_{DD}$, which exceeds $\vert V_{Tp}\vert$, so the PMOS is ON and operates in the triode region with negligible voltage drop. The output node is charged to $V_{DD}$.

- In **Region 2**, $V_{in}$ crosses $V_{Tn}$, turning the NMOS ON. The NMOS enters saturation because $V_{DS} = V_{out}$ is high. The PMOS remains in triode but its resistance increases as $V_{in}$ rises. Current flows, and the output begins to discharge.

- In **Region 3**, both transistors are in saturation. The VTC is at its steepest negative slope, providing high DC gain. This is the critical transition point $V_{inv}$.

- In **Region 4**, the NMOS enters triode while the PMOS remains in saturation. The output is now being pulled strongly to ground.

- In **Region 5**, $V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ forces $V_{SG} < \vert V_{Tp}\vert$, turning the PMOS OFF. The NMOS in triode pulls the output firmly to 0 V.

#### Part (b): Derivation and Numerical Computation

Starting from the saturation-current equality at $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_{inv}$:

$$
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_{inv} - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{inv} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2
$$

Solving (as derived in Section 3.1):

$$
V_{inv} = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}
$$

**Numerical computation:**

Step 1 — Compute the ratio $k_p / k_n$:

$$
\frac{k_p}{k_n} = \frac{\mu_p (W/L)_p}{\mu_n (W/L)_n} = \frac{\mu_p \cdot 4}{2\mu_p \cdot 2} = \frac{4}{4} = 1
$$

Step 2 — Therefore $\sqrt{k_p/k_n} = 1$.

Step 3 — Substitute into the formula:

$$
V_{inv} = \frac{0.5 + 1 \cdot (3.3 - 0.5)}{1 + 1} = \frac{0.5 + 2.8}{2} = \frac{3.3}{2} = 1.65\,\text{V}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{V_{inv} = 1.65\,\text{V} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Define **Noise Margins** ($NM_H$ and $NM_L$). Derive their expressions in terms of the critical voltages, and compute them for a symmetric CMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 1.8\,\text{V}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS inverter has $(W/L)_n = 3$, $(W/L)_p = 6$, $V_{Tn} = 0.4\,\text{V}$, $\vert V_{Tp}\vert = 0.4\,\text{V}$, and $V_{DD} = 2.5\,\text{V}$, with $\mu_n = 2.5\,\mu_p$. Determine: (i) the switching threshold $V_{inv}$, and (ii) whether this inverter is symmetric or skewed. Justify. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a): Noise Margin Derivation

**Definitions:**

- **$NM_H$ (Noise Margin High):** The amount of noise that can be叠加 (superimposed) on a logic '1' input without causing a logic error at the output.

$$
\boxed{NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}}
$$

- **$NM_L$ (Noise Margin Low):** The amount of noise that can be叠加 on a logic '0' input without causing a logic error.

$$
\boxed{NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}}
$$

For a symmetric CMOS inverter ($V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} = 0$, $V_{IL} \approx 3V_{DD}/8$, $V_{IH} \approx 5V_{DD}/8$):

$$
NM_H = V_{DD} - \frac{5V_{DD}}{8} = \frac{3V_{DD}}{8}
$$

$$
NM_L = \frac{3V_{DD}}{8} - 0 = \frac{3V_{DD}}{8}
$$

For $V_{DD} = 1.8\,\text{V}$:

$$
NM_H = NM_L = \frac{3 \times 1.8}{8} = \frac{5.4}{8} = 0.675\,\text{V}
$$

#### Part (b): Switching Threshold Computation

Step 1 — Compute $k_p / k_n$:

$$
\frac{k_p}{k_n} = \frac{\mu_p \cdot 6}{\mu_n \cdot 3} = \frac{6}{3 \times 2.5} = \frac{6}{7.5} = 0.8
$$

Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{k_p/k_n}$:

$$
\sqrt{0.8} = 0.8944
$$

Step 3 — Substitute into the $V_{inv}$ formula:

$$
V_{inv} = \frac{0.4 + 0.8944 \cdot (2.5 - 0.4)}{1 + 0.8944} = \frac{0.4 + 0.8944 \times 2.1}{1.8944}
$$

Computing the numerator:

$$
0.4 + 1.8782 = 2.2782
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{inv} = \frac{2.2782}{1.8944} = 1.2025\,\text{V}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{V_{inv} \approx 1.20\,\text{V}}
$$

**Symmetry check:** For a perfectly symmetric inverter, $V_{inv} = V_{DD}/2 = 1.25\,\text{V}$. Since $V_{inv} = 1.20\,\text{V} \neq 1.25\,\text{V}$, the inverter is **slightly skewed** toward the LOW output state (because $V_{inv}$ shifted downward, meaning the PMOS is relatively weaker than the NMOS in this design).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students frequently forget to take the **square root** of $k_p/k_n$ in the denominator — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the **negative sign** in $|V_{Tp}|$ — the magnitude must be used because $V_{Tp}$ itself is negative for PMOS.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing the regions — remember: PMOS is in **saturation** when $V_{SD} > V_{SG} - |V_{Tp}|$, and in **triode** when $V_{SD} < V_{SG} - |V_{Tp}|$. Drawing the VTC with wrong region labels costs full marks in graphical questions.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Computing noise margins using the **wrong reference polarity** ($V_{IH} - V_{OH}$ instead of $V_{OH} - V_{IH}$) — examiners deduct 1 mark for this.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ✅ The **VTC** is the plot of $V_{out}$ vs $V_{in}$ and defines all static parameters of a logic gate.
- ✅ The CMOS inverter VTC has **five operating regions** based on transistor modes (cutoff / triode / saturation).
- ✅ Critical voltages: $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} = 0$, $V_{M} = V_{DD}/2$ (symmetric case).
- ✅ **Switching threshold formula:** $V_{inv} = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}\,(V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$.
- ✅ **Noise Margins** (symmetric case): $NM_H = NM_L = 3V_{DD}/8 \approx 0.375\,V_{DD}$.
- ✅ A **steeper VTC** = better noise immunity = more robust digital design.
- ✅ To shift $V_{inv}$ **upward**, make PMOS stronger (increase $W_p$). To shift $V_{inv}$ **downward**, make NMOS stronger.
- ✅ Always take the **square root** of $k_p/k_n$, never forget it.
- ✅ The PMOS threshold $V_{Tp}$ is **negative**; always use $|V_{Tp}|$ in formulas.
- ✅ The **steepest slope** of the VTC occurs at $V_{inv}$ where both transistors are in saturation.
- ✅ **Ratioed logic** (skewed inverters) intentionally uses $k_p \neq k_n$ to favor one output state.
- ✅ KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes VTC derivations, region identification, and noise-margin computation in **Part B (14-mark)** questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMOS Logic Gates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CMOS Logic Gates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) Logic Gates** are static digital logic families built using complementary pairs of **p-channel MOS (pMOS)** pull-up transistors and **n-channel MOS (nMOS)** pull-down transistors on a single silicon substrate. The output node is always driven to a low-impedance connection to either $V_{DD}$ (logic **1**) or $V_{SS}$ (logic **0**) for any stable input combination, ensuring **ratioless**, **full-rail**, **noise-immune** switching.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 1)**, CMOS logic gates form the bedrock of digital VLSI design. The module emphasizes the **structure**, **static behavior**, **switching threshold**, and **layout** of the inverter, NAND, NOR, and complex (AOI/OAI) gates, as well as the CMOS **transmission gate** used as a bidirectional switch.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Railroad Switch**
> Imagine a railroad switchyard where two levers work in **perfect opposition**:
> - The **green lever (nMOS)** connects the track to the *ground valley* (0 V).
> - The **red lever (pMOS)** connects the track to the *mountain peak* ($V_{DD}$).
> - When the input signal is **HIGH**, the green lever opens (nMOS ON) and the red lever closes (pMOS OFF) — the train rolls into the valley (**0**).
> - When the input signal is **LOW**, the red lever opens (pMOS ON) and the green lever closes (nMOS OFF) — the train climbs to the peak (**1**).
> - **Crucially, both levers are NEVER open at the same time** — so no current "leaks" from mountain to valley. This opposition is what gives CMOS its almost-zero static power dissipation.

This **complementary symmetry** is the soul of CMOS. In every stable state, one network is ON and the other is OFF, guaranteeing:

- **Full output voltage swing**: $0 \le V_{OUT} \le V_{DD}$
- **Extremely low static power**: ideally **0 W**
- **High input impedance**: gates draw no DC current
- **High noise immunity** due to sharp transfer characteristics

## 1.3 CMOS in the KTU 2024 Scheme Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard CMOS Metrics Mandated by the Syllabus:**
> - **Supply Voltage ($V_{DD}$)** : typically **1.8 V – 5 V** depending on technology node.
> - **Threshold Voltage ($V_T$)** : nMOS $\approx$ **0.4 V – 0.7 V**, pMOS $\approx$ **-0.4 V to -0.7 V**.
> - **Transistor Count Rule** : A static CMOS gate with **N inputs** uses **2N transistors** (N pMOS + N nMOS).
> - **Logical Effort (LE)** of a CMOS inverter = **1** (the reference gate).

## 1.4 Why CMOS Dominates Modern VLSI

| Property | CMOS Behavior | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| Static Power | $\approx 0$ | Battery-friendly, dense integration |
| Noise Margin | $\approx 0.4 \, V_{DD}$ | Robust against cross-talk |
| Fan-Out | Very High (driven by load capacitance) | Cascadable logic |
| Scalability | Excellent (down to sub-7 nm) | Moore's Law compliance |
| Manufacturing | Single well-process available | Low cost per die |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CMOS Inverter — The Heart of All CMOS Gates

The CMOS inverter is the **canonical reference circuit**. Every other CMOS gate is a structural generalization of it.

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology
- **pMOS** transistor: Source tied to $V_{DD}$, Drain tied to **Output**, Gate tied to **Input ($V_{IN}$)**.
- **nMOS** transistor: Source tied to $V_{SS}$ (ground), Drain tied to **Output**, Gate tied to **Input ($V_{IN}$)**.
- The two transistors are fabricated in a common **n-well** structure on a p-type substrate.

### 2.1.2 Operational States

| Input $V_{IN}$ | nMOS State | pMOS State | Output $V_{OUT}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| LOW (0 V) | **OFF** (cut-off) | **ON** (linear) | HIGH = $V_{DD}$ |
| HIGH ($V_{DD}$) | **ON** (linear) | **OFF** (cut-off) | LOW = 0 V |
| Transition | Both conducting (saturation) | Both conducting (saturation) | Switches sharply |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The region where **both** transistors conduct simultaneously is the **switching region** of the Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC). It is brief but determines the noise margins and the **switching threshold ($V_M$)**.

## 2.2 CMOS NAND Gate (N-Input)

A 2-input CMOS NAND requires **4 transistors**:
- **2 pMOS** transistors in **parallel** between $V_{DD}$ and output.
- **2 nMOS** transistors in **series** between output and ground.

**Logic Behavior**:
- If **ANY** input is LOW → corresponding pMOS is ON, corresponding nMOS is OFF → output is pulled HIGH (1).
- Only when **ALL** inputs are HIGH → both pMOS OFF, both nMOS ON → output is pulled LOW (0).

## 2.3 CMOS NOR Gate (N-Input)

A 2-input CMOS NOR requires **4 transistors**:
- **2 pMOS** transistors in **series** between $V_{DD}$ and output.
- **2 nMOS** transistors in **parallel** between output and ground.

**Logic Behavior**:
- If **ANY** input is HIGH → corresponding nMOS is ON → output pulled LOW (0).
- Only when **ALL** inputs are LOW → all pMOS ON, all nMOS OFF → output pulled HIGH (1).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Series-stacked transistors **degrade performance**. A 4-input NAND (4 nMOS in series) is *faster* than a 4-input NOR (4 pMOS in series) because nMOS electrons have **2–3× higher mobility** than pMOS holes. This is why KTU problems often prefer NAND over wide NOR.

## 2.4 Complex CMOS Gates (AOI / OAI)

> [!NOTE]
> **AOI (AND-OR-Invert)** and **OAI (OR-AND-Invert)** gates are built by generalizing the pull-up and pull-down networks using the **dual network principle**: the pMOS pull-up network is the **dual** of the nMOS pull-down network (series ↔ parallel swap).

## 2.5 CMOS Transmission Gate (TG / Pass Gate)

A transmission gate is a **bidirectional analog switch** made of **one pMOS + one nMOS in parallel**, with **inverted control signals** ($\phi$ and $\overline{\phi}$).

- When $\phi = 1$: both transistors conduct → switch is **CLOSED** (low resistance, $\approx R_{ON}$).
- When $\phi = 0$: both transistors OFF → switch is **OPEN** (high impedance, $\approx \infty$).

It passes both **0** and **1** cleanly (unlike a single nMOS pass transistor which degrades the HIGH level).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD EQUATIONS FOR KTU 2024 ESE — MEMORIZE THESE.**

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Switching Threshold $V_M$ | $V_M = \dfrac{r \cdot V_{DD}}{1 + r}$ , where $r = \dfrac{k_{p}}{k_{n}}$ | Point where $V_{IN} = V_{OUT}$ | Volts |
| Noise Margin High $NM_H$ | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | HIGH-state noise immunity | Volts |
| Noise Margin Low $NM_L$ | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | LOW-state noise immunity | Volts |
| Logic Low Output $V_{OL}$ | $\approx 0$ V (ideal CMOS) | Output low voltage | Volts |
| Logic High Output $V_{OH}$ | $\approx V_{DD}$ (ideal CMOS) | Output high voltage | Volts |
| Static Power $P_{static}$ | $P_{static} = I_{leakage} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Power when output is stable | Watts |
| Dynamic Power $P_{dyn}$ | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Switching power (dominant) | Watts |
| Short-Circuit Power $P_{SC}$ | $P_{SC} = I_{peak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Power during transition | Watts |
| Propagation Delay $t_{p}$ | $t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ | RC delay model | Seconds |
| Total Energy per Switch | $E = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ | Energy dissipated per transition | Joules |
| Power-Delay Product (PDP) | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p$ | Figure of merit | Joules |
| Transistor Sizing Ratio | $\dfrac{W}{L}\bigg\vert_{nMOS} = \dfrac{\mu_p}{\mu_n} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L}\bigg\vert_{pMOS}$ | To match rise/fall times | Dimensionless |
| Fan-Out (FO4) | $FO4 = \dfrac{C_{L,gate}}{4 \cdot C_{inv}}$ | Standard delay unit | Number |
| Series nMOS Resistance | $R_{series,n} = N \cdot R_{n,unit}$ | N stacked nMOS resistance | Ohms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of These Equations:**
> - The **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** is used by VLSI designers to benchmark logic families — lower PDP = better technology.
> - The **switching threshold $V_M$** is tuned to $V_{DD}/2$ for symmetric noise margins, which is the standard design target.
> - The **dynamic power equation** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V^2 f$ is the single most important equation in modern low-power VLSI design — it explains why chip voltages have dropped from 5 V to sub-1 V over the decades.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Switching Threshold $V_M$ for the CMOS Inverter

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Find the input voltage at which the output voltage equals the input voltage — i.e., $V_{IN} = V_{OUT} = V_M$.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Set Up the Current Balance

At the switching threshold, the nMOS transistor operates in **saturation** and the pMOS transistor also operates in **saturation**. By **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the output node, the current charging the output capacitance must equal the current discharging it. In steady state during the transition:

$$I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Substitute the Saturation Current Equations

For an nMOS in saturation:
$$I_{Dn} = \frac{k_n}{2}\,(V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_n}{2}\,(V_M - V_{Tn})^2$$

For a pMOS in saturation (with $|V_{Tp}|$ used for magnitude):
$$I_{Dp} = \frac{k_p}{2}\,(V_{SG} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2$$

where $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ and $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p$.

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Equate and Simplify

$$\frac{k_n}{2}\,(V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2$$

Taking the square root of both sides (assuming a symmetric threshold with $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert = V_T$):

$$\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_T) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - V_T)$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Define the Transconductance Ratio $r$

Let $r = \dfrac{k_p}{k_n}$. Then $\dfrac{\sqrt{k_p}}{\sqrt{k_n}} = \sqrt{r}$. Substituting:

$$(V_M - V_T) = \sqrt{r}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - V_T)$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Solve for $V_M$

Expanding the right-hand side:
$$V_M - V_T = \sqrt{r} \cdot V_{DD} - \sqrt{r} \cdot V_M - \sqrt{r} \cdot V_T$$

Collecting $V_M$ terms on the left:
$$V_M + \sqrt{r} \cdot V_M = \sqrt{r} \cdot V_{DD} + V_T - \sqrt{r} \cdot V_T$$

$$V_M\,(1 + \sqrt{r}) = \sqrt{r}\,(V_{DD} - V_T) + V_T$$

Dividing through by $(1 + \sqrt{r})$:

$$V_M = \frac{\sqrt{r}\,(V_{DD} - V_T) + V_T}{1 + \sqrt{r}}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6: Special Case for Symmetric Inverter

For a **symmetric inverter** designed such that $V_M = V_{DD}/2$, we set $r = 1$, i.e., $k_n = k_p$. Since $k \propto \mu \cdot (W/L)$:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n$$

Since $\mu_n \approx 2.5 \mu_p$ for typical silicon, we need $(W/L)_p \approx 2.5 \cdot (W/L)_n$ — meaning **pMOS transistors are made ~2.5× wider** than nMOS to match drive strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Master Formula:**
> $$\boxed{V_M = \frac{r\,V_{DD}}{1 + r} \quad \text{when } V_T \text{ is small}}$$
> This is the simplified form that KTU examiners expect in the 14-mark derivations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Propagation Delay $t_p$ (RC Delay Model)

### 3.2.1 Step 1: Model the Inverter as an RC Network

The CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$ can be modeled as a switch with equivalent on-resistance $R_{eq}$ in series with $C_L$.

### 3.2.2 Step 2: Write the Charging Equation

When the output is charging from 0 to $V_{DD}$ through the pMOS:

$$V_{OUT}(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t / R_{eq} C_L}\right)$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3: Find the 50% Point ($t_{pLH}$)

Set $V_{OUT}(t) = 0.5 \, V_{DD}$:

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-t_{pLH} / (R_{eq} C_L)}$$

$$e^{-t_{pLH} / (R_{eq} C_L)} = 0.5$$

$$-\frac{t_{pLH}}{R_{eq} C_L} = \ln(0.5) = -0.693$$

$$t_{pLH} = 0.693 \, R_{eq} C_L \approx 0.69 \, R_{eq} C_L$$

Similarly, $t_{pHL} = 0.69 \, R_{eq,n} C_L$ for the nMOS discharge path.

### 3.2.4 Step 4: Average Propagation Delay

$$t_p = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2} = 0.69 \, C_L \left(\frac{R_{eq,p} + R_{eq,n}}{2}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Dynamic Power Dissipation

### 3.3.1 Step 1: Energy Stored in a Capacitor

When a capacitor $C_L$ is charged from 0 to $V_{DD}$, the energy drawn from the supply is:

$$E_{supply} = \int_0^{\infty} V_{DD} \cdot i(t) \, dt = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \, dV = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2: Energy Stored in the Capacitor

$$E_{stored} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3: Energy Dissipated in the pMOS (Charging)

$$E_{dissipated} = E_{supply} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

### 3.3.4 Step 4: Energy Per Full Cycle

A full cycle (0→1 then 1→0) dissipates energy in **both** pMOS and nMOS:

$$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

### 3.3.5 Step 5: Power = Energy × Frequency × Activity Factor

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$

where $\alpha$ is the **switching activity factor** (probability of a 0→1 transition per clock cycle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This $V_{DD}^{2}$ dependence is why power supply voltage is the single most aggressively scaled parameter in modern VLSI.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CMOS Inverter — Schematic & Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD["VDD SUPPLY RAIL"]
    PMOS["PMOS PULL UP TRANSISTOR"]
    NMOS["NMOS PULL DOWN TRANSISTOR"]
    VIN["INPUT VIN"]
    VOUT["OUTPUT VOUT NODE"]
    VSS["GROUND VSS RAIL"]
    CIN["INPUT LOAD CAPACITANCE"]
    COUT["OUTPUT LOAD CAPACITANCE CL"]

    VDD --> PMOS
    PMOS --> VOUT
    VOUT --> NMOS
    NMOS --> VSS
    VIN --> PMOS
    VIN --> NMOS
    VIN -.-> CIN
    VOUT -.-> COUT
```

## 4.2 CMOS 2-Input NAND Gate — Transistor Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD["VDD"]
    P1["PMOS TRANSISTOR M1 INPUT A"]
    P2["PMOS TRANSISTOR M2 INPUT B"]
    N1["NMOS TRANSISTOR M3 INPUT A"]
    N2["NMOS TRANSISTOR M4 INPUT B"]
    OUT["OUTPUT Y"]
    GND["GROUND VSS"]
    INA["INPUT A"]
    INB["INPUT B"]

    VDD --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> OUT
    OUT --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> GND
    INA --> P1
    INA --> N1
    INB --> P2
    INB --> N2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two pMOS (M1, M2) are drawn in **parallel** between $V_{DD}$ and OUT. The two nMOS (M3, M4) are drawn in **series** between OUT and GND. This is the structural hallmark of a CMOS NAND gate.

## 4.3 CMOS 2-Input NOR Gate — Transistor Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD["VDD RAIL"]
    P1["PMOS M1 INPUT A"]
    P2["PMOS M2 INPUT B"]
    N1["NMOS M3 INPUT A"]
    N2["NMOS M4 INPUT B"]
    OUT["OUTPUT Y"]
    GND["GROUND VSS"]
    INA["INPUT A"]
    INB["INPUT B"]

    VDD --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> OUT
    OUT --> N1
    N2 --> N1
    N1 --> GND
    INA --> P1
    INA --> N1
    INB --> P2
    INB --> N2
```

## 4.4 CMOS Transmission Gate — Bidirectional Switch

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["NODE A"]
    B["NODE B"]
    NDEV["NMOS PASS TRANSISTOR"]
    PDEV["PMOS PASS TRANSISTOR"]
    CTRL["CONTROL PHI HIGH"]
    CTRLN["INVERTED CONTROL PHI BAR LOW"]

    A --> NDEV
    NDEV --> B
    A --> PDEV
    PDEV --> B
    CTRL --> NDEV
    CTRLN --> PDEV
```

## 4.5 CMOS Logic Family — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    CMOSFAM["CMOS STATIC LOGIC FAMILY"]
    INV["CMOS INVERTER 2 TRANSISTORS"]
    NAND["CMOS NAND 2N TRANSISTORS"]
    NOR["CMOS NOR 2N TRANSISTORS"]
    CPLG["COMPLEX GATES AOI OAI"]
    TG["TRANSMISSION GATES BIDIRECTIONAL"]

    CMOSFAM --> INV
    CMOSFAM --> NAND
    CMOSFAM --> NOR
    CMOSFAM --> CPLG
    CMOSFAM --> TG

    INV --> INVAPP["REFERENCE GATE LE EQUALS 1"]
    NAND --> NANDAPP["PARALLEL PMOS SERIES NMOS"]
    NOR --> NORAPP["SERIES PMOS PARALLEL NMOS"]
    CPLG --> CPLGAPP["DUAL NETWORK PRINCIPLE"]
    TG --> TGAPP["FULL RAIL BIDIRECTIONAL SWITCH"]
```

## 4.6 Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) — Switching Behavior

```mermaid
graph TD
    VTC["VTC OF CMOS INVERTER"]
    REG1["REGION 1 VIN LESS THAN VTN NMOS OFF PMOS ON VOUT EQUALS VDD"]
    REG2["REGION 2 VIN BETWEEN VTN AND VM BOTH ON TRANSITION VOUT FALLS"]
    REG3["REGION 3 VIN AT VM BOTH IN SATURATION VOUT EQUALS VIN"]
    REG4["REGION 4 VIN BETWEEN VM AND VDD MINUS VTP PMOS WEAKENS VOUT LOW"]
    REG5["REGION 5 VIN GREATER THAN VDD MINUS VTP PMOS OFF NMOS ON VOUT EQUALS 0"]

    VTC --> REG1
    VTC --> REG2
    VTC --> REG3
    VTC --> REG4
    VTC --> REG5
```
