where $R_{i \to source}$ is the **path resistance** from the input source to each capacitor node $C_i$, computed along the unique path in the tree.

**Step 2 — 4-Stage Inverter Chain Setup (1 mark)**

Consider 4 inverters in cascade, each with output resistance $R$ and intrinsic capacitance $C$ at each intermediate node. The final load is $C_L$.

**Step 3 — Path Resistance Calculation (2 marks)**

| Node | Capacitor | Path Resistance from Source |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C$ | $R$ |
| 2 | $C$ | $2R$ |
| 3 | $C$ | $3R$ |
| 4 | $C_L$ | $4R$ |

**Step 4 — Apply Elmore Formula (1 mark)**

$$t_{p,Elmore} = R \cdot C + 2R \cdot C + 3R \cdot C + 4R \cdot C_L = 6RC + 4R C_L$$

**Step 5 — Generalization (1 mark)**

For $N$ stages:

$$\boxed{t_{p,Elmore} = \frac{N(N+1)}{2} R C + N R C_L}$$

This shows **quadratic growth** of delay with chain length — the motivation for **buffer insertion (tapering)**.

---

#### Part (b) — NAND2 Worst-Case Delay [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify Worst Case (1 mark)**

In a 2-input NAND, the worst-case $t_{PHL}$ occurs when **both NMOS** are ON (parallel, equivalent resistance = $R_n/2$). The worst-case $t_{PLH}$ occurs when **only one PMOS** is ON (series through two PMOS — but in NAND2 they are in parallel; need to recheck topology).

**Correction:** In CMOS NAND2:
- NMOS are in **series** → worst-case $t_{PHL}$ uses $2 R_n$.
- PMOS are in **parallel** → worst-case $t_{PLH}$ uses $R_p$.

**Step 2 — Worst-Case Resistance for $t_{PHL}$ (2 marks)**

$$R_n = \frac{1}{k_n' \cdot (W_n/L_n) \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})} = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 2 \cdot (1.0 - 0.3)} = \frac{1}{2.8 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3571 \, \Omega$$

For series NMOS in worst case: $R_{n,series} = 2 R_n = 7143 \, \Omega$.

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Resistance for $t_{PLH}$ (1 mark)**

$$R_p = \frac{1}{80 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4 \cdot 0.7} = \frac{1}{2.24 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 4464 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $t_{PLH}$ (1 mark)**

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot 4464 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-15} = 615.9 \, ps$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $t_{PHL}$ (1 mark)**

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot 7143 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-15} = 985.7 \, ps$$

**Step 6 — Worst-Case Average Delay (1 mark)**

$$t_{p,worst} = \frac{615.9 + 985.7}{2} = 800.8 \, ps \approx 801 \, ps$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.69 factor:** Many students write $t_p = R C$ directly. KTU requires the **ln(2) ≈ 0.69** factor unless explicitly told otherwise. **Penalty: 1 mark lost.**
> 2. **Using wrong $W/L$ ratio:** In NAND2/NOR2, the **series/parallel** topology drastically changes effective resistance. Students often use a single $R_n$ or $R_p$ incorrectly. **Penalty: 2 marks lost.**
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing $fF$ with $pF$, or $\mu A/V^2$ with $mA/V^2$. Always convert to base SI units (F, A/V², V). **Penalty: 1 mark lost.**
> 4. **Not specifying the measurement criterion:** The KTU answer MUST mention the **50% measurement point** of the waveform. **Penalty: 1 mark lost.**
> 5. **Confusing propagation delay with rise time:** $t_r$ and $t_p$ are different metrics. **Penalty: 2 marks lost.**

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your study wall!)**

- ✅ **Propagation delay** = time between 50% input and 50% output transitions, measured at $0.5 V_{DD}$.
- ✅ **Two directional delays:** $t_{PLH}$ (low→high, PMOS charges) and $t_{PHL}$ (high→low, NMOS discharges).
- ✅ **Average delay formula:** $t_p = (t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}) / 2$.
- ✅ **Single-pole RC result:** $t_p = 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, C_L$. The "0.69" = $\ln(2)$.
- ✅ **MOSFET ON-resistance:** $R_{ON} = 1 / [k' \cdot (W/L) \cdot (V_{GS} - V_T)]$.
- ✅ **NMOS vs PMOS:** NMOS has higher mobility → lower $R_n$ → faster $t_{PHL}$. PMOS is ~2-3× slower.
- ✅ **To reduce delay:**
  - Increase $W/L$ (wider transistor) — at the cost of area and input capacitance.
  - Increase $V_{DD}$ — at the cost of dynamic power ($P \propto V_{DD}^2$).
  - Decrease $V_T$ — at the cost of leakage current.
  - Insert buffers (tapering) to break long RC chains.
- ✅ **Elmore delay** generalizes single-pole RC to RC trees: $t_{Elmore} = \sum R_i C_i$.
- ✅ **Long chain delay grows quadratically** with the number of stages (without buffering).
- ✅ **Worst-case delay** in multi-input gates (NAND/NOR) occurs when transistors are **stacked in series**.
- ✅ **Industry metric:** Propagation delay is normalized as **FO4 (Fan-Out-of-4) inverter delay** — typically 20-30 ps per FO4 in modern nanometer CMOS.
- ✅ **Trade-off triangle:** Delay ↔ Power ↔ Area — every optimization moves at least one vertex.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 favourite:** Numerical problems with CMOS inverter using given $W/L$, $k'$, $V_{DD}$, $V_T$, $C_L$. Practice at least 5 such problems.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 — Propagation Delay Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 standards. Aligned with PECST415 VLSI Design (2024 Scheme) Course Outcomes CO1 and CO2, Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), and L4 (Analyze).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Sequential Logic Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Sequential Logic Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequential Logic Circuit**: A digital circuit whose output depends not only on the **present inputs** but also on the **past history of inputs** (i.e., the current *state* of the circuit). This is achieved through the use of **memory elements** (latches or flip-flops) and **feedback paths** from outputs back to inputs.

Mathematically, the output and next state of a sequential circuit are expressed as:

$$
O(t) = f(I(t), S(t))
$$

$$
S(t+1) = g(I(t), S(t))
$$

where $I(t)$ is the present input vector, $S(t)$ is the present state, $O(t)$ is the present output, and $S(t+1)$ is the **next state**.

This contrasts with a **combinational logic circuit**, whose output depends solely on the current inputs:

$$
O(t) = f(I(t))
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Memory Light Switch"**
>
> Imagine a staircase light switch:
> - **Combinational logic** is like a normal light — the bulb is ON only while you are pressing the switch. Release it, and the bulb is OFF. There is **no memory** of past actions.
> - **Sequential logic** is like a **staircase toggle switch** — one press turns it ON, the next press turns it OFF, regardless of who pressed it or when. The switch *remembers* the previous position. That *remembrance* is the **state**.
>
> A digital flip-flop is essentially a tiny electronic version of that toggle switch — it stores **1 bit of state**.

## 1.3 Classification of Sequential Circuits

Sequential circuits are broadly classified into two categories:

| Type | Description | Memory Behavior |
|------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Synchronous Sequential Circuit** | Memory elements are updated only at discrete instants governed by a **clock signal**. | State changes synchronized to clock edges. |
| **Asynchronous Sequential Circuit** | No global clock; state changes occur **immediately** in response to input changes. | State changes are event-driven. |

## 1.4 Bistable Element — The Heart of Sequential Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Bistable Element** is the fundamental memory cell. It has **two stable states** (Logic 0 and Logic 1) and can remain in either state indefinitely as long as power is supplied. This is the physical basis of every latch and flip-flop in VLSI design.

The **basic two-inverter loop** (cross-coupled inverters) is the simplest bistable:

- Inverter 1 output $\rightarrow$ Inverter 2 input
- Inverter 2 output $\rightarrow$ Inverter 1 input
- Two stable equilibria: $Q = 0$ or $Q = 1$

## 1.5 Key Terminology

- **State**: The collective memory of past inputs stored at a given time.
- **State Variable**: The binary value held in a memory element.
- **Clock Signal**: A periodic waveform that synchronizes state updates in synchronous circuits.
- **Edge-Triggered**: The memory element responds only to a clock transition (rising or falling edge).
- **Level-Triggered**: The memory element responds to the *level* (high or low) of the clock.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparison: Combinational vs Sequential Logic

| Feature | Combinational Logic | Sequential Logic |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Memory** | None | Present (latches/FFs) |
| **Output depends on** | Present inputs only | Present inputs + past inputs (state) |
| **Feedback** | Absent | Present (output $\to$ input) |
| **Timing** | No clock dependency (intrinsic delays only) | Clock-driven (synchronous) or input-driven (async) |
| **Examples** | Adder, MUX, Decoder, Encoder | Register, Counter, Shift Register, FSM |
| **Design Complexity** | Lower | Higher |

## 2.2 Types of Memory Elements (Latches and Flip-Flops)

### 2.2.1 Latches (Level-Sensitive)
A latch is transparent (output follows input) when the enable/clock signal is at an **active level** (HIGH or LOW). It is "opaque" otherwise.

- **SR Latch (Set-Reset)**: Has Set (S) and Reset (R) inputs.
- **D Latch (Data/Transparent Latch)**: Has a single Data (D) input; eliminates the invalid condition of SR.
- **JK Latch**: Eliminates the invalid state by toggling when both J = K = 1.
- **T Latch (Toggle)**: Toggles the output when T = 1.

### 2.2.2 Flip-Flops (Edge-Triggered)
A flip-flop samples its inputs only at the **active clock edge** (rising or falling). It is *insensitive* to inputs at all other times.

- **SR, D, JK, T flip-flops**: Edge-triggered versions of the corresponding latches.
- **Master-Slave Flip-Flop**: Built by cascading two latches (master and slave) with **inverted clock phases**. It effectively achieves edge-triggered behavior using level-sensitive latches.

## 2.3 Critical Timing Parameters (KTU High-Priority)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every VLSI sequential circuit design revolves around these four timing parameters. They are the **single most-tested topic** in KTU Module 1.

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Units |
|-----------|--------|------------|---------------|
| **Propagation Delay** | $t_{pd}$ or $t_{p}$ | Time taken for a change at the input to propagate to the output. | **ns** (nanoseconds) |
| **Setup Time** | $t_{su}$ | Minimum time the data input must be **stable BEFORE** the active clock edge. | **ns** |
| **Hold Time** | $t_{h}$ | Minimum time the data input must remain **stable AFTER** the active clock edge. | **ns** |
| **Clock-to-Q Delay** | $t_{cq}$ or $t_{clk\text{-}Q}$ | Time from active clock edge to output transition. | **ns** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $t_{clk\text{-}Q(min)} = t_{cq(min)}$ | Minimum clock-to-output delay | Hold time analysis |
| 2 | $t_{clk\text{-}Q(max)} = t_{cq(max)}$ | Maximum clock-to-output delay | Setup time analysis |
| 3 | $T_{clk} \geq t_{cq(max)} + t_{comb(max)} + t_{su}$ | **Minimum clock period** for correct synchronous operation | Maximum clock frequency |
| 4 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk(min)}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{cq(max)} + t_{comb(max)} + t_{su}}$ | **Maximum operating clock frequency** | Performance analysis |
| 5 | $t_{cq(min)} + t_{comb(min)} \geq t_{h}$ | **Hold time constraint** (must be satisfied for any clock period) | Design verification |
| 6 | $S(t+1) = g(I(t), S(t))$ | Next-state function | State transition analysis |
| 7 | $O(t) = f(I(t), S(t))$ | Output function (Mealy machine) | Output computation |
| 8 | $O(t) = f(S(t))$ | Output function (Moore machine) | Output computation |
| 9 | $Q_{n+1} = D$ | Next state of a D flip-flop | Characteristic equation |
| 10 | $Q_{n+1} = J \overline{Q_{n}} + \overline{K}\, Q_{n}$ | Next state of a JK flip-flop | Characteristic equation |

> **Note on Symbol Usage**: In all formulas above, $\vert x \vert$ would be written using `\vert` to avoid markdown table conflicts. However, since none of the timing equations require absolute value, this rule is naturally satisfied.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sequential logic circuits form the **backbone of every modern digital system**:

- **Microprocessors & CPUs**: Billions of flip-flops hold register values, pipeline state, and cache data.
- **DRAM & SRAM Memory**: Each memory cell is essentially a sequential element (1-bit storage).
- **Communication Protocols**: UART, SPI, I²C controllers use shift registers (sequential logic).
- **FPGA & ASIC Design**: Every synthesized RTL block contains a finite state machine (FSM) — a sequential circuit.
- **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)**: Pipelined filters and accumulators rely on precise timing of FFs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Clock Period and Maximum Operating Frequency

This is the **most important derivation** in KTU Module 1 for VLSI Design. We derive the constraint that guarantees reliable synchronous operation.

### 3.1.1 Setup Time Constraint — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Define the data arrival path.**
Consider a flip-flop $FF_1$ driving combinational logic, which in turn drives another flip-flop $FF_2$. The data launched by $FF_1$ at the **rising edge of clock cycle $k$** must arrive at the input of $FF_2$ **before the next rising edge of cycle $k+1$**.

**Step 2: Identify the time budget.**
The total time available between two consecutive rising clock edges is the **clock period $T_{clk}$**. The data must traverse three delays in this period:

- (a) Clock-to-Q delay of $FF_1$: $t_{cq}$
- (b) Combinational logic delay: $t_{comb}$
- (c) Setup time of $FF_2$: $t_{su}$

**Step 3: Formulate the inequality.**

$$
T_{clk} \;\geq\; t_{cq(max)} + t_{comb(max)} + t_{su}
$$

**Step 4: Derive the minimum clock period.**

$$
T_{clk(min)} \;=\; t_{cq(max)} + t_{comb(max)} + t_{su}
$$

**Step 5: Derive the maximum clock frequency.**

$$
f_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{clk(min)}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{t_{cq(max)} + t_{comb(max)} + t_{su}}
$$

### 3.1.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A synchronous VLSI system has the following parameters:
- $t_{cq(max)} = 0.5\,\text{ns}$
- $t_{comb(max)} = 2.0\,\text{ns}$
- $t_{su} = 0.3\,\text{ns}$

**Find:** (a) Minimum clock period, (b) Maximum operating frequency.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Minimum Clock Period:**

$$
T_{clk(min)} \;=\; t_{cq(max)} + t_{comb(max)} + t_{su}
$$

$$
T_{clk(min)} \;=\; 0.5\,\text{ns} + 2.0\,\text{ns} + 0.3\,\text{ns}
$$

$$
T_{clk(min)} \;=\; 2.8\,\text{ns}
$$

**Part (b) — Maximum Operating Frequency:**

$$
f_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{clk(min)}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2.8 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}}
$$

$$
f_{max} \;=\; 3.571 \times 10^{8}\,\text{Hz} \;\approx\; 357.1\,\text{MHz}
$$

### 3.1.3 Hold Time Constraint — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Define the data contamination path.**
Hold time violation occurs when data launched by $FF_1$ at clock edge $k$ arrives at $FF_2$ **too quickly** — i.e., it changes the input of $FF_2$ *before* the hold time window has elapsed.

**Step 2: Formulate the inequality.**

$$
t_{cq(min)} + t_{comb(min)} \;\geq\; t_{h}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**
Unlike setup time, the hold time constraint is **independent of the clock period**. It is a property of the data path itself and is satisfied (or violated) at design-time, regardless of clock frequency. If violated, the circuit **fails at any clock frequency**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Characteristic Equation of a D Flip-Flop

A D flip-flop's behavior is captured by its characteristic equation, which describes the next state $Q_{n+1}$ as a function of the present state and inputs.

**Step 1: Truth Table of D Flip-Flop (rising-edge triggered).**

| D | $Q_n$ | $Q_{n+1}$ |
|---|-------|-----------|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 |

**Step 2: Observe the pattern.** The next state equals the D input, regardless of the present state:

$$
Q_{n+1} = D
$$

**Step 3: This is a trivial identity**, but it is the **most-used characteristic equation in VLSI** because D flip-flops are the most common storage element in modern design.

## 3.3 Derivation: Characteristic Equation of a JK Flip-Flop

**Step 1: Truth Table of JK Flip-Flop.**

| J | K | $Q_n$ | $Q_{n+1}$ | Operation |
|---|---|-------|-----------|-----------|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | No change |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | No change |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | Reset |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | Reset |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | Set |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | Set |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | Toggle |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | Toggle |

**Step 2: Write a K-map for $Q_{n+1}$ with inputs $J$, $K$, $Q_n$.**

**Step 3: Derive the simplified expression.**

$$
Q_{n+1} \;=\; J\,\overline{Q_n} + \overline{K}\,Q_n
$$

**Step 4: Verify with J = K = 1 (toggle condition):**

$$
Q_{n+1} \;=\; 1 \cdot \overline{Q_n} + \overline{1} \cdot Q_n \;=\; \overline{Q_n} + 0 \;=\; \overline{Q_n}
$$

This correctly indicates a toggle, confirming the derivation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Master-Slave Flip-Flop Operation

The master-slave configuration is the **classical method** to convert a level-sensitive latch into an edge-triggered flip-flop.

| Phase | Clock Level | Master Latch | Slave Latch | Overall Output Q |
|-------|------------|--------------|-------------|------------------|
| Phase 1 | CLK = HIGH | **Transparent** (accepts input) | **Opaque** (holds) | Holds previous value |
| Phase 2 | CLK falling edge | Becomes opaque (latches) | Becomes transparent briefly | **Transfers master's latched value** |
| Phase 3 | CLK = LOW | **Opaque** (holds) | **Transparent** (tracks master) | Tracks master's value |
| Phase 4 | CLK rising edge | Becomes transparent | Becomes opaque | Holds transferred value |

**Net Effect:** The output changes *only* at the **falling edge of the clock**, emulating negative-edge-triggered behavior. Inputs are sampled at the **rising edge** (when master becomes transparent) and propagated to output at the **falling edge**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Classification of Digital Logic

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the hierarchical classification of digital logic circuits, with sequential logic at the center of our focus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DIGITAL LOGIC CIRCUITS] --> B[Combinational Logic]
    A --> C[Sequential Logic]
    B --> B1[Adders and Subtractors]
    B --> B2[Multiplexers and Demultiplexers]
    B --> B3[Encoders and Decoders]
    B --> B4[Comparators]
    C --> D[Synchronous Sequential]
    C --> E[Asynchronous Sequential]
    D --> D1[Latches Level Sensitive]
    D --> D2[Flip Flops Edge Triggered]
    D --> D3[Registers and Counters]
    D --> D4[Finite State Machines]
    E --> E1[Fundamental Mode Circuits]
    E --> E2[Pulse Mode Circuits]
    D1 --> D1a[SR Latch]
    D1 --> D1b[D Latch]
    D1 --> D1c[JK Latch]
    D1 --> D1d[T Latch]
    D2 --> D2a[SR Flip Flop]
    D2 --> D2b[D Flip Flop]
    D2 --> D2c[JK Flip Flop]
    D2 --> D2d[T Flip Flop]
    D2 --> D2e[Master Slave Flip Flop]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Coupled NOR-Based SR Latch Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the internal architecture of a cross-coupled NOR-based SR latch — the foundational building block.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[S Input] --> N1[NOR Gate 1]
    R[R Input] --> N2[NOR Gate 2]
    N1 --> Qbar[Q Bar Output]
    N2 --> Q[Q Output]
    Qbar --> N2
    Q --> N1
    N1 -. Feedback via Q Bar .-> N2
    N2 -. Feedback via Q .-> N1
```

**Operation Summary:**

- **S = 1, R = 0**: $Q = 1$, $\overline{Q} = 0$ (Set state)
- **S = 0, R = 1**: $Q = 0$, $\overline{Q} = 1$ (Reset state)
- **S = 0, R = 0**: $Q$ and $\overline{Q}$ hold their previous values (Memory state)
- **S = 1, R = 1**: $Q = 0$, $\overline{Q} = 0$ — **Invalid / Forbidden state** (both outputs equal)

## 4.3 D Flip-Flop Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the functional architecture of a positive-edge-triggered D flip-flop, including its internal buffering and the master-slave-style isolation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    D[D Input] --> BUF1[Input Buffer]
    CLK[Clock Signal] --> EDGE[Edge Detector]
    EDGE --> CTRL[Sampling Control]
    BUF1 --> CTRL
    CTRL --> LATCH[Internal Latch]
    LATCH --> BUF2[Output Buffer]
    BUF2 --> Q[Q Output]
    BUF2 --> QB[Q Bar Output]
```

## 4.4 Synchronous Sequential Circuit Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the canonical structure of a **synchronous sequential circuit**, showing the feedback from memory elements back to combinational logic.

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[External Inputs] --> CL[Combinational Logic Block]
    CLK[Clock Signal] --> MEM[Memory Elements Latches or FFs]
    MEM --> FB[Feedback Path State]
    FB --> CL
    CL --> OUT[External Outputs]
    MEM --> OUT
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **combinational logic** computes both the **next state** and the **outputs**.
- The **memory elements** store the current state and feed it back to the combinational logic on the next clock cycle.
- The **clock** synchronizes when the memory elements accept new values.

## 4.5 Mealy vs Moore Machine Block Diagrams

The following Mermaid block diagram contrasts the two canonical finite state machine (FSM) architectures.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph MEALY[MEALY MACHINE]
        MI1[Inputs] --> MC[Combinational Logic]
        MS[State Memory] --> MC
        MC --> MO1[Outputs]
        MC --> MS
        MO1 -. depends on inputs AND state .-> MI1
    end
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph MOORE[MOORE MACHINE]
        P1[Inputs] --> PC[Combinational Logic]
        PS[State Memory] --> PC
        PC --> PS
        PS --> PO[Outputs]
        PO -. depends ONLY on state .-> P1
    end
```

**Key Distinction:**

- **Mealy Machine**: Output = $f(\text{Inputs}, \text{State})$ — outputs can change asynchronously with input changes.
- **Moore Machine**: Output = $f(\text{State})$ — outputs change **only** on clock edges, making them inherently synchronized and glitch-free.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between combinational and sequential logic circuits. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Combinational Logic | Sequential Logic |
|--------|--------------------|------------------|
| Memory | No memory element | Contains memory (latches/FFs) |
| Output dependency | Output depends only on **present inputs** | Output depends on **present inputs and past history (state)** |
| Feedback | No feedback path | Feedback from output/memory to input |
| Timing behavior | Purely delay-based (no clock) | Clock-driven (synchronous) or event-driven (asynchronous) |
| **Examples** | Full adder, Multiplexer, Decoder, Encoder | Shift register, Counter, Flip-flop, FSM |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for key difference, 1 for examples)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define setup time and hold time of a flip-flop. Why are these parameters critical in VLSI design?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Setup Time ($t_{su}$)**: The minimum time interval **before** the active clock edge during which the data input must remain stable. If the data changes within $t_{su}$ of the clock edge, the flip-flop may enter a **metastable state**, producing unpredictable output.

- **Hold Time ($t_h$)**: The minimum time interval **after** the active clock edge during which the data input must remain stable. Violation causes the same metastability risk as setup time violation.

- **Why Critical in VLSI**: As clock frequencies scale into the gigahertz range (modern CPUs at 3–5 GHz, FPGAs beyond 500 MHz), the timing budget per cycle shrinks to sub-nanosecond levels. Setup and hold constraints **directly determine the maximum operating frequency** and are fundamental to **timing closure** during physical design.

*(3 marks: 1 for each definition, 1 for VLSI relevance)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Question A:** **(a)** Explain the architecture and operation of a **master-slave D flip-flop** with a neat block diagram. Discuss how it eliminates the race-around condition present in a basic JK latch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A synchronous VLSI system has the following timing parameters:
- $t_{cq(max)} = 0.8\,\text{ns}$, $t_{cq(min)} = 0.3\,\text{ns}$
- $t_{comb(max)} = 3.5\,\text{ns}$, $t_{comb(min)} = 1.2\,\text{ns}$
- $t_{su} = 0.4\,\text{ns}$, $t_h = 0.3\,\text{ns}$

Calculate: (i) **Minimum clock period**, (ii) **Maximum clock frequency**, (iii) Verify whether the **hold time constraint** is satisfied. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for A(a):**

A master-slave D flip-flop consists of **two D latches** connected in series, with **inverted clock phases** driving them.

**Block Structure:**

- **Master Latch**: Active (transparent) when CLK = HIGH. During this time, it accepts the D input.
- **Slave Latch**: Active (transparent) when CLK = LOW. It receives the master's stored value only after the clock transitions to LOW.

**Operation Cycle:**

1. **CLK = HIGH**: Master is transparent and follows D. Slave is opaque, holding the previous output Q. The output Q **does not change** even though the master is updating.
2. **CLK Transition (HIGH to LOW)**: Master becomes opaque and latches the value it was tracking. Slave becomes transparent, and the latched master value propagates to Q.
3. **CLK = LOW**: Master holds its value. Slave is transparent and tracks the (now-stable) master output.
4. **CLK Transition (LOW to HIGH)**: Slave becomes opaque, freezing Q. Master becomes transparent again, ready to accept the next D.

**Race-Around Condition in JK Latch:**
In a basic JK latch (level-triggered), when J = K = 1 and CLK remains HIGH, the output toggles continuously as long as the clock is active — leading to an **unpredictable final state** (the "race-around" problem). This occurs when $t_{pd} < T_{clk(\text{high})}$.

**How Master-Slave Eliminates Race-Around:**
In the master-slave configuration, the slave latch is **opaque** while the master is transparent (i.e., during the entire HIGH phase of CLK). The output can change **only when the master becomes opaque and the slave becomes transparent** — at the **clock edge**. Thus, the output toggles at most **once per clock cycle**, completely eliminating the race-around condition.

*(7 marks: 2 for architecture, 2 for operation, 3 for race-around analysis)*

**Model Solution for A(b):**

**(i) Minimum Clock Period:**

$$
T_{clk(min)} \;=\; t_{cq(max)} + t_{comb(max)} + t_{su}
$$

$$
T_{clk(min)} \;=\; 0.8\,\text{ns} + 3.5\,\text{ns} + 0.4\,\text{ns}
$$

$$
T_{clk(min)} \;=\; 4.7\,\text{ns}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Clock Frequency:**

$$
f_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{clk(min)}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{4.7 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}}
$$

$$
f_{max} \;\approx\; 212.77\,\text{MHz}
$$

**(iii) Hold Time Verification:**

$$
t_{cq(min)} + t_{comb(min)} \;\geq\; t_h \;\;?
$$

$$
0.3\,\text{ns} + 1.2\,\text{ns} \;\geq\; 0.3\,\text{ns} \;\;?
$$

$$
1.5\,\text{ns} \;\geq\; 0.3\,\text{ns} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Hold time constraint is satisfied** (1.5 ns $\geq$ 0.3 ns).

*(7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii) including the inequality check and conclusion)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Question B:** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **SR latch using cross-coupled NOR gates**. Derive its characteristic table and discuss all four input combinations, including the forbidden state. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Mealy and Moore machines** in detail. For a sequence detector that detects the pattern "101" in an input bit stream, write the state transition tables for both models. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for B(a):**

**Architecture:**
An SR latch using cross-coupled NOR gates consists of two NOR gates where the output of each gate is fed back as one of the inputs of the other gate.

- $S$ (Set) is the input to NOR Gate 1.
- $R$ (Reset) is the input to NOR Gate 2.
- $Q$ is the output of NOR Gate 2; $\overline{Q}$ is the output of NOR Gate 1.

**Boolean Equations:**

$$
Q \;=\; \overline{R + \overline{Q}} \;=\; \overline{R} \cdot Q
$$

$$
\overline{Q} \;=\; \overline{S + Q} \;=\; \overline{S} \cdot \overline{Q}
$$

**Characteristic Table (Step-by-Step Derivation):**

**Case 1: $S = 0$, $R = 0$ (Memory / Hold State):**

$$
Q \;=\; \overline{0} \cdot Q \;=\; Q
$$

$$
\overline{Q} \;=\; \overline{0} \cdot \overline{Q} \;=\; \overline{Q}
$$

Output **holds** its previous value. This is the **memory** state.

**Case 2: $S = 1$, $R = 0$ (Set State):**

$$
\overline{Q} \;=\; \overline{1 + Q} \;=\; 0
$$

$$
Q \;=\; \overline{0 + 0} \;=\; 1
$$

Output is **set** to $Q = 1$.

**Case 3: $S = 0$, $R = 1$ (Reset State):**

$$
Q \;=\; \overline{1 + \overline{Q}} \;=\; 0
$$

$$
\overline{Q} \;=\; \overline{0 + 0} \;=\; 1
$$

Output is **reset** to $Q = 0$.

**Case 4: $S = 1$, $R = 1$ (Forbidden / Invalid State):**

$$
Q \;=\; \overline{1 + \overline{Q}} \;=\; 0
$$

$$
\overline{Q} \;=\; \overline{1 + Q} \;=\; 0
$$

Both outputs become **0 simultaneously**, violating the complementary relationship $Q \cdot \overline{Q} = 0$. When both inputs return to 0 simultaneously, the **final state is unpredictable** (depends on minute propagation delay differences) — hence **forbidden**.

**Summary Table:**

| S | R | $Q_{n+1}$ | State |
|---|---|-----------|-------|
| 0 | 0 | $Q_n$ | Hold / Memory |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | Reset |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Set |
| 1 | 1 | — | **Forbidden** |

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram description, 3 for derivation, 2 for forbidden state discussion)*

**Model Solution for B(b):**

**Comparison Table: Mealy vs Moore Machine**

| Feature | Mealy Machine | Moore Machine |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Output depends on | Inputs + Present State | **Present State only** |
| Number of states (typically) | Fewer | More |
| Output timing | May change asynchronously (with input) | Changes only on clock edge |
| Glitch susceptibility | Higher | Lower |
| Implementation | Output logic at combinational path | Output decoded from state register |
| Formula | $O = f(I, S)$ | $O = f(S)$ |

**State Transition Table for "101" Sequence Detector (Mealy Machine):**

States: $S_0$ (no match), $S_1$ ("1" seen), $S_2$ ("10" seen), $S_3$ ("101" — output asserted).

| Present State | Input X = 0 | Input X = 0 Output | Input X = 1 | Input X = 1 Output |
|---------------|-------------|-------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| $S_0$ | $S_0$ | 0 | $S_1$ | 0 |
| $S_1$ | $S_2$ | 0 | $S_1$ | 0 |
| $S_2$ | $S_0$ | 0 | $S_3$ | **1** |
| $S_3$ | $S_2$ | 0 | $S_1$ | 0 |

*Note: Mealy outputs are associated with **input transitions**, so they appear in the same row as the next state and depend on the input value.*

**State Transition Table for "101" Sequence Detector (Moore Machine):**

In Moore, the output is tied to the state. We add a new state $S_3$ whose sole purpose is to assert the output.

| Present State | Output Z | Input X = 0 (Next State) | Input X = 1 (Next State) |
|---------------|----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| $S_0$ | 0 | $S_0$ | $S_1$ |
| $S_1$ | 0 | $S_2$ | $S_1$ |
| $S_2$ | 0 | $S_0$ | $S_3$ |
| $S_3$ | **1** | $S_2$ | $S_1$ |

*Note: Moore output is associated with the **present state** only. Notice that $S_3$ is required (more states) to produce the output, but the output is glitch-free.*

*(7 marks: 2 for comparison, 2 for Mealy table, 3 for Moore table with explanation)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing latch and flip-flop terminology**: A latch is *level-sensitive*; a flip-flop is *edge-triggered*. Using these terms interchangeably will cost you 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the hold time constraint is independent of clock period**: Many students incorrectly treat hold time as another setup-style constraint. The hold equation $t_{cq(min)} + t_{comb(min)} \geq t_h$ has **no $T_{clk}$ term** — it must hold for **all** clock periods.
> 3. **Sign errors in timing calculations**: When computing $f_{max}$, students often forget to convert nanoseconds to seconds. $1\,\text{ns} = 10^{-9}\,\text{s}$. A wrong conversion yields an answer off by a factor of $10^9$.
> 4. **Mealy vs Moore table formatting**: In Mealy tables, outputs go in the **input column rows**; in Moore tables, outputs are a **separate state column**. Mixing these formats is a frequent error.
> 5. **Skipping the forbidden state discussion in SR latches**: KTU examiners specifically look for the $S = R = 1$ invalid case discussion. Omitting it loses 2 marks easily.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Introduction to Sequential Logic Circuits**

- **Definition**: Sequential logic = combinational logic + memory + feedback.
- **State equation**: $S(t+1) = g(I(t), S(t))$; Output equation: $O(t) = f(I(t), S(t))$.
- **Two main categories**: Synchronous (clock-driven) and Asynchronous (event-driven).
- **Bistable element**: Two stable states, forms the basis of all memory. Built using cross-coupled inverters or NOR/NAND gates.
- **Latch** = level-sensitive; **Flip-flop** = edge-triggered.
- **Master-slave FF** = two latches with inverted clock phases; eliminates race-around condition in JK latches.
- **Four critical timing parameters**: $t_{cq}$, $t_{su}$, $t_h$, $t_{comb}$.
- **Minimum clock period**: $T_{clk(min)} = t_{cq(max)} + t_{comb(max)} + t_{su}$.
- **Maximum clock frequency**: $f_{max} = 1 \,/\, T_{clk(min)}$.
- **Hold time constraint**: $t_{cq(min)} + t_{comb(min)} \geq t_h$ — must hold for **any** clock period.
- **D flip-flop characteristic equation**: $Q_{n+1} = D$.
- **JK flip-flop characteristic equation**: $Q_{n+1} = J\overline{Q_n} + \overline{K}Q_n$.
- **SR latch forbidden state**: $S = R = 1$ produces $Q = \overline{Q} = 0$ (invalid).
- **Mealy output**: $O = f(I, S)$ — can glitch, fewer states.
- **Moore output**: $O = f(S)$ — synchronized, glitch-free, may need more states.
- **Race-around condition**: Occurs in JK latch when $J = K = 1$ and clock pulse width $>$ $t_{pd}$; eliminated by master-slave or edge-triggered design.
- **Modern VLSI design** uses D flip-flops almost exclusively due to their single-input simplicity and race-free behavior.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flip-Flops and Latches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flip-Flops and Latches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **VLSI Design**, a **sequential element** is a memory cell that stores a binary state. It comes in two principal forms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LATCH (Level-Sensitive Memory Element):**
> A bistable memory circuit that is **transparent** (i.e., the output follows the input) whenever the enable/clock signal is asserted at an active logic level, and that **holds** (latches) the last observed input value when the enable is de-asserted. Latches are classified as **level-sensitive** because they respond to the *level* (high or low) of the control signal, not the transition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FLIP-FLOP (Edge-Triggered Memory Element):**
> A bistable memory circuit built by cascading two latches in a **master–slave** configuration (or using an equivalent edge-sensitive structure), so that data is sampled **only on the active edge** (rising `0→1` or falling `1→0`) of the clock. The output is isolated from the input at all other times.

The unifying **characteristic equation** of a clocked memory cell is:

$$
Q_{next} = f(D,\, Q_{current})
$$

where $D$ is the data input, $Q_{current}$ is the present state, and $Q_{next}$ is the state after the active clock event.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Water Tap (Latch = Level Sensitive):**
> Imagine a water tap connected to a bucket. **While your hand is on the tap (clock = HIGH)**, water flows in and the bucket level *follows* whatever you do. The moment you **release the tap (clock goes LOW)**, the bucket *holds* the last water level — even if the tap input pressure changes. The bucket "remembers" because of the level of your hand — a *level-sensitive* action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Camera Shutter (Flip-Flop = Edge Triggered):**
> A DSLR camera only captures the scene at the *instant* the shutter button is pressed (a *falling/rising edge*). Before and after that instant, the sensor is decoupled from the scene. Even if the scene changes wildly, only the snapshot at the edge is stored. A flip-flop works exactly like this — it samples data on the **clock edge**, not the clock level.

---

## 1.3 Why Latches and Flip-Flops are Different in VLSI

| Property | Latch | Flip-Flop |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity | **Level-sensitive** | **Edge-triggered** |
| Transparency | Transparent when enabled | Never transparent |
| Race Condition Risk | **Yes** (if used carelessly) | **Minimal** |
| Typical Use | Temporary storage, DRAMS, sense amps | Registers, pipeline stages, FSMs |
| Clock Loading | Lower (1 phase) | Higher (2 phases / true clock) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** In synchronous VLSI pipelines, **flip-flops are the default** because they guarantee that a signal is sampled *exactly once per clock cycle*, avoiding race-through hazards that plague level-sensitive latches.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants / Metrics to Remember

- **Threshold voltage $V_{tn}, V_{tp}$** — typically **0.4 V** for NMOS, **−0.4 V** for PMOS in a 0.25 µm process.
- **Supply voltage $V_{DD}$** — modern deep-submicron: **0.7 V – 1.2 V**.
- **Clock frequency $f_{clk}$** — system-level parameter, often **GHz** range.
- **Minimum channel length $L_{min}$** — process-defined, e.g., **45 nm, 28 nm, 7 nm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SR Latch — Foundation Bistable

### 2.1.1 Cross-Coupled NOR SR Latch

Two NOR gates cross-coupled form the canonical SR latch. Truth table:

| S | R | Q (next) | Q̄ (next) | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Q (hold) | Q̄ (hold) | **Memory** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **Reset** |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **Set** |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **Forbidden** (race) |

Characteristic equation (excluding forbidden state):

$$
Q_{next} = S + \overline{R}\cdot Q
$$

with the **constraint** $S \cdot R = 0$.

### 2.1.2 Cross-Coupled NAND SR̄ Latch

Active-low inputs. Forbidden state becomes $S = R = 1$, the memory state becomes $S = R = 0$:

$$
Q_{next} = \overline{S} + (\overline{R}\cdot Q)
$$

---

## 2.2 The Gated D Latch (Transparent Latch)

Adding two series AND/NAND gates in front of an SR latch yields a **gated D latch**. Because the $D$ input is also routed to $\overline{D}$ through an inverter, the $S = R = 1$ forbidden state is **structurally impossible**.

Characteristic equation:

$$
Q_{next} = D \cdot EN + \overline{EN}\cdot Q
$$

When $EN = 1$: $Q_{next} = D$ (transparent).
When $EN = 0$: $Q_{next} = Q$ (hold).

---

## 2.3 The Master–Slave D Flip-Flop (Edge-Triggered)

Two gated D latches in series, with **complementary clocks**:

- **Master latch** enabled when $CLK = 1$, slave disabled.
- **Slave latch** enabled when $\overline{CLK} = 1$, master disabled.

On the **falling edge** of $CLK$, the value frozen in the master is transferred to the slave output $Q$. This produces **negative-edge-triggered** behavior.

The characteristic equation reduces to:

$$
Q_{next} = D \quad \text{(on active clock edge)}
$$

---

## 2.4 JK Flip-Flop — The "Tunable" Flip-Flop

Solves the SR forbidden-state issue by **feeding the outputs back** to the input AND gates. When $J = K = 1$, the cell **toggles** every clock cycle.

Characteristic equation:

$$
Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q
$$

This can be derived step-by-step (see SECTION 3).

> [!NOTE]
> **Special cases of the JK flip-flop:**
> - $J = K = 0$ → **Hold**
> - $J = 1, K = 0$ → **Set**
> - $J = 0, K = 1$ → **Reset**
> - $J = K = 1$ → **Toggle** (used in counters & frequency dividers)

---

## 2.5 CMOS Implementation Families

| Family | Transistor Count | Speed | Robustness | Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Static CMOS (NAND/NOR) | High | Medium | Excellent | Low static |
| Transmission-Gate (TG) | Low (2T/bit) | Fast | Good | Low |
| C²MOS (Clocked CMOS) | Medium | Fast | Excellent | Low |
| Dynamic (pseudo-nMOS) | Lowest | Fastest | Poor (leak) | High leakage |

---

## 2.6 Timing Parameters — The VLSI Engineer's Vocabulary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** The minimum time *before* the active clock edge that the data input $D$ must be **stable**.
>
> **Hold Time ($t_{h}$):** The minimum time *after* the active clock edge that $D$ must remain **stable**.
>
> **Clock-to-Q Propagation Delay ($t_{c2q}$):** Delay from the active clock edge to a valid change at $Q$.
>
> **Contamination Delay ($t_{cd}$):** Minimum delay from clock edge to any output change (used for hold-time analysis).
>
> **Clock-to-Output delay** is sometimes split into $t_{pLH}$ (low-to-high) and $t_{pHL}$ (high-to-low).

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SR Latch char. eqn. | $Q^{+} = S + \overline{R}Q$ | — | Constraint $SR = 0$ |
| 2 | D Latch char. eqn. | $Q^{+} = D \cdot EN + \overline{EN} \cdot Q$ | — | No forbidden state |
| 3 | D Flip-Flop char. eqn. | $Q^{+} = D$ | — | On active edge only |
| 4 | JK Flip-Flop char. eqn. | $Q^{+} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q$ | — | Toggle when $J = K = 1$ |
| 5 | T Flip-Flop char. eqn. | $Q^{+} = T \oplus Q$ | — | Each clock toggles if $T = 1$ |
| 6 | Setup-time constraint | $T_{clk} \geq t_{c2q} + t_{comb} + t_{su}$ | seconds | For pipeline to function |
| 7 | Hold-time constraint | $t_{cd,FF} + t_{cd,comb} \geq t_{h}$ | seconds | Must hold on every cycle |
| 8 | Max clock frequency | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{c2q} + t_{comb,max} + t_{su}}$ | Hz | $t_{comb} = $ combinational delay |
| 9 | Dynamic power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | W | $\alpha = $ switching activity |
| 10 | Static power | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | W | Dominant in deep-submicron |
| 11 | Energy per transition | $E = C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ | J | Fundamental switching energy |
| 12 | Power-Delay Product | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_{p}$ | J | Figure-of-merit for cell design |
| 13 | D-latch transparency | $Q = D$ when $EN = 1$ | — | Otherwise $Q$ holds |
| 14 | Hold-time violation | $t_{cd,FF} + t_{cd,comb} < t_{h}$ | seconds | Causes **race-through** |
| 15 | Setup-time violation | $t_{c2q} + t_{comb} + t_{su} > T_{clk}$ | seconds | Causes **data corruption** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The $f_{max}$ equation (row 8) is what every **timing-closure engineer** uses in Synopsys PrimeTime / Cadence Tempus to sign-off a 1-million-instance SoC at **1 GHz**. Violating either the setup or hold constraint on a single flip-flop can fail the entire chip's tape-out.

---

## 2.8 The Setup vs Hold Trade-off — Visual Intuition

- **Setup time** is about ensuring the **next-cycle data** has time to *travel* through combinational logic and *stabilize* before being sampled.
- **Hold time** is about ensuring the **current-cycle data** is *not* overwritten too quickly by the next-cycle data racing through a fast combinational path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** "**Setup = Slow path (give data time to arrive). Hold = Fast path (stop data from arriving too soon).**"

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — JK Flip-Flop Characteristic Equation from First Principles

**Goal:** Starting from the SR flip-flop equation $Q^{+} = S + \overline{R}Q$, derive the JK equation $Q^{+} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q$.

**Step 1 — Write the SR equation:**

$$
Q^{+} = S + \overline{R}Q
$$

**Step 2 — In a JK flip-flop, the S and R inputs are no longer external; they are generated by AND-ing the J and K inputs with the present outputs Q and Q̄ respectively:**

$$
S = J \cdot \overline{Q}
$$

$$
R = K \cdot Q
$$

> *Logic rationale:* When the cell is currently reset ($Q = 0, \overline{Q} = 1$), the J input is the only one allowed to influence the cell (because if $J = 1$ and $\overline{Q} = 1$, the AND gate outputs 1, setting the cell). When $Q = 1$, the K input dominates.

**Step 3 — Substitute S and R into the SR characteristic equation:**

$$
Q^{+} = (J \cdot \overline{Q}) + \overline{(K \cdot Q)} \cdot Q
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the second term $\overline{(K \cdot Q)} \cdot Q$ using De Morgan's law:**

$$
\overline{(K \cdot Q)} = \overline{K} + \overline{Q}
$$

Therefore:

$$
(\overline{K} + \overline{Q}) \cdot Q = \overline{K} \cdot Q + \overline{Q} \cdot Q
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Boolean identity $\overline{Q} \cdot Q = 0$:**

$$
\overline{K} \cdot Q + \overline{Q} \cdot Q = \overline{K} \cdot Q + 0 = \overline{K} \cdot Q
$$

**Step 6 — Combine with the first term:**

$$
Q^{+} = J \cdot \overline{Q} + \overline{K} \cdot Q
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{Q^{+} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify the four operating modes by truth-table substitution (sanity check):**

| J | K | Q | $J\overline{Q}$ | $\overline{K}Q$ | $Q^{+}$ | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Hold |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | Hold |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Reset |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Reset |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | Set |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | Set |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | Toggle |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Toggle |

All four expected modes confirmed. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Operating Frequency $f_{max}$

Consider a synchronous pipeline stage bounded by two flip-flops:

$$
FF_1 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Combinational Cloud} \;\longrightarrow\; FF_2
$$

**Step 1 — Data must depart $FF_1$ and arrive at $FF_2$ before the next active clock edge.** The time budget is one clock period $T_{clk}$:

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the time budget along the timing path:**

$$
T_{clk} = t_{c2q,\,FF_1} + t_{comb,\,max} + t_{su,\,FF_2} + t_{slack}
$$

where:
- $t_{c2q,\,FF_1}$ = clock-to-Q delay of the launching flip-flop,
- $t_{comb,\,max}$ = worst-case combinational delay,
- $t_{su,\,FF_2}$ = setup time of the capturing flip-flop,
- $t_{slack}$ = timing slack (must be $\geq 0$ for the design to work).

**Step 3 — At the **maximum** frequency, the slack is exactly zero:**

$$
t_{slack} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the minimum clock period and invert to get the maximum frequency:**

$$
T_{clk,\,min} = t_{c2q} + t_{comb,\,max} + t_{su}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{c2q} + t_{comb,\,max} + t_{su}}}
$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):**
Suppose $t_{c2q} = 100\,\text{ps}$, $t_{comb,\,max} = 350\,\text{ps}$, $t_{su} = 50\,\text{ps}$.

$$
T_{clk,\,min} = 100 + 350 + 50 = 500\,\text{ps}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^{-12}} = 2 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Hz} = \mathbf{2\,GHz}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hold-Time Constraint (Race-Through Condition)

**Step 1 — For the hold check, consider the *same* clock edge launching new data from $FF_1$ and the *same* edge capturing at $FF_2$.**

The new data must **not** arrive at $FF_2$ before its hold time has elapsed:

$$
t_{cd,\,FF_1} + t_{comb,\,min} \geq t_{h,\,FF_2}
$$

where $t_{cd}$ is the **contamination delay** (the earliest possible output change, not the typical $t_{c2q}$).

**Step 2 — Rearranging gives the hold constraint:**

$$
\boxed{t_{cd,\,FF} + t_{cd,\,comb} \geq t_{h}}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** If the combinational logic is *too fast* (e.g., a single inverter, $t_{cd} \approx 30\,\text{ps}$) and the flip-flop hold time is large (e.g., $t_h = 80\,\text{ps}$), a **hold violation** occurs. The fix is to insert **delay buffers** to slow the fast path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering note:** Hold-time violations are **independent of clock frequency**. They cannot be fixed by slowing the clock; you must add physical delay to the data path.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dynamic Power of a Clocked Flip-Flop Bank

**Step 1 — Each clock cycle, the internal nodes of an N-flip-flop register charge and discharge through capacitance $C_{L}$:**

$$
E_{cycle} = \alpha \cdot N \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the clock frequency to obtain average dynamic power:**

$$
P_{dyn} = \dfrac{E_{cycle}}{T_{clk}} = \alpha \cdot N \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

**Step 3 — Add static (leakage) power for total power:**

$$
P_{total} = \alpha \cdot N \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk} + V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in VLSI:** A 64-bit register file in a modern CPU contains 64 flip-flops. At $f_{clk} = 3\,\text{GHz}$, $V_{DD} = 0.85\,\text{V}$, $C_{L} = 5\,\text{fF}$, $\alpha = 0.1$, the dynamic power alone is:
> $$
> P_{dyn} = 0.1 \times 64 \times 5 \times 10^{-15} \times (0.85)^{2} \times 3 \times 10^{9} \approx 7\,\mu\text{W}
> $$
> Tiny per-register, but a billion-transistor chip has **thousands** of such registers — that is why **clock gating** and **power gating** are mandatory in modern SoCs.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Latch vs Flip-Flop Trade-off

| Design Aspect | Latch (TG) | Flip-Flop (MS-FF) | Flip-Flop (C²MOS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transistors per bit | ~8 | ~20 | ~12 |
| Setup time | Short | Longer | Medium |
| Hold time | ≈ 0 | Finite | Finite |
| Race-through risk | Higher | None | None |
| Clock load | 1 phase | 2 phases (true & bar) | 2 phases |
| Soft-error robustness | Low | High | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CMOS Transmission-Gate (TG) D Latch — Transistor-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Data Input D] -->|gate| TG1[TG1: NMOS + PMOS]
    CLK[Clock CLK] -->|gate N| TG1
    CLKbar[Inverted Clock CLK_BAR] -->|gate P| TG1
    TG1 -->|out| nodeX[Internal Node X]
    nodeX -->|in| INV1[Inverter 1]
    INV1 -->|out| nodeQ[Output Node Q]
    nodeQ -->|feedback| INV2[Inverter 2]
    INV2 -->|out| nodeQbar[Hold Node Q_HOLD]
    nodeQbar -->|in| TG2[TG2: NMOS + PMOS]
    TG2 -->|out| nodeX
    CLKbar -->|gate N| TG2
    CLK -->|gate P| TG2
    nodeQ --> OUTQ[Final Q Output]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style CLK fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style nodeQ fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600
```

> **Operating rule:** When $CLK = 1$ (and $\overline{CLK} = 0$), **TG1 ON** and **TG2 OFF** → the latch is **transparent** ($Q$ follows $D$). When $CLK = 0$, **TG1 OFF** and **TG2 ON** → the cross-coupled inverters hold the value.

---

## 4.2 Master–Slave D Flip-Flop — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    D[Data D] -->|D in| ML[Master Latch EN = CLK]
    ML -->|Q_master| SL[Slave Latch EN = CLK_BAR]
    SL -->|Q| OUT[Output Q]
    CLK[Clock CLK] -->|direct| ML
    CLK -->|via inverter| SL
    style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style ML fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style SL fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style OUT fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600
    style CLK fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
```

> **Sampling instant:** The data is **transferred from master to slave** precisely at the **falling edge of CLK** (since the slave becomes transparent only when $CLK = 0$). Hence this is a **negative-edge-triggered** flip-flop.

---

## 4.3 Timing Parameter Flow — Setup/Hold/Clock-to-Q

```mermaid
graph TD
    T0[Clock rising edge] --> TSU[Setup Window: D must be stable BEFORE edge]
    TSU --> T0
    T0 --> TCQ[Clock-to-Q delay t_c2q]
    TCQ --> TCOMB[Combinational Logic Delay t_comb]
    TCOMB --> THOLD[Hold Window: D must remain stable AFTER edge]
    THOLD --> T1[Next Clock rising edge]
    T1 --> TSU2[Setup for next cycle]
    style T0 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style T1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style TSU fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style THOLD fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style TCQ fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style TCOMB fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600
```

---

## 4.4 CMOS Static NAND Gate (Building Block of SR Latch)

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD[V_DD rail] --> P1[PMOS P1]
    VDD --> P2[PMOS P2]
    P1 -->|drain| MID[Internal Node]
    P2 -->|drain| MID
    MID --> N1[NMOS N1]
    N1 -->|drain| N2[NMOS N2]
    N2 -->|source| GND[Ground]
    A[Input A] -->|gate| P1
    A -->|gate| N1
    B[Input B] -->|gate| P2
    B -->|gate| N2
    MID --> OUT[Output Y = NAND A,B]
    style VDD fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style GND fill:#333333,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style OUT fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600
```

> Two **PMOS in parallel** (pull-up network) and two **NMOS in series** (pull-down network) form the canonical 2-input NAND. Two such gates cross-coupled give a NAND-based $\overline{S}\overline{R}$ latch.

---

## 4.5 JK Flip-Flop Internal Feedback Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    J[J Input] --> AND1[AND gate 1]
    Qbar[Q_bar feedback] --> AND1
    AND1 -->|S| SRFF[SR Latch Core]
    K[K Input] --> AND2[AND gate 2]
    Q[Q feedback] --> AND2
    AND2 -->|R| SRFF
    SRFF --> Q
    SRFF --> Qbar
    style J fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style K fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style SRFF fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style Q fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600
    style Qbar fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600
```

> The cross-feedback of $Q$ and $\overline{Q}$ to the input AND gates is what makes the $J = K = 1$ state a legal **toggle** instead of the forbidden race condition of the SR latch.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]
**Differentiate between a latch and a flip-flop. State one application where a latch is preferred over a flip-flop.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Latch | Flip-Flop |
|---|---|---|
| Triggering | **Level-sensitive** (transparent when enable is active) | **Edge-triggered** (samples data on clock edge) |
| Timing risk | Susceptible to race-through hazards | Race-free |
| Transistor count | Lower (e.g., 6T–8T for TG latch) | Higher (e.g., 20T for master–slave) |

**Application where latch is preferred:** **DRAM sense amplifiers** and **pulse-generators** in high-speed I/O interfaces, where the level-sensitive nature allows a wider sampling window and a smaller area. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Define the following terms with respect to sequential CMOS circuits: (i) Setup time, (ii) Hold time, (iii) Clock-to-Q delay.**

**Model Answer (3 marks = 1 mark each):**

1. **Setup time ($t_{su}$):** The minimum time interval **before the active clock edge** during which the data input $D$ must remain stable for the flip-flop to reliably capture the value. (1 mark)

2. **Hold time ($t_h$):** The minimum time interval **after the active clock edge** during which $D$ must remain stable to prevent corruption of the captured value. (1 mark)

3. **Clock-to-Q delay ($t_{c2q}$):** The propagation delay from the active clock edge to the point at which the output $Q$ becomes valid. Typically $t_{c2q} = \max(t_{pLH},\, t_{pHL})$. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — [14 Marks]

**(a)** Draw the CMOS schematic of a **transmission-gate (TG) based D latch**. Explain its operation when $CLK = 1$ and when $CLK = 0$. Why is this cell called a *transparent* latch? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **maximum clock frequency** of a synchronous pipeline given $t_{c2q} = 120\,\text{ps}$, combinational delay $t_{comb} = 480\,\text{ps}$, and setup time $t_{su} = 60\,\text{ps}$. What happens to $f_{max}$ if the supply voltage is reduced from $1.0\,\text{V}$ to $0.7\,\text{V}$? Justify qualitatively. **[7 marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — CMOS TG D Latch [7 marks]

**Schematic (already shown in SECTION 4.1):** Two transmission gates TG1 and TG2, two cross-coupled inverters (INV1 forming Q, INV2 forming the storage node), with $CLK$ and $\overline{CLK}$ driving the gates of the TG transistors. (Diagram 3 marks)

**Operation when $CLK = 1$** ($\overline{CLK} = 0$):
- TG1's NMOS gate is HIGH and PMOS gate is LOW → **TG1 is ON** (low resistance).
- TG2's NMOS gate is LOW and PMOS gate is HIGH → **TG2 is OFF** (high resistance).
- The input $D$ is connected through TG1 to the internal node, and the output $Q$ follows $D$ exactly. (2 marks)

**Operation when $CLK = 0$**:
- TG1 OFF, TG2 ON. The cross-coupled inverters form a bistable that holds the last value of $D$ at $Q$. (1 mark)

**Why "transparent"?** Because during $CLK = 1$ the output $Q$ is *electrically shorted* to input $D$ through TG1 — the latch behaves like a wire, i.e., it is *transparent* to data. (1 mark)

##### Part (b) — $f_{max}$ Derivation [7 marks]

**Step 1 —** Apply the formula derived in SECTION 3.2:

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{c2q} + t_{comb,\,max} + t_{su}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{120 \times 10^{-12} + 480 \times 10^{-12} + 60 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{660 \times 10^{-12}} = \dfrac{10^{12}}{660}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{max} \approx 1.515\,\text{GHz}}
$$

**Step 3 — Effect of reducing $V_{DD}$ from 1.0 V to 0.7 V:**

Lower $V_{DD}$ reduces the overdrive voltage $(V_{GS} - V_{th})$ of every transistor, which **decreases the drive current** $I_D$ in saturation:

$$
I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\mu_n C_{ox}\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}
$$

Since $t_{c2q}$ and $t_{comb}$ are inversely proportional to $I_D$, **all delays increase**, hence $f_{max}$ **decreases**. In practice, $f_{max}$ might drop from ~1.5 GHz to ~0.9 GHz. (3 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often forget to convert ps → s before taking the reciprocal. **Common error:** writing $f_{max} = 1/660 = 0.0015\,\text{Hz}$ — wrong by 12 orders of magnitude! Always carry the $10^{-12}$ factor carefully.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — [14 Marks]  (Alternative choice)

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **master–slave D flip-flop** built from two gated D latches. Show that this structure is **negative-edge-triggered**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A **JK flip-flop** has the characteristic equation $Q^{+} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q$. Starting from this equation, **derive the expression for the T (toggle) flip-flop** and explain its use as a **frequency divider by 2**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — Master–Slave D Flip-Flop [7 marks]

**Circuit (already shown in SECTION 4.2):** Two gated D latches in series. The **master** is enabled by $CLK$; the **slave** is enabled by $\overline{CLK}$. (2 marks)

**Phase 1 — $CLK = 1$ (master transparent, slave holds):**
- Master output tracks $D$ in real time.
- Slave is disabled, so its output $Q$ is held.
- Effectively, $D$ is being **loaded** into the master. (2 marks)

**Phase 2 — $CLK$ falls ($CLK = 0$, master holds, slave transparent):**
- Master is now isolated; it freezes the value of $D$ that was present at the falling edge.
- Slave becomes transparent and copies the master's value to $Q$.
- Therefore $Q$ changes **only on the falling edge of $CLK$** — negative-edge-triggered behavior. (2 marks)

**Formal proof:** Let $M$ be master output. During $CLK = 1$: $M = D$. At falling edge: $M$ freezes to $D_{sampled}$. During $CLK = 0$: $Q = M = D_{sampled}$. So $Q_{next} = D$ only on falling edge. (1 mark)

##### Part (b) — T Flip-Flop Derivation [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Start with the JK characteristic equation:**

$$
Q^{+} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q
$$

**Step 2 — Tie J and K together to a single input T** (so $J = K = T$):

$$
Q^{+} = T\overline{Q} + \overline{T}Q
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the right-hand side as the XOR of $T$ and $Q$:**

$$
\boxed{Q^{+} = T \oplus Q}
$$

(2 marks for the algebra)

**Step 4 — Truth table verification:**

| T | Q | $Q^{+}$ | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Hold |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Hold |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Toggle |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Toggle |

(2 marks)

**Step 5 — Use as frequency divider by 2:**

When $T = 1$ permanently, the output $Q$ toggles on every active clock edge. If the input clock has frequency $f_{clk}$, the output $Q$ has frequency $f_{clk}/2$. The output is a **square wave** with 50% duty cycle. This is the fundamental building block of **binary counters** and **frequency synthesizers** in digital PLLs. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** A common error is writing $Q^{+} = T + Q$ or $Q^{+} = T \cdot Q$ (i.e., using OR/AND instead of XOR). Always verify against the truth table: if $T = 1$ and $Q = 0$, $Q^{+}$ must be **1** (toggle to set state), which only XOR satisfies.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MISTAKES in Flip-Flop / Latch questions (compiled from KTU valuation keys):**
> 1. **Confusing "transparent" with "opaque":** Forgetting that a latch is transparent when $EN = 1$ and holds when $EN = 0$ (or vice versa for active-low enables). Costs: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up SR and JK truth tables:** Writing $S = R = 1$ as "toggle" for the SR latch — that is the JK behavior, not SR. The SR $S = R = 1$ state is **forbidden** (both outputs go to 0, race on release). Costs: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw the inverter for $\overline{CLK}$** in master–slave flip-flop diagrams. Costs: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Wrong units in $f_{max}$ calculation:** Not converting ps to seconds. Costs: **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Ignoring the hold-time constraint:** Students often only solve the setup-time equation and forget that $t_{cd} + t_{cd,comb} \geq t_h$ is a *separate*, frequency-independent constraint. Costs: **2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
> - ✅ **Latch = level-sensitive**, **Flip-Flop = edge-triggered**. Always state this first.
> - ✅ **SR latch forbidden state** is $S = R = 1$ for NOR-based; $S = R = 0$ for NAND-based.
> - ✅ **D latch char. eqn.:** $Q^{+} = D \cdot EN + \overline{EN} \cdot Q$. No forbidden state because $S = \overline{R}$ structurally.
> - ✅ **D flip-flop char. eqn.:** $Q^{+} = D$ (on the active edge only).
> - ✅ **JK char. eqn.:** $Q^{+} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q$. Toggle mode when $J = K = 1$.
> - ✅ **T char. eqn.:** $Q^{+} = T \oplus Q$. When $T = 1$ permanently → divide-by-2.
> - ✅ **Master–slave FF:** Two latches in series with complementary clocks → edge-triggered.
> - ✅ **Setup-time constraint:** $T_{clk} \geq t_{c2q} + t_{comb} + t_{su}$.
> - ✅ **Hold-time constraint:** $t_{cd} + t_{cd,comb} \geq t_h$ (frequency-independent).
> - ✅ **$f_{max}$ formula:** $f_{max} = 1/(t_{c2q} + t_{comb,\,max} + t_{su})$.
> - ✅ **Dynamic power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f_{clk}$. Reducing $V_{DD}$ quadratically reduces power.
> - ✅ **CMOS TG latch** uses 2 transistors per TG (1 NMOS + 1 PMOS) → 4 transistors for the two TGs + 4 for the two inverters = **8 transistors per bit**.
> - ✅ **C²MOS flip-flop** is preferred for its robustness against clock overlap and charge sharing.
> - ✅ **Race-through hazard** exists only in latches; flip-flops are immune because the master and slave are never simultaneously transparent.
> - ✅ **Glossary must-know terms for KTU viva:** transparency window, contamination delay, metastability, clock skew, jitter, clock-to-Q, hold violation, setup violation, slack, timing margin.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timing Analysis - Set-up Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Timing Analysis - Set-up Time**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Setup Time ($T_{setup}$ or $t_{su}$):** The **minimum time interval** required for the **data input (D)** of a sequential element (Flip-Flop or Latch) to remain **stable BEFORE the active clock edge** arrives, so that the data is reliably sampled and stored.

- **Symbol Used in KTU Board Exams:** $T_{su}$, $t_{setup}$, or $S$
- **Standard Unit:** **seconds (s)**, commonly expressed in **picoseconds (ps)** or **nanoseconds (ns)** for sub-micron CMOS technologies.
- **Where it is measured:** Between the **50% transition point** of the data signal and the **50% transition point** of the active clock edge.
- **Default KTU Value Assumed (for unspecific problems):** $T_{setup} = 0.5 \text{ ns}$ to $2 \text{ ns}$ for standard CMOS flip-flops.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Train Door Closing" Scenario:**
> Imagine you are trying to board a train. The doors close sharply at a specific scheduled time (the **active clock edge**). Setup time is the rule that says: *"You must be fully inside the train at least, say, 2 seconds BEFORE the doors close."* If you are still running toward the door 1 second before closing, the system cannot guarantee your safe entry — you might get stuck halfway, leading to a malfunction.
>
> In digital circuits, the **data** must arrive at the flip-flop input **before** the clock edge by a minimum margin $T_{su}$, otherwise the flip-flop enters an unpredictable, oscillating state called **metastability**.

---

### 1.3 Why Setup Time is Critical in VLSI

- Determines the **maximum clocking frequency ($f_{max}$)** of any synchronous digital system.
- A setup violation causes the flip-flop to enter a **metastable state** for an unpredictable duration.
- Directly affects the **critical path delay** in a pipelined processor.
- Mandatory parameter for **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** tools in ASIC/FPGA design flows.

---

### 1.4 Physical Origin of Setup Time in CMOS

Inside a master-slave D flip-flop, the master latch samples the data on one clock phase and the slave on the other. The internal nodes (especially those driving the storage capacitor at the output of the first inverter pair) require a finite time to charge/discharge to a valid logic level. The **setup time is therefore not arbitrary** — it is a function of:

$$
T_{su} \approx \frac{C_{internal} \cdot \Delta V}{I_{drive}}
$$

where:
- $C_{internal}$ = parasitic capacitance at the internal storage node
- $\Delta V$ = voltage swing required to flip the latch
- $I_{drive}$ = drive current of the input transistor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** Setup time is measured **at the input pin of the flip-flop** — NOT at the output of the previous combinational logic. This is a common mark-deduction point.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Stage Flip-Flop Timing Model

A standard edge-triggered D flip-flop has two internal latches (master + slave). The **setup time** is the time required for the master latch to **reliably capture** the data before the clock transition disables the sampling path.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Before clock edge:** Data $D$ must be stable so that node $X$ (internal storage point) settles to the correct value.
2. **At clock edge:** The master latch becomes opaque (holds the value).
3. **After clock edge:** The slave latch opens and passes the held value to output $Q$.
4. **Setup window:** Steps 1 → 2 constitute the setup time requirement.

---

### 2.2 Setup Time in a Sequential Pipeline Path

Consider two flip-flops (FF1 → combinational cloud → FF2) driven by the same clock:

```
D ──► [FF1] ──► Q ──► [Combinational Logic] ──► D' ──► [FF2]
                ▲                                        ▲
                │                                        │
              CLK                                       CLK
```

The data must travel from FF1 to FF2's data input and remain stable for $T_{su}$ before the next clock edge.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table lists every equation tested in KTU 2024 ESE for setup time analysis. Memorize these for direct 3-mark and 14-mark problems.

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Standard Symbol Convention** |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{clk} \geq T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup} - T_{skew}$ | Minimum clock period to avoid setup violation | $T_{cq}$ = Clock-to-Q delay, $T_{comb}$ = combinational delay, $T_{skew}$ = clock skew |
| $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cq} + T_{comb,max} + T_{setup} - T_{skew}}$ | Maximum operating frequency | $T_{comb,max}$ = worst-case (longest) combinational path |
| $T_{slack,setup} = T_{clk} - (T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup} - T_{skew})$ | Setup slack (must be $\geq 0$) | Positive slack = no violation |
| $T_{cq} = t_{phl}$ or $t_{plh}$ | Clock-to-Q propagation delay | Depends on output transition direction |
| $T_{skew} = T_{clk,FF2} - T_{clk,FF1}$ | Difference in clock arrival times | Positive = clock at FF2 arrives later |
| $T_{d,path} = T_{cq,FF1} + \sum T_{comb,i}$ | Total data arrival time at FF2 input | Sum of all gate + interconnect delays |
| $T_{required} = T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{setup}$ | Latest required arrival time of data | For setup check |
| $T_{setup,violation} \iff T_{d,path} > T_{required}$ | Condition for setup violation | Must be FALSE for correct operation |

> **Critical Reminder:** Use **$\vert$** (or `\vert`) instead of the pipe character `|` in inline math, since the markdown parser can otherwise break.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microprocessor Design:** Determines the maximum clock frequency of Intel/AMD CPUs. A 5 GHz CPU has $T_{clk} = 200 \text{ ps}$; the entire setup path must fit within this.
- **FPGA Timing Closure:** Vivado/Quartus STA tools report setup slack; designers must meet positive slack to guarantee bitstream functionality.
- **ASIC Sign-off:** Cadence Tempus and Synopsys PrimeTime check setup time across millions of paths at PVT (Process, Voltage, Temperature) corners.
- **High-Speed Serial Links:** Setup/hold directly limit the maximum baud rate of PCIe, USB, and DDR memory interfaces.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Setup Time Constraint Equation

We derive the minimum clock period to avoid setup violation for a standard FF1 → combinational logic → FF2 pipeline.

**Step 1: Define the data arrival time at FF2's input.**

The data launched by FF1 at clock edge $k$ must propagate through the combinational logic and arrive at FF2's $D$ input.

$$
T_{arrival} = T_{cq} + T_{comb}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* $T_{cq}$ is the delay from the $k^{th}$ clock edge until $Q$ becomes valid; $T_{comb}$ is the subsequent delay through the combinational network.

**Step 2: Define the required arrival time at FF2's input.**

For FF2 to correctly capture the data, the data must arrive **at least $T_{setup}$ before** the $(k+1)^{th}$ clock edge at FF2. If the clock at FF2 is delayed by $T_{skew}$ relative to FF1:

$$
T_{required} = T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{setup}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Total time available is $T_{clk} + T_{skew}$ (extra due to clock skew); we subtract the setup time window because data must be stable BEFORE the edge.

**Step 3: Apply the setup constraint.**

For correct operation, the data must arrive **no later than** the required time:

$$
T_{arrival} \leq T_{required}
$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$
T_{cq} + T_{comb} \leq T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{setup}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for the minimum clock period.**

$$
T_{clk} \geq T_{cq} + T_{comb} - T_{skew} + T_{setup}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{clk,min} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup} - T_{skew}}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* We rearranged the inequality to isolate $T_{clk}$ on the left side, which is the standard form tested in KTU board examinations.

**Step 5: Derive the maximum operating frequency.**

Since $f = 1/T$:

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{cq} + T_{comb,max} + T_{setup} - T_{skew}}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* We use the **worst-case** (maximum) combinational delay, because setup time checks are done on the slowest path. This is the path that limits the system speed.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A digital system has $T_{cq} = 0.3 \text{ ns}$, $T_{comb,max} = 1.8 \text{ ns}$, $T_{setup} = 0.2 \text{ ns}$, and clock skew $T_{skew} = 0.1 \text{ ns}$. Calculate (a) the minimum clock period, (b) the maximum operating frequency, and (c) the setup slack if the actual clock period is 3 ns.

**Solution (a): Minimum Clock Period**

$$
T_{clk,min} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup} - T_{skew}
$$

$$
T_{clk,min} = 0.3 + 1.8 + 0.2 - 0.1
$$

$$
T_{clk,min} = 2.2 \text{ ns}
$$

**Solution (b): Maximum Frequency**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \frac{1}{2.2 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
f_{max} \approx 454.5 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Solution (c): Setup Slack**

$$
T_{slack} = T_{clk} - T_{clk,min} = 3.0 - 2.2 = 0.8 \text{ ns}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Since slack is **positive (0.8 ns)**, the design has a 0.8 ns timing margin and operates correctly.

---

### 3.3 Setup Violation Analysis (Negative Slack)

If the clock period is reduced below $T_{clk,min}$, slack becomes negative:

$$
T_{slack} < 0 \implies \text{SETUP VIOLATION}
$$

**Consequence:** FF2 enters **metastability**, where its output oscillates between 0 and 1 for an unbounded duration $\tau$, given by:

$$
T_{res} = \tau \cdot \ln\left(\frac{\Delta V_{initial}}{\text{Threshold}}\right)
$$

where $\tau$ is the time constant of the regenerative feedback loop inside the flip-flop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Note:** A setup violation does NOT always produce an immediate logic error — it produces a *probabilistic* error, with the Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) given by:
> $$MTBF = \frac{e^{T_{slack}/\tau}}{T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}$$
> Designers target MTBF > several years for production silicon.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Setup Time vs. Hold Time

> [!NOTE]
> This comparative matrix is high-yield for KTU 14-mark comparative questions.

| **Parameter** | **Setup Time** | **Hold Time** |
|---|---|---|
| **Window Position** | BEFORE active clock edge | AFTER active clock edge |
| **Limits** | Maximum clock frequency | Minimum clock period (no lower bound on $T_{clk}$) |
| **Effect of Violation** | Metastability, data not captured | Race condition, next-cycle data corruption |
| **Effect of Skew** | Skew helps (relaxes) | Skew hurts (tightens) |
| **Measured From** | Data transition to clock edge | Clock edge to next data transition |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Setup Time Timing Waveform (Conceptual)

The diagram below illustrates the **setup time window** relative to the active clock edge:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Data D stable period] --> B[Setup Time Window Tsu]
    B --> C[Active Clock Edge rising 50%]
    C --> D[Clock to Q delay Tcq]
    D --> E[Output Q transitions]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style C fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style E fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
```

**Key Visual Points:**
- The **yellow region** labeled $T_{su}$ is the setup time window — data must be stable throughout this period.
- The **red region** is the active clock edge (sampling instant).
- The **green region** is the Clock-to-Q delay, after which the output $Q$ becomes valid.

---

### 4.2 Sequential Pipeline Block Diagram (Setup Path)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph LaunchPath[Launch Flip-Flop FF1]
        L1[Data Input D1] --> L2[Master Latch]
        L2 --> L3[Slave Latch]
        L3 --> L4[Output Q1]
    end
    L4 --> CB[Combinational Logic Cloud]
    CB --> P1[Internal Node A]
    P1 --> P2[Data Input D2]
    subgraph CapturePath[Capture Flip-Flop FF2]
        P2 --> P3[Master Latch FF2]
        P3 --> P4[Setup Time Window Tsu]
        P4 --> P5[Output Q2]
    end
    CLKA[Clock Net] -.-> LaunchPath
    CLKB[Clock Net with Skew] -.-> CapturePath
    style CB fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style P4 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style CLKA fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style CLKB fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Interpretation:**
- **Blue blocks:** Clock distribution network reaching both flip-flops (with potential skew between them).
- **Orange block:** Combinational logic determining $T_{comb}$.
- **Red block:** The setup time window $T_{su}$ at the capture flip-flop — the most timing-critical region.

---

### 4.3 Setup Time Violation — Metastability Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[Data arrives late: T_arrival > T_required] --> N2[Setup Violation Detected]
    N2 --> N3[FF2 enters Metastable State]
    N3 --> N4{Resolution before next edge?}
    N4 -->|Yes| N5[Correct output: low MTBF risk]
    N4 -->|No| N6[Data corruption: system failure]
    style N1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style N2 fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c
    style N3 fill:#ba68c8,stroke:#4a148c
    style N4 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style N5 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style N6 fill:#ef5350,stroke:#b71c1c
```

**Interpretation:** When data arrives too close to the clock edge (within $T_{su}$), the flip-flop cannot resolve the logic level before the next clock cycle, leading to potential data corruption in downstream stages.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

Define **setup time** in the context of a digital flip-flop. State its significance in synchronous circuit design.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Setup time ($T_{su}$) is defined as the **minimum time interval** for which the data input $D$ of a flip-flop must remain **stable before the active clock edge** to ensure reliable data capture.

**Significance (1 mark):** In synchronous circuit design, setup time determines the **maximum operating frequency** ($f_{max}$) of the system. A setup violation results in metastability and unreliable data storage, making setup time a critical parameter in Static Timing Analysis (STA) and high-speed digital design.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

What happens if the **setup time** of a flip-flop is violated? Briefly explain **metastability**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

When setup time is violated, the data input changes too close to the active clock edge, and the flip-flop cannot resolve the input to a stable logic level (0 or 1) within the clock-to-Q delay.
