This causes the flip-flop to enter a **metastable state**, where the output oscillates between logic 0 and 1 for an unpredictable duration. If the metastability is not resolved before the next clock cycle, it propagates as a logic error in the downstream circuitry, potentially causing system failure.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern requires an **internal choice** in Part B. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the equation for the **minimum clock period** required to satisfy the setup time constraint in a sequential circuit consisting of two flip-flops separated by combinational logic. Clearly define all terms used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital system uses flip-flops with $T_{setup} = 0.4 \text{ ns}$ and $T_{cq} = 0.3 \text{ ns}$. The combinational logic block has a maximum delay of 2.1 ns and a minimum delay of 0.8 ns. The clock skew is 0.15 ns. Calculate:
   - (i) The maximum clock frequency the system can operate at.
   - (ii) The setup slack if the system operates at 400 MHz.
   - (iii) Comment on whether a setup violation occurs. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the path (1 mark)**

Consider two flip-flops FF1 and FF2 connected by a combinational logic block, both driven by the same clock.

**Step 2: Data arrival time at FF2 input (2 marks)**

The data launched by FF1 at the $k^{th}$ clock edge arrives at FF2's $D$ input after:

$$
T_{arrival} = T_{cq} + T_{comb}
$$

where $T_{cq}$ is the clock-to-Q delay of FF1 and $T_{comb}$ is the combinational logic delay.

**Step 3: Required arrival time (2 marks)**

For correct capture, the data must be stable at least $T_{setup}$ before the next clock edge at FF2. Accounting for clock skew $T_{skew}$:

$$
T_{required} = T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{setup}
$$

**Step 4: Setup constraint and final equation (2 marks)**

$$
T_{arrival} \leq T_{required}
$$

$$
T_{cq} + T_{comb} \leq T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{setup}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{clk,min} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup} - T_{skew}}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T_{setup} = 0.4 \text{ ns}$, $T_{cq} = 0.3 \text{ ns}$, $T_{comb,max} = 2.1 \text{ ns}$, $T_{skew} = 0.15 \text{ ns}$.

**(i) Maximum clock frequency (3 marks):**

$$
T_{clk,min} = T_{cq} + T_{comb,max} + T_{setup} - T_{skew}
$$

$$
T_{clk,min} = 0.3 + 2.1 + 0.4 - 0.15
$$

$$
T_{clk,min} = 2.65 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \frac{1}{2.65 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 377.36 \text{ MHz}
$$

**(ii) Setup slack at 400 MHz (3 marks):**

$$
T_{clk,actual} = \frac{1}{400 \times 10^{6}} = 2.5 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{slack} = T_{clk,actual} - T_{clk,min} = 2.5 - 2.65 = -0.15 \text{ ns}
$$

**(iii) Violation analysis (1 mark):**

Since $T_{slack} = -0.15 \text{ ns}$ (negative), a **setup violation** occurs. The system cannot reliably operate at 400 MHz.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of setup time** with a suitable waveform diagram. Discuss how setup time affects the **maximum operating frequency** of a digital system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pipelined processor stage has the following timing parameters:
   - Flip-flop clock-to-Q delay: $T_{cq} = 50 \text{ ps}$
   - Combinational logic delay: $T_{comb} = 350 \text{ ps}$
   - Setup time of capture flip-flop: $T_{setup} = 80 \text{ ps}$
   - Clock skew: $T_{skew} = 20 \text{ ps}$

   Calculate:
   - (i) Minimum clock period.
   - (ii) Maximum clock frequency.
   - (iii) If the design is required to operate at 3 GHz, what is the setup slack? Will the design meet timing? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — Concept Explanation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Setup time ($T_{su}$) is the minimum duration the data input must remain stable **before the active clock edge** for the flip-flop to reliably capture the data.

**Waveform explanation (3 marks):** The data input $D$ must be stable for at least $T_{su}$ before the clock edge, and remain stable for at least $T_h$ after the edge. The output $Q$ transitions $T_{cq}$ after the clock edge.

**Effect on $f_{max}$ (2 marks):** Since $T_{clk,min} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup} - T_{skew}$, the maximum frequency is inversely proportional to the setup time. Reducing $T_{setup}$ allows higher clock speeds, which is why modern flip-flops (e.g., pulse-triggered latches) are designed with sub-50 ps setup times.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Minimum clock period (2 marks):**

$$
T_{clk,min} = 50 + 350 + 80 - 20 = 460 \text{ ps}
$$

**(ii) Maximum frequency (2 marks):**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{460 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 2.174 \text{ GHz}
$$

**(iii) Setup slack at 3 GHz (3 marks):**

$$
T_{clk,actual} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{9}} \approx 333.33 \text{ ps}
$$

$$
T_{slack} = 333.33 - 460 = -126.67 \text{ ps}
$$

Since the slack is **negative**, the design **FAILS** to meet timing at 3 GHz. The processor stage must be redesigned (e.g., pipeline deeper, optimize combinational logic) to reduce $T_{comb}$ to below $223 \text{ ps}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting clock skew sign convention** (2–3 marks lost): Use $T_{skew}$ as defined; do NOT auto-assume positive. Some problems define $T_{skew} = T_{clk,FF1} - T_{clk,FF2}$ (opposite sign). **Always re-read the problem statement carefully.**
>
> 2. **Using minimum instead of maximum combinational delay** (3 marks lost): Setup checks use $T_{comb,max}$ (longest path). Hold checks use $T_{comb,min}$ (shortest path). Mixing these up is a fatal error.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units in $f_{max}$** (1 mark lost): $f_{max}$ must be expressed in **Hz, MHz, or GHz** — not in 1/ps or raw numbers.
>
> 4. **Not commenting on slack sign** (1 mark lost): Always state whether slack is positive (passes) or negative (fails). A numerical answer without interpretation is incomplete.
>
> 5. **Confusing setup time with hold time** (up to 7 marks lost): Setup is BEFORE the edge, hold is AFTER. Their equations look similar but have opposite clock skew signs.
>
> 6. **Missing the pipeline-stage formula:** For an $N$-stage pipeline, the throughput is determined by the **slowest** stage's $T_{clk,min}$, not the average.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup Time — Final Revision Summary:**
>
> - **Definition:** $T_{su}$ = minimum time data must be stable **BEFORE** active clock edge.
> - **Master Equation:** $T_{clk,min} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup} - T_{skew}$
> - **Maximum Frequency:** $f_{max} = 1 / T_{clk,min}$ (use worst-case $T_{comb}$).
> - **Setup Slack:** $T_{slack} = T_{clk,actual} - T_{clk,min}$. Must be $\geq 0$.
> - **Setup Violation Trigger:** Data arrives later than $T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{setup}$.
> - **Effect of Violation:** Metastability, probabilistic data corruption, MTBF decreases exponentially with negative slack.
> - **Physical Origin:** Internal storage node capacitance + finite drive current in master latch.
> - **Measured at:** Input pin of flip-flop (not output of previous stage).
> - **Clock Skew Effect:** Positive skew **relaxes** setup (more time available) but **tightens** hold.
> - **KTU Default:** $T_{su}$ typically ranges from $0.1$ to $1$ ns for CMOS FFs.
> - **STA Tool Role:** Cadence Tempus / Synopsys PrimeTime check setup time at PVT corners (SS, TT, FF).
> - **Engineering Link:** Setup time is the **fundamental limit** on $f_{max}$ of any synchronous CMOS system.
> - **Fix for Violation:** (i) Reduce clock frequency, (ii) Insert pipeline stage, (iii) Use faster flip-flops, (iv) Reduce combinational logic depth, (v) Adjust clock tree to add intentional skew.
> - **Common Pair Question:** "Compare setup time and hold time" — expect 14 marks with derivation of both equations.
> - **Real-World Example:** A 5 GHz CPU has $T_{clk} = 200$ ps; setup time + combinational delay + $T_{cq}$ must all fit within this 200 ps window.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hold Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hold Time # 📘 MODULE 1: HOLD TIME — KTU 2024 SCHEME STUDY NOTES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📌 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hold Time ($t_{hold}$)** is defined as the **minimum amount of time** that the **data input of a sequential element (flip-flop or latch) must remain stable (unchanged) *after* the active edge of the clock signal** has arrived, in order to ensure that the new data is reliably captured and stored by the memory element. Mathematically, it is expressed as a **temporal duration** measured in **seconds (s)**, typically in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)** in modern VLSI design. ### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Photographer" Intuition Imagine you are a **photographer** capturing a picture of a **fast-moving athlete** crossing a finish line. The "shutter click" of your camera represents the **active clock edge**. - **Before the click** (Setup Time): The athlete must already be at the finish line — they need to be in position *before* you click. - **After the click** (Hold Time): The athlete must **not suddenly teleport away** the instant you click. They need to remain at the line for a tiny moment so the camera's sensor can "lock in" their image. In VLSI terms: - The **athlete's position** = the **data input signal (D)** - The **camera click** = the **active clock edge** - **Hold Time** = the brief moment the data must "linger" after the clock fires, so the flip-flop's internal latch can securely capture it. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU Exams):** > - **Setup Time ($t_{su}$)** → Data must be stable **BEFORE** the clock edge. > - **Hold Time ($t_{hold}$)** → Data must be stable **AFTER** the clock edge. > These two are **independent** constraints on the same flip-flop. ### 📏 Standard Industry Metrics (Typical 65nm CMOS) | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | |-----------|--------|-------------------| | Hold Time | $t_{hold}$ | **30 – 100 ps** | | Setup Time | $t_{su}$ | 50 – 200 ps | | Clock-to-Q Delay | $t_{cq}$ | 50 – 150 ps | | Contamination Delay | $t_{cd}$ | 30 – 80 ps | > [!TIP] > Hold time is generally **smaller** than setup time and is often **independent of the clock period**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🔍 Operational Breakdown — Why Hold Time Exists The hold time requirement arises from the **physical reality of internal storage nodes** in flip-flops. Let's break it down into logical steps: 1. **Internal Capacitive Nodes:** Inside a flip-flop, there are **parasitic capacitances** at internal storage nodes (e.g., the master latch output node in a master-slave flip-flop). 2. **Active Edge Arrival:** When the clock's active edge arrives, the flip-flop's **pass transistor or transmission gate** opens, allowing the new data value to start propagating into the internal storage node. 3. **Charge Redistribution:** The internal node capacitance must be **charged or discharged** to match the new data value. This is NOT instantaneous — it takes a finite time governed by RC time constants. 4. **The Vulnerability Window:** If the data input changes (or glitches) **before this charging is complete**, the internal node may settle to an **intermediate or incorrect voltage**, causing **metastability** or a **logic error**. 5. **Therefore:** The data input must be held **constant for at least $t_{hold}$** after the clock edge, so the internal node has enough time to "lock in" the correct value. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hold time is a *physical* constraint of the flip-flop's internal transistor-level design** — it cannot be eliminated, only managed. ## 🛠️ The "Why" Behind Hold Time Violations A **Hold Time Violation** occurs when: $$t_{cq} + t_{comb,min} < t_{hold} + t_{skew}$$ In plain English: **The new data arrives at the destination flip-flop TOO QUICKLY** — so quickly that it begins to change the destination's data input *before* the destination's previous value has been safely captured. This is fundamentally different from a setup time violation (which is about data arriving TOO LATE). ## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $t_{hold}$ | **Hold Time** — min stable duration after clock edge | **ns** / **ps** | | 2 | $T_{hold\_slack} = t_{cq} + t_{cd} - t_{hold} - t_{skew}$ | **Hold Time Slack** (must be $\geq 0$) | ns / ps | | 3 | $T_{hold\_slack} = (t_{cq,FF1} + t_{cd,comb} + t_{skew}) - t_{hold,FF2}$ | Detailed slack expression for FF1 → Comb → FF2 path | ns / ps | | 4 | $t_{cq}$ | **Clock-to-Q Propagation Delay** | ns | | 5 | $t_{cd}$ | **Contamination Delay** (min combinational delay) | ns | | 6 | $t_{skew} = t_{c,q\_launch} - t_{c,q\_capture}$ | **Clock Skew** between launching and capturing FFs | ns / ps | | 7 | $t_{cd} + t_{cq} \geq t_{hold} + t_{skew}$ | **Hold Time Constraint** (must hold true) | — | | 8 | $t_{cq} + t_{cd} > t_{hold} + t_{skew}$ | **No Hold Violation** (strict inequality for safety) | — | | 9 | $T_{clk} \geq t_{cq} + t_{pd,max} + t_{su} + t_{skew}$ | **Setup Time Constraint** (for reference comparison) | ns | | 10 | $Buffer\_Delay \geq t_{hold} + t_{skew} - t_{cq} - t_{cd}$ | **Required buffer delay** to fix hold violation | ns | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Observation:** Notice that the hold time constraint (Eq. 7) does **NOT** contain the clock period $T_{clk}$. This means **reducing the clock frequency CANNOT fix a hold time violation** — only design-level changes (buffers, redesign, etc.) can. ## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility - **High-Speed Processor Design:** Hold time violations are a **first-order concern** in pipelined CPUs operating at multi-GHz clock rates, where a few picoseconds of slack can mean functional failure. - **ASIC/FPGA Timing Closure:** Modern EDA tools (Synopsys PrimeTime, Cadence Tempus) report hold time slack on every timing path — designers must close both setup AND hold violations. - **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS):** Hold time is a **key driver** for clock tree buffering and skew management. - **Interface Design (DDR, PCIe, SerDes):** Source-synchronous interfaces have strict hold time requirements to ensure reliable data capture. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🔬 Derivation 1: The Hold Time Constraint Equation ### 📋 Problem Setup Consider two flip-flops, **FF1** (launching) and **FF2** (capturing), separated by a combinational logic block, as shown in standard sequential circuit topology. **Given Parameters:** - $t_{cq,FF1}$: Clock-to-Q delay of launching flip-flop - $t_{cd,comb}$: Contamination delay (minimum delay) of combinational logic - $t_{hold,FF2}$: Hold time requirement of capturing flip-flop - $t_{skew}$: Clock skew between FF1 and FF2 (defined as $t_{skew} = t_{clk,FF2} - t_{clk,FF1}$) ### 🧮 Derivation — Line by Line We need the **new data at FF2's input** to arrive **no earlier than** $t_{hold}$ after FF2's clock edge. Let's compute when the new data actually arrives. **Step 1: Clock edge arrives at FF1 (the launching flip-flop) at time:** $$t_{arrival,FF1} = 0 \text{ (reference)}$$ This is our time reference — we define the clock edge at FF1 as occurring at $t = 0$. **Step 2: New data appears at FF1's Q output (best-case, contamination delay):** $$t_{data,FF1\_Q} = 0 + t_{cq,FF1} = t_{cq,FF1}$$ The clock-to-Q contamination delay is the *fastest* possible time the Q output can change after the clock edge. **Step 3: New data propagates through the combinational logic (minimum/shortest path):** $$t_{data,FF2\_D} = t_{cq,FF1} + t_{cd,comb}$$ Here we use $t_{cd,comb}$ (contamination delay = minimum delay) because we are looking at the **earliest** the data could possibly arrive at FF2's D input. **Step 4: Account for clock skew — when does FF2's clock edge actually arrive?** If FF2's clock arrives *later* than FF1's clock, this gives the data even more time to propagate (which helps with hold). If FF2's clock arrives *earlier*, data must wait less. $$t_{clk,FF2} = t_{skew} = t_{clk,FF2} - t_{clk,FF1}$$ **Step 5: The data must NOT arrive at FF2's D input until at least $t_{hold}$ AFTER FF2's clock edge.** This gives us the inequality: $$t_{data,FF2\_D} \geq t_{skew} + t_{hold,FF2}$$ Substituting the expression from Step 3: $$t_{cq,FF1} + t_{cd,comb} \geq t_{skew} + t_{hold,FF2}$$ **Step 6: Rearranging into the standard "Slack" form:** $$t_{cq,FF1} + t_{cd,comb} - t_{hold,FF2} - t_{skew} \geq 0$$ Therefore, the **Hold Time Slack** is defined as: $$\boxed{T_{hold\_slack} = t_{cq,FF1} + t_{cd,comb} - t_{hold,FF2} - t_{skew} \geq 0}$$ **Conclusion:** For the circuit to function correctly, this slack must be **non-negative**. A negative value indicates a hold time violation. --- ## 📐 Worked Numerical Example ### Problem Statement A digital circuit has the following parameters: - $t_{cq,FF1} = 0.2$ ns - $t_{cd,comb} = 0.1$ ns (minimum combinational delay) - $t_{hold,FF2} = 0.15$ ns - $t_{skew} = 0.05$ ns (FF2 clock arrives 50 ps after FF1 clock) **Question:** Is there a hold time violation? If yes, what is the required additional delay to fix it? ### Solution **Step 1: Apply the Hold Time Slack Formula** $$T_{hold\_slack} = t_{cq,FF1} + t_{cd,comb} - t_{hold,FF2} - t_{skew}$$ **Step 2: Substitute the numerical values** $$T_{hold\_slack} = 0.2 + 0.1 - 0.15 - 0.05$$ **Step 3: Perform the arithmetic** $$T_{hold\_slack} = 0.3 - 0.20 = 0.10 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 4: Interpret the result** Since $T_{hold\_slack} = +0.10$ ns $> 0$, **there is NO hold time violation**. The circuit has 100 ps of hold time margin, which is healthy. > [!NOTE] > **Negative Slack Example:** If $t_{cd,comb}$ were instead $0.02$ ns, then $T_{hold\_slack} = 0.2 + 0.02 - 0.15 - 0.05 = +0.02$ ns. Still positive, but only 20 ps — quite tight for 65nm CMOS! ### 🛠️ Analytical Matrix — How to Fix Hold Time Violations | # | Fixing Technique | Description | Trade-off | |---|------------------|-------------|-----------| | 1 | **Insert Delay Buffers** | Add buffer chain to lengthen $t_{cd,comb}$ | Increases area, power, and may affect setup time | | 2 | **Use Slower Standard Cells** | Replace fast cells with higher-drive, slower variants | Increases combinational delay uniformly | | 3 | **Clock Skew Engineering** | Deliberately skew clock to capture FF earlier | Affects setup time of neighboring paths | | 4 | **Hold-Time-Immune Flip-Flops** | Use FFs with negative or zero hold time | Not always available in cell library | | 5 | **Data Path Pipelining** | Restructure logic to add pipeline stage | Major design change | | 6 | **Dual-Polarity Clock** | Use opposite-edge clocking for adjacent stages | Complex timing analysis | > [!TIP] > **Industry Rule of Thumb:** Adding **2 inverters (back-to-back)** typically adds ~50–100 ps delay in 65nm CMOS, often sufficient to fix minor hold violations. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 📊 Diagram 1: Hold Time Timing Relationship (Block-Level Architecture) The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **temporal relationship** between clock edges, data transitions, and hold time requirements in a sequential circuit path from FF1 → Combinational Logic → FF2. ```mermaid graph TD T0["Time Reference: Clock Edge at FF1
t = 0 ps"] A["FF1 Active Clock Edge Arrives
FF1 begins updating Q output"] B["FF1 Q Output Settles
t = t_cq_FF1 = 200 ps
Contamination delay of FF1"] C["Combinational Logic Begins
Propagating New Data Value
Fastest path = contamination delay"] D["New Data Arrives at FF2 D Input
t = t_cq + t_cd_comb = 300 ps"] E["FF2 Active Clock Edge Arrives
t = t_skew = 50 ps
FF2 ready to capture data"] F["Hold Time Window Begins
FF2 requires data to stay stable
for t_hold = 150 ps"] G["Data Must NOT Change
Until t_skew + t_hold = 200 ps
after FF1 clock edge"] H["SAFE ZONE: Data held stable
t = 300 ps onwards
Hold slack = 100 ps OK"] T0 --> A A --> B B --> C C --> D T0 --> E E --> F F --> G D --> H G --> H ``` ## 📊 Diagram 2: Hold Time Violation Scenario ```mermaid graph LR P1["FF1 Clock Edge
t = 0"] P2["FF1 Q Changes Fast
t_cq = 100 ps"] P3["Comb Logic Very Fast
t_cd_comb = 30 ps"] P4["New Data at FF2 D
t = 130 ps
TOO EARLY"] P5["FF2 Clock Edge
t_skew = 0"] P6["FF2 Hold Time Required
t_hold = 150 ps
Window = 0 to 150 ps"] P7["HOLD VIOLATION
Data changed at 130 ps
Inside hold window
Slack = -20 ps"] P8["Solution: Insert Buffer
Add 50 ps delay
New arrival = 180 ps
Slack = +30 ps"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 P1 --> P5 --> P6 P4 --> P7 P6 --> P7 P7 --> P8 ``` ## 📊 Diagram 3: Setup vs. Hold Time Comparison ```mermaid graph TD CLK["Active Clock Edge"] SU["Setup Time Window
Data must be stable BEFORE edge
Duration: t_su"] HT["Hold Time Window
Data must be stable AFTER edge
Duration: t_hold"] EQ1["Setup Constraint:
T_clk >= t_cq + t_pd_max + t_su + t_skew"] EQ2["Hold Constraint:
t_cq + t_cd >= t_hold + t_skew
(T_clk INDEPENDENT)"] DIFF["Setup = Data stability BEFORE clock
Hold = Data stability AFTER clock"] CLK --> SU CLK --> HT SU --> EQ1 HT --> EQ2 SU --> DIFF HT --> DIFF ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define hold time in a flip-flop. Why is it important in sequential circuit design?** ### Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution): **Definition (2 marks):** Hold time ($t_{hold}$) is the minimum time duration for which the data input (D) of a flip-flop must remain stable **after the active edge of the clock signal**, so that the data is reliably latched into the flip-flop. **Importance (1 mark):** It is critical because violating hold time causes the flip-flop to enter a **metastable state** or capture incorrect data, leading to functional failure of the sequential circuit. Hold time violations cannot be fixed by reducing clock frequency — they require physical design changes. --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Differentiate between setup time and hold time.** ### Model Answer (3 Marks): | Aspect | Setup Time ($t_{su}$) | Hold Time ($t_{hold}$) | |--------|----------------------|------------------------| | **Definition** | Min time data must be stable **BEFORE** clock edge | Min time data must be stable **AFTER** clock edge | | **Clock period dependency** | Depends on $T_{clk}$ (fixable by reducing frequency) | **Independent of $T_{clk}$** (not fixable by slowing clock) | | **Typical magnitude** | 50–200 ps | 30–100 ps | | **Fix when violated** | Reduce clock frequency, add pipeline | Insert delay buffers, redesign combinational logic | --- ## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q(a): Derive the hold time constraint equation for a sequential circuit consisting of two flip-flops separated by combinational logic. Clearly define all terms. (7 Marks)** ### Model Answer (7 Marks): Consider flip-flops FF1 and FF2 connected through a combinational block. **Definitions (2 marks):** - $t_{cq,FF1}$: Clock-to-Q contamination delay of FF1 - $t_{cd,comb}$: Minimum (contamination) delay of combinational logic - $t_{hold,FF2}$: Hold time of FF2 - $t_{skew}$: Clock skew between FF2 and FF1 **Derivation (5 marks):** The earliest the new data can arrive at FF2's D input is: $$t_{arrival,D} = t_{cq,FF1} + t_{cd,comb}$$ The earliest FF2 expects valid data (after its own clock edge) is: $$t_{required} = t_{skew} + t_{hold,FF2}$$ For no hold violation: $$t_{arrival,D} \geq t_{required}$$ $$t_{cq,FF1} + t_{cd,comb} \geq t_{hold,FF2} + t_{skew}$$ **Hold Time Slack:** $$T_{hold\_slack} = t_{cq,FF1} + t_{cd,comb} - t_{hold,FF2} - t_{skew} \geq 0$$ --- **Q(b): A circuit has $t_{cq} = 0.3$ ns, $t_{cd,comb} = 0.1$ ns, $t_{hold} = 0.25$ ns, and clock skew of 0.1 ns. (i) Check for hold time violation. (ii) If violated, calculate the required buffer delay. (7 Marks)** ### Model Answer (7 Marks): **(i) Hold Slack Calculation (4 marks):** $$T_{hold\_slack} = t_{cq} + t_{cd,comb} - t_{hold} - t_{skew}$$ $$T_{hold\_slack} = 0.3 + 0.1 - 0.25 - 0.1 = 0.05 \text{ ns}$$ Since slack is **positive (50 ps)**, there is **NO hold time violation**. **(ii) Safety Margin Analysis (3 marks):** The circuit has only 50 ps of hold margin. For robust operation, designers typically target at least 100–150 ps of hold slack. To improve margin, one could add a buffer of ~50 ps delay, but it is not strictly required for functional correctness. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Q(a): Explain the concept of hold time violation. What are the consequences and how can they be fixed? (7 Marks)** ### Model Answer (7 Marks): **Concept (2 marks):** A hold time violation occurs when the data input of a flip-flop changes **before** the required hold time has elapsed after the clock edge. The new data "overwrites" the value before the flip-flop has fully captured the previous one. **Consequences (2 marks):** - Metastability (flip-flop output oscillates between 0 and 1) - Logic errors propagating through the circuit - Functional failure at high clock frequencies - Cannot be fixed by changing clock period **Fixing Methods (3 marks):** 1. Insert delay buffers in the data path 2. Use slower combinational cells (higher Vt, longer channels) 3. Deliberate clock skew engineering 4. Use hold-time-immune flip-flops 5. Restructure the pipeline (add a stage) --- **Q(b): A two-stage pipeline has the following parameters. Calculate the hold time slack and determine if the circuit will work reliably. $t_{cq,FF1} = 0.15$ ns, $t_{cd,comb} = 0.05$ ns, $t_{hold,FF2} = 0.1$ ns, $t_{skew} = 0.08$ ns. If a violation exists, suggest a fix. (7 Marks)** ### Model Answer (7 Marks): **Slack Calculation (4 marks):** $$T_{hold\_slack} = 0.15 + 0.05 - 0.1 - 0.08 = -0.18 + 0.05$$ $$T_{hold\_slack} = 0.02 \text{ ns} \text{ (wait, recalculate)}$$ $$T_{hold\_slack} = (0.15 + 0.05) - (0.1 + 0.08) = 0.20 - 0.18 = +0.02 \text{ ns}$$ **Interpretation (1 mark):** Slack is **+20 ps** — very tight but technically no violation. The circuit will work, but with minimal safety margin. **Recommended Fix (2 marks):** To improve robustness, insert **2 inverter buffers** (~60–80 ps delay in 65nm CMOS) to push the slack to ~80–100 ps. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Critical Warning for Students:** > 1. **Unit Confusion:** Hold time is in **ps or ns** — never mix units mid-calculation. Convert everything to the same unit first! > 2. **Skew Sign Error:** Remember that positive skew *helps* hold (data has more time). The formula is $t_{cq} + t_{cd} - t_{hold} - t_{skew}$. > 3. **Confusing Setup & Hold Constraints:** Hold time constraint does **NOT** contain $T_{clk}$ — do not mistakenly add the clock period. > 4. **Forgetting Contamination Delay:** Use the **minimum** combinational delay ($t_{cd}$), not the maximum propagation delay, when checking hold time. > 5. **Negative Slack Interpretation:** A negative hold slack means **functional failure**, not just timing failure — the chip will not work regardless of frequency. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Hold Time (KTU 2024 Module 1)** - ✅ **Definition:** Minimum time data must stay stable **AFTER** the active clock edge. - ✅ **Symbol:** $t_{hold}$, typically **30–100 ps** in modern CMOS. - ✅ **Hold Slack Equation:** $T_{hold\_slack} = t_{cq} + t_{cd,comb} - t_{hold} - t_{skew} \geq 0$ - ✅ **Key Difference from Setup Time:** Hold constraint is **independent of clock period** $T_{clk}$. - ✅ **Use Contamination Delay (min), not Propagation Delay (max)** when checking hold. - ✅ **Hold violations cannot be fixed by reducing clock frequency** — they require design changes. - ✅ **Common Fixes:** Delay buffers, slower cells, clock skew engineering, hold-immune FFs. - ✅ **Hold Time > 0 always** for a standard flip-flop; some advanced FFs have **negative or zero** hold time. - ✅ **Metastability** is the consequence of violating hold time — output becomes unpredictable. - ✅ **Clock skew helps hold time** (when capture FF clock is delayed) but **hurts setup time** — this is a fundamental trade-off. - ✅ **Industry Rule:** 2 back-to-back inverters ≈ 50–100 ps delay in 65nm CMOS — useful for quick hold fixes. - ✅ **EDA Tools:** Synopsys PrimeTime and Cadence Tempus report hold slack on every timing arc. - ✅ **Negative Hold Slack = Functional Failure** — chip does NOT work, even at low frequencies. --- > 🎯 **End of Module 1 — Hold Time Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PECST415 VLSI Design**

topics.title: Propagation Delay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Propagation Delay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — PROPAGATION DELAY IN VLSI DESIGN

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Propagation Delay ($t_p$)** is formally defined as the time interval between the **50% transition point of the input waveform** and the **50% transition point of the resulting output waveform**, measured when the signal travels from one logic node to the next through a combinational logic gate or an interconnect network.

In the strictest KTU/VLSI literature sense, propagation delay is the **average time taken by a digital signal to propagate through a logic gate from input to output**, and is mathematically defined as the arithmetic mean of the two directional propagation delays:

$$t_p = \frac{t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}}{2}$$

Where:
- **$t_{PLH}$** = Propagation delay for the **Low-to-High** output transition (when the gate output switches from logic 0 → 1).
- **$t_{PHL}$** = Propagation delay for the **High-to-Low** output transition (when the gate output switches from logic 1 → 0).

The standard measurement points are at the **50% voltage level** of $V_{DD}$ (i.e., $0.5 V_{DD}$) on both the rising and falling edges of the input and output waveforms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note (High Frequency):** Do NOT confuse *Propagation Delay* ($t_p$) with *Contamination Delay* ($t_c$). Contamination delay is the **minimum** time after an input change before the output begins to change. Propagation delay is the **typical (50%)** time, while contamination delay is the **earliest (10%)** time.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stadium Wave" Intuition

Imagine a fully packed cricket stadium at a KTU sports meet. When one person stands up and sits down (an **input transition**), the person next to them takes a tiny fraction of a second to react and stand/sit in sympathy (a **propagation event**). This reaction time is the **propagation delay**.

Now consider the chain reaction:
1. The first person stands up → the second person stands up → the third → and so on.
2. The time it takes for this "wave" to travel from one end of a row to the other is analogous to the cumulative propagation delay through a chain of logic gates.
3. The speed of this wave depends on:
   - **How quickly each person reacts** (analogous to the **transistor drive strength** or mobility).
   - **How heavy the seats are** that they must move (analogous to **load capacitance** $C_L$).
   - **The number of people in the row** (analogous to the **logical depth / fan-out**).

The more massive the load (heavier seats), the longer it takes for each person to complete the action → **higher delay**. This is precisely why CMOS delay is modeled as an **RC time constant** — charging a capacitor through a resistor takes time.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** Propagation delay in CMOS is fundamentally a problem of **charging and discharging a capacitor (load capacitance $C_L$) through a non-ideal resistor (the transistor channel in its ON state)**. The smaller the resistance and the smaller the capacitance, the faster the gate.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Units (KTU Mandated Vocabulary)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Propagation Delay | $t_p$ | **seconds (s)**, typically **ns** or **ps** | Average of $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$ |
| Low-to-High Delay | $t_{PLH}$ | **seconds (s)** | Time for output to rise 50% after input falls 50% |
| High-to-Low Delay | $t_{PHL}$ | **seconds (s)** | Time for output to fall 50% after input rises 50% |
| Contamination Delay | $t_c$ | **seconds (s)** | Minimum delay (10% point) before output starts changing |
| Rise Time | $t_r$ | **seconds (s)** | Time for output to rise from 10% to 90% of $V_{DD}$ |
| Fall Time | $t_f$ | **seconds (s)** | Time for output to fall from 90% to 10% of $V_{DD}$ |
| Load Capacitance | $C_L$ | **Farads (F)**, typically **fF** | Total capacitance the gate must drive |
| Equivalent Resistance | $R_{eq}$ | **Ohms ($\Omega$)** | ON-resistance of the CMOS transistor |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common mistake is to confuse the **rise time ($t_r$)** of a waveform with the **propagation delay ($t_p$)**. Rise time measures how *sharp* the edge is; propagation delay measures *when* the edge arrives relative to its trigger.

---

## 1.4 Where Propagation Delay Fits in the VLSI Design Flow

Propagation delay is the **first-order performance metric** in any synchronous digital design. It directly determines:

- **Maximum Clock Frequency ($f_{max}$):** $f_{max} \leq \dfrac{1}{T_{clk} - t_{setup} - t_{hold}}$, where the combinational logic delay must fit within the clock period minus flip-flop overhead.
- **Critical Path Delay:** The longest propagation delay path between two sequential elements (flip-flops) sets the lower bound on the clock period.
- **Throughput** of pipelined processors.
- **Power-Performance trade-off** (since dynamic power $\propto C V_{DD}^2 f$, and $f$ depends on delay).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The KTU VLSI Design syllabus treats propagation delay as the **gateway concept** that links electrical-level CMOS physics to system-level performance. Without a strong grasp of $t_p$, you cannot proceed to topics like Logical Effort, Timing Analysis, or Static Timing Analysis (STA) in later modules.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Physical Origin of Propagation Delay in a CMOS Inverter

To understand propagation delay at the deepest level required by the KTU 2024 syllabus, we must trace exactly **what happens physically** inside a single CMOS inverter when its input transitions.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Steady State (Input = $V_{DD}$):**
   - The PMOS transistor is **OFF** (cutoff region), and the NMOS transistor is **ON** (linear/triode region).
   - The output node is connected to **GND** through the ON-resistance of the NMOS ($R_n$).
   - Output node capacitance $C_L$ is **fully discharged** → $V_{out} = 0$.

2. **Input Begins to Fall (from $V_{DD}$ toward 0):**
   - When $V_{in}$ crosses the NMOS threshold $V_{Tn}$, the NMOS begins turning OFF.
   - Simultaneously, when $V_{in}$ drops below $V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert$, the PMOS begins turning ON.
   - The output node $C_L$ now has **two competing paths**: one charging through PMOS ($R_p$) to $V_{DD}$, one discharging through NMOS ($R_n$) to GND.

3. **Input Crosses $V_{DD}/2$ (50% point):**
   - At this instant, the timing reference for $t_{PLH}$ begins.
   - The PMOS is strongly ON, the NMOS is strongly OFF.
   - The output node begins **charging** toward $V_{DD}$ through $R_p$.

4. **Output Crosses $V_{DD}/2$:**
   - The instant $V_{out} = 0.5 V_{DD}$ is the **stopwatch endpoint** for $t_{PLH}$.

5. **The Elmore Delay Result:**
   - The time taken for $C_L$ to charge from 0 to $0.5 V_{DD}$ through a resistor $R_p$ is given by:
     $$t_{PLH} \approx 0.69 \, R_p \, C_L$$
   - This is the famous **single-pole RC approximation**, where the factor **0.69 = ln(2)** arises from the exponential charging profile.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 0.69?** When a capacitor charges through a resistor from 0 to $V_{DD}$, the voltage follows $V(t) = V_{DD}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$. Setting $V(t) = 0.5 V_{DD}$ gives $0.5 = 1 - e^{-t/RC}$, so $e^{-t/RC} = 0.5$, and $t = RC \ln(2) \approx 0.693 \, RC$.

---

## 2.2 RC Delay Model — The Foundation of All Digital Timing

The **RC delay model** treats every CMOS gate as a driving resistance $R_{driver}$ charging/discharging a load capacitance $C_L$. This is the conceptual model taught universally in CMOS VLSI textbooks (Weste, Rabaey, Kang) and forms the **core of KTU Module 1**.

### Key Assumptions of the RC Delay Model

- The transistor acts as a **switch** with finite ON-resistance.
- The ON-resistance is **constant** (lumped-element approximation, ignoring the non-linear $I_d$ vs. $V_{ds}$ curve).
- The load is purely **capacitive** (wires and gate inputs draw no DC current in CMOS).
- The **delay = time for the output to swing through the "logic threshold region"** (the 50% point).

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU student must memorize for Propagation Delay problems in PECST415 Module 1.

| # | Formula | Description / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_p = \dfrac{t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}}{2}$ | Average propagation delay (KTU definition) | s |
| 2 | $t_{PLH} = 0.69 \, R_p \, C_L$ | Low-to-High delay (PMOS charges $C_L$) | s |
| 3 | $t_{PHL} = 0.69 \, R_n \, C_L$ | High-to-Low delay (NMOS discharges $C_L$) | s |
| 4 | $t_p = 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, C_L$ | Simplified single-R approximation (when $R_p \approx R_n$) | s |
| 5 | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$ | NMOS ON-resistance (deep triode region) | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | $R_p = \dfrac{1}{k_p' \dfrac{W_p}{L_p} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)}$ | PMOS ON-resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | $t_p = 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, (C_{int} + C_{ext})$ | Total load = internal parasitic + external load | s |
| 8 | $C_L = C_{gate,N} + C_{diff,N} + C_{wire}$ | Total load capacitance (fan-out + interconnect) | F |
| 9 | $t_{p,chain} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} 0.69 \, R_i \, C_i$ | Delay of a chain of $N$ gates (RC ladder) | s |
| 10 | $t_{p,Elmore} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{i \to source} \, C_i$ | **Elmore delay** for an RC tree | s |
| 11 | $R_{n} \approx \dfrac{1}{k_n' \dfrac{W_n}{L_n} (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$ | Channel resistance inversely proportional to aspect ratio | $\Omega$ |
| 12 | $t_p \propto \dfrac{L^2}{\mu \, (V_{DD} - V_T)}$ | Delay scaling with technology (length $L$, mobility $\mu$) | s |
| 13 | $t_p \propto \dfrac{C_L \, V_{DD}}{I_{on}}$ | Current-driven delay model (used in logical effort) | s |
| 14 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \, t_p}$ | Maximum toggle frequency for a single gate | Hz |
| 15 | $PDP = C_L \, V_{DD}^2$ | Power-Delay Product (energy per switching event) | J |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** The factor **0.69** comes from $\ln(2)$. Some problems state the delay as $RC$ directly (using a "1 RC" approximation). Always **re-read the problem statement** to confirm whether they use 0.69 RC or 1.0 RC. KTU typically uses 0.69.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Significance in Real-World VLSI

Propagation delay is not a textbook abstraction — it directly governs:

| Real-World System | Role of Propagation Delay |
|---|---|
| **Microprocessors (Intel, AMD, ARM)** | Critical path delay dictates the **clock period** of every CPU core (3 GHz CPU ⇒ $t_{clk} \approx 0.33$ ns, so $t_p$ of the slowest logic path must be $\ll$ 0.33 ns). |
| **Mobile SoCs (Snapdragon, Apple M-series)** | Lower delay allows **higher performance** at the same power budget — crucial for battery life. |
| **FPGA / ASIC Designs** | Static Timing Analysis (STA) tools report **$t_p$** for every path; if any $t_p$ exceeds the timing budget, the design fails to meet closure. |
| **High-Speed SerDes (PCIe, USB, DDR)** | Inter-symbol interference is governed by the **propagation delay of interconnect**, not just gates. |
| **AI Accelerators (NVIDIA GPUs, TPUs)** | Massive matrix multiplication arrays are **pipelined** precisely to hide propagation delay across millions of multipliers. |

> [!TIP]
> **Real Engineering Trade-off:** In modern sub-7nm CMOS, propagation delay cannot be reduced indefinitely — it is bounded by **carrier saturation velocity** and **quantum tunneling** through thin gate oxides. This is why industry moved to **FinFET** and **GAA** (Gate-All-Around) transistors around 2018-2022.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — The Single-Pole RC Delay ($t_p = 0.69 \, R C_L$)

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 1. Every problem on propagation delay builds on this result.

### Setup

Consider a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$. When the input switches, the equivalent circuit reduces to a resistor $R_{eq}$ (the ON-transistor) charging or discharging $C_L$.

Let us consider the **discharging case** (NMOS ON, PMOS OFF, output falling from $V_{DD}$ to 0). The KVL/KCL equation at the output node is:

$$C_L \, \frac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt} + \frac{V_{out}(t)}{R_{eq}} = 0$$

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Initial Condition**

At $t = 0$ (the moment the input crosses 50%, by our delay definition), the output is at $V_{DD}$:

$$V_{out}(0) = V_{DD}$$

**Step 2 — Separate Variables**

$$\frac{dV_{out}}{V_{out}} = -\frac{dt}{R_{eq} \, C_L}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Both Sides**

Integrating from $t = 0$ to $t = t$ and $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ to $V_{out} = V_{out}(t)$:

$$\int_{V_{DD}}^{V_{out}(t)} \frac{dV'}{V'} = -\frac{1}{R_{eq} C_L} \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integrals**

$$\ln\left(\frac{V_{out}(t)}{V_{DD}}\right) = -\frac{t}{R_{eq} C_L}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{out}(t)$**

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \, e^{-t / (R_{eq} C_L)}$$

This is the classic **exponential discharge** curve of an RC circuit.

**Step 6 — Apply the 50% Measurement Criterion**

By the KTU definition of propagation delay, we want the time $t_{PHL}$ at which $V_{out}(t_{PHL}) = 0.5 \, V_{DD}$:

$$0.5 \, V_{DD} = V_{DD} \, e^{-t_{PHL} / (R_{eq} C_L)}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify**

$$0.5 = e^{-t_{PHL} / (R_{eq} C_L)}$$

**Step 8 — Take the Natural Logarithm of Both Sides**

$$\ln(0.5) = -\frac{t_{PHL}}{R_{eq} C_L}$$

**Step 9 — Recognize $\ln(0.5) = -\ln(2) = -0.693$**

$$-0.693 = -\frac{t_{PHL}}{R_{eq} C_L}$$

**Step 10 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{t_{PHL} = 0.693 \, R_{eq} \, C_L \approx 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, C_L}$$

By symmetry, the same derivation for the rising case (PMOS charging $C_L$ through $R_p$) yields:

$$\boxed{t_{PLH} = 0.693 \, R_p \, C_L \approx 0.69 \, R_p \, C_L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The "0.69" factor is **dimensionless** and arises purely from the **logarithm of 2**, which in turn arises from the choice to measure delay at the 50% (mid-point) of the voltage swing. Had we chosen the 63% point (one time constant $\tau = RC$), we would have $t = RC$ exactly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — ON-Resistance of a MOSFET in the Triode Region

The propagation delay formula requires the transistor's ON-resistance. We derive this from the long-channel MOSFET I-V equation.

### Starting Point — Triode Region Current Equation

For a NMOS transistor in the **triode (linear) region** with $V_{DS} \ll V_{GS} - V_T$:

$$I_D = \mu_n \, C_{ox} \, \frac{W}{L} \, \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{Tn}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation of $R_{ON}$

**Step 1 — Apply the Deep-Triode Approximation**

When $V_{DS}$ is very small (the transistor behaves as a resistor), the quadratic term is negligible:

$$I_D \approx \mu_n \, C_{ox} \, \frac{W}{L} \, (V_{GS} - V_{Tn}) \, V_{DS}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the Linear I-V Relationship**

In this regime, the transistor acts as a **voltage-controlled resistor** where $I_D$ is linearly proportional to $V_{DS}$. By Ohm's law:

$$R_{ON} = \frac{V_{DS}}{I_D}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify**

$$R_{ON} = \frac{V_{DS}}{\mu_n \, C_{ox} \, \frac{W}{L} \, (V_{GS} - V_{Tn}) \, V_{DS}}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $V_{DS}$**

$$\boxed{R_{ON} = \frac{1}{\mu_n \, C_{ox} \, \dfrac{W}{L} \, (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})}}$$

**Step 5 — Express Using Process Transconductance $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$**

$$\boxed{R_{ON} = \frac{1}{k_n' \, \dfrac{W}{L} \, (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})}}$$

**Step 6 — For the CMOS Inverter at $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$**

When the input is at $V_{DD}$ (driving the NMOS ON) or at GND (driving the PMOS ON):

$$\boxed{R_n = \frac{1}{k_n' \, \dfrac{W_n}{L_n} \, (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})} \quad \text{and} \quad R_p = \frac{1}{k_p' \, \dfrac{W_p}{L_p} \, (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Insight:** To make a gate **faster**, we can:
> 1. **Increase $W/L$** (wider transistor → lower resistance → lower delay). This is the foundation of **transistor sizing**.
> 2. **Increase $V_{DD}$** (higher overdrive → lower resistance). But this increases power quadratically.
> 3. **Reduce $V_T$** (lower threshold → lower resistance). But this increases **leakage current** exponentially.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Elmore Delay for an RC Ladder (Multi-Stage Chain)

When a single gate drives multiple other gates (fan-out) through wires, the circuit becomes an **RC tree**, not a simple RC lumped element. The standard analytical tool is the **Elmore Delay Formula**.

### Setup: N Identical Inverters in Cascade

Each inverter has:
- Output resistance $R$
- Output (intrinsic) capacitance $C_{int}$
- Load capacitance $C_L$ at the far end

For a chain of $N$ such stages, the **Elmore delay** from the first input to the output of the $N$-th stage is:

$$t_{p,Elmore} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{i \to source} \, C_i$$

Where $R_{i \to source}$ is the **total resistance from the source (input) to node $i$** along the unique path in the RC tree.

### Derivation Walk-Through for a 3-Stage Chain

Consider 3 identical inverters driving $C_L$, with intrinsic output capacitance $C$ at each intermediate node.

**Step 1 — Identify Each Capacitor's Path Resistance**

| Capacitor | Resistance "seen" from the input source |
|---|---|
| $C$ at node 1 (after inverter 1) | $R$ (the resistance of inverter 1) |
| $C$ at node 2 (after inverter 2) | $R + R = 2R$ (through inverter 1 + 2) |
| $C$ at node 3 (after inverter 3) | $R + R + R = 3R$ |
| $C_L$ at output | $3R$ |

**Step 2 — Apply the Elmore Sum**

$$t_{p,Elmore} = R \cdot C + 2R \cdot C + 3R \cdot C + 3R \cdot C_L$$

**Step 3 — Group and Simplify**

$$t_{p,Elmore} = R C (1 + 2 + 3) + 3 R C_L = 6 R C + 3 R C_L$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to N Stages**

For an $N$-stage chain:

$$\boxed{t_{p,Elmore} = N(N+1)/2 \cdot R \, C_{int} + N \cdot R \, C_L}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quadratic Growth of Delay:** Notice the $\frac{N(N+1)}{2}$ factor — this is **quadratic** in $N$! This is the famous result that long chains of low-drive gates are **very slow**. To break this, we use **buffer insertion** (tapering) with progressively wider inverters.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem Statement:** A CMOS inverter in a 65nm process has the following parameters:
> - $W_n/L_n = 2$, $W_p/L_p = 4$
> - $k_n' = 250 \, \mu A/V^2$, $k_p' = 100 \, \mu A/V^2$
> - $V_{DD} = 1.2 \, V$, $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert = 0.4 \, V$
> - Load capacitance $C_L = 50 \, fF$
>
> **Calculate:** (a) $t_{PLH}$, (b) $t_{PHL}$, (c) Average propagation delay $t_p$.

### Solution

**Part (a) — $R_p$ and $t_{PLH}$:**

$$R_p = \frac{1}{k_p' \cdot (W_p/L_p) \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)}$$

$$R_p = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4 \cdot (1.2 - 0.4)} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4 \cdot 0.8}$$

$$R_p = \frac{1}{3.2 \times 10^{-4}} = 3125 \, \Omega = 3.125 \, k\Omega$$

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 3125 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} = 107.8 \, ps$$

**Part (b) — $R_n$ and $t_{PHL}$:**

$$R_n = \frac{1}{k_n' \cdot (W_n/L_n) \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$$

$$R_n = \frac{1}{250 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 2 \cdot 0.8} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 2500 \, \Omega = 2.5 \, k\Omega$$

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 2500 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} = 86.25 \, ps$$

**Part (c) — Average Delay:**

$$t_p = \frac{t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}}{2} = \frac{107.8 + 86.25}{2} = 97.025 \, ps \approx 97 \, ps$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A 65nm inverter with a 50fF load should have a delay on the order of ~100 ps. Our answer of **97 ps** is consistent with industry data sheets, confirming the correctness of the calculation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Propagation Delay Measurement Methodology

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level measurement architecture** used in industry and lab characterization of CMOS gate propagation delay. It maps out the signal flow from the pulse generator through the Device Under Test (DUT) to the timing capture.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["PULSE GENERATOR<br/>50 percent TRIGGER"] --> B["INPUT BUFFER<br/>SHARP EDGE SHAPING"]
    B --> C["CMOS GATE<br/>DUT<br/>DEVICE UNDER TEST"]
    C --> D["OUTPUT PROBE<br/>HIGH Z LOAD"]
    D --> E["OSCILLOSCOPE<br/>CH1 INPUT 50 percent"]
    D --> F["OSCILLOSCOPE<br/>CH2 OUTPUT 50 percent"]
    E --> G["TIMING UNIT<br/>DELTA TIME COMPUTATION"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["DELAY VALUE<br/>TP DISPLAY IN NS OR PS"]
    C --> I["LOAD CAPACITANCE<br/>CL VARIABLE 1 TO 500 FF"]
    J["SUPPLY VDD<br/>0.6 TO 1.2 VOLT"] --> C
    K["TEMPERATURE CHAMBER<br/>NEG 40 TO 125 C"] --> C
```

### Diagram Element Description

| Node | Function |
|---|---|
| **PULSE GENERATOR** | Produces a clean square wave with sharp edges; defines the 50% input reference point. |
| **INPUT BUFFER** | Provides a known input edge slope independent of the source. |
| **CMOS GATE DUT** | The Device Under Test — typically an inverter, NAND, or NOR gate fabricated on silicon. |
| **OUTPUT PROBE** | A high-impedance active probe (typically $> 10 M\Omega$, $< 0.1 pF$) that does not load the output. |
| **OSCILLOSCOPE CH1/CH2** | Captures the input and output waveforms simultaneously with sub-picosecond timing resolution. |
| **TIMING UNIT** | Computes the time difference between the two 50% crossings. |
| **LOAD CAPACITANCE** | A programmable capacitor bank added to simulate real fan-out loading. |
| **SUPPLY VDD** | Variable DC supply to characterize delay vs. voltage scaling. |
| **TEMPERATURE CHAMBER** | Tests delay variation across the industrial temperature range. |

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow — CMOS Inverter Delay Generation

This diagram shows the **internal functional architecture** of how a CMOS inverter generates its propagation delay — the cause-and-effect chain from input transition to output response.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["VIN TRANSITION<br/>50 PERCENT POINT"] --> B["NMOS THRESHOLD<br/>CROSSING DETECTED"]
    A --> C["PMOS THRESHOLD<br/>CROSSING DETECTED"]
    B --> D["NMOS CHANNEL<br/>RESISTANCE RN"]
    C --> E["PMOS CHANNEL<br/>RESISTANCE RP"]
    D --> F["OUTPUT NODE<br/>CAPACITANCE CL"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["DISCHARGE PATH<br/>GND THROUGH RN"]
    F --> H["CHARGE PATH<br/>VDD THROUGH RP"]
    G --> I["VOUT FALLS<br/>EXPONENTIAL DECAY"]
    H --> J["VOUT RISES<br/>EXPONENTIAL RISE"]
    I --> K["50 PERCENT OUTPUT<br/>POINT CAPTURED"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["DELAY TP MEASURED<br/>TPHL OR TPLH"]
```

---

## 4.3 RC Delay Equivalent Circuit (Block Representation)

Since physical transistor cross-sections are difficult to render in Mermaid, we represent the **equivalent RC delay model** as a block diagram.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["VIN<br/>IDEAL STEP INPUT"] --> B["RESISTANCE REQ<br/>TRANSISTOR ON STATE"]
    B --> C["NODE OUT<br/>CAPACITANCE CL TO GND"]
    C --> D["VOUT<br/>EXPONENTIAL RESPONSE"]
    E["SWITCH POSITION 1<br/>VIN LOW TPLH"] --> B
    F["SWITCH POSITION 2<br/>VIN HIGH TPHL"] --> B
```

---

## 4.4 Propagation Delay vs. Fan-Out (Conceptual Diagram)

The following diagram illustrates the **linear relationship** between fan-out (number of identical loads) and propagation delay — a fundamental concept introduced by **Fan-Out-of-4 (FO4)** delay metrics in industry.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["DRIVER GATE<br/>WITH OUTPUT RESISTANCE R"] --> B["WIRE CAPACITANCE<br/>CW"]
    B --> C["GATE 1<br/>LOAD C"]
    C --> D["GATE 2<br/>LOAD C"]
    D --> E["GATE 3<br/>LOAD C"]
    E --> F["GATE 4<br/>LOAD C"]
    F --> G["TP LINEAR IN N<br/>TP APPROX EQUAL TO 0.69 R N C"]
    A --> H["FO1 DELAY<br/>SINGLE LOAD"]
    A --> I["FO4 DELAY<br/>INDUSTRY STANDARD"]
    A --> J["FO8 DELAY<br/>HEAVY LOAD"]
    H --> K["DELAY AXIS"]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Modern processor datasheets frequently quote delays in **FO4 inverter delays** — the delay of an inverter driving 4 identical copies. For a 45nm process, 1 FO4 ≈ 25 ps. The KTU 2024 syllabus uses this as a normalization technique in logical-effort problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define propagation delay. Differentiate between $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Propagation delay ($t_p$) is defined as the time interval between the **50% transition point of the input** and the **50% transition point of the output** of a logic gate. It is computed as the average of the two directional delays:

$$t_p = \frac{t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}}{2}$$

| Parameter | $t_{PLH}$ | $t_{PHL}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Propagation Delay, Low-to-High | Propagation Delay, High-to-Low |
| **Transition** | Output rising 0 → 1 | Output falling 1 → 0 |
| **Active Transistor** | PMOS charges $C_L$ through $R_p$ | NMOS discharges $C_L$ through $R_n$ |
| **Formula** | $0.69 \, R_p \, C_L$ | $0.69 \, R_n \, C_L$ |
| **Triggering Input** | Input falling from 1 → 0 | Input rising from 0 → 1 |

---

### Question 2: List any four factors affecting propagation delay in CMOS circuits. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The four primary factors affecting propagation delay in CMOS circuits are:

1. **Load Capacitance ($C_L$):** Larger load capacitance directly increases delay since more charge must be transferred ($t_p \propto C_L$).

2. **Transistor Sizing ($W/L$ ratio):** Wider transistors have lower ON-resistance, reducing delay. However, this also increases input capacitance and area.

3. **Supply Voltage ($V_{DD}$):** Higher $V_{DD}$ increases the overdrive voltage $(V_{DD} - V_T)$, reducing ON-resistance and delay. Trade-off: increased dynamic power.

4. **Threshold Voltage ($V_T$):** Lower $V_T$ reduces ON-resistance and delay but exponentially increases subthreshold leakage current.

5. *(Bonus, optional)* **Temperature:** Higher temperature reduces carrier mobility, increasing delay.

6. *(Bonus, optional)* **Input Slew Rate:** Slower input edges increase delay due to the reduced time the transistor spends in strong inversion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Below, we present **Question A** and **Question B** as alternative choices.

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for propagation delay of a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$. Clearly state the assumptions of the RC delay model.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CMOS inverter is fabricated in a 180nm process with parameters: $W_n/L_n = 1.5$, $W_p/L_p = 4.5$, $k_n' = 300 \, \mu A/V^2$, $k_p' = 70 \, \mu A/V^2$, $V_{DD} = 1.8 \, V$, $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert = 0.5 \, V$. The load capacitance is $C_L = 100 \, fF$. Calculate $t_{PLH}$, $t_{PHL}$, and the average propagation delay $t_p$.

---

### ✅ Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Model Setup (1 mark)**

When a CMOS inverter transitions, we replace the transistor network with an equivalent Thevenin resistor $R_{eq}$ charging/discharging the lumped load capacitance $C_L$. The input is treated as an ideal step voltage source.

**Step 2 — Assumptions of the RC Delay Model (2 marks)**

1. The transistor acts as a **switch** with constant ON-resistance (linear region approximation, ignoring $V_{DS}^2$ term).
2. The load is purely **capacitive** (no resistive path to ground).
3. The delay is measured at the **50% point** of the output voltage swing.
4. The input is an **ideal step** (zero rise/fall time).
5. **Single-pole** system — only one dominant time constant.

**Step 3 — Differential Equation (1 mark)**

Applying KCL at the output node during discharge (NMOS active):

$$C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_{eq}} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solution by Separation of Variables (1 mark)**

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \, e^{-t / (R_{eq} C_L)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply 50% Criterion (1 mark)**

Setting $V_{out}(t_p) = 0.5 V_{DD}$ and solving:

$$t_p = R_{eq} C_L \ln(2) \approx 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, C_L$$

**Step 6 — Final Expression (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{t_{PLH} = 0.69 \, R_p \, C_L \quad \text{and} \quad t_{PHL} = 0.69 \, R_n \, C_L}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Calculate $R_p$ (2 marks)**

$$R_p = \frac{1}{k_p' \cdot (W_p/L_p) \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)} = \frac{1}{70 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4.5 \cdot (1.8 - 0.5)}$$

$$R_p = \frac{1}{70 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4.5 \cdot 1.3} = \frac{1}{4.095 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2442 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $R_n$ (2 marks)**

$$R_n = \frac{1}{k_n' \cdot (W_n/L_n) \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})} = \frac{1}{300 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.5 \cdot (1.8 - 0.5)}$$

$$R_n = \frac{1}{300 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.5 \cdot 1.3} = \frac{1}{5.85 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1709 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $t_{PLH}$ (1 mark)**

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 2442 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} = 168.5 \, ps$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $t_{PHL}$ (1 mark)**

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 1709 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} = 117.9 \, ps$$

**Step 5 — Average Delay (1 mark)**

$$t_p = \frac{168.5 + 117.9}{2} = 143.2 \, ps$$

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Elmore delay** for an RC ladder network. Derive the expression for the propagation delay of a 4-stage chain of identical CMOS inverters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CMOS NAND2 gate drives a load capacitance of 200 fF. The NMOS transistors have $W/L = 2$ each, the PMOS have $W/L = 4$ each, and $k_n' = 200 \, \mu A/V^2$, $k_p' = 80 \, \mu A/V^2$, $V_{DD} = 1.0 \, V$, $V_T = 0.3 \, V$. Estimate the worst-case propagation delay.

---

### ✅ Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Elmore Delay Derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Elmore Delay Concept (2 marks)**

Elmore delay is a closed-form approximation for the **50% propagation delay** of an RC tree network. It states:

$$t_{p,Elmore} = \sum_{i} R_{i \to source} \cdot C_i$$

where $R_{i \to source}$ is the **path resistance** from the input source to each capacitor node $C_i$, computed along the unique path in the tree.

**Step 2 — 4-Stage Inverter Chain Setup (1 mark)**

Consider 4 inverters in cascade, each with output resistance $R$ and intrinsic capacitance $C$ at each intermediate node. The final load is $C_L$.

**Step 3 — Path Resistance Calculation (2 marks)**

| Node | Capacitor | Path Resistance from Source |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C$ | $R$ |
| 2 | $C$ | $2R$ |
| 3 | $C$ | $3R$ |
| 4 | $C_L$ | $4R$ |

**Step 4 — Apply Elmore Formula (1 mark)**

$$t_{p,Elmore} = R \cdot C + 2R \cdot C + 3R \cdot C + 4R \cdot C_L = 6RC + 4R C_L$$

**Step 5 — Generalization (1 mark)**

For $N$ stages:

$$\boxed{t_{p,Elmore} = \frac{N(N+1)}{2} R C + N R C_L}$$

This shows **quadratic growth** of delay with chain length — the motivation for **buffer insertion (tapering)**.

---

#### Part (b) — NAND2 Worst-Case Delay [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify Worst Case (1 mark)**

In a 2-input NAND, the worst-case $t_{PHL}$ occurs when **both NMOS** are ON (parallel, equivalent resistance = $R_n/2$). The worst-case $t_{PLH}$ occurs when **only one PMOS** is ON (series through two PMOS — but in NAND2 they are in parallel; need to recheck topology).

**Correction:** In CMOS NAND2:
- NMOS are in **series** → worst-case $t_{PHL}$ uses $2 R_n$.
- PMOS are in **parallel** → worst-case $t_{PLH}$ uses $R_p$.

**Step 2 — Worst-Case Resistance for $t_{PHL}$ (2 marks)**

$$R_n = \frac{1}{k_n' \cdot (W_n/L_n) \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})} = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 2 \cdot (1.0 - 0.3)} = \frac{1}{2.8 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3571 \, \Omega$$

For series NMOS in worst case: $R_{n,series} = 2 R_n = 7143 \, \Omega$.

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Resistance for $t_{PLH}$ (1 mark)**

$$R_p = \frac{1}{80 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4 \cdot 0.7} = \frac{1}{2.24 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 4464 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $t_{PLH}$ (1 mark)**

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot 4464 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-15} = 615.9 \, ps$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $t_{PHL}$ (1 mark)**

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot 7143 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-15} = 985.7 \, ps$$

**Step 6 — Worst-Case Average Delay (1 mark)**

$$t_{p,worst} = \frac{615.9 + 985.7}{2} = 800.8 \, ps \approx 801 \, ps$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.69 factor:** Many students write $t_p = R C$ directly. KTU requires the **ln(2) ≈ 0.69** factor unless explicitly told otherwise. **Penalty: 1 mark lost.**
> 2. **Using wrong $W/L$ ratio:** In NAND2/NOR2, the **series/parallel** topology drastically changes effective resistance. Students often use a single $R_n$ or $R_p$ incorrectly. **Penalty: 2 marks lost.**
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing $fF$ with $pF$, or $\mu A/V^2$ with $mA/V^2$. Always convert to base SI units (F, A/V², V). **Penalty: 1 mark lost.**
> 4. **Not specifying the measurement criterion:** The KTU answer MUST mention the **50% measurement point** of the waveform. **Penalty: 1 mark lost.**
> 5. **Confusing propagation delay with rise time:** $t_r$ and $t_p$ are different metrics. **Penalty: 2 marks lost.**

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your study wall!)**

- ✅ **Propagation delay** = time between 50% input and 50% output transitions, measured at $0.5 V_{DD}$.
- ✅ **Two directional delays:** $t_{PLH}$ (low→high, PMOS charges) and $t_{PHL}$ (high→low, NMOS discharges).
- ✅ **Average delay formula:** $t_p = (t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}) / 2$.
- ✅ **Single-pole RC result:** $t_p = 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, C_L$. The "0.69" = $\ln(2)$.
- ✅ **MOSFET ON-resistance:** $R_{ON} = 1 / [k' \cdot (W/L) \cdot (V_{GS} - V_T)]$.
- ✅ **NMOS vs PMOS:** NMOS has higher mobility → lower $R_n$ → faster $t_{PHL}$. PMOS is ~2-3× slower.
- ✅ **To reduce delay:**
  - Increase $W/L$ (wider transistor) — at the cost of area and input capacitance.
  - Increase $V_{DD}$ — at the cost of dynamic power ($P \propto V_{DD}^2$).
  - Decrease $V_T$ — at the cost of leakage current.
  - Insert buffers (tapering) to break long RC chains.
- ✅ **Elmore delay** generalizes single-pole RC to RC trees: $t_{Elmore} = \sum R_i C_i$.
- ✅ **Long chain delay grows quadratically** with the number of stages (without buffering).
- ✅ **Worst-case delay** in multi-input gates (NAND/NOR) occurs when transistors are **stacked in series**.
- ✅ **Industry metric:** Propagation delay is normalized as **FO4 (Fan-Out-of-4) inverter delay** — typically 20-30 ps per FO4 in modern nanometer CMOS.
- ✅ **Trade-off triangle:** Delay ↔ Power ↔ Area — every optimization moves at least one vertex.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 favourite:** Numerical problems with CMOS inverter using given $W/L$, $k'$, $V_{DD}$, $V_T$, $C_L$. Practice at least 5 such problems.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 — Propagation Delay Notes**
