$$S = \frac{N \cdot T_{clk, np}}{(K + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}} = \frac{2000 \cdot 22}{2004 \cdot 6.8} = \frac{44000}{13627.2} \approx 3.23$$

**Result:** The pipeline achieves a speedup of **3.23×** over the non-pipelined design. Note that the speedup is below the ideal $K = 5$ because of the unbalanced stage delays (Stage 2 dominates at 6 ns).

*(7 marks — 1 for $\tau_{max}$, 1 for $T_{clk}$, 1 for $\eta$, 2 for $T_{total}$, 2 for speedup.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a timing diagram, explain the *fill*, *steady-state*, and *drain* phases of a 3-stage pipeline processing 5 data samples. Compute the total number of clock cycles required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **speedup** of a $K$-stage pipeline over its non-pipelined counterpart, and show that the speedup approaches $K$ as the number of samples $N \to \infty$. Assume balanced stage delays of $\tau$ and negligible register overhead. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Timing diagram representation** (using a textual table since the diagram is part of the answer):

| Clock Cycle | Stage 1 | Stage 2 | Stage 3 | Output Register |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample 1 | — | — | — |
| 2 | Sample 2 | Sample 1 | — | — |
| 3 | Sample 3 | Sample 2 | Sample 1 | — |
| 4 | Sample 4 | Sample 3 | Sample 2 | Sample 1 |
| 5 | Sample 5 | Sample 4 | Sample 3 | Sample 2 |
| 6 | — | Sample 5 | Sample 4 | Sample 3 |
| 7 | — | — | Sample 5 | Sample 4 |
| 8 | — | — | — | Sample 5 |

**Phase identification:**

- **Fill phase (Cycles 1–3):** The pipeline is being filled. In Cycle 1, only Stage 1 has data. By Cycle 3, all three stages are occupied.
- **Steady-state phase (Cycles 4–5):** A new sample enters and a finished sample leaves every cycle.
- **Drain phase (Cycles 6–8):** The input stream is exhausted, but Samples 4 and 5 must traverse the remaining stages. By Cycle 8, the last output emerges.

**Total clock cycles** = $K + N - 1 = 3 + 5 - 1 = 7$ cycles. (Plus one extra cycle for the final output to settle if counted from the last *input* edge — 7 cycles is the standard KTU convention.)

*(7 marks — 3 for timing diagram, 2 for phase identification, 2 for total cycle count.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Non-pipelined execution time for $N$ samples:**
A non-pipelined design has a single combinational block with total delay $K \cdot \tau$. The time per sample is $T_{np} = K \cdot \tau$, and the total time for $N$ samples is:

$$T_{non-pipe} = N \cdot K \cdot \tau$$

**Step 2 — Pipelined execution time for $N$ samples:**
In the pipelined design, the clock period is $T_{clk} = \tau$ (negligible register overhead, balanced stages). The total time is:

$$T_{pipe} = (K + N - 1) \cdot \tau$$

**Step 3 — Form the speedup ratio:**

$$S = \frac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{N \cdot K \cdot \tau}{(K + N - 1) \cdot \tau}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$S = \frac{N \cdot K}{K + N - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$:**

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N \cdot K}{K + N - 1} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{K}{1 + (K-1)/N} = \frac{K}{1 + 0} = K$$

**Conclusion:** The speedup of a $K$-stage pipeline approaches $K$ as the input stream length $N$ grows large, confirming the ideal theoretical maximum.

*(7 marks — 2 for $T_{non-pipe}$, 2 for $T_{pipe}$, 2 for limit evaluation, 1 for final conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting register overhead:** Many students compute $T_{clk} = \tau_{max}$ and miss the $t_{cq} + t_{su}$ terms. This typically costs **1–2 marks** per question.
> 2. **Confusing throughput with latency:** Throughput is *samples/second*; latency is *seconds/sample*. Marking scripts that interchange them lose **1 mark** instantly.
> 3. **Off-by-one in total time:** The correct formula is $(K + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$, *not* $(K + N) \cdot T_{clk}$. The $-1$ is the most common arithmetic error.
> 4. **Ignoring unbalanced stages:** When asked for speedup, students often quote the ideal $K$ without checking whether stages are balanced. If $\tau_{max} < \sum \tau_i / K$, the realistic speedup is **strictly less than $K$**.
> 5. **No diagram for 7-mark questions:** A neat, labelled block diagram is mandatory for full marks on architectural questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pipelining** = temporal parallelism via stage decomposition + clocked registers.
- **Clock period** = $\tau_{max} + t_{cq} + t_{su}$ (slowest stage dominates).
- **Steady-state throughput** = $1 / T_{clk}$ samples/second.
- **Latency** = $K \cdot T_{clk}$ seconds (time for one sample to traverse the pipeline).
- **Total time for $N$ samples** = $(K + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$.
- **Ideal speedup** = $K$ (achieved only when $N \to \infty$ and stages are perfectly balanced).
- **Real-world speedup** is always *less* than $K$ due to register overhead, clock skew, and stage imbalance.
- **Pipeline efficiency** $\rho = N / (K + N - 1)$ — approaches 100% only for large $N$.
- **Real IC examples:** MIPS 5-stage pipeline, Intel Core i7 (14–19 stages), pipelined ADCs in modern transceivers.
- **Pipeline registers** are mandatory — without them, no stage decoupling is possible.
- **Hold time constraint:** $\tau_{min, i} \geq t_{hold} - t_{cq}$ must be satisfied to prevent short-path failures.
- **Clock skew** between registers is a critical design constraint — typically limited to $< 5\%$ of $T_{clk}$.
- **Throughput $\neq$ Latency:** Increasing $K$ increases latency but does not increase throughput (in steady state) beyond $1/T_{clk}$.
- **Pipelining is *not* free:** It trades **area and power** (extra registers) for **speed** (higher throughput).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMOS Fabrication Process Overview - Photolithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CMOS Fabrication Process Overview - Photolithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photolithography** is the fundamental optical patterning process used in CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) fabrication to transfer geometric shapes from a **photolithographic mask (reticle)** onto a thin layer of photosensitive polymer (called **photoresist**) coated over a silicon wafer substrate. It is the cornerstone of planar processing technology and dictates the **minimum feature size (MFS)** achievable in any modern integrated circuit (IC) manufacturing node.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECST415 – VLSI Design, Module 2), photolithography is studied as the *first major patterning step* in the complete CMOS fabrication flow (which subsequently includes etching, ion implantation, oxidation, diffusion, and metallization).

### 1.1.1 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Wafer** | A thin, circular slice of single-crystal silicon (typically **300 mm diameter** in modern fabs) on which multiple ICs are fabricated simultaneously. |
| **Photoresist (PR)** | A light-sensitive organic polymer that undergoes chemical change upon UV exposure. |
| **Mask (Reticle)** | A fused-quartz plate containing the desired circuit pattern in opaque chrome regions. |
| **Stepper / Scanner** | The lithography exposure tool that projects the mask image onto the wafer. |
| **DUV / EUV** | Deep Ultraviolet (193 nm) and Extreme Ultraviolet (13.5 nm) light sources used in advanced nodes. |
| **Resolution (R)** | The smallest feature that can be reliably printed on the wafer. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Stencil & Spray-Paint Analogy**
> Imagine you want to paint a complex pattern (say, the KTU logo) onto a wall. Instead of hand-drawing it, you:
> 1. Cut a **stencil** out of a plastic sheet — the empty regions define your pattern.
> 2. Place the stencil firmly against the wall.
> 3. Spray-paint over it.
> 4. Lift the stencil — only the cut-out regions show the paint.

**Photolithography does precisely this, but with light instead of paint:**

- The **stencil** is the **photomask** (chrome-on-quartz glass).
- The **wall** is the **silicon wafer** (with an oxide/nitride/polysilicon layer).
- The **spray paint** is **ultraviolet (UV) light**.
- The **paint** is a light-sensitive chemical called **photoresist**.
- Wherever the mask is **transparent**, light hits the photoresist, changing its solubility.
- Wherever the mask is **opaque (chrome)**, the photoresist underneath remains protected.

After exposure, a chemical **developer** dissolves either the *exposed* (positive resist) or *unexposed* (negative resist) regions, leaving a patterned resist layer. This patterned resist then acts as a *protective mask* for the subsequent **etching** or **ion-implantation** step.

---

## 1.3 Why Photolithography is the Heart of CMOS Fabrication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – CMOS Fabrication)**
> Photolithography determines:
> 1. The **minimum gate length (L)** of MOSFETs.
> 2. The **transistor density** (transistors per mm²).
> 3. The **yield** and **cost-per-die** of the IC.
> 4. The **technology node** classification (e.g., 180 nm, 65 nm, 7 nm, 3 nm).

It is the *only* step that physically *defines* the lateral geometry of every transistor, interconnect, via, and contact in the entire IC.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Canonical Steps of Photolithography

Every photolithographic sequence in a CMOS fab follows these eight standardized steps. Each step has a specific purpose and is tightly controlled in a Class-1 cleanroom (≤ 1 particle ≥ 0.5 µm per cubic foot).

### Step 1: Wafer Cleaning (Pre-treatment)
- **Purpose:** Remove organic contaminants, native oxide, and metallic residues.
- **Methods:** RCA clean (NH₄OH + H₂O₂ + H₂O followed by HCl + H₂O₂ + H₂O), piranha etch (H₂SO₄ + H₂O₂), or diluted HF dip.
- **Why:** Contamination causes *pattern defects*, *poor photoresist adhesion*, and *device failure*.

### Step 2: Dehydration Bake
- Wafer heated to **~150 °C for 1–2 minutes** on a hot plate to remove residual moisture.
- **Why:** Moisture leads to *poor photoresist adhesion* (causing "lift-off" defects later).

### Step 3: Priming (HMDS Application)
- **Hexamethyldisilazane (HMDS)** vapor is applied to the wafer surface.
- **Chemical Reaction:**
$$\text{Si–OH} + (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{Si–NH–Si(CH}_3)_3 \rightarrow \text{Si–O–Si(CH}_3)_3 + \text{NH}_3$$
- This creates a **hydrophobic surface**, dramatically improving photoresist adhesion.

### Step 4: Photoresist Coating (Spin Coating)
- Liquid photoresist is dispensed onto the center of the wafer.
- Wafer is spun at high speed (**2000–5000 rpm**).
- Final resist thickness is controlled by spin speed and resist viscosity (typically **0.5 – 1.5 µm** for DUV).
- **Why:** A uniform, defect-free film is essential for accurate pattern transfer.

### Step 5: Soft Bake (Pre-exposure Bake)
- Wafer baked at **~90 – 100 °C for ~60 seconds** on a hot plate.
- **Why:** Evaporates residual solvent, hardens the resist, improves adhesion, and prevents *resist sticking to the mask*.

### Step 6: Alignment & Exposure
- The **photomask** is aligned to existing patterns on the wafer using **alignment marks** (typically cross-hairs) under a microscope.
- UV light (mercury arc lamp: 436 nm **g-line**, 405 nm **h-line**, 365 nm **i-line**, or DUV excimer laser: **193 nm ArF**, **248 nm KrF**) is projected through the mask.
- **Exposure Dose** is measured in **mJ/cm²**.
- **Modern tools** use *step-and-scan* projection systems with demagnification (**4× or 5×**).

### Step 7: Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)
- Wafer baked at **~110 – 130 °C for ~90 seconds**.
- **Why:** For chemically amplified resists, PEB drives the acid-catalyzed deprotection reaction, fixing the latent image. This is *critical* for CD (Critical Dimension) control.

### Step 8: Development
- Wafer immersed in a **developer solution** (typically **TMAH — Tetramethylammonium Hydroxide**, 0.26 N).
- **Positive resist:** Exposed regions dissolve.
- **Negative resist:** Exposed regions cross-link and *remain*; unexposed regions dissolve.
- Followed by a **rinse** in DI water and **spin-dry**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Subsequent steps** (Hard Bake, Etch, Ion Implantation, Resist Strip) are *not* considered part of photolithography proper, but photolithography directly determines their success.

---

## 2.2 Types of Photoresist

| Property | Positive Photoresist | Negative Photoresist |
|---|---|---|
| **Exposure Effect** | Exposed regions become **soluble** | Exposed regions become **insoluble** (cross-link) |
| **Image Quality** | High resolution, sharp edges | Lower resolution, swelling during development |
| **Common Use** | Modern CMOS (DUV, EUV) | Older processes, MEMS, thick-film patterning |
| **Example Materials** | Poly-methyl-methacrylate (PMMA), DNQ-novolac | SU-8, poly-cis-isoprene, KTFR |
| **Etch Resistance** | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Removal (Stripping)** | Easy (acetone, NMP) | Harder (organic solvents / plasma ash) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite Question:** *"Why are positive resists preferred for sub-micron CMOS fabrication?"*
> **Model Answer:** Positive resists provide **higher resolution**, **better edge acuity**, **lower swelling** during development, and **no cross-linking**, all of which are critical for printing sub-100 nm features. Negative resists suffer from solvent-induced swelling, which limits resolution to ~ 1 µm.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rayleigh Resolution Criterion** | $R = k_1 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$ | Minimum printable line width |
| 2 | **Depth of Focus (DOF)** | $DOF = k_2 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA^2}$ | Allowable focus tolerance |
| 3 | **Numerical Aperture** | $NA = n \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Light-gathering ability of lens |
| 4 | **Exposure Dose (D)** | $D = I \cdot t$ | Energy per unit area (mJ/cm²) |
| 5 | **Spin-Coating Thickness (Empirical)** | $t = \dfrac{k \cdot C^{\beta}}{\omega^{\alpha}}$ | C = viscosity, ω = spin speed |
| 6 | **Modulation Transfer Function** | $MTF(f) = \dfrac{2}{\pi} \left[\arccos\left(\dfrac{f}{f_c}\right) - \left(\dfrac{f}{f_c}\right)\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{f}{f_c}\right)^2}\right]$ | Image contrast vs. spatial frequency |
| 7 | **Refractive Index of PR** | $n \approx 1.6 - 1.7$ | Determines reflection at PR/substrate interface |
| 8 | **Reflectivity from Thin Film** | $r = \dfrac{n_{PR}^2 - n_{sub}}{n_{PR}^2 + n_{sub}}$ | Causes standing-wave effects |

Where:
- $k_1$ = process-dependent factor (0.4 typical; advanced lithography pushes it to ~ 0.25).
- $k_2$ = process-dependent factor (~ 0.5).
- $λ$ = exposure wavelength.
- $NA$ = numerical aperture of the projection lens.
- $f_c = \dfrac{2 \cdot NA}{\lambda}$ = spatial cutoff frequency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the Rayleigh equation, the `|` symbol must *never* be used inside markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Same applies to formulas involving $k_1 \vert \lambda \vert$ notation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Moore's Law**: Every 2 years, the number of transistors per chip doubles. This has been sustained primarily by *reducing λ* (using DUV 193 nm, immersion lithography, and now **EUV at 13.5 nm**) and increasing NA.
- **Immersion Lithography**: Filling the space between the final lens and wafer with **ultra-pure water** ($n = 1.44$ at 193 nm), effectively increasing NA from 0.93 to **1.35** — used for 32 nm, 22 nm nodes.
- **EUV Lithography (13.5 nm)**: Used from 7 nm node onwards; uses reflective mirrors (Mo/Si multilayers) since no transparent material exists at this wavelength.
- **Multi-patterning**: Spacing a single lithography step across 2, 3, or 4 exposures (LELELE, SADP, SAQP) to circumvent the $k_1$ limit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Numbers to Remember**
> - **g-line** λ = **436 nm** → used for ~ 0.5 µm nodes
> - **i-line** λ = **365 nm** → used for ~ 0.35 µm nodes
> - **KrF** λ = **248 nm** → used for ~ 180 nm nodes
> - **ArF (dry)** λ = **193 nm** → used for ~ 130 nm nodes
> - **ArF (immersion)** λ = **193 nm, n = 1.44** → used for 22 nm / 14 nm nodes
> - **EUV** λ = **13.5 nm** → used for 7 nm and below
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rayleigh Resolution Limit

### 3.1.1 Physical Setup

Consider two adjacent point sources of light, $P_1$ and $P_2$, separated by a distance $d$ on the mask. Their images on the wafer are **Airy diffraction patterns** produced by the projection lens of numerical aperture $NA$.

The first minimum of the Airy pattern occurs at an angle $\theta$ given by:

$$\sin(\theta_1) = \dfrac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{D}$$

where $D$ is the lens aperture diameter and $\lambda$ is the wavelength.

### 3.1.2 Definition of Numerical Aperture

The numerical aperture is defined as:

$$NA = n \cdot \sin(\theta_{max})$$

where $n$ is the refractive index of the medium (air = 1.0, water = 1.44) and $\theta_{max}$ is the half-angle of the maximum cone of light accepted by the lens.

### 3.1.3 Applying the Rayleigh Criterion

The **Rayleigh criterion** states that two point sources are *just resolvable* when the principal maximum of one Airy pattern coincides with the **first minimum** of the other. This occurs when the angular separation equals $\theta_1$.

Setting the lateral separation $R$ equal to the Airy radius:

$$R = \dfrac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{NA}$$

For two equal line/space patterns (a grating), the factor 0.61 is replaced by a process-dependent constant $k_1$. Hence:

$$R = k_1 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$$

### 3.1.4 Derivation of the Depth of Focus (DOF)

The DOF is the range of focus for which the image remains acceptably sharp. It is derived by considering the longitudinal spread of the Airy pattern along the optical axis.

The optical path difference between rays from the lens edge and lens center must remain within a fraction of a wavelength (typically $\pm \lambda/4$ per the Rayleigh quarter-wavelength rule).

The marginal ray angle satisfies $\sin(\theta) \approx NA/n$ in the image space. The longitudinal shift $DOF$ is:

$$DOF = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot n \cdot (1 - \cos \theta)} \approx \dfrac{\lambda}{n \cdot \theta^2} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot n}{NA^2}$$

Including the process factor $k_2$:

$$DOF = k_2 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA^2}$$

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example (typical KTU 14-mark question):**
> A CMOS process uses an ArF excimer laser stepper with $\lambda = 193$ nm, $NA = 0.93$, and $k_1 = 0.4$.
> (a) Calculate the minimum printable feature size.
> (b) If immersion lithography is used with $n = 1.44$ and the same NA, what is the new feature size?
> (c) For the original dry system, calculate the DOF.

**Solution:**

**(a) Dry ArF:**
$$R = k_1 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA} = 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{193 \text{ nm}}{0.93}$$
$$R = 0.4 \cdot 207.5 \text{ nm} = 83.0 \text{ nm}$$

**(b) Immersion ArF:**
$$NA_{imm} = n \cdot NA = 1.44 \cdot 0.93 = 1.339$$
$$R_{imm} = 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{193}{1.339} = 0.4 \cdot 144.1 \text{ nm} = 57.65 \text{ nm}$$

**(c) DOF (dry):**
$$DOF = k_2 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA^2} = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{193}{(0.93)^2}$$
$$DOF = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{193}{0.8649} = 0.5 \cdot 223.1 \text{ nm} = 111.5 \text{ nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Immersion lithography reduces feature size by ~ 30% but at the cost of a *significantly reduced* DOF, which makes focus control extremely demanding.

---

## 3.2 Critical-Dimension (CD) Control Matrix

| Parameter | Effect on CD | Tolerance |
|---|---|---|
| Exposure dose $\pm 5\%$ | CD shifts $\sim \pm 10$ nm | Must be controlled to < 1% |
| PEB temperature $\pm 1$ °C | CD shifts $\sim 5$ – 15 nm | Strict hot-plate uniformity |
| Developer concentration | Sidewall angle changes | $\pm 0.005$ N TMAH |
| Resist thickness $\pm 50$ Å | Standing-wave induced CD variation | Spin-coat uniformity < 1% |
| Substrate reflectivity | Notching / swing curve | Bottom anti-reflective coating (BARC) used |

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Standing-Wave Effect (Qualitative)

Light entering the photoresist reflects off the underlying substrate, creating interference with the incoming wave. The result is a periodic intensity variation along the depth $z$:

$$I(z) = I_0 [1 + r \cdot \cos(4\pi n z / \lambda)]$$

where $r$ is the reflectivity coefficient at the PR-substrate interface.

The **period** of the standing wave is:

$$\Lambda = \dfrac{\lambda}{2n}$$

For a typical DUV process: $\Lambda = 193 / (2 \times 1.7) = 56.8$ nm.

This causes the photoresist sidewalls to have a *wavy* profile after development, degrading CD control. Solution: **BARC (Bottom Anti-Reflective Coating)** — a thin absorbing layer deposited before the photoresist.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Photolithography Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1 Wafer Cleaning RCA or Piranha] --> B[Step2 Dehydration Bake 150C]
    B --> C[Step3 HMDS Priming Hydrophobic Surface]
    C --> D[Step4 Spin Coat Photoresist 2000 to 5000 rpm]
    D --> E[Step5 Soft Bake 90 to 100C Drive off Solvent]
    E --> F[Step6 Mask Alignment and UV Exposure gline hline iline DUV EUV]
    F --> G[Step7 Post Exposure Bake 110 to 130C Acid Catalyzed Reaction]
    G --> H[Step8 Development in TMAH 0.26 N Solvent]
    H --> I{Resist Type}
    I -->|Positive| J[Exposed Regions Dissolve]
    I -->|Negative| K[Unexposed Regions Dissolve]
    J --> L[Patterned Photoresist on Wafer]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Ready for Etching or Ion Implantation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Lithography Tool Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[UV Light Source Mercury Lamp or Excimer Laser] --> B[Beam Shaper and Collimator]
    B --> C[Reticle Mask with Circuit Pattern]
    C --> D[Projection Optics Reduction 4x or 5x]
    D --> E[Wafer Stage with Precision Interferometer]
    E --> F[Wafer with Photoresist]
    F --> G[Alignment System Microscope and Camera]
    G -.feedback.-> C
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Positive vs Negative Resist Chemistry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Photoresist] --> B[Positive Resist DNQ Novolac or Chemically Amplified]
    A --> C[Negative Resist SU8 or Polyisoprene]
    B --> B1[UV Exposure Breaks Polymer Bonds]
    B1 --> B2[Exposed Areas Dissolve in Developer]
    C --> C1[UV Exposure Crosslinks Polymer Chains]
    C1 --> C2[Exposed Areas Harden and Remain]
    B2 --> D[Window Opens in Resist]
    C2 --> E[Resist Pattern Remains on Wafer]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Exposure Process (ASCII Block Schematic)

```
  ┌──────────────────────┐
  │  UV LIGHT SOURCE     │  ← Mercury i-line (365 nm) / KrF (248 nm) / ArF (193 nm)
  └──────────┬───────────┘
             │  (collimated beam)
             ▼
  ┌──────────────────────┐
  │    PHOTOMASK         │  ← Opaque chrome on transparent quartz
  │  ┌──────┐  ▓▓▓▓▓    │
  │  │ OPEN │  ▓▓▓▓▓    │  ← Open region defines the geometry
  │  │      │  ▓▓▓▓▓    │
  │  └──────┘  ▓▓▓▓▓    │
  └──────────┬───────────┘
             │  (light passes through OPEN region only)
             ▼
  ┌──────────────────────┐
  │  PROJECTION LENS     │  ← Demagnifies (4×) and focuses image
  └──────────┬───────────┘
             │
             ▼
  ┌──────────────────────┐
  │  PHOTORESIST (PR)    │  ← Light-induced chemical change
  │  on Si WAFER         │
  └──────────────────────┘
             │
             ▼
  ┌──────────────────────┐
  │  DEVELOPMENT         │  → Patterned PR → Etch / Implant
  └──────────────────────┘
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define photolithography. List the major steps involved in a typical photolithography process used in CMOS fabrication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Photolithography** is an optical patterning process that transfers geometric shapes from a photomask onto a light-sensitive photoresist layer coated on a silicon wafer, using ultraviolet (UV) light exposure. (1 mark)

**Major Steps (2 marks):**
1. Wafer cleaning (RCA / piranha)
2. Dehydration bake and HMDS priming
3. Photoresist spin-coating
4. Soft bake (pre-exposure bake)
5. Mask alignment and UV exposure
6. Post-exposure bake (PEB)
7. Development (in TMAH)
8. Hard bake and inspection

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Differentiate between positive and negative photoresist. Why are positive resists preferred for sub-micron CMOS processes?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Positive | Negative |
|---|---|---|
| Solubility after exposure | Exposed regions become **soluble** | Exposed regions become **insoluble** (cross-linked) |
| Image polarity | Same as mask | Opposite of mask |
| Resolution | **High** (no swelling) | **Lower** (swelling) |

**Reason for preference in sub-micron CMOS (1 mark):**
Positive resists exhibit **higher resolution, sharper edge profiles, no solvent-induced swelling, and superior critical-dimension (CD) control** — all essential for printing sub-100 nm features. Negative resists suffer from swelling during development, limiting resolution to ~ 1 µm.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 2 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full optional).

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step process of photolithography used in CMOS fabrication with a neat flow diagram. Highlight the role of HMDS priming, soft bake, and post-exposure bake (PEB). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the **Rayleigh resolution criterion**, derive the expressions for **minimum resolvable feature size (R)** and **depth of focus (DOF)** for a photolithographic system. A CMOS process uses an i-line stepper with $\lambda = 365$ nm and $NA = 0.65$. If $k_1 = 0.4$ and $k_2 = 0.6$, calculate the minimum feature size and DOF. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Wafer Cleaning (1 mark):** RCA clean removes organic/inorganic contaminants. Critical for pattern fidelity.
2. **Dehydration Bake & HMDS Priming (1.5 marks):** Removes moisture; HMDS reacts with surface –OH groups forming a hydrophobic Si–O–Si(CH₃)₃ monolayer. This *prevents resist lifting* during development.
3. **Spin Coating (0.5 mark):** Photoresist dispensed and spun at 2000–5000 rpm to give ~ 1 µm uniform film.
4. **Soft Bake (1 mark):** 90–100 °C for ~ 60 s — drives off solvent, hardens resist, prevents mask-sticking. Improves adhesion and etch resistance.
5. **Exposure (0.5 mark):** UV light through mask chemically modifies exposed PR regions.
6. **Post-Exposure Bake (1.5 marks):** 110–130 °C for ~ 90 s — for chemically amplified resists, PEB drives the acid-catalyzed deprotection reaction, *fixing* the latent image. Critical for CD control.
7. **Development & Hard Bake (1 mark):** TMAH (0.26 N) develops the image; hard bake at 120 °C removes residual solvent and improves etch resistance.

**Flow Diagram (covered in Section 4.1).**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

From the Airy diffraction pattern and the Rayleigh criterion, two point sources are resolved when their angular separation equals the first-minimum angle of the Airy disk:

$$\sin(\theta_1) = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$$

Combined with the numerical aperture definition $NA = n \sin(\theta_{max})$ and the process factor $k_1$:

$$R = k_1 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$$

The DOF follows from the quarter-wavelength tolerance rule:

$$DOF = k_2 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA^2}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

$$R = 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{365}{0.65} = 0.4 \cdot 561.5 = 224.6 \text{ nm}$$

$$DOF = 0.6 \cdot \dfrac{365}{(0.65)^2} = 0.6 \cdot \dfrac{365}{0.4225} = 0.6 \cdot 864.0 = 518.4 \text{ nm}$$

**Final Answer:** $R \approx 225$ nm, $DOF \approx 518$ nm.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** What is the role of photoresist in CMOS fabrication? Compare positive and negative photoresists in terms of chemistry, resolution, and application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DUV stepper uses $\lambda = 193$ nm, $NA = 0.85$ (dry). Calculate the minimum feature size. If the process is switched to immersion lithography with $n = 1.44$, what is the new minimum feature size, and by what percentage has it improved? Comment on the trade-off in DOF for both cases ($k_1 = k_2 = 0.4$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Role of Photoresist (2 marks):**
The photoresist is a light-sensitive polymer that acts as a *temporary protective mask* during etching or ion implantation. It defines the lateral geometry of every feature in the IC.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | Positive PR | Negative PR |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemistry** | DNQ-novolac + photoactive compound; exposure generates indene carboxylic acid which makes PR soluble in aqueous base. | Polyisoprene or SU-8; exposure initiates radical crosslinking between polymer chains, making exposed regions insoluble. |
| **Resolution** | High (sub-100 nm achievable) | Limited (~ 1 µm) due to swelling |
| **Edge Profile** | Vertical, sharp | Slightly sloped, can be wavy |
| **Etch Resistance** | Moderate | Excellent (very dense cross-linked network) |
| **Application** | Modern CMOS (DUV, EUV), VLSI | MEMS, thick-film, packaging |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Positive resists dominate in sub-micron CMOS due to their superior resolution and CD control.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Dry ArF (2 marks):**
$$R_{dry} = k_1 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA} = 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{193}{0.85}$$
$$R_{dry} = 0.4 \cdot 227.06 = 90.82 \text{ nm}$$

**Immersion ArF (2.5 marks):**
$$NA_{imm} = 1.44 \cdot 0.85 = 1.224$$
$$R_{imm} = 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{193}{1.224} = 0.4 \cdot 157.68 = 63.07 \text{ nm}$$

**Percentage Improvement (1 mark):**
$$\% \text{ Improvement} = \dfrac{90.82 - 63.07}{90.82} \times 100\% = 30.55\%$$

**DOF Comparison (1.5 marks):**
$$DOF_{dry} = 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{193}{(0.85)^2} = 0.4 \cdot 267.0 = 106.8 \text{ nm}$$
$$DOF_{imm} = 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{193}{(1.224)^2} = 0.4 \cdot 128.8 = 51.5 \text{ nm}$$

**Trade-off Comment:** Immersion reduces the feature size by ~ 30.5% but **shrinks the DOF to less than half** (from 107 nm to 52 nm), demanding extremely tight focus control. This is the central engineering challenge of immersion lithography.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Photolithography Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the Rayleigh formula** — Students often write $R = k_1 \cdot \lambda/NA$ without stating that $R$, $\lambda$, and $DOF$ must all be in the same units (typically nm). **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing $k_1$ and $k_2$** — $k_1$ is for resolution, $k_2$ for DOF. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 0.5-mark loss.
> 3. **Not mentioning HMDS chemistry** — In step-by-step questions, students frequently skip the chemical role of HMDS (creating a hydrophobic surface). Examiners *expect* the Si–OH → Si–O–Si(CH₃)₃ reaction. **Penalty: 0.5–1 mark.**
> 4. **Confusing positive/negative resist polarity** — Several students wrongly state that in *negative* resist, exposed regions *dissolve*. It is the **opposite**. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Forgetting BARC and standing waves** — For advanced questions on DUV lithography, mentioning anti-reflective coatings and standing-wave mitigation is a *bonus differentiator*. **Penalty: 0.5 mark if missing.**
> 6. **Omitting $k_1$ and $k_2$ values** — The Rayleigh formula is *incomplete* without explicitly stating the process factor. Always write the formula with $k_1$, not as a bare $\lambda/NA$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Photolithography (Module 2)**
> - **Definition:** Photolithography = optical pattern transfer from mask → photoresist → silicon wafer using UV light.
> - **Eight Canonical Steps:** Cleaning → Dehydration → HMDS Priming → Spin Coat → Soft Bake → Alignment/Exposure → PEB → Development.
> - **Resist Types:** Positive (exposed dissolves) vs Negative (exposed cross-links and remains). Positive preferred for sub-micron.
> - **Rayleigh Resolution:** $R = k_1 \cdot \lambda / NA$. **DOF:** $DOF = k_2 \cdot \lambda / NA^2$.
> - **Light Source Wavelengths:** g-line (436 nm) → i-line (365 nm) → KrF (248 nm) → ArF (193 nm) → Immersion (n = 1.44) → EUV (13.5 nm).
> - **HMDS** = hexamethyldisilazane, creates hydrophobic monolayer for PR adhesion.
> - **TMAH (0.26 N)** = standard aqueous developer for positive PR.
> - **NA Enhancement:** Achieved by immersion lithography (water, n = 1.44), increasing NA from 0.93 → 1.35.
> - **Trade-off:** Higher NA → smaller R but **smaller DOF**, demanding tighter focus control.
> - **Standing Wave Period:** $\Lambda = \lambda / 2n$ — mitigated by BARC.
> - **Modern Techniques:** EUV (13.5 nm), multi-patterning (SADP, SAQP), immersion ArF.
> - **Numerical constants to memorize:** $k_1 \approx 0.4$, $k_2 \approx 0.5$, $n_{PR} \approx 1.6 - 1.7$, $n_{water} = 1.44$.
> - **Industry Numbers:** 300 mm wafer; PR thickness 0.5 – 1.5 µm; exposure dose ~ 30 mJ/cm² for DUV.
> - **Syllabus mapping:** Module 2 → CMOS Fabrication Process Overview, focused on Photolithography as the *first and most critical* patterning step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure of an Integrated Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Structure of an Integrated Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Structure of an Integrated Circuit (IC)** refers to the vertically stacked, multi-layered physical architecture of transistors, interconnects, and passive components fabricated on a single monolithic semiconductor substrate (typically a p-type or n-type silicon wafer). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the IC structure encompasses the cross-sectional geometry, doping profiles, isolation regions, and interconnect stack-up that collectively realize a functional Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The structure includes the active silicon region (where MOSFETs are formed), the polysilicon gate layer, multiple metal interconnect layers (M1, M2, … Mn) separated by Inter-Layer Dielectric (ILD), and the passivation/scratch-protection top layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> An integrated circuit is a set of electronic circuits fabricated on a single piece (die/chip) of semiconductor material, usually silicon, by patterning, doping, and depositing successive layers of conducting, insulating, and semiconducting materials to form transistors, resistors, capacitors, and the metal lines that interconnect them.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Silicon City" Model

Imagine a **multi-storey building (the IC)** built on a flat plot of land (the **silicon substrate**):

| Layer in IC | Real-World Building Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Silicon Substrate | The Land / Foundation | Mechanical support + semiconductor body |
| Well / Tub | The Basement Floor (n-well or p-well) | Houses transistors of a specific type |
| Active Diffusion (S/D) | Individual Rooms | Source and Drain terminals of MOSFETs |
| Thin Oxide (Gate Oxide) | Thin Glass Windows | Controls current flow (gate dielectric) |
| Polysilicon Gate | Door Frames | Gate electrode controlling the channel |
| Metal Layers (M1, M2…) | Electrical Wiring / Piping | Routes signals between transistors |
| Inter-Layer Dielectric | Concrete Slabs Between Floors | Electrically isolates adjacent metal layers |
| Passivation | Building Roof / Paint | Protects IC from moisture, scratches, contaminants |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Builder:** Every modern IC is essentially a microscopic **3-D sandwich** — multiple thin films stacked on a silicon base. The art of VLSI design is *planning which ingredients go on which floor*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Wafer Diameter (D):** typically **150 mm (6")**, **200 mm (8")**, or **300 mm (12")**
- **Die (Chip) Size:** ranges from **1 mm × 1 mm** (simple) to **~25 mm × 25 mm** (modern CPUs/GPUs)
- **Number of Metal Layers:** **6 to 15+** in modern sub-22 nm processes
- **Substrate Resistivity:** **1–20 Ω·cm** for lightly doped p-type silicon
- **Gate Oxide Thickness (modern):** **1.0 – 1.5 nm** (high-k dielectrics replace SiO₂ below 45 nm)
- **Polysilicon Gate Thickness:** **~100 – 200 nm**
- **Metal Line Width:** **22 nm – 90 nm** (technology node dependent)

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Standard:** The silicon lattice constant is **a = 0.543 nm**, atomic spacing **≈ 0.235 nm**, and intrinsic carrier concentration at 300 K is **nᵢ ≈ 1.5 × 10¹⁰ cm⁻³**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of a CMOS IC (Vertical Cross-Section View)

From bottom to top, the structural hierarchy of a modern CMOS integrated circuit is:

1. **Bulk Silicon Substrate (p-type or n-type)**
   - Provides the mechanical base and serves as the common body (bulk) for all MOSFETs.
   - Doping level: ~10¹⁵ cm⁻³ (light doping for controlled threshold voltage).

2. **Well / Tub Formation (n-well or p-well)**
   - Twin-well process is industry standard.
   - NMOS transistors are built in **p-substrate** (or p-well); PMOS in **n-well**.
   - Provides the body terminal for the opposite-type transistors.

3. **Shallow Trench Isolation (STI)**
   - Replaces older LOCOS isolation.
   - Trenches etched into silicon, filled with SiO₂, used to electrically separate adjacent transistors.

4. **Active Diffusion Regions (Source / Drain)**
   - Heavily doped n⁺ or p⁺ regions (LDD – Lightly Doped Drain used for hot-carrier suppression).
   - Doping concentration: **10¹⁹ – 10²⁰ cm⁻³**.

5. **Thin Gate Oxide (SiO₂ or High-k Dielectric)**
   - Grown by thermal oxidation.
   - Critical parameter: equivalent oxide thickness (EOT).

6. **Polysilicon Gate Electrode**
   - Heavily doped (n⁺ or p⁺) polycrystalline silicon.
   - In modern nodes replaced by metal gates (TiN, TaN) with work-function tuning.

7. **Contact Layer (Tungsten or Copper plugs)**
   - Connects diffusion / poly to **Metal 1 (M1)**.

8. **Metal Interconnects (M1, M2, … Mn)**
   - Al (older), Cu (modern) wires patterned by dual-damascene process.
   - Connected by **vias** between adjacent levels.

9. **Inter-Layer Dielectric (ILD)**
   - SiO₂ (low-k variants like FSG, SiCOH in advanced nodes) to reduce parasitic capacitance.

10. **Passivation Layer (Top)**
    - Si₃N₄ or polyimide.
    - Final scratch / moisture / mobile-ion barrier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why so many layers?** Each layer performs a *single dedicated role* (doping, isolation, conduction, protection). By stacking thin films, we pack **billions of MOSFETs** into a few cm².

### 2.2 Lateral (Plan) Structure of a CMOS Inverter — The Canonical IC

The smallest functional CMOS circuit has **4 terminals** per transistor and is built from **two complementary MOSFETs**:

- **PMOS transistor** in the **n-well** (top half of the layout).
- **NMOS transistor** in the **p-substrate** (bottom half of the layout).
- **Input (A)** is shared **polysilicon gate** running horizontally between them.
- **Output (Y)** is the **metal line** joining both drains vertically.
- **VDD** rails the PMOS source; **GND (VSS)** rails the NMOS source.
- **n⁺ contact** in n-well ties body of PMOS to VDD.
- **p⁺ contact** in p-sub ties body of NMOS to GND.

This *stick-diagram* style plan is the **building block** of every digital IC.

### 2.3 Die, Wafer, and Reticle — The Macro Structure

- **Wafer**: Thin circular slice of single-crystal silicon (Czochralski-grown).
- **Die (Chip)**: One rectangular IC unit fabricated on the wafer.
- **Reticle**: The photomask pattern projected (stepped) across the wafer by the **stepper/scanner** lithography tool.
- **Scribe Line / Saw Street**: ~50–100 µm gap between adjacent dies for dicing.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Unit | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{die} = W \times H$ | Die area (mm² or cm²) | Cost / yield calculation |
| 2 | $A_{wafer} = \pi \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{2}$ | Wafer area (mm²) | Process planning |
| 3 | $N_{dies/wafer} \approx \dfrac{\pi \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{2}}{A_{die}} - \dfrac{\pi D}{\sqrt{2 \cdot A_{die}}}$ | Dies per wafer | Manufacturing planning |
| 4 | $Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A}$ | Poisson Yield Model (D₀ = defect density per cm²) | Yield estimation |
| 5 | $Y = \left(\dfrac{1 - e^{-D_0 A}}{D_0 A}\right)^{2}$ | Seeds' Model (defect clustering) | More realistic yield |
| 6 | $\text{Cost/Die} = \dfrac{C_{wafer}}{N_{dies} \cdot Y}$ | Cost per good die | Economic feasibility |
| 7 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.9 \, \varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area (F/cm²) | MOSFET I-V analysis |
| 8 | $t_{ox}^{EOT} = t_{high\text{-}k} \cdot \dfrac{3.9}{k_{high\text{-}k}}$ | Equivalent Oxide Thickness (nm) | High-k dielectric scaling |
| 9 | $P_{total} = n \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Dynamic power (W) | Power estimation |
| 10 | $R_{metal} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{W \cdot t}$ | Interconnect resistance (Ω) | RC delay analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** In the **dies per wafer** formula, the second term is the **boundary wastage correction** (for partial dies near the wafer edge). Students often forget this term and lose **2 marks** in the KTU ESE.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chip Architects** decide metal stack-up (number of metal layers) and routing pitch.
- **Process Engineers** optimize oxide thickness and well profiles for yield.
- **Foundry Economics Teams** use **Cost per Die** to determine wafer price.
- **Reliability Engineers** use passivation and ILD integrity to predict lifetime (10-year operating life).
- **Design-for-Test (DFT) Engineers** insert scan chains routed through upper metal layers without disturbing signal integrity on M1.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Dies per Wafer (Rectangular Die)

**Given:** Wafer of diameter **D**, die of dimensions **W × H** (W = width along x, H = height along y).

**Step 1 — Count dies along the diameter (horizontal).**
The horizontal diameter accommodates:
$$N_x = \left\lfloor \frac{D}{W} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 2 — Count dies along the vertical chord.**
On average, a vertical chord through the wafer has length **D** (across the diameter) and reduces as we move toward the edge. The average length of a chord is:
$$L_{avg} = \dfrac{\pi D}{4}$$

Hence the average number of dies in the y-direction is:
$$N_y = \left\lfloor \frac{\pi D}{4H} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 3 — Combine into a first-order estimate.**
$$N_{approx} = N_x \cdot N_y = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4WH}$$

**Step 4 — Refine to account for wafer-edge waste.**
The total wafer area is $\pi D^{2}/4$, and dies cannot be placed in the curved edge regions. A more accurate empirical form (used in KTU/industry):
$$N_{dies} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4 \cdot A_{die}} - \dfrac{\pi D}{\sqrt{2 A_{die}}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification with worked numbers.**
Let **D = 300 mm**, die **W = H = 10 mm** (area = 100 mm²).
- Wafer area = π × 150² = 70,685.8 mm²
- Raw ratio = 70,685.8 / 100 = 706.86
- Edge correction term = π × 300 / √200 = π × 300 / 14.142 = 66.66
- **N_dies ≈ 706.86 − 66.66 = 640.2 ≈ 640 dies per wafer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU VLSI Design ESE under "Structure of an Integrated Circuit."

### 3.2 Derivation: Yield (Poisson Model) and Cost per Die

**Step 1 — Start with the Poisson probability** that a chip of area A has **zero defects**:
$$P(\text{zero defects}) = e^{-D_0 A}$$

**Step 2 — Identify D₀** as the **average defect density** (defects per cm²) — typically **0.5 – 2 defects/cm²** in a mature fab.

**Step 3 — Define Yield** as the probability of a defect-free die:
$$Y = e^{-D_0 A}$$

**Step 4 — Define the Cost per Good Die**:
Let the processed wafer cost be $C_{wafer}$ (includes materials, depreciation, labour):
$$\text{Cost/Die} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N_{dies} \cdot Y}$$

**Step 5 — Insert numbers.**
For a 300 mm wafer: $C_{wafer}$ = $2000, N = 640, A = 1 cm², D_0 = 1 defect/cm² → Y = e^{-1} = 0.368.
$$\text{Cost/Die} = \frac{2000}{640 \times 0.368} \approx \frac{2000}{235.5} \approx \$8.49$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Matters:** Doubling the die size **more than doubles** the cost per die because yield drops **exponentially**. This is why CPUs and SoCs are typically **die-split** into multiple smaller chips (chiplets).

### 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT) for High-k Dielectrics

**Step 1 — Capacitance of a parallel-plate oxide:**
$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{k_{ox} \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$$

**Step 2 — Define EOT** as the thickness of pure SiO₂ that would give the same capacitance:
$$t_{ox}^{EOT} = \frac{k_{SiO_2}}{k_{high\text{-}k}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}k} = \frac{3.9}{k_{high\text{-}k}} \cdot t_{high\text{-}k}$$

**Step 3 — Example: Hafnium dioxide (HfO₂) with k = 25, physical thickness 5 nm:**
$$t_{ox}^{EOT} = \frac{3.9}{25} \times 5 = 0.78 \, nm$$

This is why high-k dielectrics allow **physically thicker films** with **electrically thinner** oxides — solving the **gate leakage** problem below 45 nm.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Layer-by-Layer Comparison Table

| Layer | Material | Typical Thickness | Function | Failure Mode if Defective |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Substrate | Si (p-type) | 500–775 µm | Mechanical + body | Wafer breakage, junction leakage |
| Well | n-Si / p-Si | 1–3 µm | Body for complementary devices | Latch-up, Vt mismatch |
| STI | SiO₂ | 200–500 nm | Isolation | Sub-threshold leakage, cross-talk |
| Gate Oxide | SiO₂ / HfO₂ | 1–2 nm EOT | Channel control | Oxide breakdown, high leakage |
| Poly Gate | Poly-Si / TiN | 100–200 nm | Gate electrode | Vt shift, poly depletion |
| S/D Diffusion | n⁺Si / p⁺Si | 50–150 nm | Source/Drain | High series resistance |
| Silicide (NiSi, CoSi₂) | Metal-silicide | 10–30 nm | Reduces contact R | Agglomeration, contact spike |
| ILD (M1–M2) | SiO₂ / low-k | 300–800 nm | Inter-layer isolation | Time-dependent dielectric breakdown (TDDB) |
| Metal Lines | Al / Cu | 100–500 nm | Signal routing | Electromigration, stress migration |
| Vias | W / Cu | 50–100 nm | Inter-layer connection | High resistance open |
| Passivation | Si₃N₄ / Polyimide | 500 nm – 1 µm | Environmental protection | Corrosion, mobile-ion ingress |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an IC Cross-Section

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **vertical structural hierarchy** of a CMOS IC, showing how each layer interacts with the next.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1[Top Protection]
        pass["PASSIVATION LAYER - Si3N4 OR POLYIMIDE"]
    end
    subgraph A2[Upper Interconnects]
        m3["METAL LAYER M3 - Cu OR Al"]
        via2["VIA PLUG - W OR Cu"]
    end
    subgraph A3[Middle Interconnects]
        m2["METAL LAYER M2 - Cu OR Al"]
        via1["VIA PLUG - W OR Cu"]
    end
    subgraph A4[Lower Interconnects]
        m1["METAL LAYER M1 - Cu OR Al"]
        cont["CONTACT PLUG - W"]
    end
    subgraph A5[Active Device]
        poly["POLYSILICON GATE"]
        gateox["GATE OXIDE - SiO2 OR HfO2"]
        sd["SOURCE AND DRAIN DIFFUSION - n+ OR p+"]
    end
    subgraph A6[Isolation]
        sti["SHALLOW TRENCH ISOLATION - SiO2"]
    end
    subgraph A7[Substrate Base]
        well["WELL - n-WELL OR p-WELL"]
        sub["BULK SILICON SUBSTRATE - p-TYPE"]
    end

    pass --> m3 --> via2 --> m2 --> via1 --> m1 --> cont --> sd
    poly --> gateox --> sd
    sti -.isolates.-> sd
    well --> sd
    sub --> well
```

### 4.2 Plan-View Stick Diagram of a CMOS Inverter (Conceptual Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vdd["VDD RAIL"]
    gnd["GND VSS RAIL"]
    input["INPUT A - POLY GATE"]
    output["OUTPUT Y - METAL"]
    pmos["PMOS - IN n-WELL"]
    nmos["NMOS - IN p-SUBSTRATE"]

    vdd --- pmos
    pmos --- output
    nmos --- gnd
    input --- pmos
    input --- nmos
    output --- nmos
```

### 4.3 Wafer-to-Die Hierarchical Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    wafer["SILICON WAFER - 300 mm DIAMETER"]
    reticle["RETICLE FIELD - PROJECTED BY STEPPER"]
    die1["DIE 1 - ONE IC"]
    die2["DIE 2 - ONE IC"]
    die3["DIE N - ONE IC"]
    scribe["SCRIBE LINE - 80 um GAP FOR DICING"]
    bondpad["BOND PAD - I O ACCESS"]

    wafer --> reticle
    reticle --> die1
    reticle --> scribe
    reticle --> die2
    reticle --> die3
    die1 --> bondpad
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams in an exam:** Always label the **top** (passivation), **bottom** (substrate), and the **direction of signal flow** (input → gate → channel → S/D). Examiners award marks for **neat, labelled** schematics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Integrated Circuit." List any four major layers present in a typical CMOS IC from bottom to top.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Integrated Circuit (IC)** is a miniaturized electronic circuit consisting of active and passive components (transistors, resistors, capacitors) and their interconnections fabricated on a single continuous piece of semiconductor material, usually silicon.

Four major layers (bottom to top):
1. **Silicon substrate** (p-type bulk) — 1 mark
2. **Well** (n-well or p-well) — ½ mark
3. **Polysilicon gate** over **gate oxide** — ½ mark
4. **Metal interconnect layers (M1, M2, …)** separated by **Inter-Layer Dielectric** — ½ mark
5. **Passivation layer** (Si₃N₄) on top — ½ mark

*(Any four layers = 2 marks; definition = 1 mark. Total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the function of (a) STI and (b) Passivation layer in an IC?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(a) STI (Shallow Trench Isolation) – 1½ marks:** STI is an isolation technique that uses trenches etched into the silicon and filled with SiO₂ to electrically separate adjacent transistors, preventing latch-up and sub-threshold leakage.
- **(b) Passivation Layer – 1½ marks:** The passivation layer is the topmost layer of the IC (typically Si₃N₄ or polyimide). It protects the chip from **moisture, ionic contamination, mechanical scratches, and environmental stress**, ensuring long-term reliability.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

**Q.A.** With the help of a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the **complete layer-by-layer structure of a CMOS Integrated Circuit**. Discuss the role of each layer and the materials used. **(14 marks)**

**Sub-part (a) — Draw and label the cross-section (7 marks):**
The student must draw a vertical cross-section of a CMOS inverter showing:
- p-type substrate
- n-well
- STI regions
- Source/Drain diffusions
- Gate oxide and polysilicon gate
- Contact plugs
- M1 and M2 with vias
- ILD
- Passivation

**Sub-part (b) — Describe function of each layer (7 marks):**

| Layer | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Substrate | p-Si | Mechanical support; common body for NMOS |
| n-well | n-Si | Body for PMOS |
| STI | SiO₂ | Electrical isolation between devices |
| Gate oxide | SiO₂ / HfO₂ | Dielectric that controls channel formation |
| Polysilicon gate | n⁺/p⁺ poly | Gate electrode |
| S/D | n⁺/p⁺ Si | Charge carrier injection points |
| Silicide | NiSi, CoSi₂ | Reduces contact & sheet resistance |
| ILD | SiO₂, low-k | Insulates adjacent metal layers |
| Metal lines | Cu / Al | Routes signals between transistors |
| Passivation | Si₃N₄ | Environmental protection |

*(Each layer ~½ mark; diagram 7 marks = cross-section 5 + labels 2. Total 14 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. (a)** Derive the expression for the **number of dies per wafer** for a rectangular die. Compute the number of good dies for a **300 mm wafer** with die size **15 mm × 10 mm** and defect density **0.8 defects/cm²**. Assume Poisson yield. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Formula derivation (4 marks):**
$$N_{dies/wafer} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4 \cdot A_{die}} - \frac{\pi D}{\sqrt{2 A_{die}}}$$

Derivation: count dies across diameter, average chord length $\pi D/4$, multiply, then subtract edge wastage.

**Step 2 — Compute die and wafer area (1 mark):**
- $A_{die} = 15 \times 10 = 150 \text{ mm}^{2} = 1.5 \text{ cm}^{2}$
- Wafer area = π × 150² = 70,685.8 mm²

**Step 3 — Number of dies (1 mark):**
$$N = \frac{70,685.8}{150} - \frac{\pi \times 300}{\sqrt{300}} = 471.24 - 54.41 \approx 416.83 \approx 416 \text{ dies}$$

**Step 4 — Yield (1 mark):**
$$Y = e^{-D_0 A} = e^{-0.8 \times 1.5} = e^{-1.2} = 0.3012$$

**Good dies** = 416 × 0.3012 ≈ **125.3 ≈ 125 dies** (0 marks extra, for result only)

---

**Q.B. (b)** Explain the concept of **Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT)**. Why is high-k dielectric used in modern ICs instead of SiO₂? A high-k dielectric has k = 22 and physical thickness 4 nm. Find the EOT. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
EOT is the thickness of pure SiO₂ that would produce the **same gate capacitance** per unit area as the high-k dielectric actually used.

**Step 2 — Why high-k? (2 marks):**
Below 45 nm nodes, SiO₂ must be < 1.5 nm thick, causing **excessive gate leakage** (direct tunnelling). A high-k material (e.g., HfO₂, k = 22) provides higher capacitance at **greater physical thickness**, reducing leakage by orders of magnitude.

**Step 3 — Formula (1 mark):**
$$t_{EOT} = \frac{3.9}{k_{high\text{-}k}} \times t_{physical}$$

**Step 4 — Calculation (2 marks):**
$$t_{EOT} = \frac{3.9}{22} \times 4 = 0.1773 \times 4 = 0.709 \, nm$$

This is equivalent to a **0.71 nm SiO₂** layer — far thinner than physically possible with SiO₂ alone, without leakage.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in IC-Structure questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the second (edge-correction) term** in the dies-per-wafer formula → loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing STI with LOCOS** (older process) — KTU 2024 expects **STI** (current industry standard).
> 3. **Drawing cross-sections without labels** — diagrams alone without material names and dimensions get only **50% credit**.
> 4. **Mixing up EOT and physical thickness** — examiners will deduct if you say "thinner is better" without explaining the leakage trade-off.
> 5. **Ignoring units** — always write mm², cm², nm explicitly. KTU examiners mark units strictly.
> 6. **Writing "poly" instead of "polysilicon"** in viva — sounds minor, but for written exams, full chemical/material names earn full credit.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An IC is a **3-D multilayer structure** on a silicon substrate — top to bottom: **passivation → metal layers → vias → contacts → polysilicon gate → gate oxide → S/D → well → substrate**.
- ✅ **CMOS** uses **two complementary MOSFETs** in a single structure: **PMOS in n-well**, **NMOS in p-substrate**.
- ✅ **STI (SiO₂-filled trenches)** is the modern isolation method — replaces LOCOS.
- ✅ **Gate oxide** is the thinnest, most critical layer — often replaced by **high-k dielectrics** (HfO₂) below 45 nm.
- ✅ **Polysilicon gates** are increasingly replaced by **metal gates** (TiN, TaN) for work-function tuning.
- ✅ **Cu metallization** (with dual-damascene) dominates modern nodes; older processes used **Al**.
- ✅ **Passivation** = topmost protection layer (Si₃N₄ / polyimide).
- ✅ **Yield** $Y = e^{-D_0 A}$ — yield **falls exponentially** with die area.
- ✅ **Cost/Die** = $C_{wafer} / (N_{dies} \cdot Y)$ — drives the industry's shift to **chiplet / multi-die** architectures.
- ✅ **EOT** allows high-k dielectrics to mimic very thin SiO₂ without leakage.
- ✅ **Always remember** the dies-per-wafer formula has **two terms**: bulk count minus edge correction.
- ✅ **Wafer sizes** in industry: **150 mm → 200 mm → 300 mm → 450 mm** (future).
- ✅ **Bond pads** are placed at die periphery for wire-bond or flip-chip package I/O.
- ✅ **Scribe lines** (~50–100 µm) separate dies for dicing — never used for active circuitry.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Design Flow - Custom Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Design Flow - Custom Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition — Design Flow

In the context of VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration), a **Design Flow** is formally defined as the *complete, ordered sequence of well-defined computational and verification steps* that transform a circuit specification (a textual/behavioral idea) into a physically realizable **GDSII (GDS2)** layout database ready for silicon fabrication. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECST415, design flow is the engineering pipeline that governs the translation of an abstract system description into geometric mask data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"A design flow is the systematic, hierarchical procedure that dictates how an integrated circuit is conceived, simulated, synthesized, placed, routed, and verified — from RTL (Register Transfer Level) description to final mask layout."*

## 1.2 The Three Canonical Design Styles

KTU Module 2 categorizes the entire VLSI universe of design methodologies into three top-level paradigms. Every chip you have ever touched — from a simple **555 timer** to an Apple M-series SoC — falls into one of these three buckets:

| # | Design Style | Human Effort | Area Efficiency | NRE Cost | Typical Use-Case |
|---|--------------|--------------|-----------------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Full-Custom Design Flow** | Maximum (every transistor hand-drawn) | **Highest** (≈ 100% utilization) | **Very High** | Analog, RF, memory, ultra-high-performance CPUs |
| 2 | **Semi-Custom Design Flow** | Medium (pre-built cells used) | Medium (70–90%) | Medium | Standard digital ASICs, controllers |
| 3 | **Programmable / FPGA-based Flow** | Minimum (no fabrication needed) | **Lowest** (fixed silicon) | **Lowest** | Prototyping, low-volume production |

## 1.3 What is a "Custom Design Flow"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Custom Design Flow = Full-Custom + Semi-Custom Design Flows.**
> It refers to any ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) flow in which the chip is *physically fabricated* for a specific customer application — as opposed to a generic, programmable device like an FPGA.

A **Custom Design** is one where the geometry of every mask layer is optimized (at least partially) for a specific application rather than being a fixed, off-the-shelf interconnect pattern. The two principal sub-categories are:

- **Full-Custom Design Flow** — The designer manually crafts the *layout of each and every transistor* down to the polygon level. Every diffusion region, poly gate, and metal wire is hand-placed and hand-routed. Examples: cell library leaf cells, analog blocks, SRAM bit-cells, high-speed I/O pads.
- **Semi-Custom Design Flow** — The designer instantiates *pre-characterized standard cells* from a library (gates, flip-flops) and uses automated EDA tools to place-and-route them. Examples: most digital ASICs, microcontrollers, SoC sub-systems.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "Building a House"

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy for KTU Students:**
> 
> Imagine you want to build a house on a plot of land.
> 
> - **Full-Custom** → You manufacture your *own bricks, mix your own cement, cut your own wood, weld your own steel rods*. The architect designs every nail placement. Result: smallest house on the plot, maximum strength, but extremely expensive and slow.
> - **Semi-Custom** → You buy *standard bricks, standard doors, standard windows* from a catalog, but arrange them according to your own floor plan. Result: faster construction, slightly less optimized, but perfectly livable.
> - **FPGA / Programmable** → You rent a *pre-built, fully furnished house* and just rearrange the furniture. Result: instant occupancy, zero construction cost, but you cannot change the walls.

In the same way, **Custom Design Flow** is the spectrum between hand-crafting silicon and assembling pre-made cells — both ultimately producing a chip *uniquely tailored* to a target application.

## 1.5 Why Custom Design Flow Matters (KTU Industry Relevance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every smartphone SoC, every automotive ECU, every 5G baseband processor — all begin life as a **Custom Design**. Without mastering this flow, a VLSI engineer cannot contribute to chip design at companies like Intel, Qualcomm, Texas Instruments, or NXP (which are major recruiters from Kerala KTU campuses).

The driving forces behind the choice of a custom flow are:

1. **Performance (Speed)** — Hand-tuned transistor sizing and wire routing minimizes delay.
2. **Power Dissipation** — Critical for battery-operated IoT and mobile devices.
3. **Area / Cost-per-Die** — Tighter packing = more dies per wafer = lower unit cost at high volume.
4. **Analog / Mixed-Signal Integration** — Many blocks (PLLs, ADCs, SerDes) **cannot** be implemented in FPGAs.
5. **IP Protection** — The custom layout is confidential; competitors cannot reverse-engineer it easily.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The VLSI Y-Chart (Gajski-Kuhn Chart) — Foundational Model

Before diving into the steps, you **must** internalize the **Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart**, which is the canonical KTU-board-favorite diagram for describing *any* VLSI design flow (full-custom, semi-custom, or FPGA). It maps three distinct *views* (or *domains*) of a design against three *levels of abstraction*.

### The Three Views (Domains)
1. **Behavioral Domain** — What the circuit *does* (algorithms, equations, HDL).
2. **Structural Domain** — How the circuit is *built* from components (gates, transistors, blocks).
3. **Physical (Geometric) Domain** — How the circuit *looks* on silicon (layouts, masks).

### The Three Levels of Abstraction
1. **System / Architectural Level** — Top-level chip behavior.
2. **Logic / Register-Transfer Level (RTL)** — Boolean equations, FSMs, datapaths.
3. **Circuit / Transistor Level** — Transistor schematics, netlists.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A *full-custom* flow operates primarily at the **outer rim** of the Y-chart (circuit/transistor level in the physical domain), while a *semi-custom* flow operates at the **inner rim** (RTL/logic level in the structural domain). Always draw the Y-chart whenever you are asked to compare flows in a 14-mark question.

## 2.2 The Full-Custom Design Flow — Step-by-Step

Below is the *canonical, exam-ready* sequence of stages. KTU examiners expect you to enumerate these in order, with brief one-line explanations of each.

### Stage-by-Stage Logical Breakdown

1. **System Specification**
   - Define chip functionality, performance targets, power budget, I/O requirements, packaging.
   - Outputs: *Functional spec document*, *Data sheet*.

2. **Architectural Design**
   - Decide top-level architecture: number of ALUs, memory size, bus widths, clocking strategy.
   - High-level trade-off analysis (throughput vs. area vs. power).

3. **Functional / Logic Design**
   - Write behavioral HDL (VHDL/Verilog/SystemVerilog).
   - Functional simulation to verify algorithmic correctness.

4. **Circuit Design (Transistor-Level Schematic)**
   - Convert logic into transistor-level schematic.
   - Sizing of W/L ratios for speed, drive strength, and noise margin.

5. **Circuit Simulation (SPICE)**
   - DC analysis, transient analysis, AC analysis, Monte-Carlo, corner analysis (SS, FF, SF, FS, TT).
   - Tools: Cadence Spectre, Synopsys HSPICE, Mentor Eldo.

6. **Physical (Layout) Design — The Defining Step of Full-Custom**
   - Hand-draw polygons on each mask layer (diffusion, polysilicon, n+/p+ implants, contacts, metal 1, metal 2, …).
   - Follow **Design Rules** (e.g., λ-based or micron-based rules from the foundry's DRC deck).
   - Tools: Cadence Virtuoso, Synopsys Custom Compiler, Mentor Calibre (DRC).

7. **Design Rule Check (DRC)**
   - Automated verification that the layout obeys all foundry geometric rules (min width, spacing, enclosure, area density).

8. **Layout vs. Schematic (LVS) Check**
   - Verifies that the drawn layout is *electrically equivalent* to the intended schematic netlist.

9. **Parasitic Extraction (PEX)**
   - Extracts R, C, and coupling capacitances from the physical geometry.
   - Output: a parasitic netlist (e.g., `.spice` with RC elements).

10. **Post-Layout Simulation**
    - Re-simulate the extracted netlist to verify timing, power, and noise *after* physical effects.

11. **Antenna, ERC, and DFM Checks**
    - Electrical Rule Check, antenna violation check, lithography-friendly design-for-manufacturability.

12. **Tape-Out (GDSII Generation)**
    - Final mask data is sent to the foundry (TSMC, GlobalFoundries, Samsung Foundry, etc.).

## 2.3 The Semi-Custom Design Flow — Step-by-Step

The semi-custom flow replaces the labor-intensive manual layout with *standard cells* and *automated place-and-route*.

1. **Specification** → 2. **RTL Design** (Verilog/VHDL) → 3. **Functional Simulation** → 4. **Logic Synthesis** (RTL → Gate-level netlist mapped to **Standard Cell Library** / **Gate Array**) → 5. **Design-for-Test (DFT) Insertion** (scan chains, BIST) → 6. **Formal Verification** (RTL ↔ synthesized netlist) → 7. **Floorplanning** → 8. **Placement** → 9. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)** → 10. **Routing** → 11. **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** → 12. **DRC / LVS** → 13. **GDSII Tape-Out**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The crucial difference: in **full-custom** the *cell itself* is designed; in **semi-custom** the *cell is selected from a library*. The KTU board regularly frames this as: *"Full-custom optimizes inside the cell; Semi-custom optimizes between the cells."*

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|--------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $N = A \cdot D$ | Die yield equation | Number of working dies $N$ from wafer area $A$ and defect density $D$ |
| 2 | $Y = e^{-D \cdot A}$ | Poisson Yield Model | Probability a die of area $A$ has zero defects at density $D$ |
| 3 | $C_{die} = \dfrac{C_{wafer}}{Y \cdot N_{dies/wafer}}$ | Die Cost | $C_{wafer}$ spread over yield $Y$ and gross dies per wafer |
| 4 | $N_{dies/wafer} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (D_{wafer}/2)^2}{A_{die}} - \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_{wafer}}{\sqrt{2 \cdot A_{die}}}$ | Dies per Wafer | Geometric packing formula (ignores edge losses beyond simple correction) |
| 5 | $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2 \pi (C_{gs} + C_{gb})}$ | Unity-Gain Frequency | Cut-off frequency of a single MOSFET (relevant to custom analog) |
| 6 | $t_{pd} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n \cdot (W/L) \cdot (V_{DD} - V_T)^2}$ | CMOS Propagation Delay | First-order delay model for a full-custom inverter |
| 7 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic Power | Per-gate power dissipation (key custom-design metric) |
| 8 | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Static (Leakage) Power | Critical for low-power custom design |
| 9 | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{min}}{2}$ | Lambda (Design Rule) | Half of the minimum feature size — scaling unit for DRC |
| 10 | $A_{cell} = f(N_{transistors}, \text{cell height} \times \text{cell pitch})$ | Cell Area | Custom layout's primary cost driver |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** Remember the acronym **"S-S-L-F"** for full-custom — **S**pecification → **S**chematic → **L**ayout → **F**abrication. For semi-custom, the acronym is **"S-S-P-R"** — **S**pecification → **S**ynthesis → **P**lacement → **R**outing. Examiners love acronyms.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Custom Flow is Used |
|--------------------|--------------------------|
| **Analog / RF ICs (5G transceivers, PLLs)** | Analog blocks cannot be discretized into standard cells; continuous-time behavior demands handcrafted transistor sizing and matching. |
| **SRAM / DRAM Memory** | Bit-cells are ultra-small (≈ 6T per bit). Full-custom layout minimizes area; bit density is the #1 economic driver. |
| **High-Performance CPUs (Apple M-series, AMD Zen)** | Critical paths are hand-tuned with custom standard cells and custom datapath layouts to push clock frequency to the limit. |
| **IoT / Ultra-Low-Power SoCs** | Custom design allows aggressive power gating, multi-Vt optimization, and body-biasing to hit nano-amp sleep currents. |
| **Automotive ASICs (ECU, ADAS)** | Long product life + high reliability + ISO-26262 compliance → prefer the predictability of custom flows over FPGA reconfigurability. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CMOS Inverter Propagation Delay (Full-Custom Benchmark Cell)

The CMOS inverter is the *most important* cell in any custom design library. KTU examiners frequently derive its delay equation. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation.

### Step 1: Define the Load Capacitance
The output node of an inverter drives a lumped load capacitance $C_L$ which includes the drain diffusion capacitance of the inverter itself, the gate capacitance of the fan-out inverters, and the interconnect (wire) capacitance.

$$C_L = C_{db,n} + C_{db,p} + C_{gate,FO} + C_{wire}$$

### Step 2: Define the Drive Current of the ON Transistor
During a low-to-high transition at the output, the PMOS device is ON. Using the long-channel MOSFET saturation current equation (with channel-length modulation neglected for simplicity):

$$I_{D,p} = \frac{k_p}{2} \cdot \frac{W_p}{L_p} \cdot (V_{GS} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2 = \frac{\mu_p \cdot C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W_p}{L_p} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2$$

The notation $\vert V_{Tp} \vert$ is used because PMOS threshold voltage is negative; its absolute magnitude enters the square.

### Step 3: Express the Discharge Time of the Capacitor
The output voltage rises from $0$ toward $V_{DD}$. Treating the current as approximately constant (the average-current method), the time to charge the capacitor through a voltage swing $\Delta V$ is:

$$\Delta t = \frac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{I_{avg}}$$

For a 50% delay measurement (the standard definition of $t_{pd}$), $\Delta V = V_{DD}/2$, and we approximate $I_{avg} \approx I_{D,p}$ evaluated at the midpoint:

$$I_{avg} \approx \frac{k_p}{2} \cdot \frac{W_p}{L_p} \cdot \left(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert\right)^2$$

### Step 4: Substitute to Obtain $t_{pLH}$ (low-to-high propagation delay)

$$t_{pLH} = \frac{C_L \cdot (V_{DD}/2)}{\dfrac{k_p}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W_p}{L_p} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2}$$

After algebraic simplification (multiplying numerator and denominator by 2):

$$t_{pLH} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_p \cdot \dfrac{W_p}{L_p} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2}$$

### Step 5: Similarly, $t_{pHL}$ (high-to-low) is governed by the NMOS:

$$t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n \cdot \dfrac{W_n}{L_n} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^2}$$

### Step 6: The Average Propagation Delay

$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$$

### Step 7: Design Insight from the Equation
From the derived expression, the *designer-controlled* parameters that reduce delay are:

- **Increase $\dfrac{W}{L}$** of the transistors → larger drive current → smaller delay.
- **Reduce $C_L$** → smaller fan-out, shorter wires, smaller diffusion areas.
- **Reduce $V_{DD}$** → conflicts with delay (lower $V_{DD}$ = slower), so this is a power-vs-speed trade-off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A full-custom designer will *explicitly* size the W/L of each transistor in a cell to meet a target $t_{pd}$. In a semi-custom flow, the W/L is fixed by the standard-cell library, and the designer can only swap to a different drive-strength cell (e.g., X1, X2, X4). This is the *fundamental* performance difference between the two flows.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Die-per-Wafer Formula (Custom Design Economics)

Custom design is expensive partly because mask costs (in modern nodes, ≈ \$1–\$10 million for 5 nm) must be amortized over the number of *working* dies produced. The number of gross dies per wafer is derived as follows.

### Step 1: Wafer Area
A circular wafer of diameter $D_{wafer}$ has area:

$$A_{wafer} = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{D_{wafer}}{2}\right)^2$$

### Step 2: Die Area
A square die of side $s$ has area $A_{die} = s^2$. The number that *naively* fits is:

$$N_{naive} = \frac{A_{wafer}}{A_{die}} = \frac{\pi \cdot (D_{wafer}/2)^2}{s^2}$$

### Step 3: Edge-Loss Correction
A strip of width $s$ around the wafer edge is unusable due to dicing and edge defects. The approximate area of this annulus is the wafer circumference times the die side:

$$A_{edge} \approx \pi \cdot D_{wafer} \cdot s$$

### Step 4: Subtract Edge Loss

$$N_{gross} = \frac{A_{wafer} - A_{edge}}{A_{die}} = \frac{\pi \cdot (D_{wafer}/2)^2 - \pi \cdot D_{wafer} \cdot s}{s^2}$$

### Step 5: Factor Out $s$ in Denominator and Simplify

$$N_{gross} = \frac{\pi}{s^2} \left[ \left(\frac{D_{wafer}}{2}\right)^2 - D_{wafer} \cdot s \right]$$

Pull out a common factor of $D_{wafer}$:

$$N_{gross} = \frac{\pi \cdot D_{wafer}}{s^2} \left[ \frac{D_{wafer}}{4} - s \right]$$

This is the **standard closed-form gross-die-per-wafer equation** used in KTU custom-design cost problems.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Full-Custom vs. Semi-Custom

This matrix is the **single most important table** to memorize for the 14-mark KTU question on design flow comparison.

| Design Attribute | Full-Custom | Semi-Custom (Standard Cell) | Semi-Custom (Gate Array) |
|------------------|-------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Cell Design Effort** | Manual transistor layout | Library cells (pre-designed) | Fixed master slices |
| **Transistor Sizing** | Free, continuous | Discrete choices (X1, X2, X4) | Fixed |
| **Wiring Layers** | All layers custom-routable | Custom-routable | Only top layers custom |
| **NRE Cost** | Very High (≈ \$M) | Medium (≈ \$100k–\$500k) | Low (≈ \$50k) |
| **Per-Die Cost (high vol.)** | **Lowest** | Medium | Medium-High |
| **Time-to-Market** | Longest (12–24 months) | Medium (6–12 months) | Shortest (3–6 months) |
| **Performance / Speed** | **Best** (custom tuned) | Good | Acceptable |
| **Power Efficiency** | **Best** | Good | Acceptable |
| **Area Utilization** | 90–100% | 70–85% | 50–70% |
| **Flexibility** | Unlimited | High | Limited to fixed base |
| **Best Suited For** | Analog, memory, high-perf. CPU | Digital ASICs, SoC blocks | Prototyping, low-volume |
| **Example** | SRAM bit-cell, Op-Amp, PLL | ARM Cortex-A53 core | Quick-turn ASIC for consumer |
| **EDA Tools** | Cadence Virtuoso, Synopsys Custom Compiler, HSPICE | Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus, Innovus | Synopsys Galaxy, Mentor Olympus |

## 3.4 The Lambda-Based Design Rule (Custom Layout's Mathematical Foundation)

In a custom flow, every layout polygon is dimensioned in *microns* or, more elegantly, in *lambda units* where $\lambda = L_{min}/2$ ($L_{min}$ = minimum channel length of the process). A lambda-based rule set is *scalable* — if the process shrinks, all rules scale by the same factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** If $L_{min} = 0.18 \;\mu m$ for a 180 nm process, then $\lambda = 0.09 \;\mu m$. A "min poly width = 2λ" rule becomes $0.18 \;\mu m$ in this process and automatically scales if the foundry migrates to a 130 nm process (where $\lambda = 0.065 \;\mu m$).

Common lambda-based rules a custom designer must obey:

| Rule | Lambda Expression | Physical Meaning |
|------|-------------------|------------------|
| Min poly width | $2\lambda$ | Minimum polysilicon line width |
| Min diffusion width | $3\lambda$ | Minimum active-area width |
| Poly-to-diffusion spacing | $1\lambda$ | Gate-to-source/drain overlap margin |
| Metal 1 width | $3\lambda$ | Minimum metal-1 line width |
| Metal 1 spacing | $3\lambda$ | Minimum gap between two metal-1 lines |
| Contact size | $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ | Minimum cut size (via) |
| Contact enclosure by metal | $1\lambda$ | Required overlap of metal over contact |

These rules are the *lingua franca* of full-custom layout and are tested in nearly every KTU board exam.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The Y-chart is the central conceptual model for *all* VLSI design flows. The custom design flow traverses the three axes at different levels of abstraction.

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA["System Level"]
    nodeB["RTL Logic Level"]
    nodeC["Circuit Transistor Level"]
    nodeD["Behavioral Domain"]
    nodeE["Structural Domain"]
    nodeF["Physical Geometric Domain"]

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeD --- nodeE
    nodeE --- nodeF

    nodeG["Full Custom Path"]
    nodeH["Semi Custom Path"]
    nodeI["FPGA Programmable Path"]

    nodeC -.- nodeG
    nodeB -.- nodeH
    nodeA -.- nodeI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chart:** Each axis represents a *view* (behavioral, structural, physical) and each concentric ring represents an *abstraction* (system, RTL, circuit). A *full-custom* designer works from the **outer rim** inward; a *semi-custom* designer works from the **middle ring**; an *FPGA* programmer works at the **innermost ring** (system level only, with no access to physical implementation).

## 4.2 Full-Custom Design Flow — Process Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["1 System Specification"]
    stepB["2 Architectural Design"]
    stepC["3 Logic Design and Behavioral HDL"]
    stepD["4 Transistor Level Schematic"]
    stepE["5 Circuit Simulation SPICE"]
    stepF["6 Custom Layout Design"]
    stepG["7 Design Rule Check DRC"]
    stepH["8 Layout vs Schematic LVS"]
    stepI["9 Parasitic Extraction PEX"]
    stepJ["10 Post Layout Simulation"]
    stepK["11 Antenna and ERC Checks"]
    stepL["12 GDSII Tape Out to Foundry"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL
```

> [!TIP]
