Memorize this trinity. - ✅ **Verilog's 4 values:** `0, 1, x, z` — `x` is a *bug detector*, not an error. - ✅ **VHDL's 9 values:** `U, X, 0, 1, Z, W, L, H, -` — `-` (don't care) is critical for synthesis. - ✅ **Three modeling styles:** Structural (gate-level), Dataflow (continuous assignment), Behavioral (procedural). - ✅ **`wire` vs `reg` in Verilog:** `reg` does **not** always mean a flip-flop; it means "assigned in a procedural block." - ✅ **`<=` vs `=` in Verilog:** Use `<=` in clocked `always` (non-blocking, for flip-flops); use `=` in combinational `always` (blocking, for plain logic). - ✅ **`signal` vs `variable` in VHDL:** `signal` updates at end of process; `variable` updates immediately. `signal` is global, `variable` is local. - ✅ **Sensitivity list rule:** Must include ALL signals read on the RHS — use `always @(*)` in Verilog and `process(all)` (VHDL-2008) for safety. - ✅ **Standard logic package** in VHDL: `IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164` — must include for any modern design. - ✅ **Synthesis rule:** Synthesis ignores sensitivity list; simulator uses it. Mismatch = bugs. - ✅ **Testbench blocks:** `initial` in Verilog, separate `process` in VHDL — both can drive stimulus. - ✅ **Concurrency math:** Discrete-event simulation with delta cycles — every signal change schedules a re-evaluation at the same simulated time. - ✅ **Standard exam phrases to recognize:** "Write HDL code," "Explain the design flow," "Compare VHDL and Verilog," "Differentiate signal and variable" — these are the four most common 14-mark question stems. - ✅ **KTU Internal Choice:** Part B offers A vs B. Practice both question types for the same module to maximize readiness. --- > **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — HARDWARE DESIGN USING HDL** > *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L1–L5.*

topics.title: Design Verification - Simulation Using Testbench
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Design Verification - Simulation Using Testbench # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Design Verification** is the systematic process of establishing that a hardware design (RTL, gate-level, or transistor-level) correctly implements the intended functionality as specified in the design specification. Under the KTU 2024 scheme (Outcome-Based Education framework), verification is treated as a *first-class engineering discipline*, distinct from validation. In the context of **RTL (Register Transfer Level) design**, verification is overwhelmingly performed using a **Testbench** — a non-synthesizable HDL construct (written in Verilog/SystemVerilog) that wraps around the **Design Under Test (DUT)** and performs three cardinal functions: 1. **Stimulus Generation** — applying input vectors (test patterns) to the DUT. 2. **Response Capture / Monitoring** — observing the DUT's outputs and internal states. 3. **Checking / Comparison** — comparing actual responses against expected (golden) values. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The phrase *"Simulation Using Testbench"* specifically refers to *dynamic, event-driven functional simulation* using HDL simulators (e.g., ModelSim, QuestaSim, Icarus Verilog, Vivado XSim). Formal verification and FPGA emulation are **outside** the scope of this module. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you have designed an **automatic coffee machine** (the **DUT**). Before shipping it, you must *verify* it works. You cannot just trust the design — you must *stimulate* it: - **Stimulus Block** → You press different buttons (espresso, cappuccino, hot water) at different times. This is the *driver* — it applies test vectors. - **DUT** → The coffee machine itself. It receives your button presses and produces a beverage. - **Monitor / Checker** → A quality-control inspector stands beside the machine, watching the output. Each time the machine produces a beverage, the inspector compares it with the *expected drink* (golden model). If wrong, the inspector raises a flag (error). - **Testbench** → The entire lab setup — the room, the test fixtures, the inspector, and the report-generation system — that surrounds the DUT and validates it. In Verilog/SystemVerilog terms, the `module` keyword is used to *declare* both the design and the testbench, but unlike the design, the testbench has **no external ports** — it is a *self-contained top-level harness* that *instantiates* the DUT. ### 1.3 Why Testbenches Are Non-Synthesizable > [!NOTE] > **Key Definition — Non-Synthesizable Code:** > Testbench constructs use simulation-only constructs such as `$display`, `$monitor`, `$finish`, `#delays`, `initial` blocks, file I/O, and dynamic constructs. These have **no physical hardware equivalent** — they are ignored by synthesis tools but fully supported by event-driven simulators. | Property | Design Module | Testbench Module | |---|---|---| | Has input/output ports | Yes | **No** (top-level) | | Synthesizable | Yes (RTL) | **No** | | Uses `initial` block | Generally not | **Yes — heavily** | | Uses `#` delays | Rarely | **Yes** | | Drives clocks | Receives clocks | **Generates clocks** | | Purpose | Implement logic | **Verify logic** | ### 1.4 The Verification Flow at a Glance The standard KTU 2024 verification flow proceeds as follows: 1. **Read specification** → derive verification plan. 2. **Write testbench** in Verilog/SystemVerilog. 3. **Compile** with HDL simulator. 4. **Run simulation** (event-driven time advancement). 5. **Observe waveforms** (VCD/FSDB files) or check **self-checking log output**. 6. **Debug** mismatches. 7. **Achieve coverage goals** (code coverage + functional coverage). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 70% Rule of Verification Effort:** Industry-standard practice (and the spirit of KTU's outcome-based pedagogy) is that verification consumes **~70% of the total design effort**, while design writing is only ~30%. A bug caught at the RTL stage costs ~10× less than one caught after fabrication. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Verilog Testbench — The Three Pillars A well-engineered testbench is decomposed into **three functional blocks**, each of which maps to a SystemVerilog class in advanced verification methodologies (UVM), or to discrete Verilog modules/tasks in KTU-level exam questions. ### Pillar 1: Stimulus Generator (Driver) - **Role:** Generate clock, reset, and directed/random input transactions. - **Key Constructs:** `initial` block, `always` block with `#delay`, `repeat`, `for` loops, `case` statements, `$random`, `$urandom`. - **Why needed:** The DUT is *passive* — without stimuli, the simulator will simply have a `x` (unknown) state. Stimuli *excite* the DUT into state transitions. ### Pillar 2: Design Under Test (DUT) Instance - **Role:** The RTL design being verified. - **Mechanism:** Plain Verilog `module instantiation` from within the testbench top module. - **Naming Convention in KTU exam answers:** Use `DUT uut (.clk(clk), .rst(rst), …);` — the alias `uut` stands for *Unit Under Test*. ### Pillar 3: Response Checker (Monitor / Scoreboard) - **Role:** Compare actual DUT outputs against expected outputs. - **Two flavours in KTU scope:** - **Manual checking via `$display` / `$monitor`** — prints values to console; *human* decides pass/fail. - **Self-checking testbench** — uses `if-else` logic to compare and `if(actual !== expected) $display("ERROR"); $finish;`. ## 2.2 Event-Driven Simulation Semantics Verilog uses a **discrete-event simulation** model. The simulator maintains a *time wheel* and a *list of pending events*. Time advances only when no more events are scheduled at the current time. **Simulation Time Strata (from the IEEE 1364 / 1800 LRM):** 1. **Active region** — Blocking assignments (`=`), continuous assignments, `$display` evaluations, primitive evaluations. 2. **Inactive region** — `#0` delays. 3. **NBA (Non-Blocking Assignment) region** — `<=` assignments are committed here, ensuring race-free RTL semantics. 4. **Postponed region** — `$monitor`, `$strobe`, `$finish`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** The order of `$display` (Active region) vs `$strobe` (Postponed region) frequently appears in KTU questions. `$strobe` prints the *final, settled* value of a signal at the end of the current time slot, whereas `$display` may print *stale* values for non-blocking-assigned signals. ## 2.3 Clock Generation Patterns ### Pattern A — Ideal Clock (Most common in KTU exam answers) ```verilog initial begin clk = 0; forever #5 clk = ~clk; // Period = 10 time units → 50% duty cycle end ``` ### Pattern B — Clock with Phase Delay ```verilog initial begin clk = 0; #3 forever #5 clk = ~clk; // Phase shift of 3 time units end ``` ### Pattern C — Finite-Duration Clock ```verilog initial begin clk = 0; repeat (10) #5 clk = ~clk; // 10 toggles → 5 full periods end ``` ## 2.4 Reset Generation Patterns A **synchronous or asynchronous reset** is applied to the DUT during the first few clock cycles. ```verilog initial begin rst = 1; // Assert reset #20; // Hold reset for 20 time units rst = 0; // De-assert reset end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Asynchronous Reset Caveat:** If reset is asynchronous, it must be released *synchronously* (in testbench: `#1; rst = 0;`) to avoid race conditions with the clock edge. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Verification Metrics | Metric / Equation | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Clock Period | $T_{clk} = 2 \times t_{half}$ | time units | `t_half` is the half-period delay | | Clock Frequency | $f_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | Hz / MHz / GHz | $f_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot t_{delay}}$ in testbench | | Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{t_{high}}{T_{clk}} \times 100$ | % | For symmetric clock, $D = 50\%$ | | Code Coverage | $C_{code} = \dfrac{\text{lines/branches hit}}{\text{total lines/branches}} \times 100$ | % | Statement, branch, toggle, FSM coverage | | Functional Coverage | $C_{func} = \dfrac{\text{cross-coverage bins hit}}{\text{total cross-bins defined}} \times 100$ | % | User-defined in covergroups | | Testbench Reusability | $R = \dfrac{\text{Number of DUTs verified by TB}}{\text{Number of TBs in project}}$ | ratio | Higher is better (DRY principle) | | Bug Escape Rate | $BER = \dfrac{\text{Bugs found post-silicon}}{\text{Total bugs}}$ | fraction | Target: $\rightarrow 0$ | | Simulation Time Advance | $\Delta t_{sim} = t_{next\_event} - t_{current}$ | time units | Discrete-event scheduler | > [!NOTE] > **All `time` quantities in Verilog testbenches use the simulator's default time scale.** By convention, KTU answers assume **1 ns = 1 time unit** unless stated otherwise. Use `` `timescale 1ns/1ps `` directive at the top of every testbench file. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pre-silicon validation:** Avoids the **$1M+ mask cost** of fabricating a faulty chip. - **Regression testing:** Testbenches become *regression suites* run nightly on thousands of test cases. - **Hardware-software co-verification:** Testbenches can host C-models via DPI (Direct Programming Interface) in SystemVerilog. - **Compliance testing:** Industry standards (PCIe, USB, DDR) mandate formal verification methodologies with structured testbenches. - **FPGA prototyping:** The same testbench can drive both simulation and real FPGA hardware via co-simulation (e.g., via JTAG). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Complete Worked Example — Verifying a 4-Bit Synchronous Counter This is a **canonical KTU exam problem**. We will derive a *self-checking* testbench step by step. ### Step 1 — The Design Under Test (DUT) ```verilog // 4-bit Synchronous Up Counter with synchronous reset and active-high enable module counter_4bit ( input wire clk, input wire rst, input wire enable, output reg [3:0] count ); always @(posedge clk) begin if (rst) count <= 4'b0000; else if (enable) count <= count + 1; else count <= count; end endmodule ``` ### Step 2 — Derive the Expected Behavior (Golden Model Logic) For verification, we must mathematically describe the expected count sequence: $$ \text{count}[n+1] = \begin{cases} 0000_2, & \text{if } rst = 1 \text{ at clock edge} \\ \text{count}[n] + 1 \pmod{16}, & \text{if } rst = 0 \text{ and } enable = 1 \\ \text{count}[n], & \text{if } rst = 0 \text{ and } enable = 0 \end{cases} $$ This piecewise definition is the *golden reference model*. ### Step 3 — Design the Stimulus Sequence We will apply **three phases**: 1. **Phase 1 — Reset active:** `rst = 1, enable = 0` for 25 ns → expect `count = 0000`. 2. **Phase 2 — Counting with enable high:** `rst = 0, enable = 1` for 16 clock cycles → expect 0, 1, 2, …, 15, 0, 1, …. 3. **Phase 3 — Counting frozen:** `enable = 0` → expect `count` to hold its last value. ### Step 4 — Construct the Self-Checking Testbench ```verilog `timescale 1ns/1ps module tb_counter_4bit; reg clk; reg rst; reg enable; wire [3:0] count; reg [3:0] expected; // ---- DUT Instance ---- counter_4bit DUT ( .clk (clk), .rst (rst), .enable (enable), .count (count) ); // ---- Clock Generator ---- initial begin clk = 0; forever #5 clk = ~clk; // 100 MHz clock end // ---- Stimulus + Self-Checker ---- integer errors; initial begin errors = 0; expected = 4'b0000; // Phase 1: Apply reset rst = 1; enable = 0; #23; // Hold reset just before clock edge if (count !== 4'b0000) begin $display("ERROR @%0t: Reset did not clear count. Got %b", $time, count); errors = errors + 1; end // Release reset @(negedge clk); rst = 0; enable = 1; expected = 4'b0000; // Phase 2: Count 0 -> 15 -> 0 (20 cycles) repeat (20) begin @(posedge clk); #1; // Sample after NBA region settles if (count !== expected) begin $display("ERROR @%0t: count=%b expected=%b", $time, count, expected); errors = errors + 1; end expected = expected + 1; end // Phase 3: Freeze count @(negedge clk); enable = 0; @(posedge clk); #1; if (count !== expected - 1) begin $display("ERROR @%0t: enable=0 should freeze count", $time); errors = errors + 1; end // ---- Final Report ---- if (errors == 0) $display("TEST PASSED at time %0t", $time); else $display("TEST FAILED with %0d errors at time %0t", errors, $time); $finish; end // ---- Waveform Dump ---- initial begin $dumpfile("counter.vcd"); $dumpvars(0, tb_counter_4bit); end // ---- Watchdog Timer ---- initial begin #1000 $display("TIMEOUT: Simulation exceeded 1000 ns"); $finish; end endmodule ``` ### Step 5 — Derivation of Clock Period from Testbench The clock is toggled with `#5` delay, meaning the half-period is **5 ns**. $$ T_{clk} = 2 \times t_{delay} = 2 \times 5\,\text{ns} = 10\,\text{ns} $$ $$ f_{clk} = \frac{1}{T_{clk}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}} = 100\,\text{MHz} $$ $$ \text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{t_{high}}{T_{clk}} \times 100 = \frac{5}{10} \times 100 = 50\% $$ ### Step 6 — Derivation of the Sample Delay The `#1` delay after `@(posedge clk)` ensures the sample point falls **after the NBA region** of the simulator, where non-blocking assignments are committed. Without this delay, the checker would observe the *old* value of `count`. $$ t_{sample} = t_{posedge} + t_{NBA} + \epsilon $$ where $\epsilon$ (here $= 1$ ns) is a small guard band to avoid race conditions. ### Step 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Testbench Patterns | Testbench Element | Verilog Construct | Purpose | Common Mistake | |---|---|---|---| | Clock | `forever #5 clk = ~clk;` | Periodic stimulus | Forgetting to use `initial` block | | Reset | `#20 rst = 0;` | Initialize DUT | Releasing reset asynchronously | | Apply input | `data = 8'hA5;` | Drive stimulus | Using `#delay` between bits incorrectly | | Sample output | `@(posedge clk); #1;` | Read after NBA | Sampling before NBA region | | Compare | `if (out !== expected)` | Functional check | Using `==` instead of `!==` (X-comparison) | | Report | `$display` | Console output | Missing `$finish` to halt simulator | | Waveform | `$dumpfile / $dumpvars` | VCD generation | Forgetting `0` for hierarchical dump | | Random | `$random(seed)` | Constrained stimuli | Using deterministic seed only | ### Step 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Checking Strategies | Strategy | Implementation | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---| | **Waveform Inspection** | `$dumpvars` + GTKWave | Visual, intuitive | Manual, error-prone, not scalable | | **`$display` / `$monitor`** | Print values to console | Simple, lightweight | Human must read & judge | | **Self-Checking** | `if (actual !== expected)` with `$finish` | Automated, repeatable | Requires golden model | | **Reference Model** | Behavioral `module` in parallel | Captures complex logic | Effort to write correctly | | **Assertion-Based** | `assert property` (SVA) | Localizes failure | Requires SystemVerilog | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Self-Checking Testbenches Are the Gold Standard.** KTU exam questions awarding **>7 marks** almost always expect a self-checking approach with explicit `if-else` error counting and a final pass/fail `$display`. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Testbench Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stimulus Generator
Driver] -->|Inputs| B[DUT
Design Under Test] B -->|Outputs| C[Response Monitor
Checker] D[Golden Reference Model] -->|Expected Values| C C -->|Pass or Fail| E[Report Generator
$display] F[Clock Generator] -->|clk| B G[Reset Generator] -->|rst| B F -.->|clk| C G -.->|rst| C ``` > The **driver** applies inputs, the **monitor** watches outputs, and the **golden model** (or inline expected-value logic) supplies the reference for comparison. ## 4.2 Verification Flow — From Specification to Sign-Off ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Design Specification] --> B[Verification Plan] B --> C[Write Testbench] C --> D[Compile HDL] D -->|Errors| C D -->|OK| E[Run Simulation] E --> F[Check Output] F -->|Mismatch| G[Debug] G --> C F -->|Match| H[Measure Coverage] H -->|Goal Met| I[Sign-Off] H -->|Goal Not Met| J[Add New Tests] J --> C ``` ## 4.3 Discrete-Event Simulation Time Wheel ```mermaid flowchart TB T0[Time 0 ns
Initial Evaluations] T5[Time 5 ns
Clock Toggles] T10[Time 10 ns
Clock Toggles NBA Commit] T15[Time 15 ns
Clock Toggles] T20[Time 20 ns
Reset Released] T0 --> T5 --> T10 --> T15 --> T20 style T10 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > At **T = 10 ns**, the second clock edge occurs. `always @(posedge clk)` blocks execute, and their non-blocking assignments are committed in the NBA region. A well-written testbench samples `count` *after* this region to read the updated value. ## 4.4 Detailed Self-Checking Testbench Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Stim[Stimulus Block] S1[Clock Generator
forever #5 clk = ~clk] S2[Reset Generator
initial begin rst = 1 #20 rst = 0 end] S3[Input Driver
repeat 20 cycles apply enable = 1] end subgraph DUT_Block[DUT] D1[counter_4bit module] end subgraph Check[Checker Block] M1[Sample on posedge clk] M2[Compare count vs expected] M3{count == expected?} M4[Increment errors] M5[Display PASS or FAIL] end S1 --> D1 S2 --> D1 S3 --> D1 D1 --> M1 M1 --> M2 M2 --> M3 M3 -->|No| M4 M3 -->|Yes| M5 M4 --> M5 ``` ## 4.5 Coverage Closure Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Define Covergroups] --> B[Run Simulation] B --> C[Collect Coverage] C --> D{Coverage >= 100%} D -->|Yes| E[Verification Sign-Off] D -->|No| F[Identify Uncovered Bins] F --> G[Add Constrained Random Tests] G --> B ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q1.** Differentiate between *synthesizable* and *non-synthesizable* Verilog code. Why is a testbench non-synthesizable? **Model Answer:** A **synthesizable** Verilog construct has a direct hardware mapping — flip-flops, gates, muxes — and can be translated by synthesis tools into a gate-level netlist. Examples: `always @(posedge clk)`, `case`, `if-else` with clear register inference. A **non-synthesizable** construct has no physical hardware equivalent and is *ignored* by synthesis tools but fully supported by simulators for testbench purposes. Examples: `` `timescale ``, `$display`, `$monitor`, `$finish`, `initial` blocks (in test context), file I/O (`$fopen`, `$fwrite`), `#delays`. A **testbench** is non-synthesizable because: 1. It is a *stimulus generator* — it drives inputs and checks outputs; there is no physical boundary. 2. It uses *simulation-only* constructs (`$display`, file I/O) to *report* results, which have no silicon equivalent. 3. It contains *no ports* at the top level — it is a self-contained harness surrounding the DUT. > **Marks distribution:** 1 mark for synth definition, 1 mark for non-synth definition, 1 mark for "why testbench is non-synth". --- **Q2.** Explain the role of `$monitor` and `$strobe` system tasks. Why might `$strobe` give more accurate results in a non-blocking-assignment-heavy design? **Model Answer:** `$monitor` continuously prints the values of its argument list whenever *any* of them change, evaluated in the **Active region** of the simulation cycle. Therefore, for signals assigned via non-blocking `<=`, `$monitor` may print *stale* values because the NBA region has not yet committed the new value. `$strobe` prints the values of its arguments only *once* at the end of the current time step, in the **Postponed region** — after the NBA region has settled. Hence `$strobe` shows the *final committed* values. In an RTL design full of non-blocking assignments (pipelined logic, FSMs, counters), `$strobe` gives accurate results, while `$monitor` may show race-condition artefacts. > **Marks distribution:** 1.5 marks for each function, 1 mark extra for the NBA-region argument. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** --- ### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Verilog testbench. List the major blocks and describe the role of each. **[7 Marks]** **b)** Write a complete self-checking Verilog testbench to verify a 4-bit synchronous up-counter with synchronous active-high reset and active-high enable. The testbench must: - Generate a 100 MHz clock, - Apply reset for at least 2 clock cycles, - Apply 20 counting cycles with `enable = 1`, - Freeze the counter for 3 cycles with `enable = 0`, - Count and report errors, and emit a final PASS / FAIL message. **[7 Marks]** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — Testbench Architecture [7 Marks]** A Verilog testbench is a *self-contained top-level module* that wraps around the Design Under Test (DUT) and verifies its functional correctness via dynamic simulation. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ TESTBENCH │ │ │ │ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ │ Stimulus │───▶│ DUT │──┐ │ │ │ Generator │ │ (Design) │ │ │ │ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ │ │ │ ▲ ▼ │ │ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ │ Golden │─────────────▶│ Response │ │ │ │ Reference │ │ Checker │ │ │ └──────────────┘ └──────┬───────┘ │ │ ▼ │ │ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ │ Report │ │ │ │ ($display) │ │ │ └──────────────┘ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` **Block-by-Block Description (6 marks — 1.5 each):** 1. **Stimulus Generator (Driver):** Produces clock (`forever #5 clk = ~clk;`), reset (`rst = 1; #20; rst = 0;`), and directed/random data inputs. Uses `initial` and `always` blocks with delays or event controls. 2. **Design Under Test (DUT):** The actual RTL design instantiated as `DUT uut (...)`. Receives stimuli and produces outputs. 3. **Golden Reference Model:** Computes the expected output for every input stimulus — either inline (e.g., `expected = expected + 1;`) or as a parallel behavioral module. 4. **Response Checker:** Samples DUT outputs (typically `@(posedge clk); #1;`) and compares with golden values using `if (actual !== expected) errors = errors + 1;`. The `!==` (case-inequality) is crucial to detect `x`/`z` mismatches. 5. **Report Generator:** Emits a summary message via `$display` and terminates simulation with `$finish`. --- **Part (b) — Self-Checking Testbench Code [7 Marks]** ```verilog `timescale 1ns/1ps module tb_counter; reg clk, rst, enable; wire [3:0] count; reg [3:0] expected; integer errors; // ---- DUT ---- counter_4bit DUT (.clk(clk), .rst(rst), .enable(enable), .count(count)); // ---- Clock: 100 MHz (period = 10 ns) ---- initial begin clk = 0; forever #5 clk = ~clk; end // ---- Stimulus + Checker ---- initial begin errors = 0; expected = 4'b0000; rst = 1; enable = 0; #25; // Hold reset for 2.5 clock cycles @(negedge clk); rst = 0; enable = 1; // ---- 20 cycles of counting ---- repeat (20) begin @(posedge clk); #1; if (count !== expected) begin $display("ERR @%0t: count=%b exp=%b", $time, count, expected); errors = errors + 1; end expected = expected + 1; end // ---- 3 cycles frozen ---- @(negedge clk); enable = 0; repeat (3) begin @(posedge clk); #1; if (count !== expected - 1) begin $display("ERR @%0t: count=%b should hold %b", $time, count, expected - 1); errors = errors + 1; end end // ---- Report ---- if (errors == 0) $display("TEST PASSED at %0t", $time); else $display("TEST FAILED with %0d errors at %0t", errors, $time); $finish; end // ---- Waveform ---- initial begin $dumpfile("counter.vcd"); $dumpvars(0, tb_counter); end endmodule ``` **Design Under Test (for completeness, 1 mark):** ```verilog module counter_4bit ( input wire clk, rst, enable, output reg [3:0] count ); always @(posedge clk) begin if (rst) count <= 4'b0000; else if (enable) count <= count + 1; else count <= count; end endmodule ``` > **Mark Allocation (Part b):** Clock generation (1) + Reset sequence (1) + DUT instantiation (1) + Self-checking loop (2) + Final report + `$finish` (1) + Waveform dump (0.5) + Comments/indentation (0.5). --- ### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **a)** Explain the *discrete-event simulation* model used by Verilog. With a timing diagram, illustrate the four regions: Active, Inactive, NBA, and Postponed. Why is the NBA region critical for race-free RTL design? **[7 Marks]** **b)** Write a Verilog testbench to verify a 2-to-4 decoder using exhaustive input patterns. The testbench must include: - A clock-free stimulus pattern (use `#10` delays only), - A self-checking mechanism with an `if-else` block, - Use `$finish` after exhausting all 4 input combinations, - Print a final pass/fail summary. **[7 Marks]** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — Discrete-Event Simulation Model [7 Marks]** Verilog uses **discrete-event simulation**, in which the simulator maintains a *time-ordered event queue*. Time advances in discrete steps only when no events are scheduled at the current time. **The Four Regions (2 marks — diagram + 2 marks description):** ``` Time ──────────────────────────────────────────────▶ ┌──────────┐ ┌───────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ t = T│ Active │ │ Inactive │ │ NBA │ │Postponed │ │ region │→ │ region │→ │ region │→ │ region │ └──────────┘ └───────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ ``` 1. **Active Region:** Blocking assignments (`=`), continuous assignments (`assign`), `always` block evaluations triggered by level-sensitive events, `$display` evaluations. 2. **Inactive Region:** `#0` delays and explicit zero-delay scheduling land here. 3. **NBA (Non-Blocking Assignment) Region:** All non-blocking assignments (`<=`) scheduled in the Active region are *committed* here, reading the value of RHS evaluated earlier and updating the LHS atomically. 4. **Postponed Region:** `$monitor`, `$strobe`, `$finish` execute here — by this point, all signal values for the current time slot have fully settled. **Why NBA is Critical for Race-Free RTL [3 marks]:** In an RTL design with multiple `always` blocks, if all assignments used `=` (blocking), the *order of block execution* would determine final values, causing simulation-synthesis mismatch and race conditions. By using `<=`, the RHS is *sampled* in the Active region but the LHS is *updated* in the NBA region, ensuring: - **Determinism:** All RHS values reflect the *pre-edge* state. - **Race-free:** Two `always` blocks writing to the same signal at the same edge see consistent values. - **Synthesizability:** NBA maps directly to flip-flop behavior in hardware. A classic example is a non-blocking register transfer: ```verilog always @(posedge clk) begin a <= b; // samples b at edge b <= a; // samples old a at edge end ``` With blocking `=`, this would be order-dependent; with `<=`, swap happens correctly. --- **Part (b) — Decoder Testbench [7 Marks]** ```verilog `timescale 1ns/1ps module tb_decoder_2to4; reg [1:0] in; wire [3:0] out; integer i, errors; // ---- DUT ---- decoder_2to4 DUT (.in(in), .out(out)); // ---- Stimulus + Self-Check ---- initial begin errors = 0; for (i = 0; i < 4; i = i + 1) begin in = i[1:0]; #10; // Allow combinational settle case (in) 2'b00: if (out !== 4'b0001) begin $display("ERR @%0t: in=%b out=%b exp=0001", $time, in, out); errors = errors + 1; end 2'b01: if (out !== 4'b0010) begin $display("ERR @%0t: in=%b out=%b exp=0010", $time, in, out); errors = errors + 1; end 2'b10: if (out !== 4'b0100) begin $display("ERR @%0t: in=%b out=%b exp=0100", $time, in, out); errors = errors + 1; end 2'b11: if (out !== 4'b1000) begin $display("ERR @%0t: in=%b out=%b exp=1000", $time, in, out); errors = errors + 1; end endcase end if (errors == 0) $display("DECODER TEST PASSED"); else $display("DECODER TEST FAILED with %0d errors", errors); $finish; end endmodule // ---- Design ---- module decoder_2to4 ( input wire [1:0] in, output wire [3:0] out ); assign out = (in == 2'b00) ? 4'b0001 : (in == 2'b01) ? 4'b0010 : (in == 2'b10) ? 4'b0100 : 4'b1000; endmodule ``` > **Mark Allocation (Part b):** `for` loop covering 4 patterns (1) + `#10` settle delay (0.5) + Self-checking `if-else` (3) + `$finish` (0.5) + Pass/Fail summary (1) + DUT code (1). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting `$finish`** — the simulator runs forever, and the examiner does not see any output. **−2 marks.** > 2. **Using `==` instead of `!==`** — bit-wise X and Z values are not detected. **−1 mark per occurrence.** > 3. **Sampling output *before* the NBA region** (i.e., immediately at `@(posedge clk)` without a small `#1` guard). **−1 to −2 marks.** > 4. **Clock period error:** Writing `#10 clk = ~clk;` thinking it gives a 10 ns period, when actually it gives a 20 ns period. The correct period is $2 \times \text{delay}$. **−1 mark.** > 5. **No `$dumpvars` / `$dumpfile`:** Waveform dump missing; examiner cannot verify behaviour. **−0.5 to −1 mark** in 7+ mark questions. > 6. **Missing `timescale directive:** Without it, the simulator uses a default scale that may not match expected timing. **−0.5 mark.** > 7. **Reset applied for too few cycles** — DUT may not initialize correctly. Examiner expects *at least 2 clock cycles* of reset for synchronous designs. **−1 mark.** > 8. **No comments** — the examiner cannot trace your logic. In KTU 2024, "self-explanatory code" is not acceptable. **−0.5 to −1 mark.** --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision — Master These Before the Exam** - ✅ A **testbench** is a *non-synthesizable*, *portless*, top-level Verilog module whose sole purpose is to **verify** a DUT. - ✅ The **three pillars** of any testbench are: **(1) Stimulus Generator**, **(2) DUT Instance**, **(3) Response Checker**. - ✅ **Clock** is generated using `initial begin clk = 0; forever #T/2 clk = ~clk; end`. Period $= 2 \times$ half-period delay. - ✅ **Reset** is held for **at least 2 clock cycles** to ensure proper initialization. - ✅ Sampling must occur **after the NBA region** — use `@(posedge clk); #1;` to avoid race conditions. - ✅ Use **`!==`** (4-state inequality) for checker comparisons, not `!=` or `==`. - ✅ **`$display`** runs in the Active region; **`$strobe`** runs in the Postponed region and shows final committed values. - ✅ **`$monitor`** continuously prints when arguments change; `$strobe` prints once at the end of the time slot. - ✅ **Self-checking testbenches** with `errors` counter + final `$display("TEST PASSED/FAILED")` + `$finish` is the **gold standard** for full-mark answers. - ✅ **`$dumpfile` / `$dumpvars`** generate VCD files for waveform viewing in GTKWave / ModelSim. - ✅ The **discrete-event simulation** model has 4 regions: **Active → Inactive → NBA → Postponed**. - ✅ **Non-blocking assignments `<=`** sample in Active, update in NBA — guaranteeing race-free RTL semantics. - ✅ Coverage metrics: **Code Coverage** (lines/branches hit) + **Functional Coverage** (user-defined bins). - ✅ A testbench should include a **watchdog timer** to prevent infinite simulation (`#MAX_TIME $finish;`). - ✅ Exhaustive testing is feasible only for small DUTs; for large DUTs, use **constrained random** + **functional coverage**. ---

topics.title: Property Checking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Property Checking # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Property Checking** is a formal verification methodology in VLSI Design that mathematically proves whether a hardware design (modeled as a finite-state machine or transition system) satisfies a set of formally specified properties (expressed in temporal logic) by exhaustively exploring all reachable states of the design. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, property checking is positioned under the broader umbrella of **Functional Verification**, complementing dynamic simulation. While simulation checks the design on a finite set of input vectors, property checking verifies the design over **all possible input sequences and state combinations** within a bounded depth. ### The Two Pillars of Property Checking | Pillar | Description | |---|---| | **Model** | A mathematically precise representation of the design (RTL, gate-level, or transistor-level netlist) | | **Property** | A formal specification of intended behavior, expressed in a temporal logic (LTL, CTL, SVA, PSL) | > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 emphasizes that property checking is the **cornerstone of formal verification** and is heavily tested under CO3 (Apply formal methods for design verification) in Module 3. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation) Imagine you have purchased a brand new **automatic washing machine**. - **Simulation** is like testing the machine with a few sets of clothes — silk, cotton, denim. You verify it works *on those specific loads*. - **Property Checking** is like reading the *engineering blueprint* and mathematically proving that **for ANY load, ANY water level, and ANY program cycle**, the machine will never open its door while the drum is spinning (a safety property), and that the program will *eventually* finish (a liveness property). > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** Property checking doesn't run testbenches. It asks: *"Is it mathematically IMPOSSIBLE for the design to violate this rule?"* If the answer is yes, the property holds for every conceivable input — forever. ## 1.3 Why Property Checking Matters in VLSI - **Corner-case Bug Detection:** Catches bugs that simulation misses (e.g., bus contention, FIFO overflow, deadlock). - **Exhaustive State Coverage:** No stimulus-generation blind spots. - **Shorter Verification Cycles:** Once the property is written, no additional testbench development is needed. - **Compliance:** Mandated for safety-critical domains (automotive ISO 26262, avionics DO-254, medical IEC 62304). ## 1.4 Property Checking in the KTU 2024 Verification Flow $$ \text{Verification Methodology} = \underbrace{\text{Simulation}}_{\text{Dynamic}} + \underbrace{\text{Property Checking (Model Checking)}}_{\text{Static, Formal}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping: > - **CO3:** Apply property specification languages to express design intent. > - **CO4:** Analyze model checking algorithms and counter-example interpretation. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Property Checking Pipeline Property checking is executed in **five sequential phases**, each critical to the KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus. ### Phase 1 — Model Construction The design (Verilog/VHDL RTL) is converted into a formal model $\mathcal{M}$, typically a **Kripke Structure**. $$ \mathcal{M} = (S, S_0, R, L) $$ Where: - $S$ = Finite set of states (memory elements + primary inputs encoded) - $S_0 \subseteq S$ = Set of initial states - $R \subseteq S \times S$ = Transition relation - $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ = Labeling function (maps each state to a set of atomic propositions that hold in it) - $AP$ = Set of atomic propositions (signals/conditions in the design) ### Phase 2 — Property Specification The intended behavior is written as a temporal logic formula $\varphi$. Two dominant logics exist: | Logic | Full Name | Branching Model | Operator Symbols | |---|---|---|---| | **LTL** | Linear Temporal Logic | Linear (single path) | $\mathbf{G}, \mathbf{F}, \mathbf{X}, \mathbf{U}, \mathbf{R}$ | | **CTL** | Computation Tree Logic | Branching (tree of paths) | $\mathbf{AG}, \mathbf{AF}, \mathbf{AX}, \mathbf{EG}, \mathbf{EF}, \mathbf{EX}, \mathbf{AU}, \mathbf{EU}$ | > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 specifies that students must be able to **distinguish LTL from CTL** and write formulas for common bus protocols (AMBA AXI, APB). ### Phase 3 — Model Checking Algorithm The model checker explores the state space and determines: $$ \mathcal{M}, s \models \varphi \quad \text{(M satisfies phi at state s)} $$ The algorithm is recursive and uses **fixed-point computation** for CTL and **automata-theoretic** techniques for LTL. ### Phase 4 — Verification Result - **Property Holds:** $\mathcal{M} \models \varphi$ — proof certificate generated. - **Property Fails:** Counter-example (witness trace) is produced. ### Phase 5 — Counter-Example Analysis A counter-example is a concrete sequence of input values and state transitions that violates $\varphi$. The engineer debugs the RTL based on this trace. ## 2.2 The Two Fundamental Property Classes > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the single most heavily tested concept in KTU Module 3 — must be memorized.** ### A. Safety Properties ("Something bad never happens") - They state that the system **never** enters an undesirable state. - Violated by a *finite* counter-example. - Example: *"Grant and Request are never asserted simultaneously"* $$ \mathbf{G} \,(\neg (\text{grant} \land \text{request})) $$ ### B. Liveness Properties ("Something good eventually happens") - They state that the system **must** eventually reach a desirable state. - Can only be violated by an *infinite* counter-example (a loop). - Example: *"Every request is eventually granted"* $$ \mathbf{G} \,(\text{request} \rightarrow \mathbf{F} \, \text{grant}) $$ ### C. Fairness Properties (KTU Bonus) Used to prune unfair paths in liveness proofs. Expressed as: $$ \mathbf{G} \, \mathbf{F} \, \phi \quad \text{(phi is infinitely often true)} $$ ## 2.3 The Classic Mutual Exclusion Verification (CTL) Consider two processes $P_1, P_2$ sharing a critical section. The mutual exclusion property in CTL is: $$ \varphi = \mathbf{AG} \, \neg (P_1.\text{crit} \land P_2.\text{crit}) $$ This means: *On all paths (A), in all states globally (G), the two processes are never simultaneously in the critical section.* ## 2.4 Property Specification Languages (PSL) | Language | Origin | Used In | |---|---|---| | **PSL (Property Specification Language)** | Accellera (IEEE 1850) | Industry standard, multi-language | | **SVA (SystemVerilog Assertions)** | IEEE 1800 | SystemVerilog designs | | **CTL/LTL** | Academic | Model checkers (NuSMV, SPIN) | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — every KTU Module 3 question maps back to these operators and formulas.** ### Temporal Logic Operator Summary | Operator | LTL Meaning | CTL Equivalent | Plain English | |---|---|---|---| | $\mathbf{G} \phi$ | Globally $\phi$ | $\mathbf{AG} \phi$ | $\phi$ holds in every future state | | $\mathbf{F} \phi$ | Finally / Eventually $\phi$ | $\mathbf{AF} \phi$ | $\phi$ will hold at some future state | | $\mathbf{X} \phi$ | Next $\phi$ | $\mathbf{AX} \phi$ | $\phi$ holds in the next state | | $\phi \, \mathbf{U} \, \psi$ | $\phi$ Until $\psi$ | $\mathbf{A}(\phi \mathbf{U} \psi)$ | $\phi$ holds until $\psi$ becomes true | | $\phi \, \mathbf{R} \, \psi$ | $\phi$ Releases $\psi$ | — | $\psi$ holds until $\phi$ (or forever) | | $\mathbf{G} \mathbf{F} \phi$ | Fairness | — | $\phi$ occurs infinitely often | ### State-Space Complexity Bound $$ N_{states} = 2^{\sum_{i=1}^{k} b_i} $$ Where $k$ is the number of memory elements (flip-flops/latches) and $b_i$ is the bit-width of the $i$-th element. For $n$ 1-bit FFs, $N_{states} = 2^n$ — this is the **state explosion problem**. ### Bounded Model Checking (BMC) Depth Formula $$ \text{BMC} : \text{check} \, \mathcal{M} \models \mathbf{E}\mathbf{F}_{\leq k} \neg \phi $$ The property is checked up to a depth bound $k$, using a SAT solver. The formula is unrolled $k$ times. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **AMBA AXI Protocol Verification:** Properties ensure that `WVALID` is asserted only when `AWVALID` is asserted and the channel is ready. - **FIFO Correctness:** Properties verify no underflow/overflow, no data loss, ordered delivery. - **Power-Sequencer Verification:** Liveness properties ensure wake-up sequences always complete. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Modeling a 2-Bit Counter as a Kripke Structure A 2-bit up-counter has the following behavior: $\text{count}_{t+1} = (\text{count}_t + 1) \mod 4$. Let atomic propositions be $AP = \{a, b, c\}$ where: - $a$ = "counter is 0" - $b$ = "counter is 1 or 2" - $c$ = "counter is 3" ### Step 1: Enumerate States The counter has 2 flip-flops, so: $$ N_{states} = 2^2 = 4 \implies S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\} $$ Mapping: $s_0 = 00, s_1 = 01, s_2 = 10, s_3 = 11$. ### Step 2: Define Initial States $$ S_0 = \{s_0\} \quad \text{(counter resets to 0)} $$ ### Step 3: Build Transition Relation $R$ $$ R = \{(s_0, s_1), (s_1, s_2), (s_2, s_3), (s_3, s_0)\} $$ ### Step 4: Define Labeling Function $L$ $$ L(s_0) = \{a\}, \quad L(s_1) = \{b\}, \quad L(s_2) = \{b\}, \quad L(s_3) = \{c\} $$ The full Kripke structure is $\mathcal{M} = (\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}, \{s_0\}, R, L)$. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: CTL Model Checking of Mutual Exclusion Consider two concurrent processes $P_1$ and $P_2$, each can be in states `N` (non-critical) or `C` (critical). The transition system is: $$ S = \{N_1N_2, \, C_1N_2, \, N_1C_2, \, C_1C_2\} $$ The system has a hardware lock variable $L \in \{0, 1, 2\}$. The transition rules are: - From $N_1N_2$: $P_1$ can take lock → $C_1N_2$ (sets $L=1$) - From $N_1N_2$: $P_2$ can take lock → $N_1C_2$ (sets $L=2$) - From $C_1N_2$: $P_1$ releases → $N_1N_2$ - From $N_1C_2$: $P_2$ releases → $N_1N_2$ - **$C_1C_2$ is unreachable** (mutual exclusion enforced) ### Property to Check $$ \varphi = \mathbf{AG} \, \neg (C_1 \land C_2) $$ ### Step 1: Identify States Violating $\neg (C_1 \land C_2)$ The atomic sub-formula $\neg (C_1 \land C_2)$ fails only in state $C_1C_2$. So states satisfying $\neg (C_1 \land C_2)$ are: $$ S_{sat} = \{N_1N_2, \, C_1N_2, \, N_1C_2\} $$ ### Step 2: Check Initial State Is $S_0 \subseteq S_{sat}$? If the initial state is $N_1N_2$, yes. So $\mathcal{M}, N_1N_2 \models \neg (C_1 \land C_2)$ holds. ### Step 3: Recursive CTL Check (Sat($\mathbf{AG} \phi$)) We need to verify that for all reachable states, $\neg (C_1 \land C_2)$ holds globally. This is a fixed-point computation: $$ \text{Sat}(\mathbf{AG} \phi) = \text{Sat}(\phi) \cap \text{Pre}(\text{Sat}(\mathbf{AG} \phi)) $$ Where $\text{Pre}(Y)$ is the set of states with at least one successor in $Y$. ### Step 4: Convergence Starting with $Y_0 = S_{sat}$: $$ Y_1 = S_{sat} \cap \text{Pre}(Y_0) $$ Since $C_1C_2$ is unreachable, no transition enters it, so $Y_1 = Y_0 = \{N_1N_2, C_1N_2, N_1C_2\}$. **Conclusion:** The fixed point converges, and $\mathcal{M} \models \mathbf{AG} \neg (C_1 \land C_2)$ holds. **Mutual exclusion is verified.** ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: LTL Verification of a Simple Traffic Light A traffic light cycles Green → Yellow → Red → Green. Define $G$ = "Green is on", $Y$ = "Yellow is on", $R$ = "Red is on". ### Property 1 (Safety): Red and Green are never simultaneously on $$ \varphi_1 = \mathbf{G} \, \neg (R \land G) $$ ### Property 2 (Liveness): Once Red is observed, Green will eventually appear $$ \varphi_2 = \mathbf{G} \, (R \rightarrow \mathbf{F} \, G) $$ ### Property 3 (Liveness, Stronger): Every state cycles infinitely $$ \varphi_3 = \mathbf{G} \mathbf{F} \, G \land \mathbf{G} \mathbf{F} \, R $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Verification Approaches | Parameter | Simulation | Property Checking | Emulation | |---|---|---|---| | **Coverage** | Sampled (incomplete) | Exhaustive (full state space) | Sampled (real hardware) | | **Stimulus Required** | Yes (testbench) | No | Yes (real vectors) | | **Speed** | Slow for deep logic | Fast for control logic | Very fast | | **Capacity** | Unlimited | Limited by state explosion | Unlimited | | **Bug Class Caught** | Functional on tests | Corner-case, deadlocks, races | System-level issues | | **Counter-example** | Logged waveform | Formal witness trace | Waveform capture | | **When to Use** | Early RTL bring-up | Mature RTL, protocol blocks | Full-chip, software-ware co-simulation | ## 3.5 Derivation: State Explosion Mitigation via Cone of Influence (COI) Reduction Given a property $\varphi$ referencing signals $\{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_m\}$, the **Cone of Influence** is the transitive closure of all flip-flops that can affect any $s_i$. Let the full design have $n$ flip-flops and the COI contain $n'$ flip-flops. The state-space reduction is: $$ \frac{N_{reduced}}{N_{full}} = \frac{2^{n'}}{2^n} = 2^{n'-n} $$ For a property that only references 5 signals in a 1M-FF design, $n'$ may be 50, giving: $$ \text{Ratio} = 2^{-999950} $$ This is why **property scoping is critical** to practical model checking. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Property Checking Verification Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[RTL Design
Verilog VHDL] --> nodeB[Model Builder
Compiler Frontend] nodeC[Property Spec
PSL SVA CTL LTL] --> nodeD[Property Loader
Formula Parser] nodeB --> nodeE[Formal Model
Kripke Structure M] nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF[State Space Explorer
Model Checker Engine] nodeF --> nodeG{Property Holds?} nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH[Proof Certificate
Property SAT] nodeG -- No --> nodeI[Counter-Example
Witness Trace] nodeH --> nodeJ[Verification Report
Pass] nodeI --> nodeK[RTL Debug
Waveform Analysis] nodeK --> nodeL[Fix Design] nodeL --> nodeA ``` ## 4.2 Architecture of a Generic Model Checker ```mermaid flowchart LR inp1[Design .v .vhd] --> parseA[HDL Parser] inp2[Property .sva .psl] --> parseB[Property Parser] parseA --> model[Kripke Model M] parseB --> tlogic[Temporal Logic Formula] model --> engine[Verification Engine] tlogic --> engine engine --> algo{Algorithm Type} algo --> bmc[Bounded Model Checking
SAT Solver] algo --> ico[IC3 PDR Engine] algo --> sym[Symbolic BDD Engine] bmc --> result ico --> result sym --> result result[YES or NO plus Counter-Example] --> out[Output to User] ``` ## 4.3 Property Classification Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Property phi] --> q1{Does it state
something bad never happens?} q1 -- Yes --> safety[SAFETY PROPERTY
G not bad] q1 -- No --> q2{Does it state
something good eventually happens?} q2 -- Yes --> liveness[LIVENESS PROPERTY
F good or G implies F] q2 -- No --> q3{Is it about
infinite occurrence?} q3 -- Yes --> fairness[FAIRNESS PROPERTY
GF condition] q3 -- No --> complex[COMPLEX PROPERTY
Combination of above] safety --> logic1[Express in LTL or CTL AG] liveness --> logic2[Express in LTL F or CTL AF] fairness --> logic3[Express in LTL GF] complex --> logic4[Decompose into
sub-properties] ``` ## 4.4 Property Checking in the SoC Verification Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Layer1[Block Level] A1[IP Module 1 Model Check] A2[IP Module 2 Model Check] end subgraph Layer2[Cluster Level] B1[Subsystem Assertion-Based Verification] end subgraph Layer3[SoC Level] C1[Full Chip Property Checking with Abstraction] end Layer1 --> Layer2 Layer2 --> Layer3 A1 --> tool[Commercial Tools
Cadence JasperGold
Synopsys VC Formal
Siemens Questa] A2 --> tool B1 --> tool C1 --> tool tool --> report[Unified Verification Dashboard] ``` ## 4.5 The CTL Operator Tree Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TD root[Path Quantifier A or E] root --> all[AG AF AX All Paths] root --> exists[EG EF EX Some Path] all --> state[State Quantifier G F X U] exists --> state state --> atomic[Atomic Proposition
Signal Boolean Expr] ``` ## 4.6 Counter-Example Trace Anatomy ```mermaid gantt title Counter-Example Trace for Property Violation dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Clock Cycles Cycle 0 Initial State :a0, 0, 1ms Cycle 1 Transition 1 :a1, 1, 1ms Cycle 2 Transition 2 :a2, 2, 1ms Cycle 3 VIOLATION State :crit, 3, 1ms Cycle 4 Property Fails Here :fail, 4, 1ms ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023] > Differentiate between **Safety** and **Liveness** properties in property checking. Give one VLSI example of each. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Safety Property | Liveness Property | |---|---|---| | Guarantee | "Bad thing never happens" | "Good thing eventually happens" | | Counter-example | Finite trace | Infinite trace (loop) | | Logic form | $\mathbf{G} \neg \phi$ | $\mathbf{G} \phi \rightarrow \mathbf{F} \psi$ or $\mathbf{F} \phi$ | | VLSI Example | Grant and Request never asserted together | Every request is eventually granted | | VLSI Example 2 | FIFO never overflows | Reset is eventually deasserted | **Conclusion:** Both property classes are essential — safety ensures correctness, liveness ensures progress. A complete verification suite must include both. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] > What is the **state explosion problem** in model checking? List two techniques to mitigate it. **Model Answer:** The state explosion problem refers to the exponential growth of the state space with the number of memory elements: $$ \text{State Count} = 2^{\sum b_i} $$ For a design with $n$ 1-bit flip-flops, the state space is $2^n$ states, which becomes infeasible for $n > 50$ with explicit-state methods. **Mitigation Techniques:** 1. **Cone of Influence (COI) Reduction** — Remove state variables that do not affect the property being checked. 2. **Symbolic Model Checking using BDDs** — Represent sets of states as Boolean functions rather than explicit enumerations. 3. **Abstraction** — Replace concrete variables with abstract ones (e.g., data values → intervals). 4. **Bounded Model Checking (BMC)** — Limit the search depth to a finite bound $k$, offloading the work to a SAT solver. 5. **Partial Order Reduction** — Eliminate interleavings of independent concurrent events. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] > **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the architecture and working of a **Model Checker**. Describe the phases from design input to counter-example generation with a neat diagram. > > **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Consider a 2-process mutual exclusion system modeled as a Kripke structure with states $S = \{N_1N_2, C_1N_2, N_1C_2\}$ (state $C_1C_2$ is unreachable). Verify the CTL property $\varphi = \mathbf{AG} \neg (C_1 \land C_2)$ using the fixed-point algorithm. #### Model Solution for Part (a): **Architecture of a Model Checker (7 marks breakdown):** 1. **Frontend / Parser Module [2 marks]:** Reads HDL (Verilog/VHDL), builds an internal intermediate representation (IR), then constructs the Kripke structure $\mathcal{M} = (S, S_0, R, L)$. 2. **Property Loader [1 mark]:** Parses the property file (PSL/SVA/CTL/LTL) into a syntax tree and converts temporal logic formulas into an internal canonical form (e.g., negation normal form). 3. **Verification Engine [3 marks]:** The core algorithm. Implements one or more of: - Explicit-state DFS/BFS (e.g., SPIN for LTL) - Symbolic BDD-based fixed-point (e.g., NuSMV for CTL) - SAT-based Bounded Model Checking (BMC) - IC3 / Property-Directed Reachability (PDR) 4. **Backend / Reporter [1 mark]:** Outputs either a proof certificate (pass) or a counter-example trace (fail). The trace includes clock cycles, signal values, and a path through the state space. **Working Phases:** - Phase 1: Model $\mathcal{M}$ is built from RTL. - Phase 2: Property $\varphi$ is parsed. - Phase 3: State space is explored using one of the algorithms. - Phase 4: Fixed-point or SAT solver determines $\mathcal{M} \models \varphi$. - Phase 5: Result is reported with optional counter-example. #### Model Solution for Part (b): **Given:** Kripke structure with $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$, $S_0 = \{s_0\}$, transitions as described. **Property:** $\varphi = \mathbf{AG} \neg (C_1 \land C_2)$ **Step 1 [2 marks]:** Identify atomic sub-formula evaluation. Let $\psi = \neg (C_1 \land C_2)$. The set of states satisfying $\psi$: $$ \text{Sat}(\psi) = \{N_1N_2, C_1N_2, N_1C_2\} = S $$ Since $C_1C_2$ is not in $S$, all states in $S$ satisfy $\psi$. **Step 2 [2 marks]:** Compute the set of states where $\mathbf{AG} \psi$ holds using the fixed-point characterization: $$ \text{Sat}(\mathbf{AG} \psi) = \bigcap_{i \geq 0} Y_i $$ where $Y_0 = S$ and $Y_{i+1} = \text{Sat}(\psi) \cap \text{Pre}(Y_i)$. **Step 3 [2 marks]:** Compute iterations. - $Y_0 = S = \{N_1N_2, C_1N_2, N_1C_2\}$ - $\text{Pre}(Y_0) = \{N_1N_2, C_1N_2, N_1C_2\}$ (all states have a successor in $Y_0$ since $C_1C_2$ is unreachable) - $Y_1 = S \cap \text{Pre}(Y_0) = S$ - Fixed point reached at $i = 0$. **Step 4 [1 mark]:** Check initial state membership: $S_0 = \{N_1N_2\} \subseteq \text{Sat}(\mathbf{AG} \psi)$. ✓ **Conclusion [optional]:** $\mathcal{M}, N_1N_2 \models \mathbf{AG} \neg (C_1 \land C_2)$. **Property verified.** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] > **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare **LTL** and **CTL** in detail. Give the syntax and semantics of at least four temporal operators in each. State one property that can be expressed in CTL but NOT in LTL, and vice-versa. > > **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Write LTL formulas for the following AMBA APB protocol properties: > (i) Once asserted, `PREADY` must remain high until the transfer completes. > (ii) The bus is never granted to two masters simultaneously. > (iii) After a reset, the system must eventually enter an idle state. #### Model Solution for Part (a): **Comparison Table [3 marks]:** | Feature | LTL | CTL | |---|---|---| | Model | Linear (single path) | Branching (computation tree) | | Path quantifier | Implicit (only A) | Explicit (A and E) | | State quantifier | G, F, X, U, R | Must be paired with path quantifier (AG, EF, etc.) | | Expressiveness | Linear-time properties | Branching-time properties | | Algorithm | Automata-theoretic | Fixed-point / BDD | | Example tool | SPIN, CBMC | NuSMV, Cadence SMV | **LTL Operators [2 marks]:** | Operator | Syntax | Semantics | |---|---|---| | $\mathbf{G} \phi$ | Globally $\phi$ | $\phi$ holds at all future states on this path | | $\mathbf{F} \phi$ | Finally $\phi$ | $\phi$ holds at some future state on this path | | $\mathbf{X} \phi$ | Next $\phi$ | $\phi$ holds at the immediate next state | | $\phi \, \mathbf{U} \, \psi$ | $\phi$ Until $\psi$ | $\phi$ holds at all states until $\psi$ becomes true | **CTL Operators [2 marks]:** | Operator | Syntax | Semantics | |---|---|---| | $\mathbf{AG} \phi$ | All paths, Globally $\phi$ | On every path, $\phi$ holds at every state | | $\mathbf{AF} \phi$ | All paths, Finally $\phi$ | On every path, $\phi$ eventually holds | | $\mathbf{EF} \phi$ | Exists path, Finally $\phi$ | On some path, $\phi$ eventually holds | | $\mathbf{A}(\phi \mathbf{U} \psi)$ | All paths, $\phi$ Until $\psi$ | On every path, $\phi$ holds until $\psi$ | **Asymmetry in Expressiveness [1 mark]:** - **CTL-only property:** "There exists a path where $p$ is always true" → $\mathbf{EG} \, p$ (LTL $\mathbf{G} p$ means *on ALL paths*, which is stronger than $\mathbf{EG} p$). - **LTL-only property:** "Whenever $p$ holds, $q$ holds from that moment onward" → $\mathbf{G} (p \rightarrow \mathbf{G} q)$. This is not expressible in CTL because CTL cannot quantify over "from this moment onward" on the same path with nested G. #### Model Solution for Part (b): **LTL Property (i):** *PREADY remains high until transfer completes* Let `PREADY` be signal $r$, and `transfer_done` be signal $d$. $$ \varphi_1 = \mathbf{G} \, (r \rightarrow r \, \mathbf{U} \, d) $$ **LTL Property (ii):** *No two masters granted simultaneously* Let $g_1, g_2$ be the grant signals for master 1 and master 2. $$ \varphi_2 = \mathbf{G} \, \neg (g_1 \land g_2) $$ **LTL Property (iii):** *After reset, eventually idle* Let `reset` be $r$ and `idle` be $i$. $$ \varphi_3 = \mathbf{G} \, (r \rightarrow \mathbf{F} \, i) $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:** > - **Mixing LTL/CTL operators:** Writing $\mathbf{AG} \, \mathbf{F} \, p$ in LTL is invalid — $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{E}$ are CTL-exclusive. > - **Confusing $\mathbf{U}$ and $\mathbf{R}$:** $\mathbf{U}$ is "until"; $\mathbf{R}$ is "releases" (dual of $\mathbf{U}$). Using the wrong one changes semantics entirely. > - **Forgetting path quantifiers in CTL:** Every CTL state quantifier **must** be preceded by $\mathbf{A}$ or $\mathbf{E}$. > - **In BMC, not specifying the bound $k$:** Always state the bound explicitly when describing bounded model checking. > - **Treating safety as universal and liveness as existential:** This is *false* — both can be expressed with $\mathbf{A}$ or $\mathbf{E}$ quantifiers in CTL. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Property Checking** - **Definition:** Property checking = formally proving design satisfies temporal logic properties over **all** reachable states. - **Kripke Structure:** 4-tuple $\mathcal{M} = (S, S_0, R, L)$ — the formal model used by model checkers. - **Two Property Classes:** - **Safety:** "Bad things never happen" → $\mathbf{G} \neg \text{bad}$ — finite counter-example. - **Liveness:** "Good things eventually happen" → $\mathbf{G}(\phi \rightarrow \mathbf{F} \psi)$ — infinite counter-example. - **Fairness:** $\mathbf{G} \mathbf{F} \phi$ — conditions that hold infinitely often. - **LTL vs CTL:** - LTL = Linear (single path), operators: $\mathbf{G}, \mathbf{F}, \mathbf{X}, \mathbf{U}, \mathbf{R}$. - CTL = Branching (tree of paths), operators: $\mathbf{AG}, \mathbf{AF}, \mathbf{AX}, \mathbf{EF}, \mathbf{EG}, \mathbf{EX}, \mathbf{AU}, \mathbf{EU}$ — **path quantifier mandatory**. - $\mathbf{EG} p$ is CTL-only; $\mathbf{G}(p \rightarrow \mathbf{G} q)$ is LTL-only. - **Property Specification Languages:** **PSL** (IEEE 1850, universal), **SVA** (SystemVerilog native), **CTL/LTL** (academic). - **Model Checking Algorithm:** Fixed-point computation for CTL, automata-theoretic for LTL, SAT-based for Bounded Model Checking. - **State Explosion:** $2^n$ states for $n$ flip-flops. Mitigations: COI reduction, BDDs, abstraction, BMC, partial order reduction. - **Bounded Model Checking (BMC):** Unrolls transition relation $k$ times, checks $\mathbf{E} \mathbf{F}_{\leq k} \neg \varphi$ via SAT solver. Trades completeness for scalability. - **Counter-Example:** Witness trace produced when property fails. Used for RTL debug. - **Tool Awareness (KTU expects knowledge of):** Cadence JasperGold, Synopsys VC Formal, Siemens Questa Formal, open-source NuSMV. - **Verification Stack:** Block-level (full property checking) → Cluster-level (ABV with assertions) → SoC-level (property checking with abstraction). - **CO3/CO4 Mapping:** CO3 — property specification; CO4 — model checking algorithm analysis. - **AMBA Protocol Application:** Always be ready to write LTL/CTL properties for AXI/APB handshakes, grants, ready/valid, and reset sequences. ---

topics.title: Equivalence Checking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equivalence Checking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Equivalence Checking in VLSI Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition

**Equivalence Checking** is a formal verification methodology in digital VLSI design that mathematically proves (or disproves) the functional equivalence between two representations of a design — typically a **Reference (Golden) Design** and an **Implementation (Revised) Design** — without exhaustively enumerating all possible input vectors. It answers the fundamental question: *"Does the modified circuit behave identically to the original circuit under all valid input conditions?"*

Formally, two designs `D1` and `D2` are **functionally equivalent** if and only if:

$$\forall \vec{x} \in \{0,1\}^n : D_1(\vec{x}) = D_2(\vec{x})$$

where $\vec{x}$ represents all possible input vectors applied at the primary inputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Equivalence Checking is the process of verifying that a synthesized, optimized, or modified netlist preserves the exact input-output behavior of the original Register-Transfer Level (RTL) specification. It is a critical step in the **Logical Synthesis** and **Design Closure** phases of the VLSI design flow.

### 1.1 Two Primary Classes of Equivalence

| Class | Definition | Typical Application |
|-------|------------|---------------------|
| **Combinational Equivalence Checking (CEC)** | Verifies equivalence when both designs have **identical state encodings** (no internal memory mismatches). | RTL-to-RTL, RTL-to-gate-level post-synthesis, gate-to-gate post-optimization. |
| **Sequential Equivalence Checking (SEC)** | Verifies equivalence when state encoding, latching, or timing may differ. | Retiming, pipelining, clock-gating, FSM state encoding changes. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine two translators independently translating the same English novel into Malayalam. Equivalence Checking is like an automated proof-checker that reads *both* translations and certifies that every sentence in Translation-A conveys the **exact same meaning** as the corresponding sentence in Translation-B, without a human having to read and compare line-by-line.
>
> In VLSI terms, the **RTL** is the original English novel, the **synthesized netlist** is the first translation, and an **optimized netlist** (post place-and-route cleanup) is the second translation. Equivalence checking mathematically guarantees no semantic drift occurred.

### 1.3 Position in the KTU 2024 VLSI Design Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Equivalence checking sits **between Synthesis and Place & Route** and is also re-run after every major **logic optimization** pass (e.g., constant propagation, technology mapping, retiming).

```
RTL (Verilog/VHDL)
    ↓  [Logic Synthesis]
Gate-Level Netlist (Pre-Opt)
    ↓  [Combinational Equivalence Check]  ←  CEC
Gate-Level Netlist (Post-Opt)
    ↓  [Technology Mapping]
Mapped Netlist
    ↓  [Sequential Equivalence Check]  ←  SEC
Final Netlist → Place & Route
```

### 1.4 Why Equivalence Checking is Mandatory (KTU Exam Weight)

**Verification gap** in modern SoCs: A typical 5nm ASIC can have **>10 billion** transistors. Exhaustive simulation at even 1 THz would take longer than the age of the universe. Equivalence checking is the only **tractable, mathematically rigorous** solution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Formal Verification Problem

Equivalence Checking is a specific instance of the broader **Formal Verification** problem. The general formal model of a synchronous sequential circuit `M` is the 6-tuple:

$$M = \langle I, O, S, S_0, \delta, \lambda \rangle$$

where:
- $I$ = Set of input symbols
- $O$ = Set of output symbols
- $S$ = Set of internal states (flip-flop states)
- $S_0$ = Initial state(s)
- $\delta : S \times I \rightarrow S$ = Next-state transition function
- $\lambda : S \times I \rightarrow O$ = Output function (Mealy) or $\lambda : S \rightarrow O$ (Moore)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Combinational circuits are the degenerate case where $|S| = 1$ (no state), which makes CEC dramatically simpler than SEC.

## 2.2 Equivalence Checking — Operational Logic Steps

The standard EDA-tool flow for **Combinational Equivalence Checking (CEC)** proceeds in these structured steps:

1. **Parse Both Netlists:** The tool reads the HDL/gate-level descriptions of `D1` (reference) and `D2` (implementation).
2. **Identify Corresponding PIs/POs:** The user provides a **mapping** of primary inputs to primary inputs and primary outputs to primary outputs.
3. **Identify Corresponding Internal Nodes:** Using structural hashing and simulation-based matching, the tool heuristically pairs internal flip-flops and gates.
4. **Build a Product Machine (Miter):** Construct a comparator circuit that XORs each corresponding output pair and ORs the results. Equivalence is reduced to proving the Miter output is identically `0`.
5. **Apply Decision Procedure:** Run a Boolean satisfiability (SAT) or Binary Decision Diagram (BDD) solver to prove the Miter is a tautology of unsatisfiability.
6. **Report Counterexample (if any):** If inequivalent, the SAT solver returns a *distinguishing input vector* (counterexample).

## 2.3 The Miter Construction — The Heart of CEC

A **Miter** is the canonical data structure for equivalence checking. Given two designs `D1` and `D2` sharing $n$ primary inputs and $m$ primary outputs, the Miter is:

$$M(\vec{x}) = \bigvee_{j=1}^{m} \left( D1_{o_j}(\vec{x}) \oplus D2_{o_j}(\vec{x}) \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** `D1 ≡ D2` if and only if `M ≡ 0` (i.e., `M` is the constant-zero function for all $\vec{x} \in \{0,1\}^n$). Equivalently, `M` is **unsatisfiable** (no input makes `M = 1`).

## 2.4 Core Algorithmic Methods

### Method A: Boolean Methods (BDD-Based)
- Build **Binary Decision Diagrams** (canonical, reduced, ordered — the **ROBDD**) for the output functions of both designs.
- Two functions are equivalent **if and only if** their ROBDDs are **graph-isomorphic** (identical structure).
- **Limitation:** BDDs can suffer from the **variable-ordering problem** causing exponential memory blow-up.

### Method B: Satisfiability (SAT-Based)
- Translate the Miter into **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)**.
- Invoke a SAT solver (e.g., DPLL, CDCL, GRASP) to check if the CNF is satisfiable.
- **Satisfiable** → designs are **NOT equivalent** (SAT solver returns a counterexample).
- **Unsatisfiable** → designs **ARE equivalent** (proof completed).
- **Advantage:** Modern SAT solvers (e.g., based on Conflict-Driven Clause Learning) scale to millions of clauses.

### Method C: Structural / Random Simulation
- Randomly simulate both designs with thousands of vectors.
- Used as a **pre-filter** to quickly catch obvious bugs before invoking heavy formal engines.
- **Limitation:** Cannot *prove* equivalence (only disprove).

### Method D: Sequential Equivalence Checking
- For designs differing in state encoding, latches, or pipelining, a **reachability analysis** or **inductive proof** is used.
- Key technique: **K-step induction** — prove equivalence for all reachable states within `K` clock cycles.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula/symbol that can appear in a KTU ESE question on this topic. **Memorize this table verbatim.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbol/Variable | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Functional Equivalence | $\forall \vec{x} : D_1(\vec{x}) = D_2(\vec{x})$ | $\vec{x} \in \{0,1\}^n$ | Definition of equivalence |
| 2 | Miter Function | $M(\vec{x}) = \bigvee_{j=1}^{m}(D1_{o_j} \oplus D2_{o_j})$ | $m$ = # outputs | CEC reduction |
| 3 | Equivalence Theorem | $D1 \equiv D2 \iff M \equiv 0$ | — | Core CEC theorem |
| 4 | SAT Equivalence | $D1 \equiv D2 \iff \text{CNF}(M) \text{ is UNSAT}$ | CNF = Conjunctive Normal Form | SAT-based check |
| 5 | BDD Equivalence | $D1 \equiv D2 \iff \text{ROBDD}(D1) = \text{ROBDD}(D2)$ | ROBDD = Reduced Ordered BDD | BDD-based check |
| 6 | XOR Difference Metric | $\Delta = \sum_{j=1}^{m}(D1_{o_j} \oplus D2_{o_j})$ | $\Delta = 0$ ⇒ equivalent | Hamming-style diff |
| 7 | Miter CNF Size (est.) | $O(N \cdot K)$ clauses | $N$ = gates, $K$ = clauses/gate | SAT complexity |
| 8 | Sequential Reachability | $\text{Reach}(S_0) = \bigcup_{k=0}^{\infty} \text{Img}^{k}(S_0)$ | $S_0$ = initial states | SEC over state space |
| 9 | Inductive Equivalence | $D1(s) = D2(s) \Rightarrow D1(\delta(s,i)) = D2(\delta(s,i))$ | $s$ = state, $i$ = input | K-induction |
| 10 | Simulation Coverage | $\text{Cov} = 1 - (1 - 2^{-n})^V$ | $V$ = vectors, $n$ = inputs | Random sim confidence |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Intel, AMD, Qualcomm** rely on CEC in their synthesis flows; a single missed equivalence bug can cost millions in respin.
> - **FPGA vendors** (Xilinx, Altera/Intel) use CEC to verify that their place-and-route tool has not altered logic functionality.
> - **Formal property verification (FPV)** and **equivalence checking** together form the two pillars of formal EDA, the latter being more automated and far less labor-intensive to set up.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Miter Reduction for a 2-Output Circuit

**Problem Setup:** Consider two combinational designs `D1` and `D2` with the same 2-bit primary input $x = (x_1, x_2)$ and 2-bit output $y = (y_1, y_2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D1: \quad & y_1 = x_1 \cdot x_2 \\
& y_2 = x_1 \oplus x_2 \\
D2: \quad & y_1 = x_1 \land x_2 \quad \text{(same as D1)} \\
& y_2 = \overline{x_1 \odot x_2} \quad \text{(XNOR complement)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify corresponding output pairs:**
- Pair 1: $D1_{o_1}$ and $D2_{o_1}$ → both are $x_1 \land x_2$ (visually identical).
- Pair 2: $D1_{o_2}$ and $D2_{o_2}$ → differ in representation: $D1_{o_2} = x_1 \oplus x_2$ and $D2_{o_2} = \overline{x_1 \odot x_2}$.

**Step 2 — Construct per-output XOR differences:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_1(\vec{x}) &= D1_{o_1}(\vec{x}) \oplus D2_{o_1}(\vec{x}) = (x_1 x_2) \oplus (x_1 x_2) = 0 \\
\delta_2(\vec{x}) &= D1_{o_2}(\vec{x}) \oplus D2_{o_2}(\vec{x}) = (x_1 \oplus x_2) \oplus \overline{(x_1 \odot x_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify $\delta_2$ using Boolean identities:**

The XNOR (equivalence) operator satisfies $\overline{a \odot b} = a \oplus b$. Therefore:

$$
\delta_2(\vec{x}) = (x_1 \oplus x_2) \oplus (x_1 \oplus x_2) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Form the Miter output:**

$$
M(\vec{x}) = \delta_1 \lor \delta_2 = 0 \lor 0 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Equivalence Theorem:**

Since $M \equiv 0$ for all $\vec{x}$, by the Miter theorem $D1 \equiv D2$. The designs are **functionally equivalent** even though their gate-level netlists differ.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — SAT-Based Counterexample Search

**Problem Setup:** Two 1-bit output designs with 2-bit input $\vec{x} = (x_1, x_2)$:

$$
D1: \quad f_1 = x_1 \cdot x_2, \qquad D2: \quad f_2 = x_1 + x_2 \pmod{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Form the Miter:**

$$M(\vec{x}) = f_1 \oplus f_2 = (x_1 x_2) \oplus (x_1 \oplus x_2)$$

**Step 2 — Convert to CNF using Tseitin transformation:**

Introduce auxiliary variables $a = x_1 x_2$ and $b = x_1 \oplus x_2$. The Miter CNF is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(a \lor \lnot x_1 \lor \lnot x_2) \land (\lnot a \lor x_1) \land (\lnot a \lor x_2) \\
\land\ & (b \lor \lnot x_1 \lor x_2) \land (b \lor x_1 \lor \lnot x_2) \land (\lnot b \lor x_1 \lor x_2) \land (\lnot b \lor \lnot x_1 \lor \lnot x_2) \\
\land\ & (M \lor \lnot a \lor \lnot b) \land (M \lor a \lor b) \land (\lnot M \lor \lnot a \lor b) \land (\lnet M \lor a \lor \lnot b)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Assert $M = 1$ and invoke SAT solver:**

Adding the unit clause $(M)$, the SAT solver will find a satisfying assignment. Tracing back:
- $M = 1$ ⇒ $a$ and $b$ must differ.
- Try $a = 0, b = 1$: requires $x_1 = 0$ and $x_1 \oplus x_2 = 1$ ⇒ $x_1 = 0, x_2 = 1$.

**Step 4 — Counterexample identified:** $\vec{x} = (0, 1)$ yields $f_1 = 0$ but $f_2 = 1$, so the designs are **NOT equivalent**. SAT solver returns $(x_1, x_2) = (0, 1)$ as the distinguishing input.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Coverage of Random Simulation

**Problem:** A circuit has $n = 20$ primary inputs. How many random vectors $V$ are required to achieve **99% confidence** that a bug (if any) will be detected, assuming the bug is triggered by exactly one input pattern?

**Step 1 — Probability of missing a specific vector in one trial:**

$$P_{\text{miss}} = 1 - 2^{-n} = 1 - 2^{-20} = 1 - 9.5367 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.999999$$

**Step 2 — Probability of missing it after $V$ independent trials:**

$$P_{\text{still miss}} = (1 - 2^{-n})^V$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V$ when coverage is 99%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - (1 - 2^{-n})^V &= 0.99 \\
(1 - 2^{-n})^V &= 0.01 \\
V \ln(1 - 2^{-n}) &= \ln(0.01) \\
V &= \frac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(1 - 2^{-20})} \approx \frac{-4.6052}{-9.5367 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 4{,}828{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** Roughly **4.83 million** random vectors are required — and this only **detects** bugs, never *proves* their absence. **This is why exhaustive formal equivalence checking is indispensable.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right EC Method

| Design Scenario | Recommended Method | Justification |
|------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| RTL-to-gate after standard synthesis | **CEC + BDD/SAT** | Identical state, no internal FF mismatch |
| Post-retiming optimization | **SEC with k-induction** | Flip-flops may be re-positioned |
| Technology mapping change (e.g., NAND → NOR) | **CEC + SAT** | Same combinational structure |
| FSM re-encoding (binary → one-hot) | **SEC with reachability** | State bits differ |
| Clock-gating insertion | **SEC with property-driven** | New sequential behavior |
| Pipelining (latency change) | **SEC with latency matching** | Output shifted by N cycles |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Canonical Miter Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pi[Primary Inputs x1..xn] --> D1["Design D1 (Reference)"]
    pi --> D2["Design D2 (Implementation)"]
    D1 --> out1["D1 outputs y1..ym"]
    D2 --> out2["D2 outputs y1..ym"]
    out1 --> xorBlock["Pairwise XOR: delta_j = yj_D1 XOR yj_D2"]
    out2 --> xorBlock
    xorBlock --> orGate["OR Reduction: M = OR delta_1 ... delta_m"]
    orGate --> miterOut["Miter Output M(x)"]
    miterOut --> decision{"M is identically 0 ?"}
    decision -- YES --> equivResult["D1 EQUIVALENT to D2 (PROVEN)"]
    decision -- NO --> satSolver["SAT Solver returns counterexample vector"]
    satSolver --> counterEx["Counterexample: x that distinguishes D1 from D2"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Miter is the heart of CEC. The XOR gates compute per-output differences; the OR gate aggregates them. If the OR output is *ever* `1`, the SAT solver produces the specific input pattern that caused it.

## 4.2 Equivalence Checking in the VLSI Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RTL Design (Verilog/VHDL)"] --> B["Logic Synthesis Tool"]
    B --> C["Gate-Level Netlist Pre-Opt"]
    C --> D{"Equivalence Check (CEC)"}
    D -- Equivalent --> E["Optimized Gate Netlist"]
    D -- NOT Equivalent --> F["Bug Report / Counterexample"]
    F --> B
    E --> G["Technology Mapping"]
    G --> H["Mapped Netlist"]
    H --> I{"Sequential EC (SEC)"}
    I -- Equivalent --> J["Final Netlist to P&R"]
    I -- NOT Equivalent --> K["Induction / Reachability Analysis"]
    K --> J
    J --> L["Layout / GDSII"]
```

## 4.3 Combinational vs Sequential Equivalence — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Two Designs D1, D2 to Compare"] --> q1{"Same number of FFs and same state encoding?"}
    q1 -- YES --> cec["Use CEC: BDD or SAT on Miter"]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{"Same latency, but different state encoding?"}
    q2 -- YES --> sec1["Use SEC: K-induction / correspondence"]
    q2 -- NO --> q3{"Different latency (e.g., pipelined)?"}
    q3 -- YES --> sec2["Use SEC: Latency-shifted comparison + retiming proof"]
    q3 -- NO --> sec3["Use SEC: Full reachability analysis (expensive)"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to choose a method, the *first* question to ask is: "Do the two designs share the same state-holding elements in the same order?" If **yes** → CEC; if **no** → SEC.

## 4.4 KTU High-Yield Diagram: BDD for the Equivalence Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    f1["Boolean Function f1 of Design D1"] --> robdd1["Build ROBDD1 (canonical form)"]
    f2["Boolean Function f2 of Design D2"] --> robdd2["Build ROBDD2 (canonical form)"]
    robdd1 --> cmp{"Graph Isomorphism Check"}
    robdd2 --> cmp
    cmp -- ISOMORPHIC --> eq["f1 EQUIVALENT f2 (PROVEN via canonical form)"]
    cmp -- NOT ISOMORPHIC --> neq["f1 NOT EQUIVALENT f2"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define equivalence checking. Why is it preferred over exhaustive simulation in modern VLSI design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Equivalence checking is a **formal verification technique** that mathematically proves two designs (a reference design and a modified/implementation design) exhibit identical input-output behavior for all possible input combinations. Two designs `D1` and `D2` are equivalent if $\forall \vec{x} : D1(\vec{x}) = D2(\vec{x})$.

It is preferred over exhaustive simulation because:
1. **Scalability:** A modern ASIC has $2^n$ input patterns where $n$ can exceed 100, making exhaustive simulation computationally infeasible.
2. **Exhaustiveness:** Equivalence checking provides a *mathematical proof* valid for *all* inputs, not a statistical sample.
3. **Automation:** Unlike simulation, it does not require manual testbench creation; the tool compares netlists directly.
4. **Bug Localization:** When inequivalence is detected, a SAT-based tool returns a *distinguishing counterexample* input vector.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Combinational Equivalence Checking (CEC) and Sequential Equivalence Checking (SEC).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CEC | SEC |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **State** | Assumes identical state encoding | Handles differing state encodings |
| **Complexity** | Polynomial in many cases | PSPACE-complete in general |
| **Method** | BDD / SAT on Miter | Reachability / k-induction |
| **Use case** | RTL-to-gate, post-synthesis | Retiming, pipelining, FSM re-encoding |
| **Tool runtime** | Seconds to minutes | Hours to days for large designs |
