## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Worked Example: Lee's Algorithm on a 5×5 Grid

Let us route **Net A** from $S = (1,1)$ to $T = (5,5)$ on a 5×5 grid with one **obstacle** at $(3,3)$.

> The grid uses 1-based indexing: row increases downward, column increases rightward.

**Step 1 — Initialization.** Set the source cell $(1,1)$ with **label = 0** and mark it as the wavefront $W_0$.

$$W_0 = \{(1,1)\}, \quad \text{label}(1,1) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Wave 1 expansion.** Visit all 4-neighbors of $W_0$ that are not obstacles. The neighbors of $(1,1)$ are $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$.

$$W_1 = \{(1,2), (2,1)\}$$
$$\text{label}(1,2) = 1, \quad \text{label}(2,1) = 1$$

**Step 3 — Wave 2 expansion.** Visit unvisited 4-neighbors of $W_1$.

- Neighbors of $(1,2)$: $(1,3)$, $(2,2)$ (since $(1,1)$ is already visited).
- Neighbors of $(2,1)$: $(3,1)$, $(2,2)$.
- Add unique unvisited cells: $\{(1,3), (2,2), (3,1)\}$.

$$W_2 = \{(1,3), (2,2), (3,1)\}$$
$$\text{label}(1,3) = 2, \quad \text{label}(2,2) = 2, \quad \text{label}(3,1) = 2$$

**Step 4 — Wave 3 expansion.** Visit unvisited 4-neighbors of $W_2$, **excluding the obstacle** $(3,3)$.

- Neighbors of $(1,3)$: $(1,4)$, $(2,3)$.
- Neighbors of $(2,2)$: $(1,2)$ visited, $(2,1)$ visited, $(2,3)$, $(3,2)$.
- Neighbors of $(3,1)$: $(2,1)$ visited, $(3,2)$, $(4,1)$.
- Unique unvisited: $\{(1,4), (2,3), (3,2), (4,1)\}$.

$$W_3 = \{(1,4), (2,3), (3,2), (4,1)\}$$
$$\text{label} = 3 \text{ for all}$$

**Step 5 — Wave 4 expansion.**

- Neighbors of $(1,4)$: $(1,5), (2,4)$.
- Neighbors of $(2,3)$: $(1,3)$ visited, $(2,2)$ visited, $(2,4)$, $(3,3)$ **obstacle — skip**.
- Neighbors of $(3,2)$: $(2,2)$ visited, $(3,1)$ visited, $(3,3)$ **obstacle**, $(4,2)$.
- Neighbors of $(4,1)$: $(3,1)$ visited, $(4,2)$, $(5,1)$.
- Unique: $\{(1,5), (2,4), (4,2), (5,1)\}$.

$$W_4 = \{(1,5), (2,4), (4,2), (5,1)\}$$
$$\text{label} = 4 \text{ for all}$$

**Step 6 — Wave 5 expansion.** We need $(5,5)$.

- Neighbors of $(1,5)$: $(2,5)$.
- Neighbors of $(2,4)$: $(1,4)$ visited, $(2,3)$ visited, $(2,5)$, $(3,4)$.
- Neighbors of $(4,2)$: $(3,2)$ visited, $(4,1)$ visited, $(4,3)$, $(5,2)$.
- Neighbors of $(5,1)$: $(4,1)$ visited, $(5,2)$.
- Unique: $\{(2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2)\}$.

$$W_5 = \{(2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2)\}$$
$$\text{label} = 5 \text{ for all}$$

**Step 7 — Wave 6 expansion.**

- Neighbors of $(2,5)$: $(1,5)$ visited, $(3,5)$.
- Neighbors of $(3,4)$: $(2,4)$ visited, $(3,3)$ obstacle, $(3,5)$, $(4,4)$.
- Neighbors of $(4,3)$: $(3,3)$ obstacle, $(4,2)$ visited, $(4,4)$, $(5,3)$.
- Neighbors of $(5,2)$: $(4,2)$ visited, $(5,1)$ visited, $(5,3)$.
- Unique: $\{(3,5), (4,4), (5,3)\}$.

$$W_6 = \{(3,5), (4,4), (5,3)\}$$
$$\text{label} = 6 \text{ for all}$$

**Step 8 — Wave 7 expansion (Target Hit!).**

- Neighbors of $(3,5)$: $(2,5)$ visited, $(4,5)$, $(3,4)$ visited.
- Neighbors of $(4,4)$: $(3,4)$ visited, $(4,3)$ visited, $(4,5)$, $(5,4)$.
- Neighbors of $(5,3)$: $(4,3)$ visited, $(5,2)$ visited, $(5,4)$.
- **Target $(5,5)$ is reached** via neighbor $(4,5)$ or $(5,4)$.

$$W_7 = \{(4,5), (5,4), (5,5)\}$$
$$\text{label}(5,5) = 7 \quad \Rightarrow \text{STOP PROPAGATION}$$

**Step 9 — Traceback.** Starting from $(5,5)$ with label 7, find a neighbor with label 6, then 5, ..., down to 0.

| Step | Current Cell | Label | Move To (label − 1) | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 9.1 | $(5,5)$ | 7 | $(4,5)$ | Up |
| 9.2 | $(4,5)$ | 6 | $(3,5)$ | Up |
| 9.3 | $(3,5)$ | 5 | $(2,5)$ | Up |
| 9.4 | $(2,5)$ | 4 | $(1,5)$ | Up |
| 9.5 | $(1,5)$ | 3 | $(1,4)$ | Left |
| 9.6 | $(1,4)$ | 2 | $(1,3)$ | Left |
| 9.7 | $(1,3)$ | 1 | $(1,2)$ | Left |
| 9.8 | $(1,2)$ | 0 | $(1,1)$ | Left ← Source |

**Final Path:** $(1,1) \to (1,2) \to (1,3) \to (1,4) \to (1,5) \to (2,5) \to (3,5) \to (4,5) \to (5,5)$.

**Manhattan Length:**

$$L = |5-1| + |5-1| = 4 + 4 = 8 \text{ cells}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** Since the obstacle is at $(3,3)$ and our path goes **around the top and right edges**, the path length 8 is indeed the **shortest possible** path under the obstacle constraint. ✅

### 3.2 Detailed Worked Example: Left-Edge Algorithm

Consider a channel with **5 nets** with the following pin spans (left, right):

| Net | Left x-coord | Right x-coord |
|---|---|---|
| N1 | 1 | 6 |
| N2 | 2 | 8 |
| N3 | 4 | 7 |
| N4 | 5 | 9 |
| N5 | 8 | 10 |

**Step 1 — Sort by leftmost x-coordinate:**

$$\text{Sorted order: } N1(1,6), N2(2,8), N3(4,7), N4(5,9), N5(8,10)$$

**Step 2 — Track 1 assignment:**

- Place **N1** on Track 1. Last right coordinate = 6.
- Check N2: left = 2 ≤ 6. ❌ **Conflict.** Defer N2.
- Check N3: left = 4 ≤ 6. ❌ **Conflict.** Defer N3.
- Check N4: left = 5 ≤ 6. ❌ **Conflict.** Defer N4.
- Check N5: left = 8 > 6. ✅ **Place N5** on Track 1. Last right = 10.
- **Track 1:** {N1, N5}

**Step 3 — Track 2 assignment (deferred nets: N2, N3, N4):**

- Place **N2** on Track 2. Last right = 8.
- Check N3: left = 4 ≤ 8. ❌ Conflict. Defer.
- Check N4: left = 5 ≤ 8. ❌ Conflict. Defer.
- **Track 2:** {N2}

**Step 4 — Track 3 (deferred: N3, N4):**

- Place **N3** on Track 3. Last right = 7.
- Check N4: left = 5 ≤ 7. ❌ Conflict. Defer.
- **Track 3:** {N3}

**Step 5 — Track 4:**

- Place **N4** on Track 4. Last right = 9.
- **Track 4:** {N4}

**Result:**

| Track | Nets Assigned | Span |
|---|---|---|
| Track 1 | N1, N5 | 1–6, 8–10 |
| Track 2 | N2 | 2–8 |
| Track 3 | N3 | 4–7 |
| Track 4 | N4 | 5–9 |

**Total Tracks Used = 4.**

**Channel Density Check:**

$$\delta = \max_{x} (\text{count of nets crossing column } x)$$

- Column 5: N1, N2, N3, N4 cross → count = 4.
- Column 8: N2, N5 cross → count = 2.

$$\delta = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Lower bound} = 4 \text{ tracks}$$

**Conclusion:** Our Left-Edge solution achieves the **optimal 4 tracks** = the theoretical lower bound. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **General theorem:** Left-Edge is **optimal** when no vertical constraints exist. When vertical constraints are present (a net must be above another), Left-Edge alone is insufficient, and a **Constraint Graph-based algorithm** must be used.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Algorithm | Type | Optimality | Memory | Speed | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lee's (Maze)** | Global | ✅ Optimal shortest path | High ($O(V)$) | Slow | Small grids, 2-pin nets |
| **Line-Probe** | Global | ❌ Not guaranteed | Low | Fast | Large chips, congested regions |
| **Steiner Tree (Hanan)** | Global | ✅ Optimal (within Hanan grid) | Medium | Medium | Multi-pin nets |
| **Left-Edge** | Detailed | ✅ Optimal without vert. constraints | Low | Fast | Channel routing |
| **Dogleg** | Detailed | ✅ Better than Left-Edge | Low | Medium | High-density channels |
| **YACR / Greedy** | Detailed | Near-optimal | Medium | Fast | General channels |
| **Maze (in detailed)** | Detailed | ✅ Optimal | High | Slow | Switchbox routing |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the difference between Global Routing and Detailed Routing? List any two metrics used to evaluate the quality of a routing solution.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Global Routing | Detailed Routing |
|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Coarse grid (GCells) | Fine grid (design rules) |
| **Output** | Net-to-region assignment | Exact track + via assignment |
| **Objective** | Minimize congestion, wire length | Minimize vias, DRC violations |

**Two Quality Metrics:** (1) **Total Wire Length** (Manhattan sum across all nets), (2) **Channel Density** (lower bound on number of tracks required). *(Any two of: congestion, via count, critical path delay.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Briefly explain the concept of a "Steiner point" in global routing. State Hanan's theorem.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Steiner point** is an *additional junction* introduced on a multi-pin net to reduce total wire length below what a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) would achieve. It is a point **not originally a pin**, used to "branch" the routing.

**Hanan's Theorem:** *"There exists an optimal Rectilinear Steiner Tree for any pin set in which every Steiner point lies at the intersection of a vertical line and a horizontal line drawn through one of the pins."* This reduces the candidate Steiner points to a finite grid of at most $n^2$ points for an $n$-pin net.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Lee's Maze Routing Algorithm** step by step. Discuss its time and space complexity. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Apply Lee's Algorithm to find the shortest path from $S = (2,2)$ to $T = (4,4)$ on a 5×5 grid with obstacles at $(2,4)$ and $(3,3)$. Show all wave expansion steps and the traceback. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lee's Algorithm — Step by Step (7 marks):**

1. **Label the source cell** $S$ with 0. Place it in the first wavefront $W_0$.
2. **Wave expansion:** For each cell in the current wavefront $W_k$, examine its 4 (or 8) neighbors. Any unvisited, non-obstacle neighbor is labeled $k+1$ and added to $W_{k+1}$.
3. **Target check:** After each wave, check if the target $T$ is reached. If yes, stop.
4. **Traceback:** From $T$, move to a neighbor with label one less than the current cell, repeating until reaching $S$. This gives the shortest path.
5. **Complexity:**
   - Time: $O(k \cdot V)$ where $k$ is path length and $V$ is grid size.
   - Space: $O(V)$ for storing labels.
6. **Diagram:** (A 5×5 grid with concentric wave labels 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 emanating from S; the traceback arrows shown in red).

**(b) Worked Solution (7 marks):**

Grid is 5×5, indexed (row, col) with row 1 at top.

Obstacles: $(2,4)$ and $(3,3)$.

**Wave 0:** $W_0 = \{(2,2)\}$, label 0.

**Wave 1:** Neighbors of $(2,2)$ = $\{(1,2), (3,2), (2,1), (2,3)\}$. All are free.
$W_1 = \{(1,2), (3,2), (2,1), (2,3)\}$, label 1.

**Wave 2:** Examine each cell in $W_1$:
- $(1,2)$ → $(1,1), (1,3)$ (assuming $(2,2)$ already visited).
- $(3,2)$ → $(3,1), (4,2), (3,3)$ obstacle skipped.
- $(2,1)$ → $(1,1)$ already queued, $(3,1)$ already queued.
- $(2,3)$ → $(1,3)$ queued, $(2,4)$ obstacle, $(3,3)$ obstacle.

$W_2 = \{(1,1), (1,3), (3,1), (4,2)\}$, label 2.

**Wave 3:**
- $(1,1)$ → $(1,2)$ visited.
- $(1,3)$ → $(1,4)$.
- $(3,1)$ → $(4,1)$.
- $(4,2)$ → $(4,1)$ already queued, $(4,3)$, $(5,2)$.

$W_3 = \{(1,4), (4,1), (4,3), (5,2)\}$, label 3.

**Wave 4:**
- $(1,4)$ → $(1,5)$.
- $(4,1)$ → $(5,1)$.
- $(4,3)$ → $(4,4)$ **TARGET REACHED** — partial, but not the last wave.

Add $(1,5), (5,1), (4,4)$ to $W_4$ with label 4. Since $(4,4) = T$, **STOP**.

**Traceback from $T = (4,4)$ (label 4):**
- $(4,4)$ → neighbor with label 3 → $(4,3)$.
- $(4,3)$ → label 2 → $(4,2)$.
- $(4,2)$ → label 1 → $(3,2)$.
- $(3,2)$ → label 0 → $(2,2)$ = Source.

**Path:** $(2,2) \to (3,2) \to (4,2) \to (4,3) \to (4,4)$.

**Manhattan Length:**

$$L = |4-2| + |4-2| = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ cells}$$

This is the **shortest possible** path, since the two obstacles are not on this route. ✅

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** What is a **Channel** in VLSI routing? Explain the **Left-Edge Algorithm** for channel routing with a suitable example. Discuss its limitations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A channel has 6 nets with pin spans: N1(1,5), N2(2,7), N3(3,6), N4(4,8), N5(5,9), N6(7,10). Apply the Left-Edge Algorithm to determine the minimum number of tracks required. Verify against the channel density lower bound. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Channel and Left-Edge Algorithm (7 marks):**

A **channel** is a rectangular routing region situated between two parallel rows of standard cells. Pins are located only on the **top and bottom boundaries** of the channel. The goal of channel routing is to assign horizontal wire segments (on a designated metal layer) to each net, using the **minimum number of horizontal tracks**, while ensuring no two nets share a track segment.

**Left-Edge Algorithm (Hashimoto & Stevens, 1971):**

1. Sort all nets by their **leftmost pin coordinate** in ascending order.
2. Process nets in sorted order. Assign each net to the **lowest available track**.
3. A net can be placed on track $T$ only if its **leftmost pin lies strictly to the right** of the rightmost pin of the **previously placed net** on track $T$.
4. If a net cannot fit on any existing track, open a **new track**.

**Limitations:**
- Does **not** allow doglegs (horizontal jogs), which can lead to non-optimal results when vertical constraints exist.
- Cannot handle **cyclic vertical constraints** (nets forming a cycle in the Vertical Constraint Graph).
- Restricted to **two-layer channel routing**.

**(b) Worked Example (7 marks):**

Given nets: N1(1,5), N2(2,7), N3(3,6), N4(4,8), N5(5,9), N6(7,10).

**Step 1 — Sort by leftmost x-coordinate:**

| Order | Net | Span |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | N1 | (1, 5) |
| 2 | N2 | (2, 7) |
| 3 | N3 | (3, 6) |
| 4 | N4 | (4, 8) |
| 5 | N5 | (5, 9) |
| 6 | N6 | (7, 10) |

**Step 2 — Track 1:**
- Place N1 (left=1, right=5). Last right = 5.
- N2: left=2 ≤ 5. ❌ Defer.
- N3: left=3 ≤ 5. ❌ Defer.
- N4: left=4 ≤ 5. ❌ Defer.
- N5: left=5 ≤ 5. ❌ Defer (must be **strictly greater**).
- N6: left=7 > 5. ✅ Place N6. Last right = 10.

**Track 1: {N1, N6}**

**Step 3 — Track 2 (deferred: N2, N3, N4, N5):**
- Place N2 (left=2, right=7). Last right = 7.
- N3: left=3 ≤ 7. ❌ Defer.
- N4: left=4 ≤ 7. ❌ Defer.
- N5: left=5 ≤ 7. ❌ Defer.

**Track 2: {N2}**

**Step 4 — Track 3 (deferred: N3, N4, N5):**
- Place N3 (left=3, right=6). Last right = 6.
- N4: left=4 ≤ 6. ❌ Defer.
- N5: left=5 ≤ 6. ❌ Defer.

**Track 3: {N3}**

**Step 5 — Track 4 (deferred: N4, N5):**
- Place N4 (left=4, right=8). Last right = 8.
- N5: left=5 ≤ 8. ❌ Defer.

**Track 4: {N4}**

**Step 6 — Track 5:**
- Place N5 (left=5, right=9).

**Track 5: {N5}**

**Total tracks used = 5.**

**Step 7 — Channel Density Verification:**

| Column $x$ | Nets Crossing |
|---|---|
| 1 | N1 → 1 |
| 2 | N1, N2 → 2 |
| 3 | N1, N2, N3 → 3 |
| 4 | N1, N2, N3, N4 → **4** |
| 5 | N1, N2, N3, N4, N5 → **5** |
| 6 | N2, N3, N4, N5 → 4 |
| 7 | N2, N4, N5, N6 → 4 |
| 8 | N4, N5, N6 → 3 |
| 9 | N5, N6 → 2 |
| 10 | N6 → 1 |

$$\delta = \max_x \text{count}(x) = 5 \quad \text{(at column 5)}$$

**Conclusion:** The Left-Edge algorithm produces a solution with **5 tracks = channel density = 5**. This is **optimal** ✅, as the channel density is the theoretical lower bound on the number of tracks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "left > previous right" with "left ≥ previous right"** — students often allow N5 (left=5) to share track 1 with N1 (right=5), but the Left-Edge rule is **strictly greater**. This is a 1-mark deduction in valuation.
> 2. **Forgetting the channel density verification** — always compute $\delta$ and compare with the number of tracks used to prove optimality.
> 3. **Confusing Channel and Switchbox** — channels have pins on 2 opposite sides; switchboxes have pins on all 4 sides. Examiners award 0 if the algorithm is mismatched to the region type.
> 4. **Not labelling wave numbers clearly in Lee's algorithm** — every wavefront must have an explicit label and the traceback must show decreasing labels (e.g., 5 → 4 → 3 → 2 → 1 → 0).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Routing = Global (regions) + Detailed (exact tracks)**. The two phases are sequential; global first, detailed second.
- **Lee's Algorithm** is a *wave-propagation* algorithm that **guarantees the shortest path** on a uniform grid. Complexity: $O(kV)$ time, $O(V)$ space.
- **Line-Probe Algorithm** uses *probing lines* from source and target; it is faster and uses less memory than Lee's, but does **NOT** guarantee optimality.
- **Steiner Trees** minimize wire length for **multi-pin nets** by introducing extra branching points. **Hanan's Theorem** restricts candidate Steiner points to a finite grid of size at most $n^2$.
- **Channel Routing** is a 2-sided routing problem; pins are on top and bottom only. The **Left-Edge Algorithm** assigns nets to tracks in order of leftmost pin, but **disallows doglegs**.
- **Dogleg Algorithm** allows horizontal jogs and can produce better (fewer tracks) results than Left-Edge, especially in dense channels.
- **Channel Density $\delta$** is the theoretical lower bound on the number of horizontal tracks; any valid routing must use at least $\delta$ tracks.
- **Switchbox Routing** is a 4-sided routing problem (pins on all four sides); it is **NP-hard** and harder than channel routing.
- **Over-the-Cell (OTC) Routing** places wires *over* the cell rows, eliminating the channel entirely in modern designs.
- **Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)** encodes which net must be placed above another; cycles in the VCG mean no valid 2-layer channel routing exists.
- The **time-space trade-off** in routing algorithms is critical: Lee's is memory-heavy but optimal; Line-Probe is memory-efficient but sub-optimal.
- **KR evaluation criteria (KTU 2024):** Examiners will specifically check (1) correct identification of routing region type, (2) sorted-order in Left-Edge, (3) explicit wave labels in Lee's, and (4) optimality verification using channel density.

> **Module 3 Routing Mastery Formula (Unofficial Mnemonic):**
> **L**ee's **L**abels **L**ayer-by-Layer (wavefronts),
> **L**eft-Edge **L**ays tracks from **L**eftmost pin,
> **L**ower bound = **D**ensity,
> **L**ess is more: **fewer tracks, fewer vias, less congestion** = better routing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Project
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micro Project

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **Micro Project** in the context of the VLSI Design course (PECST415) is a **small-scale, team-based or individual design-implementation-verification exercise** that integrates the theoretical concepts acquired in the module into a functional Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) circuit or system. As per the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, the micro project is mapped to specific **Course Outcomes (COs)** and is designed to develop higher-order Bloom's Taxonomy skills — particularly **Apply (L3), Analyze (L4), and Create (L6)**.

The micro project follows the **standard VLSI Design Flow** (sometimes called the **Y-chart** or **Gajski-Kuhn chart**), covering domains from system specification down to physical fabrication-ready layout (GDS-II). For academic purposes, the project typically ends at the **post-layout simulation** or **FPGA/ASIC synthesis** stage, bypassing actual silicon fabrication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board Standard):**
> "A Micro Project is a mini version of a full-fledged VLSI chip design project where students identify a small digital/analog/mixed-signal circuit problem, design it using CMOS technology, simulate it using industry-grade EDA tools, verify functional and timing correctness, and document the entire design flow as per industry report standards."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect tasked with designing a small, single-room studio apartment**:

- **System Specification (What to build?)** = The client's brief — "I need a room with a bed, study table, and a window."
- **Architecture Design (How does it look on paper?)** = The 2D floor plan and 3D elevation.
- **Logic Design (How does it work electrically?)** = Wiring diagram for lights, fans, and switches.
- **Circuit Design (What specific components?)** = Choosing specific brands of switches (e.g., CMOS NAND gates instead of BJTs).
- **Physical Design (Where do bricks and wires actually go?)** = The actual construction blueprint with exact dimensions.
- **Verification (Does it work?)** = Turning on the lights and testing each switch.

A **VLSI Micro Project is precisely this process — but for an electronic chip** instead of a building. The "room" is a circuit (like an adder, multiplier, or memory cell), the "blueprint" is the **schematic**, the "construction blueprint" is the **layout**, and the "testing" is **simulation**.

---

## 1.3 Why Micro Projects? — The KTU 2024 OBE Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Education Insight:**
> KTU introduced micro projects to bridge the **theory-practice gap** that traditional lecture-only pedagogy fails to address. By forcing students to take a circuit from **specification to verified layout**, micro projects operationalize ABET/KTU graduate attributes such as:
> - **Design Thinking** (CO: Design/Development of Solutions)
> - **Modern Tool Usage** (CO: Create, Select, and Apply Tools)
> - **Individual and Team Work** (CO: Teamwork)
> - **Communication** (CO: Technical Report Writing)

**Standard Metrics Expected in a VLSI Micro Project (in bold for emphasis):**
- **Target Technology Node: 180 nm / 90 nm / 45 nm CMOS** (in academic settings, often 180 nm)
- **Supply Voltage (V_DD): 1.8 V** (typical for 180 nm)
- **Operating Frequency: 10 MHz – 1 GHz** (depending on design)
- **Power Budget: μW to mW range** (for academic designs)
- **Area Constraint: Number of transistors or layout area in μm²**
- **Verification: 100 % functional coverage via testbench**

---

## 1.4 Scope Boundaries in the KTU 2024 Scheme

The micro project is **bounded** to avoid becoming a full semester project. KTU 2024 specifies:

| Boundary Parameter | Constraint |
|---|---|
| Duration | 4 – 6 weeks (within one module teaching cycle) |
| Team Size | 2 – 4 students |
| Complexity | 50 – 500 transistors equivalent |
| Design Level | Up to Physical Design (Layout) |
| Fabrication | NOT required; ends at GDS-II / post-layout sim |
| Documentation | 10 – 20 page report + presentation |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** A micro project in PECST415 is judged on the **completeness of the design flow followed**, **correctness of simulation results**, and **clarity of documentation** — not on the complexity of the chosen circuit.

---

## 1.5 Typical Micro Project Categories in VLSI

1. **Combinational Logic Designs** — Adders (Ripple Carry, Carry Look-Ahead), Multiplexers, Encoders.
2. **Sequential Logic Designs** — Flip-Flops, Registers, Counters, FSMs.
3. **Arithmetic Building Blocks** — Multipliers, ALUs, Comparators.
4. **Memory Designs** — SRAM Cell (6T), DRAM Cell, ROM.
5. **Analog/Mixed-Signal** — Op-Amps, Comparators, ADC/DAC (advanced projects).
6. **System-Level** — UART, SPI controller, simple processor datapath.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The VLSI Design Flow — The Heart of Every Micro Project

Every VLSI micro project, regardless of whether the target is a simple inverter or a complex ALU, follows the **Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart Design Flow**. The Y-chart has three domains:
- **Behavioral Domain** (what the circuit does)
- **Structural Domain** (how it is built from components)
- **Geometric Domain** (physical layout)

For an academic micro project, this is linearized into the following **6 mandatory phases**:

### Phase 1: Problem Definition & Specification
- Identify the circuit (e.g., "Design a 4-bit Ripple Carry Adder").
- Document I/O ports, bit-width, clock frequency, power, area constraints.
- Write a **functional specification** document.

### Phase 2: RTL / Schematic Design
- Express the design either in:
  - **Hardware Description Language (HDL):** VHDL / Verilog
  - **Schematic Capture:** Using EDA tools (Cadence Virtuoso, Synopsys, Mentor)
- For Module 3 of PECST415, schematic-based CMOS design is emphasized.

### Phase 3: Functional Simulation (Pre-Layout)
- Write a **testbench** that applies all possible input combinations (or a representative subset).
- Verify output waveforms match expected truth table.
- **Tool:** ModelSim, QuestaSim, Vivado, or Cadence ncsim.

### Phase 4: Logic Synthesis (for HDL projects)
- Convert HDL code into a **gate-level netlist** using a standard cell library.
- Reports generated: **Area, Timing, Power**.
- **Tool:** Synopsys Design Compiler, Xilinx Vivado, Yosys (open-source).

### Phase 5: Physical Design (Layout)
- For schematic projects: **Manual layout** using CMOS design rules (lambda rules or micron rules).
- For HDL projects: **Automatic Place & Route (P&R)**.
- Output: **GDS-II file** (the final "blueprint" for fabrication).
- **Tool:** Cadence Innovus, Synopsys IC Compiler, Magic (open-source), Electric VLSI.

### Phase 6: Post-Layout Verification
- **DRC (Design Rule Check):** Verifies geometric rules (e.g., minimum width, spacing).
- **LVS (Layout Versus Schematic):** Verifies that layout matches the schematic netlist.
- **Post-Layout Simulation:** Re-simulate with **extracted parasitics (RC)** to verify timing closure.

---

## 2.2 Why Each Phase Matters — The Underlying "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hidden Truth of VLSI Design:**
> Up to **70 % of the design cycle time** is spent in **verification**, not design. A circuit that simulates correctly at the schematic level **may fail at the layout level** because of:
> - **Parasitic capacitances** slowing down signals.
> - **IR drop** in power rails.
> - **Antenna effects** during fabrication.
> - **Crosstalk** between adjacent wires.
> 
> Hence, KTU examiners reward students who **simulate at every stage** and document the **differences between pre-layout and post-layout results**.

---

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for Micro Projects

The following formulas are the **most commonly tested** in micro project viva voce and report evaluation.

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Application in Micro Project | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | Used in current/voltage calculations | F/m² |
| $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | Saturation drain current (long-channel) | Sizing transistors for drive strength | A (Amperes) |
| $t_p = \dfrac{0.69 R_{eq} C_{eq}$ | Propagation delay (RC model) | Estimating timing for layout iteration | s (seconds) |
| $t_p = \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 I_D}$ | Propagation delay in CMOS gate | Calculating critical path delay | s (seconds) |
| $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Dynamic power dissipation | Power budget verification | W (Watts) |
| $P_{static} = I_{leak} V_{DD}$ | Static (leakage) power | Low-power designs | W (Watts) |
| $V_{IH} = \dfrac{1}{8}(3 V_{DD} + 2 V_{th})$ | Input high voltage (noise margin calc) | Noise margin analysis | V (Volts) |
| $V_{IL} = \dfrac{1}{8}(3 V_{DD} - 2 V_{th})$ | Input low voltage (noise margin calc) | Noise margin analysis | V (Volts) |
| $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Noise margin high | Robustness verification | V (Volts) |
| $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Noise margin low | Robustness verification | V (Volts) |
| $Power\ Delay\ Product (PDP) = P_{avg} \cdot t_p$ | Energy efficiency metric | Comparing design variants | J (Joules) |
| $Energy\ Delay\ Product (EDP) = PDP \cdot t_p$ | Combined energy-performance metric | Optimization trade-off | J·s |
| $A = N_{transistors} \times W_{avg} \times L_{min}$ | Approximate area estimate | Layout area report | μm² |
| $Fan\ Out = \dfrac{C_{load}}{C_{input}}$ | Loading factor | Buffer insertion decisions | dimensionless |
| $A_{DRC} = \dfrac{N_{DRC\ violations}}{N_{total\ checks}} \times 100\%$ | DRC pass percentage | Layout verification metric | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Never use the vertical pipe `|` in your formulas when writing reports — LaTeX editors will break. Use `\vert` or `\mid` as in $\vert V_{GS} - V_{th} \vert$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why Industry Values Micro Project Experience:**
> Every semiconductor company (Intel, AMD, Qualcomm, Texas Instruments, NXP, MediaTek) recruits engineers who have **hands-on tool experience**. A micro project that uses **Cadence / Synopsys / Mentor Graphics** tools gives the graduate a **6-month head start** over peers who only know theory. Additionally, the **report writing and presentation skills** cultivated during the micro project are directly transferable to **IP block documentation** in industry.

The micro project also serves as a **portfolio piece** that students can showcase during **campus placements** — particularly for VLSI and embedded systems roles.

---

## 2.5 The Project Lifecycle (Gantt-Style Breakdown for KTU 2024)

| Week | Activity | Deliverable |
|---|---|---|
| Week 1 | Problem selection, literature survey, specification | Problem statement document |
| Week 2 | Schematic/RTL design + functional simulation | Schematic + waveform screenshots |
| Week 3 | Synthesis (if HDL) / Pre-layout verification | Synthesis report (Area, Timing, Power) |
| Week 4 | Layout design + DRC + LVS | GDS-II / layout screenshots |
| Week 5 | Post-layout simulation + optimization | Final waveforms + comparison tables |
| Week 6 | Report writing + Presentation | Final report + PPT |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Sizing a CMOS Inverter for a Micro Project

> **Problem:** A student is designing a **CMOS inverter** in **180 nm technology** as part of a micro project. Given:
> - $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V
> - $V_{th,n} = \vert V_{th,p} \vert = 0.4$ V
> - $\mu_n C_{ox} = 270$ μA/V²
> - $\mu_p C_{ox} = 70$ μA/V²
> - Required symmetric rise/fall time → **Pull-up to Pull-down ratio (Wp/Lp) : (Wn/Ln) ≈ 2.5 : 1**
> - Default length $L = 0.18$ μm
> 
> **Find:** (a) The (W/L) ratios of NMOS and PMOS to achieve symmetric drive strength. (b) The static power dissipation. (c) The noise margins.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Determine the W/L ratio for symmetric current drive.**

For symmetric drive strength, the PMOS current must equal the NMOS current at the switching threshold:

$$I_{D,n} = I_{D,p}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W_n}{L_n} (V_{GS} - V_{th,n})^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \dfrac{W_p}{L_p} (V_{GS} - \vert V_{th,p} \vert)^2$$

Since $V_{th,n} = \vert V_{th,p} \vert$, the squared terms cancel, leaving:

$$\mu_n \dfrac{W_n}{L_n} = \mu_p \dfrac{W_p}{L_p}$$

$$\dfrac{W_p \vert L_n}{W_n \vert L_p} = \dfrac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} = \dfrac{270}{70} = 3.857$$

Taking $L_n = L_p = 0.18$ μm (minimum length for both):

$$\dfrac{W_p}{W_n} = 3.857 \approx 3.86$$

**Final Answer (a):** 
- **NMOS: (W/L)_n = 1 (i.e., 0.18 μm / 0.18 μm)**
- **PMOS: (W/L)_p = 3.86 (i.e., 0.69 μm / 0.18 μm)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Standard Practice:** The textbook ratio of 2.5:1 is a **rough approximation**. For a precise academic project, always calculate the exact ratio from your technology's $\mu_n$ and $\mu_p$ values.

---

**Step 2: Calculate the static power dissipation.**

In steady state (input held at logic 0 or logic 1), one of the transistors is OFF. Ideally, **no current flows from V_DD to GND**, so:

$$P_{static} = I_{leak} \times V_{DD} \approx 0 \text{ W (ideal CMOS)}$$

In practice (accounting for subthreshold leakage in 180 nm):

$$P_{static} = I_{off} \times V_{DD} = (10 \text{ nA}) \times (1.8 \text{ V}) = 18 \text{ nW}$$

**Final Answer (b):** $P_{static} = 18$ **nW** (dominant dynamic power in CMOS).

---

**Step 3: Calculate the noise margins.**

The noise margins for a symmetric CMOS inverter (in saturation region analysis) are:

$$V_{IH} = \dfrac{1}{8} (3 V_{DD} + 2 V_{th}) = \dfrac{1}{8} (3 \times 1.8 + 2 \times 0.4) = \dfrac{1}{8} (5.4 + 0.8) = \dfrac{6.2}{8} = 0.775 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{IL} = \dfrac{1}{8} (3 V_{DD} - 2 V_{th}) = \dfrac{1}{8} (5.4 - 0.8) = \dfrac{4.6}{8} = 0.575 \text{ V}$$

For ideal output swings: $V_{OH} = V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, $V_{OL} = 0$ V.

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 1.8 - 0.775 = 1.025 \text{ V}$$

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 0.575 - 0 = 0.575 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answer (c):** 
- $NM_H = 1.025$ **V** (very robust)
- $NM_L = 0.575$ **V** (acceptable)

> [!TIP]
> **Symmetric NM indicator:** A well-designed inverter should have $NM_H \approx NM_L$. Here they differ; to make them more symmetric, students may need to **re-size the PMOS** or use a **modified beta ratio**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Dynamic Power Calculation for a 4-bit Adder

> **Problem:** As part of the micro project, a student designs a **4-bit Ripple Carry Adder** with a clock frequency of **100 MHz**. The total switched capacitance is estimated at **50 fF** per transition. The activity factor (probability of switching) is **0.2**. Calculate the dynamic power dissipation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the dynamic power equation.**

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**

- $\alpha = 0.2$
- $C_L = 50 \text{ fF} = 50 \times 10^{-15}$ F
- $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V
- $f = 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^6$ Hz

**Step 3: Compute step by step.**

$$V_{DD}^2 = (1.8)^2 = 3.24 \text{ V}^2$$

$$\alpha \cdot C_L = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} = 10 \times 10^{-15} = 1 \times 10^{-14} \text{ F}$$

$$P_{dynamic} = (1 \times 10^{-14}) \times (3.24) \times (100 \times 10^6)$$

$$P_{dynamic} = 1 \times 10^{-14} \times 3.24 \times 10^8$$

$$P_{dynamic} = 3.24 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{dynamic} = 3.24 \text{ μW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Micro Project Insight:** This 3.24 μW figure is typical for small academic designs. Real industry chips (like a smartphone SoC) consume **watts to tens of watts** because of billions of transistors switching at GHz frequencies. Use this to highlight the **scalability challenge** in your report.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Design Choices in a Micro Project

> **Scenario:** A team is choosing between implementing an **8-bit Carry Look-Ahead (CLA) Adder** vs an **8-bit Ripple Carry Adder (RCA)** for their micro project. They must decide based on **area, speed, and power**.

| Design Metric | Ripple Carry Adder (RCA) | Carry Look-Ahead Adder (CLA) | Best Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gate Count (transistors)** | ~ 5 × N (full adders) = 40 gates for 8-bit | ~ 30 × N (with PG logic) = 240 gates for 8-bit | RCA (smaller area) |
| **Critical Path Delay** | $t_p \approx N \times t_{FA} = 8 \times t_{FA}$ | $t_p \approx 4 \times t_{gate}$ (logarithmic) | CLA (faster) |
| **Dynamic Power (at 100 MHz)** | Lower (fewer gates) | Higher (more switching nodes) | RCA (lower power) |
| **Design Complexity (for students)** | Low (modular, easy to layout) | High (complex PG logic, routing) | RCA (easier project) |
| **Number of I/O Pins** | 16 inputs + 9 outputs = 25 | 16 inputs + 9 outputs = 25 | Tie |
| **Best Suited For** | Low-power, area-constrained apps | High-speed computing apps | Context-dependent |

> [!TIP]
> **For a KTU micro project, RCA is recommended** for first-time teams because:
> 1. It teaches hierarchical design (full adder as a building block).
> 2. It is easy to lay out manually.
> 3. It produces clean waveforms that are easy to document.
> 
> CLA is recommended for **higher-attainment students** seeking distinction grades.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Framework: How to Choose a Micro Project Topic

A KTU 2024 evaluator scores topic selection based on the following decision matrix:

| Criterion | Weight | How to Score (0–5) |
|---|---|---|
| **Relevance to Module 3 syllabus** | 30 % | 5 = Direct mapping; 0 = Unrelated |
| **Feasibility within 6 weeks** | 25 % | 5 = Comfortable scope; 0 = Too ambitious |
| **Demonstrates CMOS/physical design** | 20 % | 5 = Full layout; 0 = Pure HDL |
| **Tool availability in college lab** | 15 % | 5 = Licensed tool; 0 = No tool |
| **Innovativeness** | 10 % | 5 = Novel; 0 = Textbook example |
| **Total Score** | 100 % | Sum × weights |

A score above **70 %** indicates a strong topic choice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The VLSI Design Flow (Y-Chart) — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Definition and Specification] --> B[RTL or Schematic Design]
    B --> C[Functional Simulation]
    C --> D{Simulation Passed?}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Logic Synthesis and Gate Level Netlist]
    E --> F[Physical Design Layout]
    F --> G[DRC Design Rule Check]
    G --> H{DRC Clean?}
    H -- No --> F
    H -- Yes --> I[LVS Layout vs Schematic]
    I --> J{LVS Match?}
    J -- No --> F
    J -- Yes --> K[Post Layout Simulation with Parasitics]
    K --> L{Timing Closed?}
    L -- No --> F
    L -- Yes --> M[GDS II Ready for Fabrication]
    M --> N[Final Report and Presentation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart shows the **iterative nature** of the VLSI design flow. The loop-back arrows (No paths) emphasize that **verification failures** send the design back to an earlier stage — this is a key KTU viva question.

---

## 4.2 Micro Project Execution Timeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Week 1: Topic Selection and Literature Review] --> W2[Week 2: Schematic and RTL Design]
    W2 --> W3[Week 3: Functional Simulation and Verification]
    W3 --> W4[Week 4: Synthesis and Physical Layout]
    W4 --> W5[Week 5: DRC LVS and Post Layout Sim]
    W5 --> W6[Week 6: Report Writing and Viva]
    W6 --> OUT[Final Submission to KTU Evaluation]
```

---

## 4.3 CMOS Inverter Schematic — The Simplest Micro Project Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VDD[V rail at 1.8 V] --> PMOS_D[PMOS Drain]
    IN[Input Signal IN] --> PMOS_G[PMOS Gate]
    IN --> NMOS_G[NMOS Gate]
    PMOS_D --> OUT_NODE[Output Node OUT]
    PMOS_S[PMOS Source] --> VDD
    NMOS_D[NMOS Drain] --> OUT_NODE
    NMOS_S[NMOS Source] --> GND[Ground 0 V]
    OUT_NODE --> LOAD[Load Capacitance C sub L]
    LOAD --> GND
```

> [!TIP]
> **For Micro Project Reports:** Always annotate your schematics with **transistor dimensions** (W/L ratios) calculated as shown in Section 3.1. Examiners look for **justified sizing**, not arbitrary values.

---

## 4.4 The Report Structure (Block Diagram of a KTU Micro Project Report)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TITLE[Title Page] --> ABS[Abstract 150 to 250 words]
    ABS --> INTRO[Chapter 1 Introduction and Objectives]
    INTRO --> LIT[Chapter 2 Literature Review]
    LIT --> SPEC[Chapter 3 Design Specifications]
    SPEC --> IMPL[Chapter 4 Design Implementation]
    IMPL --> SCH[Section 4.1 Schematic or RTL]
    SCH --> SIM[Section 4.2 Functional Simulation]
    SIM --> SYN[Section 4.3 Synthesis Results]
    SYN --> LAY[Section 4.4 Physical Layout]
    LAY --> VER[Section 4.5 Verification DRC LVS]
    VER --> POST[Section 4.6 Post Layout Simulation]
    POST --> RESULT[Chapter 5 Results and Comparison]
    RESULT --> CONC[Chapter 6 Conclusion and Future Scope]
    CONC --> REF[References IEEE Format]
    REF --> APP[Appendices Tool Scripts and Datasheets]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Rubric (Typical Distribution):**
> - **Problem Definition & Specs: 10 marks**
> - **Design & Implementation: 25 marks**
> - **Simulation Results: 25 marks**
> - **Layout & Verification: 20 marks**
> - **Report Quality & Viva: 20 marks**
> 
> **Total: 100 marks** (often scaled to 50 internal marks).

---

## 4.5 EDA Tool Ecosystem Map for Micro Projects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FRONT_END[Front End Design] --> SIM_TOOLS[Simulation Tools]
    FRONT_END --> SYN_TOOLS[Synthesis Tools]
    BACK_END[Back End Design] --> PD_TOOLS[Physical Design Tools]
    BACK_END --> VER_TOOLS[Verification Tools]
    SIM_TOOLS --> S1[Cadence ncsim]
    SIM_TOOLS --> S2[ModelSim]

    PD_TOOLS --> P3[Magic Open Source]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Open-Source Alternative:** Students in colleges without commercial EDA licenses can use **eSim + Ngspice** (FOSSEE, IIT Bombay) or **Icarus Verilog + GTKWave** for HDL-based micro projects. This is officially recommended by KTU for resource-constrained institutions.
