<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: Define a "Micro Project" in the context of the VLSI Design course (PECST415) as per the KTU 2024 scheme. List any four phases of the VLSI design flow that a micro project must cover.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Micro Project** in PECST415 is a small-scale, team-based or individual design exercise that takes a VLSI circuit from **system specification to a verified physical layout (GDS-II)**, demonstrating the application of CMOS design principles learned in the module.

The four mandatory phases of the VLSI design flow that a micro project must cover are:

1. **Problem Definition & Specification** — Documenting the I/O, bit-width, timing, power, and area constraints.
2. **Schematic / RTL Design** — Drawing the transistor-level schematic (or writing HDL code) of the circuit.
3. **Functional Simulation** — Verifying logical correctness using a testbench before physical design.
4. **Physical Design (Layout) & Verification** — Drawing the layout, performing **DRC** (Design Rule Check) and **LVS** (Layout vs Schematic) checks, and running **post-layout simulation** with extracted parasitics.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + Four phases with 1-line explanation each (1.5).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: What is DRC and LVS in VLSI design? Why are they essential before fabrication?**

**Model Answer:**

**DRC (Design Rule Check):** DRC is a verification step that checks whether the physical layout conforms to the **geometric and spacing rules** mandated by the fabrication process (e.g., minimum poly width = 0.18 μm, minimum metal-to-metal spacing = 0.2 μm). It ensures the design is **manufacturable** without violating the foundry's process constraints.

**LVS (Layout vs Schematic):** LVS is a verification step that compares the **transistor-level netlist extracted from the layout** against the **original schematic/HDL netlist** to confirm that they are topologically identical. It ensures that **what was designed** matches **what was laid out**.

**Why essential before fabrication:**
- A DRC-clean layout guarantees that the **foundry can manufacture** the chip without yield issues (e.g., shorts, opens, antenna failures).
- An LVS-clean layout guarantees **functional equivalence** with the intended design — without it, a fabricated chip may behave completely differently.
- Together, they form the **"go/no-go gate"** for tape-out.

**Mark Distribution:** DRC definition (1) + LVS definition (1) + Importance (1).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **skip the post-layout simulation step** and only show pre-layout results. This typically costs **3–4 marks** out of 14.
> - Students often **forget to compare pre-layout vs post-layout waveforms** and **tabulate the area, delay, and power metrics**. Examiners expect a **comparison table**.
> - Vague phrases like "the design works correctly" without **numerical evidence** (e.g., delay = 120 ps, power = 3.24 μW) are penalized heavily.
> - Reports missing a **proper reference section (IEEE format)** lose 1 mark.
> - Students who cannot answer **"why this topology?"** in the viva lose another 1–2 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

**Q: As part of your VLSI Micro Project, you are required to design a 4-bit Ripple Carry Adder (RCA) using CMOS technology in 180 nm. Answer the following:**

**(a)** Draw the **transistor-level schematic of a 1-bit Full Adder** using complementary CMOS (C-CMOS) logic. Explain the operation of the Sum and Carry outputs. List the **W/L ratios** of all transistors for symmetric drive strength. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Perform **functional simulation** of the 4-bit RCA for the input vectors: A = 0101, B = 0011, Cin = 0, and A = 1111, B = 0001, Cin = 1. **Tabulate the expected and simulated outputs**. Calculate the **dynamic power dissipation** assuming a clock frequency of 200 MHz, average switched capacitance of 80 fF, and activity factor of 0.25. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Full Adder Equations**

$$Sum = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}$$

$$C_{out} = (A \cdot B) + (C_{in} \cdot (A \oplus B))$$

**Step 2: CMOS Transistor Implementation (C-CMOS Style)**

The full adder has **28 transistors** in static CMOS:
- **Sum circuit:** 14 transistors (XOR-based using transmission gates or AOI logic).
- **Carry circuit:** 14 transistors (AOI22 + OAI21).

For brevity, the most common academic implementation uses **XOR + MUX topology**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A_IN[A] --> XOR1_IN1[XOR Gate Input 1]
    B_IN[B] --> XOR1_IN2[XOR Gate Input 2]
    XOR1_OUT[XOR Output A XOR B] --> XOR2_IN1[XOR Gate Input 1]
    CIN_IN[Carry In] --> XOR2_IN2[XOR Gate Input 2]
    XOR2_OUT[XOR Output Sum] --> SUM_OUT[Sum Output]
    A_IN --> AND1_A[AND Gate Input A]
    B_IN --> AND1_B[AND Gate Input B]
    XOR1_OUT --> AND2_A[AND Gate Input A XOR B]
    CIN_IN --> AND2_B[AND Gate Input C sub in]
    AND1_OUT[AND1 Output A AND B] --> OR1_A[OR Gate]
    AND2_OUT[AND2 Output] --> OR1_B[OR Gate]
    OR1_OUT[OR Output] --> COUT_OUT[Carry Out]
```

**Step 3: Transistor Sizing for Symmetric Drive**

In 180 nm: $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 3.86$

- **NMOS W/L = 0.36 μm / 0.18 μm = 2** (double minimum for drive)
- **PMOS W/L = 1.39 μm / 0.18 μm ≈ 7.7** (using ratio 3.86)
- **PMOS W/L = 2 × 3.86 ≈ 7.7** for symmetric inverter-based gates.

**Step 4: Explanation of Operation (for marks)**

- **Sum = 0** when an odd number of inputs are 1 (0, 2, or all 3 inputs 1 are even → wait, correct: Sum = 1 when 1 or 3 of the inputs are 1).
- **Carry = 1** when at least two of the three inputs (A, B, Cin) are 1.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Truth Table Verification for 4-bit RCA**

| A (4-bit) | B (4-bit) | Cin | Sum (4-bit) | Cout |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0101 (5) | 0011 (3) | 0 | 1000 (8) | 0 |
| 1111 (15) | 0001 (1) | 1 | 0001 (1) | 1 |

**Step 2: Simulation Setup**

- Apply inputs A and B in parallel.
- Use a **testbench** with a clock-driven input pattern.
- Capture outputs after sufficient setup time (e.g., 10 ns after input change).

**Expected Output Waveform Observations:**
- For test 1: Sum transitions from 0000 → 1000 after ~5 ns propagation delay.
- For test 2: Sum = 0001, Cout = 1.

**Step 3: Simulated vs Expected Comparison Table**

| Test Vector | Expected Sum | Simulated Sum | Match? | Expected Cout | Simulated Cout | Match? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0101 + 0011 + 0 | 1000 | 1000 | ✓ | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| 1111 + 0001 + 1 | 0001 | 0001 | ✓ | 1 | 1 | ✓ |

**Step 4: Dynamic Power Calculation**

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

Substituting:
- $\alpha = 0.25$
- $C_L = 80$ fF $= 80 \times 10^{-15}$ F
- $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V
- $f = 200$ MHz $= 200 \times 10^6$ Hz

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.25 \times 80 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.8)^2 \times 200 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.25 \times 80 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.24 \times 2 \times 10^8$$

$$P_{dynamic} = 12.96 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{dynamic} = 12.96 \text{ μW}}$$

**Conclusion:** The 4-bit RCA meets the specification with $P_{dynamic} = 12.96$ μW, which is well within typical low-power design budgets.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]`**

**Q: Your team is undertaking a VLSI Micro Project to design a 6T SRAM cell in 180 nm CMOS technology. Answer the following:**

**(a)** Draw the **complete transistor-level schematic of the 6T SRAM cell** with proper labeling of all nodes (BL, BLB, WL, V_DD, GND). Explain the **read stability** and **write ability** using the **butterfly curve** concept. List the W/L ratios of all six transistors with justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Perform **DRC and LVS** on the layout. **Tabulate the pre-layout and post-layout** values of read delay, write delay, and hold static noise margin (HSNM). If the post-layout delay is **15 % higher** than the pre-layout delay of 250 ps, calculate the **new post-layout delay and the percentage area overhead** if the layout area is 1.2 μm × 1.0 μm and the active area is 0.9 μm². **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: 6T SRAM Cell Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VDD_RAIL[V rail V sub DD] --> M4_D[PMOS M4 Drain]
    VDD_RAIL --> M6_D[PMOS M6 Drain]
    M4_S[PMOS M4 Source] --> M1_D[NMOS M1 Drain]
    M1_S[NMOS M1 Source] --> GND_RAIL[Ground]
    M6_S[PMOS M6 Source] --> M3_D[NMOS M3 Drain]
    M3_S[NMOS M3 Source] --> GND_RAIL
    M4_G[PMOS M4 Gate] --> Q_NODE[Storage Node Q]
    M1_G[NMOS M1 Gate] --> Q_NODE
    M6_G[PMOS M6 Gate] --> QBAR_NODE[Storage Node Q bar]
    M3_G[NMOS M3 Gate] --> QBAR_NODE
    Q_NODE --> M2_G[Access NMOS M2 Gate]
    QBAR_NODE --> M5_G[Access NMOS M5 Gate]
    M2_D[Access M2 Drain] --> BL[Bit Line BL]
    M5_D[Access M5 Drain] --> BLB[Bit Line Bar BLB]
    M2_S[Access M2 Source] --> Q_NODE
    M5_S[Access M5 Source] --> QBAR_NODE
    WL_IN[Word Line WL] --> M2_G
    WL_IN --> M5_G
```

**Step 2: Six Transistor Identification**

| Transistor | Type | Function |
|---|---|---|
| M1, M3 | NMOS (Driver) | Pull-down of cross-coupled inverters |
| M4, M6 | PMOS (Load) | Pull-up of cross-coupled inverters |
| M2, M5 | NMOS (Access) | Connect storage nodes to bit lines during R/W |

**Step 3: W/L Ratios (180 nm)**

For **read stability** (must not flip during read):
- Driver NMOS must be **stronger** than access NMOS.
- **Typical ratio: (W/L)_driver : (W/L)_access = 2 : 1** (or 1.5:1 in some literature).

Standard academic sizing:
- M1, M3 (Driver NMOS): W/L = 0.36 / 0.18 = **2**
- M4, M6 (Load PMOS): W/L = 0.54 / 0.18 = **3** (for symmetric inverter)
- M2, M5 (Access NMOS): W/L = 0.27 / 0.18 = **1.5**

**Step 4: Butterfly Curve Explanation (for marks)**

The butterfly curve is the **voltage transfer characteristic (VTC)** of the two cross-coupled inverters plotted on the same axis with axes swapped. It is used to:
- **Read Stability:** Determined by the **static noise margin (SNM)** — the largest square that fits in the eyes of the butterfly curve. Higher SNM = more stable.
- **Write Ability:** Determined by **write margin (WM)** — how easily the cell can be flipped during a write operation. Higher WM = easier to write.

For 6T SRAM, the cell must satisfy **both** read stability AND write ability, which is a fundamental design trade-off.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Pre-Layout vs Post-Layout Comparison Table**

| Metric | Pre-Layout Value | Post-Layout Value (after 15 % increase) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Read Delay** | 180 | 180 × 1.15 = 207 | ps |
| **Write Delay** | 220 | 220 × 1.15 = 253 | ps |
| **HSNM** | 250 | 220 (estimated reduction due to parasitics) | mV |
| **Layout Area** | 1.2 × 1.0 = 1.2 | 1.2 | μm² |
| **Active Area** | 0.9 | 0.9 | μm² |

**Step 2: Calculate the New Post-Layout Delay**

Pre-layout delay = 250 ps
Post-layout delay = 250 × 1.15 = **287.5 ps**

**Step 3: Calculate the Percentage Area Overhead**

Total layout area = $1.2 \text{ μm} \times 1.0 \text{ μm} = 1.2 \text{ μm}^2$
Active area (transistors only) = $0.9 \text{ μm}^2$

Area overhead = Total area - Active area = $1.2 - 0.9 = 0.3 \text{ μm}^2$

Percentage area overhead:

$$\text{Area Overhead \%} = \dfrac{\text{Overhead Area}}{\text{Active Area}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Area Overhead \%} = \dfrac{0.3}{0.9} \times 100\% = 33.33\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Post-Layout Delay} = 287.5 \text{ ps}, \quad \text{Area Overhead} = 33.33\%}$$

**Step 4: DRC and LVS Explanation (for marks)**

- **DRC Check:** Verifies that all **poly widths ≥ 0.18 μm**, **metal spacing ≥ 0.2 μm**, **n+ to p+ spacing ≥ 0.4 μm**, etc. A clean DRC report is required before tape-out.
- **LVS Check:** Extracts the **layout netlist** (using tools like Calibre) and compares it against the **schematic netlist** to confirm **device count, connectivity, and parameter match**. Any mismatch (e.g., missing transistor, swapped nets) is flagged.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for VLSI Micro Project (Module 3):**

- [x] A **Micro Project** in PECST415 follows the **VLSI design flow** from specification to GDS-II.
- [x] The **Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart** has three domains: Behavioral, Structural, Geometric.
- [x] **Six mandatory phases**: Specification → Schematic/RTL → Functional Sim → Synthesis → Layout → Post-Layout Sim.
- [x] **DRC** = geometric rule check; **LVS** = layout vs schematic connectivity check.
- [x] **Dynamic Power** equation: $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — dominant power in CMOS.
- [x] **Static Power** in ideal CMOS is zero, dominated by **leakage** in deep submicron.
- [x] **PMOS : NMOS sizing ratio** for symmetric drive = $\mu_n / \mu_p$ (≈ 3.86 in 180 nm).
- [x] **Noise Margins**: $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$, $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$.
- [x] **Propagation Delay**: $t_p = 0.69 R_{eq} C_{eq}$ in RC model; $t_p = C_L V_{DD} / (2 I_D)$ in current-based model.
- [x] **Pre-layout simulation** does NOT include wire parasitics; **post-layout** does — leading to 10–25 % delay increase typically.
- [x] **6T SRAM cell** requires **read stability vs write ability trade-off**, sized via the **butterfly curve** and **SNM**.
- [x] Common project topics: **Adder, Multiplier, MUX, Counter, SRAM cell, FSM controller, UART**.
- [x] Open-source EDA tools: **eSim, Ngspice, Magic, Electric VLSI, Icarus Verilog, Yosys** — used in resource-constrained colleges.
- [x] Report must follow **IEEE format** for references; report length = **10–20 pages**; team size = **2–4 students**.
- [x] KTU evaluation rubric = **Problem Definition (10) + Implementation (25) + Simulation (25) + Layout/Verification (20) + Report/Viva (20) = 100 marks**.
- [x] Always include a **comparison table** of **pre-layout vs post-layout** results — examiners expect this explicitly.
- [x] For **180 nm CMOS**: $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, $V_{th} \approx 0.4$ V, $\mu_n C_{ox} \approx 270$ μA/V², $\mu_p C_{ox} \approx 70$ μA/V².
- [x] **GDS-II** (Graphical Data Stream) is the final output sent to the foundry for fabrication.
- [x] Avoid **vertical pipes `|` in LaTeX formulas** — use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Examiner Heuristic for Maximum Marks:** A report that includes **(1) a justified problem statement, (2) clean schematic with transistor sizing, (3) a complete testbench with multiple vectors, (4) a pre-layout vs post-layout comparison table, (5) DRC/LVS clean screenshots, and (6) a clear conclusion with future scope** will always secure above 80 % marks, even if the chosen circuit is simple.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mealy and Moore Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mealy and Moore Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Finite State Machines (FSM)

In VLSI Design and Digital Electronics, any **sequential circuit** whose behavior is defined by a finite number of states and a deterministic set of transitions is called a **Finite State Machine (FSM)**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, every synchronous sequential circuit implemented in hardware is mathematically modeled as either a **Mealy Machine** or a **Moore Machine**, based on the way outputs are generated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box**
> A **Mealy Machine** is a 6-tuple FSM defined as $M = \{S, I, O, \delta, \lambda, s_0\}$ where the output $O$ is a function of both the **Present State** and the **Present Input**.
> A **Moore Machine** is a 6-tuple FSM defined as $M = \{S, I, O, \delta, \lambda, s_0\}$ where the output $O$ is a function of the **Present State only**.

The structural difference is captured by the output function:

- **Mealy Output Function:** $\lambda : S \times I \rightarrow O$
- **Moore Output Function:** $\lambda : S \rightarrow O$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Coffee Vending Machine

Imagine two different coffee vending machines that both detect a "₹10 coin inserted" event:

- **Mealy Coffee Machine:** A red LED (output) turns ON *only if* the current state is "₹5 collected" **AND** the user inserts a ₹5 coin right now (input). The output reacts to the *event* of insertion combined with the state. If the user holds the coin, the LED stays on, but the moment the state changes, the LED updates.
- **Moore Coffee Machine:** A red LED (output) is hard-wired to the state itself. When the machine enters the "₹10 collected" state, the LED turns ON. It does not care which coin just dropped in; it only looks at the state label.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** In a Moore machine, the output is glued to the state. In a Mealy machine, the output is glued to the transition arrow.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Highlighted

- **Clock Period ($T_{clk}$):** **Time** taken for one full clock cycle, typically in **nanoseconds (ns)** in VLSI.
- **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$):** **Time** delay through combinational logic, in **ns**.
- **Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** **Minimum time** data must be stable before clock edge, in **ns**.
- **Hold Time ($t_h$):** **Minimum time** data must remain stable after clock edge, in **ns**.

These timing metrics directly decide which machine model is preferred for a given VLSI design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Theory Breakdown

A synchronous FSM is built from two cooperating blocks:
1. **State Register (Flip-Flops):** Stores the current state $S(t)$.
2. **Combinational Logic Block:** Computes the Next State $S(t+1)$ and the Output $O(t)$.

**Mealy Model — Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1:** The circuit samples the external input $X(t)$ synchronously.
- **Step 2:** The combinational logic computes the next state $S(t+1) = \delta(S(t), X(t))$.
- **Step 3:** The combinational logic **simultaneously** computes the output $O(t) = \lambda(S(t), X(t))$.
- **Step 4:** On the next active clock edge, the flip-flops latch $S(t+1)$, becoming the new present state.
- **Why This Matters:** The output $O(t)$ can change *asynchronously* with respect to the clock because it depends on $X(t)$, which can change at any time. This makes Mealy outputs **faster** but **glitch-prone**.

**Moore Model — Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1:** The circuit samples the external input $X(t)$ synchronously.
- **Step 2:** The combinational logic computes the next state $S(t+1) = \delta(S(t), X(t))$.
- **Step 3:** The output is **purely a function of the current state**: $O(t) = \lambda(S(t))$.
- **Step 4:** On the next active clock edge, the flip-flops latch $S(t+1)$, and only then does the output change.
- **Why This Matters:** The output $O(t)$ changes **strictly synchronously** with the clock, producing **glitch-free** waveforms at the cost of being **one clock cycle slower** than the equivalent Mealy machine.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **high-yield cheat sheet** for KTU board exams. Memorize this — it appears in nearly every Module 4 question.

| Parameter | Mealy Machine | Moore Machine |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output Function | $\lambda(S, X)$ | $\lambda(S)$ |
| Output Dependency | State **AND** Input | State **only** |
| Output Timing | Asynchronous w.r.t. clock | Synchronous w.r.t. clock |
| Output Delay | Combinational delay only (Faster) | One clock cycle delay (Slower) |
| Number of States (same function) | $N_M$ (Fewer) | $\geq N_M$ (More or equal) |
| Glitch Susceptibility | **Higher** (depends on input) | **Lower** (clocked) |
| Output Decoding Logic | More complex | Simpler |
| Hazard Analysis | Must analyze input hazards | Only clock-edge hazards |
| Notation in State Diagram | Output written on **transition arrow** | Output written **inside state circle** |
| Typical VLSI Use | UART RX, Compact controllers | Pipeline control, Traffic FSMs |

### 2.3 State Relationship Between Mealy and Moore

A critical formula that KTU examiners love:

$$N_{Moore} = N_{Mealy} + (k - 1)$$

where $k$ is the number of **distinct output values** that appear on different outgoing transitions from any single Mealy state. In the simplest case where $k = 2$ (binary output 0/1):

$$N_{Moore} = N_{Mealy} + 1$$

This is why Moore machines **always need at least as many states as Mealy machines** for the same functional specification.

### 2.4 Real-World VLSI Utility

- **Mealy machines** are preferred for **high-speed data path controllers** (e.g., DRAM controllers, bus arbiters) where the output must react in less than one clock cycle.
- **Moore machines** are preferred for **safety-critical FSMs** (e.g., automotive airbag controllers, medical device controllers) where glitch-free, deterministic outputs are mandatory for **functional safety (ISO 26262)**.
- Both models are used in **Asynchronous FIFO design**, **UART protocol controllers**, and **CPU instruction decoders** within modern ASIC/FPGA flows.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sequence Detector for "101" (Overlapping)

**Problem:** Design a Mealy FSM and a Moore FSM to detect the overlapping bit sequence **"101"** from a serial input stream $X$. Output $Z = 1$ is asserted only when the pattern is detected.

#### Part A: Mealy Machine Derivation

**State Definition (Intuitive Reasoning):**
- $S_0$: Initial / No relevant prefix matched.
- $S_1$: Last input was a "1" (prefix "1" matched).
- $S_2$: Last inputs were "10" (prefix "10" matched).

**Step 1:** Evaluate each state against each possible input.

- From $S_0$ (no prefix):
  - If $X = 0 \rightarrow$ remain in $S_0$, output $Z = 0$ (no match).
  - If $X = 1 \rightarrow$ move to $S_1$, output $Z = 0$ (only "1" matched so far).
- From $S_1$ (last was "1"):
  - If $X = 0 \rightarrow$ move to $S_2$, output $Z = 0$ (we have "10" but not "101").
  - If $X = 1 \rightarrow$ remain in $S_1$, output $Z = 0$ (last "1" is still the prefix).
- From $S_2$ (last was "10"):
  - If $X = 0 \rightarrow$ return to $S_0$, output $Z = 0$ (the new bit "0" breaks the pattern).
  - If $X = 1 \rightarrow$ move to $S_1$, output $Z = 1$ (**pattern "101" detected!**).

**Step 2:** Formalize the state table.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(t+1) &= \delta(S(t), X(t)) \\
Z(t) &= \lambda(S(t), X(t))
\end{aligned}
$$

| Present State $S(t)$ | Input $X(t)$ | Next State $S(t+1)$ | Output $Z(t)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_0$ | 0 | $S_0$ | 0 |
| $S_0$ | 1 | $S_1$ | 0 |
| $S_1$ | 0 | $S_2$ | 0 |
| $S_1$ | 1 | $S_1$ | 0 |
| $S_2$ | 0 | $S_0$ | 0 |
| $S_2$ | 1 | $S_1$ | **1** |

**Number of Mealy States:** $N_M = 3$.

#### Part B: Moore Machine Derivation

**Step 1 (Critical Concept):** In a Moore machine, the output depends ONLY on the state. Therefore, whenever a "101" detection must produce $Z = 1$, that detection condition must be encoded as a **dedicated state**.

**Step 2:** Add an extra accepting state $S_3$ that has $Z = 1$ hard-coded.

| State | Meaning | Output $Z$ |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| $S_0$ | Initial / No prefix | 0 |
| $S_1$ | Saw "1" | 0 |
| $S_2$ | Saw "10" | 0 |
| $S_3$ | Saw "101" (Accept) | **1** |

**Step 3:** Build the Moore state table.

| Present State $S(t)$ | Input $X(t) = 0$ | Input $X(t) = 1$ | Output $Z(t)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_0$ | $S_0$ | $S_1$ | 0 |
| $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_1$ | 0 |
| $S_2$ | $S_0$ | $S_3$ | 0 |
| $S_3$ | $S_2$ | $S_1$ | **1** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the transitions out of $S_3$: when we are in the accepting state and a new bit arrives, we must restart the matching logic from scratch, including handling the **overlapping** case. A new "1" moves us to $S_1$ (the new prefix starts), and a "0" moves us to $S_2$ (we have "10" as the new prefix).

**Number of Moore States:** $N_{Moore} = 4 = N_{Mealy} + 1$. This **verifies the formula** $N_{Moore} = N_{Mealy} + 1$ for $k = 2$.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Mealy-to-Moore Conversion Algorithm

**General Algorithm (Tested in KTU Exams):**

- **Step 1:** Construct the Mealy state table with $N$ states.
- **Step 2:** For every Mealy state $S_i$, identify **all distinct outputs** produced on its outgoing transitions. Let this set be $\Lambda_i = \{Z_{i,1}, Z_{i,2}, \ldots\}$.
- **Step 3:** If $\Lambda_i$ contains more than one distinct value, split $S_i$ into $|\Lambda_i|$ separate Moore states — one for each distinct output value.
- **Step 4:** If $\Lambda_i$ contains a single value, keep $S_i$ unchanged with that output hard-coded.
- **Step 5:** Redraw all transitions. The output of the **destination** Moore state is the output of the corresponding Mealy transition.

**Validation on our Example:**

- $S_0$ has outputs $\{0\} \rightarrow$ no split needed.
- $S_1$ has outputs $\{0\} \rightarrow$ no split needed.
- $S_2$ has outputs $\{0, 1\} \rightarrow$ **must split** into $S_2$ (output 0) and $S_3$ (output 1).

Total Moore states = $3 + 1 = 4$. **Algorithm verified.**

### 3.3 Analytical Timing Comparison

Consider an input pattern arriving in clock cycle $T$ where the Mealy machine asserts $Z = 1$ in cycle $T$:

| Machine | When $Z$ becomes 1 | Reason |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Mealy | **In the same clock cycle** $T$ as the detecting input | Output is function of $(S, X)$; combinational delay only |
| Moore | **In the next clock cycle** $T + 1$ | Output is function of $S$ only; must wait for state register to update |

**Quantified Delay Difference:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{output, Mealy} &= t_{c-q} + t_{comb, output} \\
t_{output, Moore} &= t_{c-q} + t_{comb, NS} + T_{clk}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $t_{c-q}$ is the clock-to-Q delay of the flip-flop and $t_{comb}$ represents the combinational propagation delay. The Moore machine is slower by approximately **one full $T_{clk}$**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General FSM Block Architecture (Mermaid)

The diagram below shows the **canonical block-level architecture** of both Mealy and Moore machines, highlighting the structural difference in where the output logic taps its inputs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputs [External Interface]
        XIN[Input X t]
        CLK[Clock Signal]
    end

    subgraph statereg [State Register]
        FF1[D Flip Flop Q1]
        FF2[D Flip Flop Q2]
    end

    subgraph comb_next [Combinational Next State Logic]
        NSL[Next State Logic delta S X]
    end

    subgraph mealy_out [Mealy Output Path]
        MOL[Mealy Output Logic lambda S X]
    end

    subgraph moore_out [Moore Output Path]
        MOO[Moore Output Logic lambda S]
    end

    XIN --> NSL
    NSL --> FF1
    NSL --> FF2
    FF1 --> NSL
    FF2 --> NSL

    XIN -.-> MOL
    FF1 -.-> MOL
    FF2 -.-> MOL
    MOL --> ZM[Output Z Mealy]

    FF1 --> MOO
    FF2 --> MOO
    MOO --> ZN[Output Z Moore]

    CLK --> FF1
    CLK --> FF2

    style inputs fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style statereg fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style comb_next fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style mealy_out fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style moore_out fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Mealy output path (red) takes both the external input $X$ and the state bits, while the Moore output path (green) takes **only** the state bits. This single structural difference is the entire essence of the two models.

### 4.2 State Transition Diagram for Mealy "101" Detector

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    s0a : S0
    s1a : S1
    s2a : S2
    [*] --> s0a
    s0a --> s0a : X=0, Z=0
    s0a --> s1a : X=1, Z=0
    s1a --> s2a : X=0, Z=0
    s1a --> s1a : X=1, Z=0
    s2a --> s0a : X=0, Z=0
    s2a --> s1a : X=1, Z=1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Convention:** The output $Z$ is written **next to the input** on each transition arrow using the format `X=value, Z=value`.

### 4.3 State Transition Diagram for Moore "101" Detector

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    s0b : S0, Z=0
    s1b : S1, Z=0
    s2b : S2, Z=0
    s3b : S3, Z=1
    [*] --> s0b
    s0b --> s0b : X=0
    s0b --> s1b : X=1
    s1b --> s2b : X=0
    s1b --> s1b : X=1
    s2b --> s0b : X=0
    s2b --> s3b : X=1
    s3b --> s2b : X=0
    s3b --> s1b : X=1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Convention:** The output $Z$ is written **inside the state circle** (e.g., `S3, Z=1`). Transitions are labeled with input only, because the output is already bound to the state.

### 4.4 Mealy vs Moore Timing Waveform Comparison

```mermaid
gantt
    title Mealy vs Moore Output Timing for Input 101
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Clock
    CLK ticks    :a1, 0, 5
    section Input X
    X=10101      :a2, 0, 5
    section Mealy Output Z
    Z_M pulses on detecting 1 :crit, a3, 1, 1
    section Moore Output Z
    Z_N delayed by 1 cycle   :a4, 2, 1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Mealy output $Z_M$ appears in the **same clock cycle** as the detection input. The Moore output $Z_N$ appears **one full clock cycle later**, shifted by $T_{clk}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Mealy and Moore machines in terms of output dependency. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Mealy machine** generates its output $Z$ as a function of both the present state $S(t)$ and the present input $X(t)$, expressed as:

$$Z_{Mealy}(t) = \lambda(S(t), X(t))$$

A **Moore machine** generates its output $Z$ as a function of the present state $S(t)$ **only**, expressed as:

$$Z_{Moore}(t) = \lambda(S(t))$$

The key consequence is that the **Mealy output can change asynchronously** with the clock because it is sensitive to input changes, while the **Moore output is strictly synchronous** with the clock edge, making it more deterministic but one cycle slower.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Why does a Moore machine generally require more states than its functionally equivalent Mealy machine? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Moore machine requires more states because the output is bound to the state itself. Whenever a single Mealy state produces **multiple distinct output values** across its outgoing transitions, those outputs cannot be distinguished by the state label alone. Each distinct output value must therefore be promoted to a **separate dedicated Moore state** with that value hard-coded. Mathematically, if a Mealy state has $k$ distinct outgoing outputs, it must be split into $k$ Moore states. For the common binary-output case where $k = 2$, the relationship reduces to $N_{Moore} = N_{Mealy} + 1$.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**a)** Design a **Mealy machine** that detects the non-overlapping sequence "1101" from a serial input stream. Draw the state diagram and the state table. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Convert the Mealy machine designed in part (a) into an equivalent **Moore machine**. Draw the Moore state table and the state diagram. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q(a):**

**Step 1 — Define States:** We track the longest suffix of the input that is also a prefix of "1101".
- $S_0$: No match (initial).
- $S_1$: Last bit was "1" (prefix "1" matched).
- $S_2$: Last bits were "11" (prefix "11" matched).
- $S_3$: Last bits were "110" (prefix "110" matched).

**Step 2 — Build the State Table for Non-Overlapping Detection:**

| Present State $S(t)$ | Input $X(t) = 0$ | Input $X(t) = 1$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_0$ | $S_0 / 0$ | $S_1 / 0$ |
| $S_1$ | $S_0 / 0$ | $S_2 / 0$ |
| $S_2$ | $S_3 / 0$ | $S_2 / 0$ |
| $S_3$ | $S_0 / 0$ | $S_1 / \mathbf{1}$ |

**Notation:** Entry shown as $S_{next} / Z$.

**State Diagram (Mealy):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    ma0 : S0
    ma1 : S1
    ma2 : S2
    ma3 : S3
    [*] --> ma0
    ma0 --> ma0 : X=0 Z=0
    ma0 --> ma1 : X=1 Z=0
    ma1 --> ma0 : X=0 Z=0
    ma1 --> ma2 : X=1 Z=0
    ma2 --> ma3 : X=0 Z=0
    ma2 --> ma2 : X=1 Z=0
    ma3 --> ma0 : X=0 Z=0
    ma3 --> ma1 : X=1 Z=1
```

> **Marking Distribution (7 Marks for Part a):** State identification = 2 marks, State table = 3 marks, State diagram = 2 marks.

---

**Model Solution for Q(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify states needing split:**
- $S_0$ outputs = $\{0\}$ → keep as $S_0$ with $Z = 0$.
- $S_1$ outputs = $\{0\}$ → keep as $S_1$ with $Z = 0$.
- $S_2$ outputs = $\{0\}$ → keep as $S_2$ with $Z = 0$.
- $S_3$ outputs = $\{0, 1\}$ → **split** into $S_3$ ($Z = 0$) and $S_4$ ($Z = 1$).

**Step 2 — Moore State Table:**

| Present State | Output $Z$ | $X = 0$ Next State | $X = 1$ Next State |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_0$ | 0 | $S_0$ | $S_1$ |
| $S_1$ | 0 | $S_0$ | $S_2$ |
| $S_2$ | 0 | $S_3$ | $S_2$ |
| $S_3$ | 0 | $S_0$ | $S_4$ |
| $S_4$ | **1** | $S_0$ | $S_1$ |

**State Diagram (Moore):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    mo0 : S0 Z=0
    mo1 : S1 Z=0
    mo2 : S2 Z=0
    mo3 : S3 Z=0
    mo4 : S4 Z=1
    [*] --> mo0
    mo0 --> mo0 : X=0
    mo0 --> mo1 : X=1
    mo1 --> mo0 : X=0
    mo1 --> mo2 : X=1
    mo2 --> mo3 : X=0
    mo2 --> mo2 : X=1
    mo3 --> mo0 : X=0
    mo3 --> mo4 : X=1
    mo4 --> mo0 : X=0
    mo4 --> mo1 : X=1
```

**Verification of Formula:**

$$N_{Moore} = N_{Mealy} + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Marking Distribution (7 Marks for Part b):** State split justification = 2 marks, Moore state table = 3 marks, Moore state diagram = 2 marks.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**a)** Explain the **general algorithm** to convert a given Mealy machine into a Moore machine with a suitable example of your own. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A Mealy FSM has the following state table. Draw the corresponding **state diagram** and determine the output sequence for the input string $X = 101101$, starting from state $A$. **(7 Marks)**

| Present State | $X = 0$ | $X = 1$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | D / 0 | B / 0 |
| B | D / 1 | C / 0 |
| C | D / 0 | B / 0 |
| D | D / 0 | C / 1 |

---

**Model Solution for Q(a):**

**Conversion Algorithm (Verbatim for KTU):**

1. **Step 1:** List all states of the Mealy machine and tabulate all distinct output values produced by the outgoing transitions from each state.
2. **Step 2:** For each Mealy state, if it produces only **one unique output value** across all its outgoing transitions, retain that state in the Moore machine with that output value hard-coded.
3. **Step 3:** For each Mealy state that produces **two or more distinct output values**, split that state into multiple Moore states — one new Moore state per distinct output value. Each new state inherits the corresponding output value.
4. **Step 4:** Redirect all incoming transitions of the original Mealy state to the appropriate new Moore state, matching the output value of the original transition.
5. **Step 5:** Verify the final state count using the formula $N_{Moore} = N_{Mealy} + (k - 1)$ where $k$ is the maximum number of distinct outputs from any single Mealy state.

**Example Illustration (Self-Consistent):** Consider a Mealy machine with 2 states $P$ and $Q$. From $P$, the transitions are $P \xrightarrow{X=0/Q=0} P$ and $P \xrightarrow{X=1/Q=1} Q$. From $Q$, both transitions give output 0. Applying the algorithm: $P$ must be split into $P_0$ (output 0) and $P_1$ (output 1). $Q$ remains unchanged. The result is a Moore machine with 3 states.

> **Marking Distribution (7 Marks for Part a):** Algorithm steps = 4 marks, Example = 3 marks.

---

**Model Solution for Q(b):**

**Step 1 — State Diagram Construction:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    sta : A
    stb : B
    stc : C
    std : D
    [*] --> sta
    sta --> std : X=0 Z=0
    sta --> stb : X=1 Z=0
    stb --> std : X=0 Z=1
    stb --> stc : X=1 Z=0
    stc --> std : X=0 Z=0
    stc --> stb : X=1 Z=0
    std --> std : X=0 Z=0
    std --> stc : X=1 Z=1
```

**Step 2 — Trace the Input $X = 101101$:**

| Time Step $t$ | Input $X(t)$ | Current State $S(t)$ | Next State $S(t+1)$ | Output $Z(t)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1 | A | B | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | B | D | **1** |
| 3 | 1 | D | C | **1** |
| 4 | 1 | C | B | 0 |
| 5 | 0 | B | D | **1** |
| 6 | 1 | D | C | **1** |

**Output Sequence:** $Z = 011011$ (read left to right matching time steps 1 to 6).

> **Marking Distribution (7 Marks for Part b):** State diagram = 3 marks, Tracing table = 3 marks, Final output = 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing the output placement:** Examiners award 1 mark specifically for correctly placing $Z$ on the **transition arrow** (Mealy) vs **inside the state circle** (Moore). Getting this wrong is an instant deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the extra state in Moore conversion:** If a Mealy state $S_i$ has outputs $\{0, 1\}$, students often forget to split it into two states. This is the **#1 reason students lose 2-3 marks** on conversion problems.
> 3. **Mixing overlapping and non-overlapping rules:** When the next bit after a match is also a valid start of the pattern, students must handle the **overlap correctly**. Forgetting the "last 1 starts a new sequence" rule is a frequent trap.
> 4. **Forgetting that Moore output is delayed by one clock:** In timing diagram questions, students often draw the Moore output at the same time as the Mealy output. This loses the full 2 marks allocated to the timing diagram.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - Mealy output equation: $Z = \lambda(S, X)$ — depends on **state AND input**.
> - Moore output equation: $Z = \lambda(S)$ — depends on **state only**.
> - State count relationship: $N_{Moore} \geq N_{Mealy}$; binary output case gives $N_{Moore} = N_{Mealy} + 1$.
> - Mealy outputs can be **asynchronous and glitch-prone**; Moore outputs are **synchronous and glitch-free**.
> - Mealy is **faster by one clock cycle**; Moore is **slower but safer**.
> - In state diagrams, Mealy writes $Z$ on the **arrow**; Moore writes $Z$ **inside the circle**.
> - Conversion rule: split any Mealy state with $k$ distinct outputs into $k$ Moore states.
> - Overlapping sequence detectors: the last few bits of the pattern can start a new pattern.
> - Non-overlapping sequence detectors: after a match, the FSM resets to the initial state.
> - Moore machines are preferred in **safety-critical VLSI designs**; Mealy in **high-speed data path controllers**.
> - Always state the **clock edge sensitivity** (positive or negative) when drawing timing waveforms.
> - Always check whether the problem asks for **overlapping** or **non-overlapping** before drawing transitions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verilog HDL Design and Implementation of RISC Stored Programmed Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Verilog HDL Design and Implementation of RISC Stored Programmed Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of the Topic Components

**RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer):** A microprocessor architecture that utilizes a small, highly optimized set of instructions, where each instruction executes in a single clock cycle and performs a simple operation. The instruction format is fixed and uniform, simplifying the hardware implementation.

**Stored Programmed Machine:** A computing paradigm formulated by John von Neumann in 1945, where both the **program instructions** and the **data** reside in the same memory unit. The CPU fetches instructions sequentially (or via branch instructions) from memory, decodes them, and executes them using the datapath.

**Verilog HDL (IEEE 1364-2005):** A Hardware Description Language used to model digital systems at multiple levels of abstraction — behavioral, register-transfer level (RTL), gate-level, and switch-level. For a RISC machine, RTL modeling is the most critical abstraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Students must demonstrate the ability to (i) design the **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)**, (ii) model the **Datapath** (registers, ALU, memory bus), (iii) model the **Control Unit** (finite state machine), and (iv) integrate both into a top-level **synthesizable Verilog module** that emulates a complete stored-program execution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **chef in a kitchen (CPU)** preparing dishes according to a **recipe book lying on a counter (Memory)**.

- The recipe book contains numbered pages → each page is a **memory location** holding an **instruction word**.
- The chef reads a page (fetch), interprets it (decode), performs the action — chop, boil, fry (execute), and stores the result in a bowl (write-back to a register/memory).
- The chef does **not** memorize the recipes — they are **stored in the book** (stored-program concept).
- The book is written in a **strict format** (always "Step 1: Action | Step 2: Ingredient"), much like a **fixed-length RISC instruction format** where every 16-bit/32-bit word has the same structure.
- The chef's hands, knife, and stove are the **datapath (functional units)**. The brain deciding *which* step to do is the **control unit (FSM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **RISC vs CISC Analogy:** RISC is like cooking with a small set of versatile knives (each does one thing brilliantly) — a CISC kitchen would have 50 specialized gadgets, but RISC is faster, simpler, and easier to manufacture (fabricate as silicon).

### 1.3 Core Architectural Metrics (Standardized)

The following metrics are standard for a KTU-level academic RISC machine:

| Metric | Typical Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Word Width** | **8-bit / 16-bit** | Width of registers and ALU operands |
| **Address Bus Width** | **12-bit / 16-bit** | Maximum addressable memory locations |
| **Instruction Word Width** | **16-bit / 32-bit** | Fixed length for RISC uniformity |
| **Program Memory** | **4 KB / 64 KB** | Stores instructions |
| **Data Memory** | **4 KB / 64 KB** | Harvard may separate; von Neumann shares |
| **General Purpose Registers** | **8 × 16-bit** (R0–R7) | R0 typically hardwired to zero |
| **Clock Frequency** | **50–100 MHz** (academic) | Determines instruction throughput |
| **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** | **1–4** | For pipelined/ multi-cycle RISC |

> [!NOTE]
> **R0 = Zero Register:** In RISC architectures like MIPS, ARM, and the academic "V RISC" used in KTU labs, register R0 is **permanently tied to logical zero**. Any write to R0 is discarded. This is a powerful design simplification — it eliminates the need for dedicated zero-immediate instructions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 RISC Design Principles (The "Five R's")

A RISC machine is characterized by the following design tenets, each reflected in the Verilog implementation:

1. **Single-cycle execution:** Most simple instructions (ADD, SUB, AND, OR) complete in **one clock edge**.
2. **Fixed-length instructions:** Eliminates the need for complex instruction decoders. Typically **16-bit** for academic RISC and **32-bit** for commercial RISC (MIPS, RISC-V).
3. **Load/Store architecture:** Only **LOAD** and **STORE** instructions access memory. ALU operates **only on registers** (register-to-register operations).
4. **Hardwired control:** A finite state machine (FSM) generates control signals rather than microprogrammed ROM.
5. **Large register file:** Reduces memory traffic — operands come from registers, not memory.

### 2.2 The Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)

A typical KTU-level RISC machine features a **16-bit instruction** divided into **three fields** (3-address register format). Below is the standard academic ISA used in VTU/KTU syllabi and many textbooks:

| Opcode (4 bits) | Destination / Dest Reg (3 bits) | Source 1 / Src1 Reg (3 bits) | Source 2 / Src2 (6 bits) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `OP[15:12]` | `RD[11:9]` | `RS1[8:6]` | `RS2[5:0]` |

> **Note:** The 6-bit Source-2 field can encode either a register (3 bits) or an immediate value (6 bits), giving the designer flexibility.

**Standard Instruction Set (16 instructions minimum for KTU-level RISC):**

| Opcode (Hex) | Mnemonic | Operation | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0x0` | NOP | No operation | Control |
| `0x1` | ADD | RD ← RS1 + RS2 | R-type |
| `0x2` | SUB | RD ← RS1 − RS2 | R-type |
| `0x3` | AND | RD ← RS1 & RS2 | R-type |
| `0x4` | OR  | RD ← RS1 \vert RS2 | R-type |
| `0x5` | XOR | RD ← RS1 ⊕ RS2 | R-type |
| `0x6` | LDA | RD ← RS1 + Imm6 | I-type |
| `0x7` | STA | Mem[RS1+Imm6] ← RD | S-type |
| `0x8` | JMP | PC ← {PC[15:6], Imm10} | J-type |
| `0x9` | JZ  | If Z=1: PC ← Target | J-type |
| `0xA` | INC | RD ← RS1 + 1 | R-type |
| `0xB` | DEC | RD ← RS1 − 1 | R-type |
| `0xC` | SHL | RD ← RS1 << 1 | R-type |
| `0xD` | SHR | RD ← RS1 >> 1 | R-type |
| `0xE` | CMP | Sets Zero flag (Z) based on RS1−RS2 | R-type |
| `0xF` | HLT | Halts the processor | Control |

### 2.3 Datapath Architecture (Block-by-Block)

The RISC datapath consists of the following interconnected functional units, all of which must be modeled in Verilog:

- **Program Counter (PC):** A 12-bit/16-bit register holding the address of the next instruction. Increments by 1 every cycle, or is overwritten on a branch/jump.
- **Instruction Register (IR):** Latches the fetched instruction for decoding during the execute phase.
- **Register File:** An 8 × 16-bit (or N × W) array of registers with two read ports (RS1, RS2) and one write port (RD).
- **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit):** Performs arithmetic, logic, and shift operations. Has two operand inputs (A, B), an operation-select input (ALU_Sel), and status flags (Z, C, N, V).
- **Data Memory:** A separate RAM (in Harvard) or shared RAM (in von Neumann) used for LOAD/STORE operations.
- **Accumulator / Buses:** Internal A-bus and B-bus route register values to ALU inputs.
- **Address and Data Buffers:** Tri-state buffers isolating internal buses from external memory.

### 2.4 Control Unit — FSM-Based Design

The control unit is implemented as a **Finite State Machine** with a minimum of three states:

1. **FETCH:** PC → Memory Address; Memory[PC] → IR; PC ← PC + 1.
2. **DECODE:** Opcode extracted from IR[15:12]; control signals generated combinationally.
3. **EXECUTE:** ALU operation performed, register file written, or memory accessed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-cycle vs Single-cycle:** In a single-cycle design, ALL three states complete in one clock period (the clock period must be long enough for the slowest instruction — memory access + ALU + register write). In a multi-cycle design, each state is a separate clock phase, allowing a shorter clock period and better performance.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Memory addressable locations | $N = 2^{n}$ | where $n$ = address bus width (bits) |
| Total memory size | $M = N \times W$ | $W$ = word width (bits); units = bits |
| Memory in bytes | $M_{\text{bytes}} = N \times \dfrac{W}{8}$ | For word-addressable memory with $W$-bit words |
| PC increment | $\text{PC}_{\text{next}} = \text{PC} + 1$ | Sequential fetch |
| Branch target | $\text{PC}_{\text{target}} = \text{PC}_{\text{current}} + \text{Imm}$ | PC-relative addressing |
| Jump target | $\text{PC}_{\text{target}} = \{ \text{PC}_{\text{high}}, \text{Imm} \}$ | Absolute / direct jump |
| ALU addition (8-bit) | $S = A + B + C_{\text{in}}$ | With carry-in, result may be 9 bits |
| ALU flag (Zero) | $Z = 1$ if $S = 0$, else $Z = 0$ | Detected by NOR of all result bits |
| ALU flag (Carry) | $C_{\text{out}} = \text{MSB carry from addition}$ | For unsigned overflow detection |
| ALU flag (Negative) | $N = S_{MSB}$ | MSB of result |
| ALU flag (Overflow) | $V = C_{\text{in,MSB}} \oplus C_{\text{out,MSB}}$ | For signed two's complement overflow |
| Instruction throughput | $\text{IPS} = f_{\text{clk}} \times \dfrac{1}{\text{CPI}}$ | Instructions per second; $f_{\text{clk}}$ in Hz |
| Execution time | $T_{\text{exec}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | $N_{\text{inst}}$ = instruction count; $T_{\text{clk}} = 1/f_{\text{clk}}$ |
| CPI (multi-cycle) | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} n_i \times c_i}{\sum_{i} n_i}$ | $n_i$ = count of instruction type $i$; $c_i$ = cycles for type $i$ |
| Addressing modes supported | Register, Immediate, Direct, Indirect, Relative | RISC typically uses 3–4 modes |
| Instruction word bits | $W_{\text{inst}} = W_{\text{op}} + W_{\text{RD}} + W_{\text{RS1}} + W_{\text{RS2/Imm}}$ | Sum of all instruction fields |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Verilog implementation of a RISC stored-program machine is **not academic theatre** — it is the exact methodology used by:

- **Processor IP companies** (ARM, MIPS, RISC-V International) to design every commercial core.
- **FPGA-based embedded systems** where a soft RISC core (e.g., MicroBlaze, PicoRV32, RISC-V "V") is synthesized onto an FPGA for prototyping.
- **ASIC design houses** that take the synthesizable Verilog RTL of a RISC core and fabricate it as a custom silicon die.
- **IoT/Edge devices** that use minimal RISC cores (e.g., SiFive E20) where the small instruction set and predictable timing are critical.
- **Space-grade processors** (NASA's RAD750, ESA's LEON) which are RISC-based for radiation tolerance and verification simplicity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synthesis vs Simulation:** A Verilog RISC model is **synthesizable** when it uses only `always @(posedge clk)`, blocking/non-blocking assignments, and synchronous resets. Constructs like `#10` delays and `initial` blocks are **non-synthesizable** and exist only for **simulation testbenches**. KTU lab exams frequently test this distinction.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Memory Sizing for a 16-bit RISC Machine

**Problem (KTU-style):** A 16-bit RISC machine uses a **16-bit instruction word** and a **12-bit address bus**. Compute (a) the total program memory in bytes, and (b) the maximum number of instructions it can store.

**Step 1 — Identify the address bus width.**

$$n_{\text{addr}} = 12 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of addressable memory locations.**

Each memory location is identified by a unique address. With $n$ address bits, the number of unique locations is:

$$N_{\text{locations}} = 2^{n_{\text{addr}}} = 2^{12}$$

$$N_{\text{locations}} = 4096 \text{ locations}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the word width of memory.**

In a von Neumann RISC machine, memory is **word-addressable** with the same width as the instruction word:

$$W_{\text{word}} = 16 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total memory in bits.**

$$M_{\text{bits}} = N_{\text{locations}} \times W_{\text{word}}$$

$$M_{\text{bits}} = 4096 \times 16$$

$$M_{\text{bits}} = 65536 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to bytes.**

$$M_{\text{bytes}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{bits}}}{8} = \dfrac{65536}{8}$$

$$M_{\text{bytes}} = 8192 \text{ bytes} = 8 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 6 — Compute maximum instruction count.**

Since one instruction occupies one 16-bit word, the maximum instruction count equals the number of locations:

$$N_{\text{inst,max}} = N_{\text{locations}} = 4096 \text{ instructions}$$

> **Logic Note:** If the architecture is **byte-addressable** instead, then $N_{\text{locations}} = 2^{12} = 4096$ **bytes**, requiring $4096 \times 2 = 8192$ 16-bit instructions, doubling the instruction capacity but requiring a 2-bit byte offset in the address. This is the design choice behind MIPS and RISC-V.

### 3.2 Derivation: Instruction Encoding for a LOAD Instruction

**Problem:** Encode the instruction `LDA R3, [R1 + 5]` (Load to R3 the contents of the memory address formed by R1+5) into the 16-bit RISC instruction format: `[Opcode(4) | RD(3) | RS1(3) | Imm6(6)]`.

**Step 1 — Identify Opcode for LDA.**

From the ISA table in Section 2.2:

$$\text{Opcode}_{\text{LDA}} = 0 \times 6 = 0110_2$$

**Step 2 — Encode the destination register R3.**

R3 in 3-bit binary:

$$\text{RD} = 011_2$$

**Step 3 — Encode the source register R1.**

$$\text{RS1} = 001_2$$

**Step 4 — Encode the 6-bit immediate value `5`.**

$$5_{10} = 000101_2$$

**Step 5 — Concatenate all fields (MSB to LSB).**

$$\text{Inst} = \underbrace{0110}_{\text{Opcode}} \; \underbrace{011}_{\text{RD}} \; \underbrace{001}_{\text{RS1}} \; \underbrace{000101}_{\text{Imm}}$$

**Step 6 — Combine into 16 bits and convert to hexadecimal.**

$$\text{Inst} = 0110 \; 011 \; 001 \; 000101 = 0110011001000101_2$$

Splitting into 4-bit nibbles for hex conversion:

$$\text{Inst} = 0110 \; 0110 \; 0100 \; 0101 = 0 \times 6 \, 6 \, 4 \, 5$$

$$\text{Inst} = 0x6645$$

**Step 7 — Verification by Verilog concatenation.**

```verilog
wire [15:0] LDA_R3_R1plus5 = {4'h6, 3'd3, 3'd1, 6'd5};
// Result: 16'h6645
```

### 3.3 Derivation: ALU Zero-Flag Logic Synthesis

**Problem:** Derive the gate-level expression for the **Zero flag (Z)** output of an 8-bit ALU producing sum $S = S_7 S_6 S_5 S_4 S_3 S_2 S_1 S_0$.

**Step 1 — Definition.**

The Zero flag is asserted when the ALU result is the zero vector:

$$Z = 1 \iff S = 00000000_2$$

**Step 2 — Boolean expression.**

By definition, $Z$ is true if and only if **all** result bits are 0. Using AND of inverted bits:

$$Z = \overline{S_7} \cdot \overline{S_6} \cdot \overline{S_5} \cdot \overline{S_4} \cdot \overline{S_3} \cdot \overline{S_2} \cdot \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0}$$

**Step 3 — Recognition as an 8-input NOR gate.**

The expression $\overline{S_7 + S_6 + S_5 + S_4 + S_3 + S_2 + S_1 + S_0}$ is the canonical 8-input NOR:

$$Z = \text{NOR}(S_7, S_6, S_5, S_4, S_3, S_2, S_1, S_0)$$

**Step 4 — Verilog implementation.**

```verilog
assign Z = ~|S;  // Reduction NOR operator
// Equivalent: assign Z = ~(S[7] | S[6] | S[5] | S[4] | S[3] | S[2] | S[1] | S[0]);
```

> **Logic Note:** The reduction NOR operator `~|` is synthesizable and infers an 8-input NOR gate. This is the **most compact** Verilog idiom for the Zero flag and is expected in KTU lab viva.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Control Signal Truth Table

The control unit generates a unique pattern of signals for each opcode. Below is the analytical matrix that the FSM must implement:

| Opcode | Mnemonic | RegWrite | MemRead | MemWrite | ALUSrc | ALUOp[2:0] | Branch | Jump |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0000 | NOP | 0 | 0 | 0 | X | XXX | 0 | 0 |
| 0001 | ADD | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 000 | 0 | 0 |
| 0010 | SUB | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 001 | 0 | 0 |
| 0011 | AND | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 010 | 0 | 0 |
| 0100 | OR  | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 011 | 0 | 0 |
| 0101 | XOR | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 100 | 0 | 0 |
| 0110 | LDA | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 000 | 0 | 0 |
| 0111 | STA | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 000 | 0 | 0 |
| 1000 | JMP | 0 | 0 | 0 | X | XXX | 0 | 1 |
| 1001 | JZ  | 0 | 0 | 0 | X | XXX | Z | 0 |
| 1010 | INC | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 000 | 0 | 0 |
| 1011 | DEC | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 001 | 0 | 0 |
| 1100 | SHL | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 101 | 0 | 0 |
| 1101 | SHR | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 110 | 0 | 0 |
| 1110 | CMP | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 001 | 0 | 0 |
| 1111 | HLT | 0 | 0 | 0 | X | XXX | 0 | 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALUSrc** selects the second ALU operand: `0` = register RS2, `1` = sign-extended immediate. **ALUOp** selects the ALU function: `000`=ADD, `001`=SUB, `010`=AND, `011`=OR, `100`=XOR, `101`=SHL, `110`=SHR. **Branch** and **Jump** control the PC multiplexer.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: A Sample Program Encoding

**Program:** Compute `R3 = R1 + R2 - R1` (cancels R1, leaves R2 in R3 — useful for testing ALU bypass paths).

**Step 1 — Convert to RISC mnemonics.**

```assembly
ADD R3, R1, R2   ; R3 ← R1 + R2
SUB R3, R3, R1   ; R3 ← R3 - R1
HLT              ; halt
```

**Step 2 — Encode each instruction (16-bit).**

**Instruction 1: `ADD R3, R1, R2`**

- Opcode = `0001`, RD = `011`, RS1 = `001`, RS2 = `010`
- Binary: `0001 011 001 010` = `0001011001010010_2` = `0x1652`

**Instruction 2: `SUB R3, R3, R1`**

- Opcode = `0010`, RD = `011`, RS1 = `011`, RS2 = `001`
- Binary: `0010 011 011 001` = `0010011011001001_2` = `0x26C9`

**Instruction 3: `HLT`**

- Opcode = `1111`, remaining bits = `0`
- Binary: `1111 000 000 000000` = `1111000000000000_2` = `0xF000`

**Step 3 — Program memory contents.**

| Address | Hex Code | Instruction |
|---|---|---|
| 0x000 | 0x1652 | ADD R3, R1, R2 |
| 0x001 | 0x26C9 | SUB R3, R3, R1 |
| 0x002 | 0xF000 | HLT |

**Step 4 — Trace execution (assuming R1=5, R2=7 initially).**

After ADD: $R3 = 5 + 7 = 12$
After SUB: $R3 = 12 - 5 = 7$ ✓ (matches expected $R2$)

> **Logic Note:** This trace demonstrates that the RISC machine **fetches, decodes, and executes** instructions sequentially from memory — the defining feature of the **von Neumann stored-program model**.

### 3.6 Derivation: Instruction Throughput vs Clock Period Trade-off

**Problem:** A RISC CPU runs at 100 MHz. Instructions A, B, C take 1, 2, and 3 cycles respectively. A program contains 100 of A, 50 of B, and 25 of C. Compute (a) average CPI, (b) total execution time, and (c) MIPS rating.

**Step 1 — Total instruction count.**

$$N = 100 + 50 + 25 = 175 \text{ instructions}$$

**Step 2 — Total clock cycles.**

$$C_{\text{total}} = (100 \times 1) + (50 \times 2) + (25 \times 3)$$

$$C_{\text{total}} = 100 + 100 + 75 = 275 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Average CPI.**

$$\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{total}}}{N} = \dfrac{275}{175} = 1.571$$

**Step 4 — Total execution time.**

$$T_{\text{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{\text{exec}} = C_{\text{total}} \times T_{\text{clk}} = 275 \times 10 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{\text{exec}} = 2750 \text{ ns} = 2.75 \, \mu s$$

**Step 5 — MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second) rating.**

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{6}}{1.571 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\text{MIPS} \approx 63.65 \text{ MIPS}$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Architecture of a 16-bit RISC Stored-Program Machine

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the full system architecture showing data flow between the major units, control signals from the FSM, and the dual-bus structure of the datapath.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT_BUS["External Interface"]
        CLK_IN["Clock Input"]
        RST_IN["Reset Input"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["Control Unit FSM"]
        FSM_DEC["Instruction Decoder"]
        FSM_SEQ["Sequencer: FETCH-DECODE-EXEC"]
    end

    subgraph DP["Datapath"]
        PC_REG["Program Counter PC 16-bit"]
        IR_REG["Instruction Register IR 16-bit"]
        REGFILE["Register File 8 x 16-bit R0 to R7"]
        ALU_UNIT["ALU 16-bit Adder Logic Shift"]
        FLAGS["Status Flags Z C N V"]
        ACC_A["A Bus 16-bit"]
        ACC_B["B Bus 16-bit"]
        RESULT_BUS["Result Bus 16-bit"]
    end

    subgraph MEM["Memory Subsystem"]
        PROG_MEM["Program Memory 64K x 16"]
        DATA_MEM["Data Memory 64K x 16"]
    end

    CLK_IN --> PC_REG
    CLK_IN --> IR_REG
    CLK_IN --> REGFILE
    RST_IN --> PC_REG
    RST_IN --> IR_REG
    RST_IN --> FSM_SEQ

    PC_REG -->|"Addr 16-bit"| PROG_MEM
    PROG_MEM -->|"Inst 16-bit"| IR_REG
    IR_REG -->|"Opcode 4-bit"| FSM_DEC
    IR_REG -->|"RD RS1 RS2 fields"| REGFILE
    IR_REG -->|"Imm6 field"| ACC_B

    FSM_DEC -->|"ALUOp RegWrite MemRead MemWrite Branch Jump"| FSM_SEQ
    FSM_SEQ -->|"Control Bus"| REGFILE
    FSM_SEQ -->|"Control Bus"| ALU_UNIT
    FSM_SEQ -->|"PC_Write Enable"| PC_REG
    FSM_SEQ -->|"IR_Load Enable"| IR_REG

    REGFILE -->|"RS1 value"| ACC_A
    REGFILE -->|"RS2 value"| ACC_B
    ACC_A --> ALU_UNIT
    ACC_B --> ALU_UNIT
    ALU_UNIT --> RESULT_BUS
    ALU_UNIT --> FLAGS
    RESULT_BUS --> REGFILE
    RESULT_BUS --> DATA_MEM
    FLAGS --> FSM_SEQ
```

### 4.2 Instruction Execution State Machine (Control Unit)

This diagram shows the three-state FSM governing fetch-decode-execute sequencing, with branch decision logic and halt transition.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> FETCH
    FETCH --> DECODE: Clock Edge / PC incremented
    DECODE --> EXECUTE: Opcode decoded and operands read
    EXECUTE --> FETCH: Normal instruction
    EXECUTE --> HALT_STATE: Opcode HLT
    EXECUTE --> BRANCH_TAKEN: JZ and Z flag set
    BRANCH_TAKEN --> FETCH: PC loaded with target
    EXECUTE --> FETCH: JMP and unconditional branch
    HALT_STATE --> [*]
```

> **Interpretation:** The FSM cycles through `FETCH → DECODE → EXECUTE → FETCH` for sequential instructions. Branch instructions transition to `BRANCH_TAKEN` which overwrites the PC. The HLT instruction latches the FSM in `HALT_STATE` indefinitely, requiring a reset to restart.

### 4.3 Detailed Datapath with Multiplexers and Buses

This expanded datapath diagram illustrates the internal multiplexer network that routes data between registers, ALU inputs, and memory under FSM control.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC["PC 16-bit"] -->|"to ProgMemAddr"| MUX1
    MUX1["MUX PC Source"] --> PROGADDR["Program Memory Address Bus"]
    PROGADDR --> PROGMEM["Program Memory"]
    PROGMEM -->|"Instruction 16-bit"| IR["Instruction Register"]
    IR --> DEC["Opcode Decoder"]
    IR --> RD_FIELD["RD 3-bit"]
    IR --> RS1_FIELD["RS1 3-bit"]
    IR --> RS2_FIELD["RS2 3-bit or Imm6 6-bit"]

    RD_FIELD --> RFWADDR["RegFile Write Addr"]
    RS1_FIELD --> RFRADDR1["RegFile Read Addr 1"]
    RS2_FIELD --> RFRADDR2["RegFile Read Addr 2"]

    RFRADDR1 --> RF["Register File 8 x 16"]
    RFRADDR2 --> RF
    RFWADDR --> RF

    RF --> BUS_A["A Bus 16-bit"]
    RF --> BUS_B["Bus B 16-bit"]
    RS2_FIELD -->|"sign extend"| IMM_EXT["Immediate Extender"]
    IMM_EXT --> MUX2["MUX ALU B Input"]
    BUS_B --> MUX2
    MUX2 --> ALU_B["ALU Operand B"]

    BUS_A --> ALU_A["ALU Operand A"]
    ALU_A --> ALU["ALU Core"]
    ALU_B --> ALU
    ALU --> ALURES["ALU Result 16-bit"]
    ALURES --> MUX3["MUX Writeback Source"]
    DATAMEM["Data Memory"] --> DATAREAD["Read Data 16-bit"]
    DATAREAD --> MUX3
    MUX3 --> RFWPORT["RegFile Write Data"]
    RFWPORT --> RF

    ALURES --> DMADDR["Data Memory Address"]
    DMADDR --> DATAMEM
    RF --> DMDATA["Write Data to Memory"]
    DMDATA --> DATAMEM

    ALU --> FLAGS_REG["Flags Z C N V"]
    FLAGS_REG --> CONDBRANCH["Conditional Branch Logic"]
    CONDBRANCH --> PC_LOAD["PC Load Enable"]
    DEC --> CONTROL["Control Signal Generator"]
    CONTROL --> MUX1
    CONTROL --> MUX2
    CONTROL --> MUX3
    CONTROL --> RF
    CONTROL --> PC_LOAD
```

This diagram shows the file/module structure recommended for a clean, hierarchical Verilog RISC implementation — the structure expected in KTU lab evaluations.
