## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of the FPGA Routing Fabric

The FPGA routing fabric consists of three physically distinct but logically coupled structures:

#### A. Connection Box (CB)
A **Connection Box** sits at the boundary of every CLB and provides the **interface** between the CLB's pins and the routing channel. It determines **how many tracks** each CLB pin can access. The CB contains programmable switches (PIPs) that connect each CLB input/output pin to a subset of the channel tracks.

- **Flexibility Factor $F_c$**: The fraction of tracks to which a CB pin connects.
  - $F_c = 1 \Rightarrow$ pin connects to **all** tracks (maximum flexibility, maximum area).
  - $F_c = 0.25 \Rightarrow$ pin connects to only $W_c/4$ tracks (minimum flexibility).

#### B. Switch Box (SB)
A **Switch Box** sits at the intersection of horizontal and vertical channels and contains the programmable switches that **change the direction** of a signal (turn from horizontal to vertical, vertical to horizontal, or continue straight). 

- **Switch Density $F_s$**: Fraction of all possible crosspoints that are programmable.
  - $F_s = 3$ (Wilton switch box) — all three other directions are switchable.
  - $F_s = 4$ (Disjoint switch box) — fewer switches, less flexible, less area.

#### C. Wire Segments
Metal wire segments of predefined lengths. The distribution of segment lengths is one of the most critical design parameters of an FPGA architecture.

### 2.2 Switch Box Topologies (KTU-Favorite Question)

There are **four classical topologies** that KTU examiners love to test:

**1. Disjoint Switch Box**
Each incoming track connects only to **one outgoing track**. Three of the six possible turns exist. Minimal switches → low area, but signal may need **two SBs** to turn.

**2. Wilton Switch Box**
Every incoming track can connect to **three outgoing tracks** (the one in its own segment + the one above + the one below). Maximum flexibility among classical SBoxes. $F_s = 3$.

**3. Universal Switch Box**
Combines Wilton and Disjoint to achieve routability with fewer switches. Optimizes the $F_s$ value.

**4. Imran Switch Box**
Designed for **area efficiency**, achieving $F_s = 3$ with reduced transistor count.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Two-Mark Quick Recall:** *"Wilton SB has flexibility $F_s = 3$; Disjoint SB has flexibility $F_s = 4$ but only 6 switches per channel; Universal SB combines them for optimum routability."*

### 2.3 PIP (Programmable Interconnect Point) Technologies — Deep Dive

| Technology | ON Resistance | OFF Resistance | Programmability | Volatility | Area / PIP | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pass Transistor (NMOS)** | ~1 kΩ | >1 GΩ | SRAM-controlled | Volatile | 6T SRAM + 1 pass = 7T | Academic |
| **CMOS Transmission Gate** | ~200 Ω | >1 GΩ | SRAM-controlled | Volatile | 6T SRAM + 2 MOS = 8T | Xilinx Virtex |
| **Multiplexer** | ~50 Ω (buffered) | N/A | SRAM select | Volatile | $n \times$ SRAM + mux | Most modern FPGAs |
| **Antifuse (ONO)** | ~50 Ω | >1 GΩ | One-time | **Non-volatile** | 1 antifuse + 1 select | Actel/AXcelerator |
| **EPROM (UV-erasable)** | ~500 Ω | >10 MΩ | Re-programmable | Non-volatile | 1 EPROM + 1 select | Altera MAX (legacy) |
| **EEPROM/Flash** | ~500 Ω | >10 MΩ | In-system reprogrammable | Non-volatile | 1 FG-MOS + select | Altera MAX-II |

### 2.4 Elmore Delay Model for Routing

The **Elmore delay** is the canonical RC delay model used in FPGA routing analysis and is essential for KTU problem-solving:

$$
\tau_{Elmore} = \sum_{i} R_{i,\text{out}} \cdot C_i
$$

For a **single uniform RC segment** of length $L$ driven by a driver of resistance $R_d$ and terminated by a load $C_L$:

$$
\tau = R_d C_L + R_d C_{\text{seg}} + R_{\text{seg}} C_L + 0.5 \, R_{\text{seg}} C_{\text{seg}}
$$

For a **distributed RC ladder** (which models a long routing wire very accurately), the delay grows as the **square of the length**:

$$
\tau_{\text{long}} = 0.5 \cdot r \cdot c \cdot L^2 + R_d \cdot c \cdot L + R_d \cdot C_L
$$

where:
- $r$ = wire resistance per unit length (Ω/µm)
- $c$ = wire capacitance per unit length (F/µm)
- $L$ = wire length (µm)
- $R_d$ = driver resistance (Ω)
- $C_L$ = load capacitance (F)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Quadratic Term Matters:** Doubling the wire length makes it **4× slower**. This is why long global lines in modern FPGAs are **buffered with active repeaters** to break the RC chain.

### 2.5 The Connection-Box Flexibility Equation

The number of programmable switches in a CB is:

$$
N_{\text{CB}} = F_c \cdot W_c \cdot (I_{\text{CLB}} + O_{\text{CLB}})
$$

where $I_{\text{CLB}}$ and $O_{\text{CLB}}$ are the number of input and output pins of the CLB.

### 2.6 Switch-Box Switch Count

For a Wilton SB of channel width $W_c$:

$$
N_{\text{SB,Wilton}} = 3 \cdot W_c
$$

For a Disjoint SB:

$$
N_{\text{SB,Disjoint}} = 6 \cdot W_c
$$

### 2.7 The Routing Area Model (Area-Bandwidth Trade-off)

Total routing area of an FPGA with $N_{\text{CLB}}$ logic blocks, channel width $W_c$, pitch $p$, and PIP area $A_{\text{PIP}}$:

$$
A_{\text{routing}} = N_{\text{CLB}} \cdot W_c \cdot p^2 + N_{\text{CLB}} \cdot A_{\text{SB}} + N_{\text{CLB}} \cdot A_{\text{CB}}
$$

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = R_d C_L + R_d C_{\text{seg}} + R_{\text{seg}} C_L + 0.5 R_{\text{seg}} C_{\text{seg}}$ | Elmore delay, single segment |
| 2 | $\tau_{\text{long}} = 0.5 r c L^2 + R_d c L + R_d C_L$ | Elmore delay, distributed line |
| 3 | $F_c = \frac{\text{Tracks connected to a CB pin}}{W_c}$ | CB flexibility metric |
| 4 | $F_s = \frac{\text{Number of switches in SB}}{6 W_c}$ | SB flexibility metric |
| 5 | $N_{\text{CB}} = F_c \cdot W_c \cdot (I+O)$ | CB switch count |
| 6 | $N_{\text{SB,Wilton}} = 3 W_c$ | Wilton SB switch count |
| 7 | $N_{\text{SB,Disjoint}} = 6 W_c$ | Disjoint SB switch count |
| 8 | $R_{on,\text{tot}} = N_{\text{pass}} \cdot R_{on,\text{each}}$ | Cumulative PIP resistance |
| 9 | $A_{\text{PIP}} = 6 \cdot A_{\text{min}} \cdot (\text{SRAM bit}) + A_{\text{pass}}$ | PIP silicon area |
| 10 | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_{\text{load}} \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic power of PIP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the **bedrock of FPGA architectural exploration** tools like VPR (Versatile Place and Route) used by Altera/Intel and academic researchers. The delay formula (2) directly determines the maximum clock frequency achievable on a routed net.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Elmore Delay for a 2-Segment Routed Path

**Problem Setup:** A net is routed through **two segments**, each of length $L/2$, connected by a switch at the SB. Driver resistance is $R_d$, segment resistance per micron is $r$, segment capacitance per micron is $c$. The load is a single CLB input of capacitance $C_L$.

**Goal:** Derive the total Elmore delay of this path.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } R_s = r \cdot \frac{L}{2} \quad \text{(resistance of each segment)} \\
&\text{Let } C_s = c \cdot \frac{L}{2} \quad \text{(capacitance of each segment)} \\
&\text{Total path resistance (one segment only since switches have negligible R):} \\
&R_{\text{path}} = R_d + R_s \\
&\text{Total path capacitance:} \\
&C_{\text{path}} = C_L + 2 C_s \\
&\text{Elmore delay (path traversed):} \\
&\tau_1 = R_d \cdot 2C_s + R_d \cdot C_L + R_s \cdot C_s + R_s \cdot C_L \\
&\text{Substitute } R_s = rL/2 \text{ and } C_s = cL/2: \\
&\tau_1 = 2 R_d c \frac{L}{2} + R_d C_L + \frac{rL}{2} \cdot \frac{cL}{2} + \frac{rL}{2} C_L \\
&\tau_1 = R_d c L + R_d C_L + \frac{r c L^2}{4} + \frac{r L C_L}{2} \\
&\text{For a long line, } R_d C_L \text{ and } rLC_L/2 \text{ are usually negligible:} \\
&\boxed{\tau_1 \approx 0.25 \, r c L^2 + R_d c L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** 
- The $0.25 rcL^2$ term confirms that **quadratic delay growth** dominates for long wires.
- The $R_d c L$ term is the **linear delay** introduced by driving the wire capacitance through the driver.

### 3.2 Derivation: Switch Count Comparison (Wilton vs Disjoint)

For a channel of width $W_c$, the SB sits at the intersection of two channels. **Every incoming track has up to 6 possible turns** (3 segments: left, straight, right; for both top and bottom).

**Wilton SB:**
- Each of the $W_c$ incoming horizontal tracks connects to **3 outgoing tracks** (one of length $W_c$ going left, one straight, one going right) in the vertical channel.
- For the full SB, we have $W_c$ horizontal segments × 3 directions + $W_c$ vertical segments × 3 directions = $6 W_c$ switches, but the Wilton is usually counted per side.

**Standard convention (what KTU expects):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{Wilton}} &= 3 W_c \quad \text{(3 switches per track × } W_c \text{ tracks)} \\
N_{\text{Disjoint}} &= 6 W_c \quad \text{(6 switches per track × } W_c \text{ tracks)} \\
\text{Ratio:} \quad \frac{N_{\text{Wilton}}}{N_{\text{Disjoint}}} &= 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Wilton uses **50% fewer switches** than Disjoint, yet provides comparable routability for typical circuits.

### 3.3 Derivation: Critical Path Delay for a Logic Block Driving a Net

**Given:** A CLB output drives a signal through $k$ PIPs in series to a CLB input. Each PIP has ON-resistance $R_{on}$ and the wire between PIPs has resistance $r L_i$ and capacitance $c L_i$.

**Total delay:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left[ R_{\text{prev}} \cdot c L_i + (R_{on} + r L_i) \cdot C_{\text{load},i} \right] \\
&\text{where } R_{\text{prev}} = R_d + \sum_{j < i} (R_{on,j} + r L_j) \\
&\text{Simplified (uniform):} \\
&\tau_{\text{total}} = k \cdot R_{on} \cdot C_L + 0.5 r c \sum L_i^2 + R_d \cdot c \sum L_i \\
&\text{For identical segments of length } L \text{ and } k = N_{\text{seg}}: \\
&\boxed{\tau_{\text{total}} = k R_{on} C_L + 0.5 r c k L^2 + R_d c k L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Each PIP adds a **resistive drop** that quadratically degrades the delay. Modern FPGAs place **active buffers (repeaters)** every 4-6 segments to linearize this.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Programming Technology on Performance

| Performance Attribute | SRAM + Pass TG | SRAM + Mux | Antifuse | Flash |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ON Resistance | ~200 Ω | <50 Ω (buffered) | ~50 Ω | ~500 Ω |
| Area per PIP | High (6T cell) | High (mux) | Very low | Medium |
| Re-programmability | In-system, infinite | In-system, infinite | **None (OTP)** | ~100-1000 cycles |
| Power (Static) | **High** (always leaky) | High | Zero | Zero |
| Power (Dynamic) | Medium | Medium | Low | Low |
| Process Compatibility | Standard CMOS | Standard CMOS | Special (ONO) | Special (FG) |
| Routing Delay | Variable | Predictable | Fastest | Medium |
| Used In | Xilinx, Lattice | All modern FPGAs | Microsemi | Altera MAX |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An FPGA has a routing channel of width $W_c = 40$. Compute the total number of switches in:
- (a) A Wilton Switch Box
- (b) A Disjoint Switch Box
- (c) If each switch occupies 4 SRAM bits (6T each), compute the SRAM area in $\mu m^2$, assuming minimum transistor area $A_{\min} = 1 \mu m^2$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) Wilton SB switches:} \quad N_W &= 3 \times 40 = 120 \text{ switches} \\
\text{(b) Disjoint SB switches:} \quad N_D &= 6 \times 40 = 240 \text{ switches} \\
\text{(c) SRAM bits per switch} &= 4 \\
\text{Total bits (Wilton)} &= 120 \times 4 = 480 \text{ bits} \\
\text{SRAM area (Wilton)} &= 480 \times 1 \mu m^2 = 480 \mu m^2 \\
\text{Total bits (Disjoint)} &= 240 \times 4 = 960 \text{ bits} \\
\text{SRAM area (Disjoint)} &= 960 \times 1 \mu m^2 = 960 \mu m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Wilton uses **120 switches / 480 µm²**; Disjoint uses **240 switches / 960 µm²** — exactly **twice** the area of Wilton.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Define Programmable Interconnect Point (PIP). List the four main technologies used to implement PIPs in FPGAs.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Programmable Interconnect Point (PIP)** is the fundamental configurable switching element in an FPGA routing architecture. It is a single electrical switch (typically a pass transistor, transmission gate, multiplexer select line, or antifuse) that can be set to either a **closed (ON)** state, electrically connecting two wire segments, or an **open (OFF)** state, isolating them. The collection of all PIP states across the FPGA defines the routing topology of the user's circuit.

The four main technologies for PIP implementation are:
1. **SRAM-controlled pass transistor or transmission gate** — volatile, reprogrammable, high area, used in Xilinx and most modern FPGAs.
2. **Antifuse** (e.g., ONO — Oxide-Nitride-Oxide) — one-time programmable, low ON resistance (~50 Ω), non-volatile, used in Microsemi/Actel.
3. **EPROM / EEPROM / Flash floating-gate transistor** — non-volatile, reprogrammable, used in Altera MAX series.
4. **Multiplexer-based selection** — SRAM-controlled MUX routes one of N inputs to a single output, used for buffered routing in modern FPGAs.

*(3 marks — full structure expected: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for technologies with example vendor)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**What is the difference between a Connection Box (CB) and a Switch Box (SB) in FPGA routing? Define the flexibility factors $F_c$ and $F_s$.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Connection Box (CB)** is the routing structure located at the periphery of a **Configurable Logic Block (CLB)**. It contains programmable switches that **connect the CLB's input/output pins to the wire tracks of the adjacent routing channel**, allowing signals to enter or leave the CLB. A CB thus provides the **input/output interface** between logic and routing.

A **Switch Box (SB)** is the routing structure located at the **intersection of horizontal and vertical routing channels**. It contains programmable switches that allow signals to **change direction** (turn a corner) or continue straight between orthogonal channels. A SB thus enables the **topology of the routing network** itself.

**Flexibility factors:**
- **Connection Box Flexibility $F_c$**: the ratio of the number of tracks to which a CB pin connects, to the total channel width $W_c$. A higher $F_c$ means more routability but more PIP area.
  
  $$F_c = \frac{\text{Tracks connected to a pin}}{W_c}$$

- **Switch Box Flexibility $F_s$**: the ratio of the actual number of switches in an SB to the maximum possible $6 W_c$. A **Wilton SB** has $F_s = 3$, while a **Disjoint SB** achieves $F_s = 4$ but with more switches.

*(3 marks — 1 for CB, 1 for SB, 1 for flexibility definitions)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Wilton switch box topology** with a neat diagram. Compare it with the **Disjoint switch box topology** in terms of flexibility, switch count, and routability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Elmore delay expression** for a single uniform RC routing segment driven by a driver of resistance $R_d$ and terminated by a load capacitance $C_L$. Use the standard $0.5 R_{\text{seg}} C_{\text{seg}}$ distributed model. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Wilton vs Disjoint Switch Box** *(7 marks)*

In an FPGA switch box, each incoming wire can turn in **6 possible directions** — 3 segments (left, straight, right) on each of the 2 perpendicular channels. A **Wilton switch box** is a topology where each incoming wire connects to **3 outgoing wires** through programmable switches, providing a flexibility of $F_s = 3$.

**Wilton SB properties:**
- Number of switches per channel side = $3 W_c$
- Maximum flexibility with minimum switches
- Any signal can reach any of the 3 outgoing tracks with just one switch

**Disjoint SB properties:**
- Number of switches per channel side = $6 W_c$ (full connectivity attempted)
- Each of the 6 directions is independently switchable
- $F_s = 4$ (theoretical maximum flexibility in classical topologies)
- Uses **double** the switches of Wilton for marginal routability gain

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Wilton SB | Disjoint SB |
|---|---|---|
| Switches per channel | $3 W_c$ | $6 W_c$ |
| Flexibility $F_s$ | 3 | 4 |
| Routability | High | High |
| Area | Lower | **2× higher** |
| Used in | Xilinx 4000, 7-series | Less common |

**Conclusion:** Wilton is **preferred** in commercial FPGAs because it provides near-optimal routability with **half** the silicon area of Disjoint.

*(Awarded marks: 1 for Wilton definition, 2 for diagram, 2 for Disjoint comparison, 2 for table/conclusion)*

---

**(b) Elmore Delay Derivation** *(7 marks)*

Consider a routing segment modeled as a **uniform RC ladder** with total resistance $R_{\text{seg}}$ and total capacitance $C_{\text{seg}}$, driven by a source of resistance $R_d$ and loaded by $C_L$.

The **Elmore delay** is the first moment of the impulse response, computed as:

$$
\tau = \sum_{i} R_{i,\text{out}} \cdot C_i
$$

where $R_{i,\text{out}}$ is the common-path resistance from the input to the output side of node $i$.

For the path: **Driver → segment → Load**, we identify three capacitors in order:
1. $C_{\text{seg}}$ (distributed wire capacitance)
2. $C_L$ (load capacitance)
3. (Driver sees no upstream capacitor beyond itself)

Computing the common-path resistance to each capacitor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Capacitor 1: } C_{\text{seg}} \text{ sees resistance } R_d \text{ (only driver) and also } 0.5 R_{\text{seg}} \text{ internal to itself} \\
&\text{Capacitor 2: } C_L \text{ sees the full driver + full segment: } R_d + R_{\text{seg}} \\
&\text{Therefore:} \\
&\tau = (R_d + 0.5 R_{\text{seg}}) \cdot C_{\text{seg}} + (R_d + R_{\text{seg}}) \cdot C_L \\
&\boxed{\tau = R_d C_L + R_d C_{\text{seg}} + R_{\text{seg}} C_L + 0.5 R_{\text{seg}} C_{\text{seg}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step interpretation of each term:**
- $R_d C_L$: time to charge the load through the driver (dominates for short wires).
- $R_d C_{\text{seg}}$: time to charge the wire capacitance through the driver.
- $R_{\text{seg}} C_L$: time to charge the load through the wire's distributed resistance.
- $0.5 R_{\text{seg}} C_{\text{seg}}$: average internal delay of the distributed line (the "self-loading" term).

For a long line, $C_{\text{seg}} \gg C_L$, and the term $0.5 R_{\text{seg}} C_{\text{seg}} = 0.5 r c L^2$ becomes the dominant quadratic term, explaining why long interconnect paths in FPGAs require **buffer insertion**.

*(Awarded marks: 1 for setup, 2 for common-path resistance identification, 2 for the algebraic derivation, 2 for the final boxed equation and physical interpretation)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three main programming technologies** for FPGA programmable interconnects: SRAM, Antifuse, and EPROM/Flash. Compare them on at least five parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A routing channel of an FPGA has width $W_c = 50$. Compute the number of programmable switches in (i) a **Wilton switch box** and (ii) a **Disjoint switch box**. If each PIP uses a 6T SRAM cell of area $0.6 \mu m^2$, find the total configuration memory area for each SB topology. Comment on the area-routability trade-off. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Programming Technologies** *(7 marks)*

**1. SRAM-based Interconnect:**
A **6-transistor CMOS SRAM cell** stores one configuration bit. This bit controls the gate of a **pass transistor** or selects a line of a **multiplexer**. When the SRAM holds '1', the pass transistor turns ON, connecting two wire segments. SRAM is **volatile** — configuration is lost on power-down and must be reloaded from external flash on every power-up.
- **Advantages:** Infinite reprogrammability, in-system, standard CMOS process.
- **Disadvantages:** Volatile, large area per PIP (~6T + 1T pass), high static leakage.
- **Used in:** Xilinx Virtex, Spartan, 7-series, Intel Cyclone, Lattice ECP.

**2. Antifuse-based Interconnect:**
An **antifuse** is the opposite of a fuse. Initially a **high-resistance open** (~1 GΩ). When a high voltage (~10-18 V) is applied, the ONO (Oxide-Nitride-Oxide) dielectric breaks down permanently, forming a **low-resistance link** (~50 Ω). This is a **one-time programmable (OTP)** technology.
- **Advantages:** Low ON resistance (fast routing), non-volatile, very small area per PIP, zero static power.
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot be reprogrammed; requires special high-voltage programming circuitry and ONO process module.
- **Used in:** Microsemi/Actel AX, RTAX, IGLOO.

**3. EPROM/Flash-based Interconnect:**
A **floating-gate MOS transistor** (similar to Flash memory cell) holds charge on an isolated gate. The charge modulates the transistor's threshold voltage, turning the PIP ON or OFF. Erased by **UV light (EPROM)** or **electrical Fowler-Nordheim tunneling (EEPROM/Flash)**.
- **Advantages:** Non-volatile, reprogrammable (100s to 1000s of cycles), in-system (for Flash).
- **Disadvantages:** Medium ON resistance (~500 Ω), requires special process, programming voltage.
- **Used in:** Altera MAX, MAX-II, early Lattice ispMACH.

**Comparison Table (5 parameters):**

| Parameter | SRAM | Antifuse | Flash |
|---|---|---|---|
| ON Resistance | ~200 Ω | ~50 Ω (best) | ~500 Ω |
| Volatility | **Volatile** | Non-volatile | Non-volatile |
| Reprogrammability | Unlimited | **None (OTP)** | 100-1000 cycles |
| Process | Standard CMOS | Special (ONO) | Special (FG) |
| Static Power | **High** | Zero | Zero |
| Routing Speed | Medium | **Fastest** | Slower |

*(Awarded marks: 1 per technology explanation, 2 for the comparison table)*

---

**(b) Switch Count and Area Computation** *(7 marks)*

**Given:** $W_c = 50$, 6T SRAM cell area = $0.6 \mu m^2$ per PIP.

**Step 1: Wilton Switch Box switch count**

$$
N_{\text{Wilton}} = 3 \times W_c = 3 \times 50 = \mathbf{150 \text{ switches}}
$$

**Step 2: Disjoint Switch Box switch count**

$$
N_{\text{Disjoint}} = 6 \times W_c = 6 \times 50 = \mathbf{300 \text{ switches}}
$$

**Step 3: SRAM area for each**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{Wilton}} &= 150 \times 0.6 \mu m^2 = \mathbf{90 \mu m^2} \\
A_{\text{Disjoint}} &= 300 \times 0.6 \mu m^2 = \mathbf{180 \mu m^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Trade-off Discussion**

The Disjoint SB consumes **twice the area** of Wilton for a marginal increase in routability. In commercial FPGAs (e.g., Xilinx), Wilton is preferred because:
- **Area cost** of doubling switches outweighs routability benefit.
- Wilton's $F_s = 3$ is sufficient for >99% of routed nets.
- Smaller area means **more CLBs per die** → higher logic density and lower cost.

**Conclusion:** Wilton is the **optimal area-routability compromise** in modern FPGA architecture.

*(Awarded marks: 1 for Wilton formula, 1 for Wilton value, 1 for Disjoint formula, 1 for Disjoint value, 1 for area calculation, 2 for trade-off discussion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing $F_s$ and $F_c$**: Students routinely mix up the two flexibility factors. Remember: $F_s$ is for **Switch Box** (turn direction), $F_c$ is for **Connection Box** (pin-to-track).
> 2. **Elmore delay sign errors**: The $0.5 R_{\text{seg}} C_{\text{seg}}$ coefficient is the most frequently dropped term. **Always include it** for full marks.
> 3. **Antifuse "fuse" misconception**: Antifuse is an **open** that becomes **closed** upon programming — the **opposite** of a fuse. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this reversal.
> 4. **PIP ON resistance direction**: SRAM + pass transistor has the **worst** ON resistance (~1 kΩ for NMOS only); buffered mux and antifuse are **better** (~50-100 Ω).
> 5. **Channel Width = SB switches**: Students forget to multiply by $W_c$ in Wilton/Disjoint counts. The answer must include the multiplication.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Mile Revision — 5-Minute Memorization List:**

- [ ] **Programmable Interconnect =** post-fab configurable routing network; ~70% of FPGA area.
- [ ] **PIP** = single programmable switch (transistor, TG, mux, antifuse).
- [ ] **Three programming technologies:** SRAM (volatile, infinite), Antifuse (OTP, non-volatile, fast), Flash/EPROM (NV, limited cycles).
- [ ] **Connection Box (CB)** = CLB pin ↔ channel tracks; flexibility $F_c$.
- [ ] **Switch Box (SB)** = horizontal ↔ vertical channels; flexibility $F_s$.
- [ ] **Wilton SB:** $3 W_c$ switches, $F_s = 3$, **half the area** of Disjoint.
- [ ] **Disjoint SB:** $6 W_c$ switches, $F_s = 4$, full connectivity attempted.
- [ ] **Elmore delay for a uniform segment:** $\tau = R_d C_L + R_d C_{\text{seg}} + R_{\text{seg}} C_L + 0.5 R_{\text{seg}} C_{\text{seg}}$.
- [ ] **Quadratic delay** of long lines: $\tau \approx 0.5 r c L^2$ — solved by **buffer insertion**.
- [ ] **Wire segment lengths:** length-1, length-2, length-4, length-N, long lines, global lines.
- [ ] **Antifuse ON resistance ≈ 50 Ω** (lowest among all technologies).
- [ ] **SRAM PIP area ≈ 6T + 1T pass = 7T minimum**.
- [ ] **Modern FPGAs** use **multiplexer-based buffered routing** for predictability and speed.
- [ ] **SRAM-based = Xilinx, Lattice, Intel**; **Antifuse = Microsemi/Actel**; **Flash = Altera MAX**.
- [ ] **Total switches in CB:** $N_{\text{CB}} = F_c \cdot W_c \cdot (I + O)$ where $I$ and $O$ are CLB pin counts.
- [ ] **Dynamic power of a switching PIP:** $P = \alpha C V_{dd}^2 f$.
- [ ] **Interconnect delay** is the **dominant performance bottleneck** in sub-100nm FPGAs.
- [ ] **Buffer insertion** linearizes the $L^2$ delay growth to $\mathcal{O}(L)$.
- [ ] **Routing region** occupies the **majority** of the die (routing > logic area).
- [ ] **FPGA = generic logic + programmable interconnect + programmable I/O + configuration memory.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmable I/O Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmable I/O Blocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programmable I/O Block (IOB):** A configurable interface circuit placed between the internal configurable logic blocks (CLBs/configurable logic) and the external physical package pins of a programmable logic device (CPLD/FPGA). The IOB enables the user to dynamically select the **direction of signal flow (input, output, or bidirectional)**, the **electrical I/O standard**, the **slew rate**, the **drive strength**, and various **on-chip termination, pull, and hysteresis options** through SRAM configuration cells or antifuse/Flash programming.

In modern FPGAs (e.g., Xilinx Spartan/Artix, Intel/Altera Cyclone), the IOB is the **boundary cell** that performs three major roles:

1. **Buffering** the fragile internal CMOS signals against the noisy external PCB environment.
2. **Translating** between the internal core supply voltage ($V_{CCINT}$) and the I/O supply voltage ($V_{CCO}$).
3. **Adapting** to many industry-standard signaling protocols (LVTTL, LVCMOS, SSTL, HSTL, LVDS, etc.) without requiring an external level shifter.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Universal Travel Adaptor":**
>
> Imagine the FPGA's internal logic is a citizen of one country (operating at $1.0\,\text{V}$ core), and every external chip on the PCB speaks a different language (3.3 V, 2.5 V, 1.8 V, differential, etc.). The **Programmable I/O Block** is like a **Universal Travel Adaptor** mounted on each pin. By rotating its prongs (configuration bits), it can:
> - Convert the voltage levels (level shifting),
> - Choose whether to send or receive (direction),
> - Add a current booster (drive strength),
> - Add a noise filter (Schmitt trigger),
> - Apply a gentle pull-up/pull-down when nothing is driving the line (keeper resistor).
>
> Without the IOB, the FPGA would burn out trying to talk to a 3.3 V memory chip, or fail to read a 1.8 V sensor correctly.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Internal core voltage ($V_{CCINT}$):** typically **1.0 V – 1.2 V** in modern 28 nm/16 nm FPGAs.
- **I/O supply voltage ($V_{CCO}$):** ranges from **1.2 V to 3.3 V** depending on the selected standard.
- **Reference voltage ($V_{REF}$):** used for referenced inputs (SSTL/HSTL), typically **$0.5 \times V_{CCO}$** or specific standard values like **0.75 V, 0.9 V, 1.25 V, 1.5 V**.
- **Drive strength options:** standard values are **2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 16, 24 mA**.
- **On-chip pull-up/pull-down resistance:** typically **50 kΩ – 100 kΩ** (weak keeper).
- **On-chip series termination ($R_{S}$):** typically **25 Ω – 50 Ω** (calibrated).
- **On-chip parallel termination ($R_{T}$):** typically **50 Ω – 100 Ω**.
- **Schmitt trigger hysteresis ($\Delta V_{H}$):** typically **0.4 V – 0.8 V**.

---

## 1.4 Where IOBs Sit in the FPGA Architecture

The IOB is positioned at the **physical periphery** of the FPGA die, surrounding the array of logic blocks. The general fabric hierarchy is:

$$\text{Package Pin} \;\rightarrow\; \text{ESD Diodes} \;\rightarrow\; \text{IOB (input/output/tristate logic + registers)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Internal Routing (CRIM/Interconnect)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CLB/Logic Block}$$

Every physical pin on the FPGA package is bonded to **one IOB**. Some high-performance pins may even have multiple IOBs multiplexed (e.g., for differential pairs: P-pin and N-pin sharing a logic resource).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Sub-Blocks of a Programmable I/O Block

A generic IOB contains three parallel data paths multiplexed onto the package pin:

### (A) Input Path (Pad → Internal Logic)
1. **ESD Protection Diodes** — clamp electrostatic discharge to $V_{CCO}$ and GND.
2. **Differential Comparator / Single-ended Buffer** — converts pad voltage to a clean CMOS rail.
3. **Optional Schmitt Trigger** — adds hysteresis for noisy signals.
4. **Optional Delay Element (IDELAY)** — programmable fine delay for source-synchronous interfaces.
5. **Optional Pull-up / Pull-down / Bus-Keeper Resistor** — default logic level.
6. **Input Flip-Flop / Input Latch / DDR Register** — captures data synchronously to the clock.
7. **SRAM Configuration Cell (CE)** — selects registered vs. combinational path.

### (B) Output Path (Internal Logic → Pad)
1. **Output Flip-Flop / DDR Register** — synchronous launch.
2. **3-State Control Flip-Flop** — controls the high-impedance state.
3. **Slew-Rate Control** — programmable **SLOW** (low EMI, long edge) or **FAST** (high speed, high noise).
4. **Drive Strength Selector** — selects output transistor sizing (2/4/6/8/12/16/24 mA).
5. **Open-Drain / Open-Source / Push-Pull Selector.**
6. **Output Buffer (Final Driver Stage)** — pushes current onto the package pin.
7. **Optional On-Chip Series Termination ($R_S$).**

### (C) Tristate / Bidirectional Path
- Shares hardware with both input and output paths.
- The 3-state control (OE) determines whether the output buffer drives the pad or releases it to high-impedance.
- When released, the input path becomes active, allowing **bidirectional bus** operation.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every numerical relation an examiner can test on the topic of Programmable I/O Blocks. Study it as a one-stop revision tool.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Standard / Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output HIGH voltage | $V_{OH} \ge V_{CCO} - 0.4$ | LVTTL @ 24 mA | V |
| 2 | Output LOW voltage | $V_{OL} \le 0.4$ | LVTTL @ 24 mA | V |
| 3 | Input HIGH threshold | $V_{IH} \ge 2.0$ | LVTTL | V |
| 4 | Input LOW threshold | $V_{IL} \le 0.8$ | LVTTL | V |
| 5 | Noise margin HIGH | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | — | V |
| 6 | Noise margin LOW | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | — | V |
| 7 | Schmitt trigger hysteresis | $\Delta V_H = V_{T+} - V_{T-}$ | typically 0.4 – 0.8 V | V |
| 8 | DC drive current limit | $I_{OL,\max} = I_{OH,\max}$ | per pin | mA |
| 9 | Pull-up equivalent resistance | $R_{PU} \approx 50\text{ k}\Omega \text{ to } 100\text{ k}\Omega$ | weak keeper | Ω |
| 10 | On-chip series termination | $R_S = Z_0 - Z_{driver}$ | matched to trace | Ω |
| 11 | On-die parallel termination | $R_T \approx 50 \text{ or } 100$ | SSTL/HSTL | Ω |
| 12 | Differential common-mode voltage | $V_{CM} = \frac{V_P + V_N}{2}$ | LVDS: 1.2 V | V |
| 13 | Differential swing | $V_{DIFF} = \vert V_P - V_N \vert$ | LVDS: 350 mV | mV |
| 14 | SSTL reference voltage | $V_{REF} = 0.5 \cdot V_{CCO}$ | Class I/II | V |
| 15 | IOB setup time | $t_{ISU} \ge t_{ISU,\min}$ | before clock edge | ns |
| 16 | IOB hold time | $t_{IH} \ge t_{IH,\min}$ | after clock edge | ns |
| 17 | IOB clock-to-out | $t_{ICO} = t_{CO,\min} + t_{delay}$ | combinational/registered | ns |
| 18 | DDR capture window | $T_{DDR} = T_{CLK}/2$ | doubled data rate | s |
| 19 | Power dissipation per pin | $P_{pin} = C_L \cdot V_{CCO}^2 \cdot f_{sw}$ | dynamic | W |
| 20 | IOB bank constraint | All pins in a bank share $V_{CCO}$, $V_{REF}$ | per bank | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The DC drive current and termination resistor formulae are critical for **signal-integrity** analysis in high-speed PCB design. The Schmitt trigger hysteresis equation is often tested for **debouncing noisy switches** in industrial applications.

---

## 2.3 Why Each Sub-Block Exists — The "Why & How"

- **ESD Diodes:** Why? Because the human body model can deliver $\pm 2\,\text{kV}$ which destroys sub-micron oxide. How? Clamp diodes shunt the surge to rails.
- **Pull-up/Pull-down:** Why? To define a default logic level when no driver is connected (avoids floating inputs). How? A weak MOSFET pulls the line to $V_{CCO}$ or GND.
- **Schmitt Trigger:** Why? Slow/noisy edges would cause metastability in CMOS. How? Two different threshold voltages ($V_{T+}$ and $V_{T-}$) create a hysteresis band.
- **DDR Register:** Why? To double data throughput without doubling the clock. How? Two flip-flops clocked on opposite edges (CLK and $\overline{\text{CLK}}$).
- **Slew-Rate Control:** Why? Fast edges radiate EMI; slow edges limit bandwidth. How? By adding programmable capacitance to the gate of the final driver transistor.
- **Drive Strength Selector:** Why? Light loads need only 2 mA; heavy bus lines need 24 mA. How? Parallel activation of N output stages of identical width.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates the rigorous derivation of three high-yield IOB calculations that are routinely tested in KTU university examinations.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bidirectional Bus Pull-Up / Pull-Down Voltage Divider

**Problem statement:** A bidirectional I/O pin is connected to a 3.3 V device through a $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ external pull-up. The FPGA's internal weak pull-down is $R_{PD} = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate the steady-state voltage seen at the pin when the FPGA is **not driving** the line.

**Step 1 — Define the equivalent circuit.**
The pad is a floating node pulled up to $V_{CCO} = 3.3\,\text{V}$ through $R_{PU} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and pulled down to GND through $R_{PD} = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$. This is a classic resistive divider.

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider rule.**
The voltage at the pad with respect to ground is:

$$
V_{PAD} = V_{CCO} \cdot \frac{R_{PD}}{R_{PU} + R_{PD}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
V_{PAD} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{50\,000}{10\,000 + 50\,000}
$$

$$
V_{PAD} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{50}{60} = 3.3 \cdot 0.8333
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer.**

$$
\boxed{V_{PAD} = 2.75\,\text{V}}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification against $V_{IH}$.**
Since $V_{PAD} = 2.75\,\text{V} > V_{IH} = 2.0\,\text{V}$ (LVTTL standard), the FPGA correctly reads logic HIGH when nothing is driving the line. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dynamic Power Dissipation per I/O Pin

**Problem statement:** A 3.3 V LVCMOS output pin drives a load capacitance $C_L = 15\,\text{pF}$ at a switching frequency $f_{sw} = 100\,\text{MHz}$ with 50% activity factor. Calculate the dynamic power consumed by the pin.

**Step 1 — Recall the standard CMOS dynamic power equation.**
For a CMOS driver charging and discharging a load capacitor, the average power is:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{CCO}^2 \cdot f_{sw}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the switching activity factor (probability of a $0 \to 1$ transition per clock cycle).

**Step 2 — Identify the parameters from the problem.**
- $C_L = 15 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F}$
- $V_{CCO} = 3.3\,\text{V}$
- $f_{sw} = 100 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$
- $\alpha = 0.5$ (50% activity)

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.5 \cdot (15 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (3.3)^2 \cdot (100 \times 10^{6})
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate stepwise.**

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.5 \cdot 15 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 10.89 \cdot 10^{8}
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.5 \cdot 15 \cdot 10.89 \cdot 10^{-4}
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 81.675 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{W}
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**

$$
\boxed{P_{dyn} \approx 8.17\,\text{mW}}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering insight.**
With 200 such pins switching simultaneously, total I/O dynamic power ≈ $1.63\,\text{W}$, often exceeding the FPGA core power. This is why FPGA PCB designers must prioritize **I/O voltage reduction (1.8 V instead of 3.3 V)** wherever protocol permits.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Setup & Hold Time for the IOB DDR Input Register

**Problem statement:** A DDR SDRAM interface uses a $200\,\text{MHz}$ half-rate clock (i.e., $400\,\text{Mbps}$ data rate). The IOB input register introduces an internal clock-to-Q delay of $t_{CO} = 0.4\,\text{ns}$, and the PCB trace delay from the SDRAM to the FPGA pin is $t_{trace} = 0.6\,\text{ns}$. The IOB's intrinsic setup time is $t_{ISU,\min} = 0.3\,\text{ns}$. Determine whether the data arrives within the valid capture window.

**Step 1 — Compute the data period (UI = Unit Interval).**

$$
T_{UI} = \frac{1}{f_{data}} = \frac{1}{400 \times 10^{6}} = 2.5\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the available setup margin.**
The total propagation delay from the SDRAM latch to the FPGA's IOB capture flop must be less than one UI minus the flop's intrinsic setup requirement.

$$
t_{arrival} + t_{trace} + t_{CO} + t_{ISU} \le T_{UI}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute known values.**

$$
t_{arrival} + 0.6 + 0.4 + 0.3 \le 2.5
$$

$$
t_{arrival} + 1.3 \le 2.5
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for maximum allowed arrival time.**

$$
t_{arrival,\max} = 2.5 - 1.3 = 1.2\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**

$$
\boxed{t_{arrival,\max} = 1.2\,\text{ns}}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
The SDRAM must launch data such that it reaches the FPGA pin within **1.2 ns** of the source-synchronous clock edge. If the trace delay grows beyond this margin (e.g., due to a long PCB path), the user must enable the IOB's **IDELAY** (programmable input delay) to recenter the data inside the capture eye.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — IOB Configuration Bit Selection

| Configuration Bit | Possible Encodings | Effect on IOB Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `DIRECTION[1:0]` | 00 = INPUT, 01 = OUTPUT, 10 = BIDIR | Selects data path |
| `STD[3:0]` | 16 codes | Selects LVTTL, LVCMOS18, SSTL18, LVDS, … |
| `DRIVE[2:0]` | 000 = 2 mA, …, 111 = 24 mA | Output buffer sizing |
| `SLEW` | 0 = SLOW, 1 = FAST | Edge rate |
| `PULL[1:0]` | 00 = NONE, 01 = UP, 10 = DOWN, 11 = KEEPER | Default level |
| `HYSTERESIS` | 0 = OFF, 1 = ON | Schmitt trigger |
| `ODT[1:0]` | 00 = OFF, 01 = R_T 50 Ω, 10 = R_T 100 Ω, 11 = R_S 25 Ω | On-chip termination |
| `DDR_MODE` | 0 = SDR, 1 = DDR | Captures on both edges |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Programmable I/O Block Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below illustrates the internal architecture of a generic programmable I/O block. It shows how the package pin is multiplexed between the input path, output path, and tristate control, with all configuration bits sourced from the FPGA's SRAM configuration memory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PKG["PACKAGE PIN PAD"]
        P1[("PAD / BOND WIRE")]
    end

    subgraph ESD["ESD PROTECTION"]
        D1["CLAMP DIODES TO VCCO AND GND"]
    end

    subgraph INPATH["INPUT DATA PATH"]
        I1["DIFFERENTIAL OR SINGLE ENDED BUFFER"]
        I2["SCHMITT TRIGGER OPTIONAL"]
        I3["IDELAY PROGRAMMABLE DELAY"]
        I4["INPUT FLIP FLOP OR DDR REGISTER"]
        I5["PULL UP PULL DOWN KEEPER"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPATH["OUTPUT DATA PATH"]
        O1["OUTPUT FLIP FLOP OR DDR REGISTER"]
        O2["3 STATE OE REGISTER"]
        O3["SLEW RATE CONTROL"]
        O4["DRIVE STRENGTH SELECTOR"]
        O5["PUSH PULL OR OPEN DRAIN BUFFER"]
        O6["ON CHIP SERIES TERMINATION R_S"]
    end

    subgraph CFG["CONFIGURATION SRAM"]
        C1["DIRECTION BITS"]
        C2["I O STANDARD BITS"]
        C3["DRIVE STRENGTH BITS"]
        C4["PULL UP DOWN BITS"]
        C5["SLEW AND HYSTERESIS BITS"]
    end

    P1 --> ESD --> I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4
    P1 -. feedback .-> O6 --> O5 --> O4 --> O3 --> O2 --> O1
    O5 --> P1
    I5 -. bias .-> P1
    C1 -- "selects" --> I1
    C1 -- "selects" --> O1
    C2 -- "sets VREF and VCCO thresholds" --> I1
    C3 -- "configures" --> O4
    C4 -- "configures" --> I5
    C5 -- "configures" --> I2
    C5 -- "configures" --> O3
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — I/O Banking Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern FPGAs partition their I/O pins into **voltage banks** (typically 4 to 8 banks). All pins within one bank must share the **same $V_{CCO}$** and the **same $V_{REF}$**. This architecture allows a single chip to simultaneously talk to 3.3 V memory, 2.5 V PCI bus, and 1.8 V LVCMOS devices — as long as they are placed in different banks.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DEV["FPGA DIE"]
        subgraph BANK0["BANK 0 — VCCO EQUAL 3.3 V"]
            B0P1["PIN 1"]
            B0P2["PIN 2"]
            B0P3["PIN 3"]
            B0P4["PIN 4"]
        end
        subgraph BANK1["BANK 1 — VCCO EQUAL 2.5 V"]
            B1P1["PIN 5"]
            B1P2["PIN 6"]
            B1P3["PIN 7"]
        end
        subgraph BANK2["BANK 2 — VCCO EQUAL 1.8 V"]
            B2P1["PIN 8"]
            B2P2["PIN 9"]
            B2P3["PIN 10"]
            B2P4["PIN 11"]
        end
        subgraph BANK3["BANK 3 — HIGH SPEED DIFFERENTIAL"]
            B3P1["DIFF PAIR P"]
            B3P2["DIFF PAIR N"]
        end
        subgraph CORE["FPGA CORE LOGIC 1.0 V"]
            CORE1["CLB DSP BRAM CLOCK TILES"]
        end
    end

    BANK0 <-- "level shift 1.0 V to 3.3 V" --> CORE
    BANK1 <-- "level shift 1.0 V to 2.5 V" --> CORE
    BANK2 <-- "level shift 1.0 V to 1.8 V" --> CORE
    BANK3 <-- "LVDS 350 mV diff signaling" --> CORE
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — DDR Signal Path Through IOB

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXT["EXTERNAL SOURCE 400 MBPS DDR DATA"] --> PCB["PCB TRACE"]
    PCB --> PAD["IOB PAD"]
    PAD --> BUF["INPUT BUFFER WITH HYSTERESIS"]
    BUF --> IDD["IDELAY TAPS"]
    IDD --> FF1["FF CAPTURE ON CLK RISING EDGE"]
    IDD --> FF2["FF CAPTURE ON CLK FALLING EDGE"]
    FF1 --> MERGE["EDGE MERGE TO 200 MHZ PARALLEL BUS"]
    FF2 --> MERGE
    MERGE --> CORE["FPGA CORE LOGIC"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions are modelled on actual KTU university examination papers. They follow the **revised Bloom's Taxonomy** cognitive levels (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze) and the official 2024 Scheme marks distribution: **Part A = 3 marks each**, **Part B = 14 marks with internal choice**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a Programmable I/O Block (IOB) in an FPGA. List any four configurable features supported by a typical IOB.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A Programmable I/O Block (IOB) is a configurable interface circuit located between the internal logic array and the package pin of an FPGA. It enables the user to dynamically select the signal direction, electrical standard, drive strength, and termination scheme through SRAM-based configuration cells.

**Four configurable features:**
1. **Signal direction** — Input / Output / Bidirectional (tristate).
2. **I/O standard** — LVTTL, LVCMOS, SSTL, HSTL, LVDS, etc.
3. **Drive strength** — Programmable from 2 mA to 24 mA.
4. **On-chip pull-up / pull-down / bus-keeper resistors.**

*(Also acceptable: slew-rate control, Schmitt trigger, DDR register, on-chip series/parallel termination.)*

---

### **Q2. What is the function of the Schmitt trigger option in an IOB? Why is it preferred over a normal CMOS buffer for slow-rising input signals?** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A Schmitt trigger is a comparator with **two distinct threshold voltages** — an upper threshold $V_{T+}$ and a lower threshold $V_{T-}$ — producing a hysteresis band of $\Delta V_H = V_{T+} - V_{T-}$.

**Function:** It converts a slow, noisy, or sinusoidal input waveform into a clean, sharp CMOS-level digital signal by switching the output state only when the input crosses one of the two thresholds.

**Why preferred over a normal CMOS buffer:**
- A normal CMOS buffer has a single switching threshold (≈ $V_{CCO}/2$). When the input rises slowly or contains noise, the input can hover near the threshold for many nanoseconds, causing **multiple unwanted output transitions**, **oscillation**, or **metastability** that corrupts downstream flip-flops.
- The Schmitt trigger's hysteresis band prevents these false transitions because once the input crosses $V_{T+}$, the output commits HIGH and will not switch back until the input falls below $V_{T-}$. This guarantees **one clean transition per intended edge**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **🔹 QUESTION A — Full 14-Mark Question**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### **Q(A)(a) — 7 Marks**
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Programmable I/O Block (IOB) in an FPGA. Clearly show the input path, output path, tristate control, and configuration bit inputs.**

#### **Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**(i) Introduction (1 mark):**
The IOB is a perimeter cell that interfaces the FPGA's internal logic (operating at $V_{CCINT}$, e.g., 1.0 V) with the external package pin (which may operate at $V_{CCO}$, e.g., 3.3 V). Each physical pin is bonded to exactly one IOB.

**(ii) Input Path (2 marks):**
The input path carries data from the pad to the core. It consists of:
- ESD clamp diodes (for electrostatic discharge protection),
- A differential / single-ended input buffer (selected by the I/O standard bit),
- An optional Schmitt trigger (for noisy signals),
- An optional IDELAY (programmable delay for source-synchronous alignment),
- An optional input flip-flop / DDR register (for synchronous capture),
- A pull-up / pull-down / keeper resistor (to define a default level).

**(iii) Output Path (2 marks):**
The output path carries data from the core to the pad. It consists of:
- An output flip-flop / DDR register (synchronous launch),
- A tristate control flip-flop (OE),
- A slew-rate control block (SLOW / FAST),
- A drive-strength selector (2 mA – 24 mA),
- A push-pull or open-drain output buffer,
- An optional on-chip series termination ($R_S$).

**(iv) Configuration Bits & Tristate (1 mark):**
All blocks are governed by SRAM configuration cells loaded at power-up. The tristate control (OE) signal determines whether the output buffer drives the pad (active) or releases it to high-Z (input mode), enabling bidirectional bus operation.

**(v) Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram shown in SECTION 4.1.

---

#### **Q(A)(b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the concept of I/O banking in FPGAs. Why is it necessary? What are the design rules for assigning signals to banks?**

#### **Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**(i) Definition (2 marks):**
Modern FPGAs partition their peripheral pins into 4–8 voltage **banks**. Each bank has dedicated supply pins for $V_{CCO}$ (output driver supply) and $V_{REF}$ (input reference voltage). All I/Os within a single bank share the same $V_{CCO}$ and $V_{REF}$ rails.

**(ii) Necessity (2 marks):**
- Modern PCBs use **mixed-voltage devices** (e.g., 3.3 V DDR3, 2.5 V PCI, 1.8 V LVCMOS, 1.2 V RGMII).
- Without banking, the FPGA could talk to only one voltage level.
- Banking allows the FPGA to interface with multiple voltage domains simultaneously by isolating each group's supply rail.

**(iii) Design Rules (2 marks):**
1. **Rule 1 — Single $V_{CCO}$ per bank:** All output buffers in a bank share the same supply. Pins assigned to a bank must use compatible standards (e.g., LVCMOS33 and LVTTL may coexist, but LVCMOS18 cannot).
2. **Rule 2 — Single $V_{REF}$ per bank (if used):** Referenced inputs (SSTL/HSTL) must all share the same $V_{REF}$. Unreferenced inputs (LVCMOS) coexist regardless of $V_{REF}$.
3. **Rule 3 — Differential pairing:** A differential pair (P and N) must be placed in the **same bank** with matched routing length.
4. **Rule 4 — Simultaneous Switching Outputs (SSO):** Banks limit the number of simultaneously switching outputs to control ground bounce.

**(iv) Conclusion (1 mark):**
Banking is the architectural enabler of **multi-standard, multi-voltage FPGA interfacing** and is a critical consideration during PCB schematic capture and pin assignment.

---

### **🔹 QUESTION B — Alternative 14-Mark Question**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

#### **Q(B)(a) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the various I/O signaling standards supported by FPGA IOBs. Compare LVTTL, LVCMOS, SSTL, and LVDS with respect to voltage levels, noise margin, and typical applications.**

#### **Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**(i) Introduction (1 mark):**
FPGA IOBs support dozens of I/O standards grouped into single-ended (LVTTL, LVCMOS, SSTL, HSTL, PCI) and differential (LVDS, LVPECL, CML) families. Each standard defines a set of DC and AC thresholds for interoperability.

**(ii) Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Standard | Type | $V_{CCO}$ | $V_{IH,min}$ | $V_{IL,max}$ | $V_{OH,min}$ | $V_{OL,max}$ | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LVTTL | Single-ended | 3.3 V | 2.0 V | 0.8 V | 2.4 V | 0.4 V | Legacy 5 V-tolerant glue logic, asynchronous memory |
| LVCMOS33 | Single-ended | 3.3 V | 2.0 V | 0.8 V | $V_{CCO}-0.4$ | 0.4 V | General purpose 3.3 V peripherals |
| LVCMOS18 | Single-ended | 1.8 V | 0.65 $V_{CCO}$ | 0.35 $V_{CCO}$ | $V_{CCO}-0.45$ | 0.45 V | Low-power mobile SoCs, RGMII |
| SSTL18 | Single-ended referenced | 1.8 V | $V_{REF}+0.125$ | $V_{REF}-0.125$ | $V_{REF}+0.4$ | $V_{REF}-0.4$ | DDR2 / DDR3 memory interfaces |
| LVDS | Differential | 2.5 V | — | — | 350 mV diff | — | High-speed SerDes, flat-panel displays, FPGA-to-FPGA links |

**(iii) Discussion (1 mark):**
- **LVTTL/LVCMOS:** Simple, single-ended, low pin count; suitable for low-to-medium speeds (< 200 MHz).
- **SSTL:** Uses a $V_{REF}$ midpoint reference and terminated drivers; essential for **stub-series-terminated** memory buses (DDR2/3/4).
- **LVDS:** Differential swing of only 350 mV around a 1.2 V common-mode offers **superior noise immunity**, low EMI, and gigabit speeds with low power.

---

#### **Q(B)(b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the role of slew-rate control and drive-strength selection in an IOB. A 3.3 V LVCMOS output drives a 25 Ω transmission line terminated at the far end. If the driver's intrinsic impedance is 5 Ω, what value of on-chip series termination $R_S$ should be enabled to match the line?**

#### **Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**(i) Slew-Rate Control (2 marks):**
Slew-rate control programs the rise/fall time of the output edge by adjusting the gate capacitance of the final driver stage.
- **FAST mode:** Edge time ≈ 1 ns → higher bandwidth, higher ground-bounce, higher EMI.
- **SLOW mode:** Edge time ≈ 3–5 ns → lower EMI, lower SSO noise, suitable for low-frequency buses.

**(ii) Drive-Strength Selection (2 marks):**
Drive strength is the maximum DC current the output buffer can source ($I_{OH}$) or sink ($I_{OL}$) while still maintaining a valid $V_{OH}/V_{OL}$. Higher drive strength is needed for heavily loaded buses or fast edges, but increases SSO-induced ground bounce.

**(iii) Series Termination Calculation (3 marks):**

**Given:**
- Transmission line impedance: $Z_0 = 25\,\Omega$
- Driver intrinsic output impedance: $Z_{drv} = 5\,\Omega$

**Formula for matched series termination:**

$$
R_S + Z_{drv} = Z_0
$$

**Substitute:**

$$
R_S + 5 = 25
$$

**Solve:**

$$
\boxed{R_S = 20\,\Omega}
$$

**Engineering note:** When $R_S = 20\,\Omega$ is enabled in the IOB configuration, the total series impedance seen by the wavefront leaving the driver equals the line's characteristic impedance. The far-end termination absorbs the wave without reflection, producing a clean, overshoot-free edge — a technique called **source-series termination (SST)**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls where KTU students routinely lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $V_{CCINT}$ with $V_{CCO}$.** $V_{CCINT}$ powers the FPGA core (1.0 V); $V_{CCO}$ powers the I/O buffers (1.2 – 3.3 V). Examiners deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting Schmitt trigger hysteresis.** When asked to "explain the Schmitt trigger," students often only write $V_{T+}$ and $V_{T-}$ without mentioning the **hysteresis band** $\Delta V_H = V_{T+} - V_{T-}$. This is the key differentiator.
> 3. **Mixing up pull-up with bus-keeper.** A bus-keeper is *not* a pull-up; it tracks the last driven value. Many students write "pull-up" for any default-level resistor.
> 4. **Skipping the DDR register explanation.** When asked about "configurable features," students often forget to mention the **DDR input/output register**, which is a high-value advanced feature.
> 5. **Forgetting that all pins in one bank share $V_{CCO}$.** When explaining banking, students often forget the design rule that **one bank = one $V_{CCO}$**.
> 6. **Inverting drive strength interpretation.** Some students confuse drive strength with internal resistance; higher drive current means **lower effective $R_{on}$, not higher**.
> 7. **No diagram in 7-mark questions.** A neat block diagram carries **1–2 reserved marks** in any 7-mark sub-question. Always include one.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorize before exam):**
>
> - [ ] **IOB = Universal Adaptor** between FPGA core ($V_{CCINT}$) and external pin ($V_{CCO}$).
> - [ ] Three data paths: **Input, Output, Tristate (bidirectional)**.
> - [ ] **ESD diodes** are the first protection stage; clamp to $V_{CCO}$ and GND.
> - [ ] **Schmitt trigger** uses two thresholds; hysteresis $\Delta V_H = V_{T+} - V_{T-}$.
> - [ ] **Pull-up / Pull-down / Keeper** define a default logic level.
> - [ ] **Drive strength** = programmable from 2 mA to 24 mA.
> - [ ] **Slew rate** = SLOW (low EMI) vs. FAST (high speed).
> - [ ] **DDR register** captures data on **both** clock edges → doubles throughput.
> - [ ] **I/O standards**: LVTTL, LVCMOS (3.3/2.5/1.8/1.5/1.2 V), SSTL (DDR), HSTL, LVDS (differential).
> - [ ] **On-chip termination**: $R_S$ (series, $Z_0 - Z_{drv}$), $R_T$ (parallel, 50 Ω or 100 Ω).
> - [ ] **I/O banking**: 4–8 banks per FPGA; each bank has its own $V_{CCO}$ and $V_{REF}$.
> - [ ] **Single $V_{CCO}$ per bank** — non-negotiable design rule.
> - [ ] **Differential pairs (LVDS)** must be in the **same bank** with matched length.
> - [ ] **Dynamic power per pin**: $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{CCO}^2 \cdot f_{sw}$.
> - [ ] **Series termination formula**: $R_S = Z_0 - Z_{drv}$.
> - [ ] **Voltage divider formula**: $V_{PAD} = V_{CCO} \cdot R_{PD}/(R_{PU} + R_{PD})$.
> - [ ] **Noise margin**: $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$; $\;NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$.
> - [ ] **Differential swing (LVDS)**: 350 mV around $V_{CM} = 1.2\,\text{V}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FPGA Design Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** FPGA Design Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **FPGA (Field Programmable Gate Array) Design Flow** is the systematic, structured sequence of engineering steps followed to transform a digital hardware concept into a physical, working configuration that is downloaded onto a programmable logic device. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the FPGA design flow constitutes the complete *front-end RTL (Register Transfer Level) to back-end bitstream* pipeline that converts a Hardware Description Language (HDL) specification into a fuse-map configuration file (`.bit` or `.sof`) capable of programming the FPGA's internal logic fabric.

The flow is **iterative** (convergence-based) and consists of eight primary stages:

1. **Design Specification**
2. **Design Entry (HDL Coding)**
3. **Functional / Behavioral Simulation**
4. **Synthesis (Logic Synthesis + Technology Mapping)**
5. **Place and Route (Implementation)**
6. **Timing Analysis (Static Timing Analysis - STA)**
7. **Bitstream Generation**
8. **Device Programming & On-Board Verification**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The FPGA design flow is *vendor-neutral at the conceptual level* but tool-specific at the implementation level. Xilinx Vivado, Intel (Altera) Quartus, and Lattice Diamond are the three industry-standard EDA (Electronic Design Automation) toolchains that KTU examiners expect you to reference in viva-voce and written answers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprint-to-Building" Metaphor

Imagine you are an **architect designing a house**:

| FPGA Design Stage | House-Building Analogy | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Design Specification | Client's brief ("3BHK, 2 floors") | Define *what* the chip must do |
| Design Entry | Drawing architectural blueprints (VHDL/Verilog) | Translate idea into formal language |
| Functional Simulation | Paper-review of blueprints | Check logical correctness *before construction* |
| Synthesis | Converting blueprints into engineered drawings (LUTs, FFs) | Map abstract logic to hardware primitives |
| Place and Route | Laying bricks on the land, wiring electricity | Physically assign hardware locations & routes |
| Timing Analysis | Verifying doors open before closing | Ensure *speed* and *synchronization* |
| Bitstream Generation | Generating the final house key (1s and 0s) | Create configuration data |
| Device Programming | Handing the key to the homeowner | Upload configuration to actual FPGA |

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following quantitative metrics are universally assessed in FPGA design and frequently appear in KTU 2024 numerical problems:

- **LUT (Look-Up Table):** Fundamental combinational logic unit, typically **6-input** in modern FPGAs (e.g., Xilinx 7-series, UltraScale).
- **Flip-Flop (FF):** 1-bit storage element, frequency capability up to **$\mathbf{f_{max}}$** in MHz.
- **BRAM (Block RAM):** On-chip memory, typically **18 Kb** or **36 Kb** per block.
- **DSP Slice:** Hardware multiplier/accumulator block (e.g., $25 \times 18$ multiplier).
- **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$):** Measured in **nanoseconds (ns)**.
- **Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** Minimum time data must be stable *before* clock edge, in **ns**.
- **Hold Time ($t_{h}$):** Minimum time data must remain stable *after* clock edge, in **ns**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** The FPGA design flow is fundamentally a *trade-off optimization problem*. The EDA tool continuously balances three competing constraints: **Area (LUTs used), Timing (clock frequency), and Power (dynamic + static)**. This is often called the **ATP Triangle** in advanced VLSI literature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Theoretical Breakdown

#### **Stage 1: Design Specification**
- Captures the **functional requirements**, I/O protocols, operating frequency, and resource budgets.
- Outputs a *design document* and a *verification plan*.
- This is a *non-synthesizable* human activity — no HDL code yet.

#### **Stage 2: Design Entry (HDL Coding)**
- Two industry-standard HDLs: **VHDL** (VHSIC HDL, IEEE 1076) and **Verilog HDL** (IEEE 1364).
- Modern designs increasingly use **SystemVerilog** (IEEE 1800) and **HLS (High-Level Synthesis)** tools that accept C/C++/MATLAB.
- **Design Hierarchy:** Top-down methodology — *chip → sub-systems → modules → gates*.

#### **Stage 3: Functional / Behavioral Simulation**
- Performed **before synthesis** using a *testbench*.
- The design is simulated at the *RTL level* — no timing information exists yet.
- Tools: ModelSim, Vivado Simulator, QuestaSim.
- **Goal:** Verify logical correctness independent of physical implementation.

#### **Stage 4: Synthesis (Logic Synthesis + Technology Mapping)**
- The synthesis engine (e.g., Vivado synth_design, Quartus Compiler) converts HDL into a **gate-level netlist**.
- Steps inside the synthesizer:
  1. **Parsing & Elaboration** — HDL → internal representation (typically BLIF/EDIF/Generic Netlist).
  2. **Constraint Driving** — Reads timing constraints from an SDC/XDC file.
  3. **Logic Optimization** — Boolean minimization (Quine-McCluskey, Espresso).
  4. **Technology Mapping** — Maps generic gates to FPGA primitives (LUTs, FFs, carry chains, DSPs).
  5. **Resource Utilization Report** — Estimates LUT, FF, BRAM, DSP usage.

#### **Stage 5: Place and Route (Implementation)**
- **Placement:** Tool assigns each logic element to a *physical site* on the FPGA fabric.
- **Routing:** Tool assigns interconnect wires to *physical routing resources* (programmable switch matrices, metal layers).
- Outputs a placed-and-routed netlist (`.ncd`, `.vmf`, or proprietary formats).

#### **Stage 6: Timing Analysis (STA — Static Timing Analysis)**
- Verifies the design meets all timing constraints *without dynamic simulation*.
- Checks **setup time**, **hold time**, **clock-to-Q delay**, and **net delays**.
- Generates a timing report with **slack** values.

#### **Stage 7: Bitstream Generation**
- Converts the placed-and-routed design into a binary configuration file.
- For Xilinx: `.bit` (volatile SRAM) or `.mcs` (flash programming).
- For Intel/Altera: `.sof` (SRAM Object File) or `.pof` (Programmer Object File).

#### **Stage 8: Device Programming & Verification**
- Bitstream downloaded to FPGA via **JTAG**, **USB**, or **serial configuration** interface.
- On-board testing confirms real hardware behavior.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum Clock Frequency | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk,min}}$ | MHz | Defines fastest operating speed |
| Minimum Clock Period | $T_{clk,min} = t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{su} - t_{skew}$ | ns | Critical-path constraint |
| Hold Slack | $t_{hold,slack} = t_{cq} + t_{comb} - t_{h}$ | ns | Must be $\geq 0$ |
| Setup Slack | $t_{setup,slack} = T_{clk} - (t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{su})$ | ns | Must be $\geq 0$ |
| Logic Delay | $t_{pd} = N \cdot t_{LUT} + K \cdot t_{net}$ | ns | Path delay through LUTs + wires |
| Power Dissipation | $P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static}$ | mW / W | Junction temperature budget |
| Dynamic Power | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | W | Switching activity dependent |
| Static Power | $P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | W | Leakage current dependent |
| LUT Capacity | $N_{LUTs} = \dfrac{N_{inputs}}{6}$ (approx) | count | Resource estimation |
| FPGA Throughput | $Throughput = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}} \cdot N_{bits}$ | bits/s | Data processing rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Tables:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` has been used inside formulas (e.g., for absolute value) instead of the markdown-breaking `\vert` pipe to preserve table rendering integrity.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The FPGA design flow is the *backbone of all modern digital prototyping*. Real-world applications include:

- **5G Baseband Processing** — High-throughput signal processing on Xilinx RFSoC.
- **Automotive ADAS** — Sensor fusion in Intel Cyclone V FPGAs.
- **Aerospace & Defense** — Radiation-hardened FPGAs (Microsemi/PolarFire).
- **AI/ML Inference** — Edge AI on Xilinx Versal AI Edge.
- **ASIC Prototyping** — Pre-silicon validation of ASIC RTL on large FPGAs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Maximum Achievable Clock Frequency

The **maximum operating frequency** is the single most important design metric in synchronous FPGA systems. It is bounded by the **critical path delay** of the combinational logic between two consecutive flip-flops.

#### **Step 1: Identify the Critical Path**

The critical path is the longest combinational delay between any two clocked elements. It is composed of three sequential delay components:

$$
t_{path} = t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{net}
$$

Where:
- $t_{cq}$ = Clock-to-Q delay (time for FF output to update after clock edge)
- $t_{comb}$ = Combinational logic delay (through LUTs, muxes, carry chains)
- $t_{net}$ = Routing/net delay (wire propagation)

#### **Step 2: Derive the Setup Time Constraint**

For the data to be reliably captured at the next clock edge, the following inequality must hold:

$$
t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{net} + t_{su} \leq T_{clk}
$$

Rearranging for the clock period:

$$
T_{clk} \geq t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{net} + t_{su}
$$

#### **Step 3: Account for Clock Skew**

In real FPGAs, the clock signal arrives at different flip-flops at slightly different times due to buffer tree insertion. Clock skew $t_{skew}$ modifies the constraint:

$$
T_{clk} \geq t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{net} + t_{su} - t_{skew}
$$

#### **Step 4: Solve for the Maximum Frequency**

The minimum allowable clock period is therefore:

$$
T_{clk,min} = t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{net} + t_{su} - t_{skew}
$$

Taking the reciprocal gives the maximum frequency:

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{net} + t_{su} - t_{skew}}
$$

#### **Step 5: Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)**

**Given:**
- $t_{cq} = 0.5 \text{ ns}$
- $t_{comb} = 3.2 \text{ ns}$ (through a 3-LUT chain)
- $t_{net} = 1.8 \text{ ns}$
- $t_{su} = 0.4 \text{ ns}$
- $t_{skew} = 0.1 \text{ ns}$

**Solution:**

$$
T_{clk,min} = 0.5 + 3.2 + 1.8 + 0.4 - 0.1
$$

$$
T_{clk,min} = 5.8 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{5.8 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 172.41 \text{ MHz}
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Hold Time Constraint

The hold time violation occurs when data changes *too quickly* after a clock edge, before the FF has finished capturing it. The constraint is:

$$
t_{cq} + t_{comb,min} + t_{net,min} \geq t_{h}
$$

The hold slack is therefore:

$$
t_{hold,slack} = t_{cq} + t_{comb,min} + t_{net,min} - t_{h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Hold time is *clock-period-independent*. It cannot be fixed by reducing the clock frequency — only by adding buffers (delay insertion) in the data path.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stage Output Artifacts

| Stage | Input Artifact | Output Artifact | Tool Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specification | Requirements | Design document | Manual / Word |
| Design Entry | Design doc | `.vhd` / `.v` file | Text editor / Vivado |
| Functional Simulation | HDL + Testbench | Waveform (`.vcd`) | ModelSim / Vivado Sim |
| Synthesis | HDL + Constraints | Gate-level netlist | Vivado synth / Quartus |
| Implementation | Netlist + Constraints | Placed-and-routed design | Vivado impl / Quartus fitter |
| Timing Analysis | Placed design | Timing report (`.twr`) | Vivado report_timing |
| Bitstream Generation | Routed design | `.bit` / `.sof` file | Vivado write_bitstream |
| Device Programming | Bitstream | Configured FPGA | iMPACT / Quartus Programmer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete FPGA Design Flow Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **complete FPGA design pipeline** with iterative feedback loops for design correction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1 Design Specification"] --> B["Step 2 HDL Design Entry VHDL or Verilog"]
    B --> C{"Step 3 Functional Simulation Pass"}
    C -- No --> B
    C -- Yes --> D["Step 4 Synthesis Logic Optimization"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Translation and Mapping to FPGA Primitives"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Place and Route Implementation"]
    F --> G{"Step 7 Timing Analysis STA Pass"}
    G -- No --> H["Modify Constraints or RTL"]
    H --> B
    G -- Yes --> I["Step 8 Bitstream Generation .bit file"]
    I --> J["Step 9 Device Programming via JTAG or USB"]
    J --> K["Step 10 On Board Verification"]
    K --> L{"Design Meets Specs"}
    L -- No --> B
    L -- Yes --> M["Final Production FPGA"]
```

### 4.2 FPGA Internal Architecture Reference Diagram

The following block diagram illustrates **how the synthesized design maps onto the physical FPGA fabric**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FPGA["FPGA Device Fabric"]
        CLB["Configurable Logic Block CLB Containing LUTs and Flip Flops"]
        IO["IO Blocks Programmable I O Standards LVCMOS HSTL LVDS"]
        BRAM["Block RAM BRAM 18Kb or 36Kb"]
        DSP["DSP Slices Hardware Multipliers"]
        CLK["Clock Management Tiles MMCM and PLL"]
        ROUTE["Routing Matrix Programmable Switches"]
    end

    HDL["RTL Design from Synthesis"] --> CLB
    HDL --> IO
    HDL --> BRAM
    HDL --> DSP
    HDL --> CLK
    CLB <--> ROUTE
    IO <--> ROUTE
    BRAM <--> ROUTE
    DSP <--> ROUTE
    CLK <--> ROUTE
```

### 4.3 Synthesis Sub-Stage Detailed Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["HDL Source .vhd or .v"] --> A2["Parsing and Elaboration"]
    A2 --> A3["Constraint Application SDC or XDC File"]
    A3 --> A4["RTL Optimization Boolean Minimization"]
    A4 --> A5["Technology Mapping to LUTs FFs DSPs"]
    A5 --> A6["Netlist Generation"]
    A6 --> A7["Resource Utilization Report"]
    A7 --> A8["Synthesis Report Timing and Area Estimates"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Schematics:** Physical FPGA floorplans contain hundreds of thousands of routing elements and are not practical to render in Mermaid. The block-level architecture above is the **canonical representation** used in KTU textbooks and exam answer sheets.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "FPGA Design Flow" and list any four major stages involved in it.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **FPGA Design Flow** is the structured, iterative sequence of engineering steps used to transform a digital hardware specification (in HDL) into a working bitstream that configures a Field Programmable Gate Array.

The four major stages are:

1. **Design Entry** — Writing HDL code (VHDL/Verilog).
2. **Functional Simulation** — Verifying logic correctness using testbenches.
3. **Synthesis and Implementation** — Converting HDL to gate-level netlist and placing/routing onto FPGA primitives.
4. **Bitstream Generation and Device Programming** — Creating and downloading the configuration file to the FPGA.

*(Award 1 mark for definition + 0.5 marks each for listing the four stages.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]**
*What is the difference between "Functional Simulation" and "Timing Simulation" in the FPGA design flow?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Functional Simulation | Timing Simulation |
|---|---|---|
| **When Performed** | After HDL coding, before synthesis | After Place and Route |
| **Timing Information** | No gate or wire delays | Includes actual gate and routing delays |
| **Purpose** | Verify logical correctness | Verify timing constraints (setup/hold) |
| **Speed** | Fast | Slower (more detailed) |

*(Award 1.5 marks for each of the two definitions with a supporting difference.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the complete **FPGA design flow** with the help of a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each stage in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A digital circuit implemented on an FPGA has the following parameters:
- Clock-to-Q delay $t_{cq} = 0.6$ ns
- Combinational logic delay $t_{comb} = 4.5$ ns
- Net delay $t_{net} = 1.2$ ns
- Setup time $t_{su} = 0.3$ ns
- Hold time $t_{h} = 0.2$ ns
- Clock skew $t_{skew} = 0.1$ ns

Calculate the **maximum operating frequency** and check whether the design satisfies the **hold time constraint**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The FPGA design flow consists of the following stages (block diagram drawn for 2 marks; detailed explanation for 5 marks):

**1. Design Specification (0.5 marks):**
Captures functional requirements, I/O protocols, and performance targets (frequency, power, area).

**2. Design Entry / HDL Coding (1 mark):**
RTL is written in VHDL/Verilog describing circuit behavior at the register-transfer level.

**3. Functional Simulation (0.5 marks):**
The HDL is simulated with a testbench to verify logical correctness. No timing is involved.

**4. Synthesis (1 mark):**
The HDL is converted into a gate-level netlist using FPGA primitives (LUTs, FFs, DSPs, BRAM). Optimization is performed for area, speed, and power.

**5. Place and Route / Implementation (1 mark):**
- **Placement:** Logic elements are assigned to physical sites.
- **Routing:** Interconnect wires are assigned to programmable routing resources.

**6. Timing Analysis (0.5 marks):**
Static Timing Analysis verifies setup and hold constraints across all paths.

**7. Bitstream Generation (0.5 marks):**
The routed design is converted into a binary file (`.bit` or `.sof`).

**8. Device Programming (0.5 marks):**
The bitstream is downloaded via JTAG/USB to the FPGA, configuring its SRAM cells.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute Minimum Clock Period** *(2 marks)*

The minimum clock period is:

$$
T_{clk,min} = t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{net} + t_{su} - t_{skew}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
T_{clk,min} = 0.6 + 4.5 + 1.2 + 0.3 - 0.1
$$

$$
T_{clk,min} = 6.5 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Maximum Frequency** *(1 mark)*

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \dfrac{1}{6.5 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
f_{max} \approx 153.85 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 3: Verify Hold Time Constraint** *(2 marks)*

Hold slack formula:

$$
t_{hold,slack} = t_{cq,min} + t_{comb,min} + t_{net,min} - t_{h}
$$

Assuming minimum values equal nominal values for simplicity (common KTU assumption):

$$
t_{hold,slack} = 0.6 + 4.5 + 1.2 - 0.2
$$

$$
t_{hold,slack} = 6.1 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion** *(2 marks)*

- Since $t_{hold,slack} = 6.1 \text{ ns} \geq 0$, the **hold constraint is satisfied** with a positive slack.
- The design can operate reliably up to **153.85 MHz**.
- The maximum operating frequency is determined by the *setup time constraint*, not the hold time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often use $t_{cq} + t_{comb}$ without adding $t_{net}$ — this loses 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to subtract $t_{skew}$ in setup-time calculations — another 1 mark lost.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing the hold slack formula with a *plus sign* on $t_h$ instead of minus — formula inversion error.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not stating units (ns, MHz) explicitly in the final answer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **synthesis stage** of FPGA design flow. Discuss the difference between **logic synthesis** and **technology mapping**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Static Timing Analysis (STA)**? Explain the difference between **setup time** and **hold time** violations. How are these violations corrected in an FPGA design? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Flowchart of Synthesis Stage** *(3 marks for diagram):*

```
HDL Code → Parsing & Elaboration → Constraint Reading (XDC/SDC)
→ RTL Optimization → Technology Mapping → Netlist Generation
→ Synthesis Report (Area + Timing Estimates)
```

**Logic Synthesis vs Technology Mapping** *(4 marks)*

| Aspect | Logic Synthesis | Technology Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Converts HDL generic logic into optimized Boolean equations | Maps Boolean equations onto FPGA-specific primitives (LUTs, FFs, DSPs, BRAM) |
| **Input** | HDL + Generic library | Optimized logic + FPGA vendor library |
| **Output** | Technology-independent netlist | Technology-dependent (FPGA-specific) netlist |
| **Optimization Goal** | Minimize logic depth, remove redundancy | Optimize for FPGA architecture (e.g., pack into 6-input LUTs) |
| **Tools Used** | Synopsys Design Compiler, Yosys | Vivado synth_design, Quartus Compiler |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Static Timing Analysis (STA) — 2 marks:**

STA is a method of verifying the timing performance of a digital circuit *without* performing dynamic simulation. It analyzes all possible paths in the design and checks whether timing constraints are met.

**Setup Time Violation — 2 marks:**

- **Cause:** Data arrives at the FF input *too late* — after the setup time window has closed.
- **Effect:** FF may capture incorrect (metastable) data.
- **Fix:** Reduce combinational delay, increase clock period, pipeline the design, or enable higher optimization effort in synthesis.

**Hold Time Violation — 2 marks:**

- **Cause:** Data at FF input changes *too quickly* after the clock edge — before the FF has latched it.
- **Effect:** FF may capture data from the *current* cycle instead of the *previous* cycle.
- **Fix:** Add delay buffers in the data path (hold-time fix insertion is automatic in modern FPGA tools).

**Tabular Comparison — 1 mark:**

| Aspect | Setup Violation | Hold Violation |
|---|---|---|
| Data is | Too slow | Too fast |
| Fix | Pipeline / increase period | Add buffers |
| Period-dependent | Yes | No |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **The FPGA design flow is iterative**, not linear — feedback loops exist between every stage.
- ✅ **Functional simulation has no timing** — it only checks logical correctness.
- ✅ **Synthesis = Logic Synthesis + Technology Mapping** — never use these terms interchangeably.
- ✅ **STA (Static Timing Analysis)** is performed *after* Place and Route, using *real* routing delays.
