- ✅ **Maximum frequency $f_{max}$** is determined by the *longest combinational path* (critical path).
- ✅ **Setup slack** must be $\geq 0$; **Hold slack** must be $\geq 0$.
- ✅ **Hold time violations are independent of clock period** — they cannot be fixed by reducing frequency.
- ✅ **Bitstream files** for Xilinx: `.bit` (volatile) / `.mcs` (non-volatile flash); for Intel: `.sof` / `.pof`.
- ✅ **Configuration interfaces:** JTAG (IEEE 1149.1), USB, Serial (SPI/QSPI), Parallel (SelectMAP).
- ✅ **Resource estimation** in synthesis reports gives *LUT*, *FF*, *DSP*, *BRAM*, and *IO* usage.
- ✅ **Vendor tools:** Vivado (Xilinx), Quartus (Intel/Altera), Diamond (Lattice), Yosys (Open-Source).
- ✅ **KTU Viva Tip:** Always mention the specific tool and FPGA family you have used in labs (e.g., *Xilinx Spartan-7 on Vivado 2023.2*).
- ✅ **HDL Languages:** VHDL (IEEE 1076) and Verilog (IEEE 1364); SystemVerilog (IEEE 1800) is the modern superset.
- ✅ **Constraints File:** XDC for Xilinx, SDC for Intel — drives synthesis and implementation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Note:** Always draw a *neat labeled block diagram* of the design flow whenever the question contains the word "explain with diagram." Examiners award **2 marks minimum** for a clean diagram in Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SoC Design on FPGA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SoC Design on FPGA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **System-on-Chip (SoC)** is a single integrated circuit (IC) that integrates **all functional components of a complete electronic system** — including a processor core, memory blocks, peripheral interfaces, interconnect fabric, and custom hardware accelerators — onto a **single semiconductor die**, typically fabricated using deep-submicron CMOS technology (e.g., **28 nm, 14 nm, 7 nm** process nodes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SoC Design on FPGA** refers to the methodology of **prototyping, validating, and implementing a complete SoC architecture on a programmable FPGA fabric**. Modern FPGAs (such as the **Xilinx Zynq-7000 / Zynq UltraScale+** and **Intel (Altera) Cyclone V SoC / Stratix 10 SoC**) integrate **hard-core ARM Cortex processors** alongside the programmable logic (PL), enabling true hardware-software co-design on a single chip.

The SoC paradigm differs from traditional board-level system design primarily through the metric of **integration density**: where a board-level system requires multiple ICs connected through PCB traces, an SoC achieves the same functionality within a single die, dramatically reducing **latency, power, and form factor**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Modern Smart Kitchen 🍳:**
>
> Imagine you want to bake a cake. A **traditional board-level system** is like having the oven, mixer, refrigerator, and timer as **separate appliances in different rooms** — you have to walk between them, wasting time and energy.
>
> An **SoC on FPGA** is like a **Swiss Army Kitchen** — a single compact device that has an oven, mixer, refrigerator, and timer all built-in. The ARM processor is the "**chef's brain**" (executing recipes/software), the FPGA fabric is the "**multi-purpose cooking surface**" (hardware accelerators for parallel tasks), the on-chip memory is the "**pantry**" (cache/BRAM), and the bus system is the "**internal conveyor belt**" (AXI/AHB interconnect).
>
> Just as a smart kitchen is faster, smaller, and more energy-efficient than scattered appliances, an SoC is faster, smaller, and more power-efficient than a multi-chip PCB system.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following metrics are **bold-marked** as they are universally tested in KTU examinations:

- **Gates Equivalents (GE):** Logical complexity normalized to 2-input NAND gate area. A typical SoC is **millions of GE** (e.g., **10 MGE to 500 MGE**).
- **Process Node (nm):** Feature size of CMOS transistors. Modern SoCs: **7 nm, 5 nm, 3 nm**.
- **Clock Frequency (f<sub>clk</sub>):** Measured in **MHz / GHz**.
- **Power Dissipation (P):** Comprises **dynamic (P<sub>dyn</sub>)** and **static/leakage (P<sub>stat</sub>)** components, measured in **mW / W**.
- **Die Area (A):** Silicon real estate in **mm²**.
- **Hardware-Software Partitioning:** The act of dividing system functionality between **software (running on processor)** and **hardware (custom logic on FPGA fabric)**.
- **IP (Intellectual Property) Core:** Pre-designed, reusable functional blocks licensed from third parties or developed in-house.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PECST415 specifically emphasizes the **SoC design flow, IP integration, bus architectures (AMBA — AHB/APB/AXI), and FPGA-based SoC prototyping** using modern platform FPGAs.

## 1.4 Why SoC on FPGA Matters in Engineering

Modern applications demanding SoC-on-FPGA solutions include:
- **5G/6G baseband processing** (high-throughput DSP)
- **Automotive ADAS** (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) — real-time image processing
- **AI/ML Edge Inference** (CNN acceleration on FPGA fabric + ARM control)
- **IoT Edge Devices** (low-power sensor fusion)
- **Aerospace & Defense** (radar, secure communications)

The core engineering advantage is **deterministic, parallel hardware execution** coupled with **flexible software programmability** — a combination unattainable with general-purpose microcontrollers or ASICs alone.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Modern SoC-FPGA Architecture

A contemporary SoC FPGA contains **four fundamental functional strata**:

| Stratum | Function | Typical Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Processing System (PS)** | Runs OS, control plane, sequential tasks | Hard-core ARM Cortex-A9/A53/A57 (multi-core) |
| **2. Programmable Logic (PL)** | Hardware acceleration, parallel DSP, custom logic | FPGA fabric (LUTs, DSP48E slices, BRAM, Flip-Flops) |
| **3. Interconnect Fabric** | High-bandwidth data transfer between PS and PL | AMBA AXI (HP, GP, ACP ports), shared memory |
| **4. I/O Peripherals** | External communication | GPIO, UART, SPI, I2C, USB, Ethernet, PCIe |

## 2.2 The SoC Design Flow — Structured Logic

The SoC design flow follows a **top-down hierarchical methodology** with verification at every stage:

1. **System Specification & Modeling**
   - Define functionality using **SystemC, MATLAB/Simulink**, or high-level languages.
   - Establish performance, power, and area (**PPA**) targets.

2. **Hardware-Software Partitioning**
   - **Software tasks:** Sequential, control-flow dominant → mapped to ARM processor.
   - **Hardware tasks:** Parallel, data-flow dominant, latency-critical → mapped to FPGA fabric.
   - *Decision metric:* **Speedup factor S = T<sub>sw</sub> / T<sub>hw</sub>**

3. **IP Core Selection & Integration**
   - **Soft IP:** Synthesizable HDL (RTL level) — flexible, technology-independent.
   - **Firm IP:** Netlist-level — gate-level, but technology-mapped.
   - **Hard IP:** Layout-level (GDSII) — fixed, optimized for performance.

4. **Bus Architecture Design (AMBA Suite)**
   - **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus):** High-speed, pipelined, for processors and high-bandwidth peripherals.
   - **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus):** Low-power, simple, for low-bandwidth peripherals (UART, GPIO).
   - **AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface):** High-performance, point-to-point, supports burst transactions, out-of-order completion. **AXI is the modern standard for SoC-FPGA interconnects (Xilinx Zynq uses AXI).**

5. **RTL Design & Synthesis**
   - HDL coding (VHDL/Verilog), logic synthesis targeting FPGA primitives.

6. **Verification & Validation**
   - Simulation (ModelSim/QuestaSim), formal verification, FPGA prototyping.

7. **Physical Design (for ASIC) / Bitstream Generation (for FPGA)**
   - Place & Route, timing closure, bitstream generation.

8. **Software Stack Development**
   - Board Support Package (BSP), device drivers, OS porting (Linux, FreeRTOS), application software.

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Architectural Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why integrate a hard-core ARM with FPGA fabric?**
>
> - **Performance:** Hard cores operate at **multi-GHz** frequencies (e.g., Cortex-A53 at **1.5 GHz** in Zynq UltraScale+), vastly faster than soft-core processors (MicroBlaze at ~200 MHz).
> - **Predictability:** Hard cores have **deterministic timing** for real-time OS.
> - **Power Efficiency:** Hard cores are pre-optimized at silicon level — lower power than equivalent soft cores.
> - **Parallelism:** Offload compute-intensive kernels (FFT, encryption, ML inference) to FPGA fabric running in parallel.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use AXI instead of AHB in modern SoCs?**
>
> AXI supports **independent read/write channels**, enabling **simultaneous bidirectional data transfer** — critical for the **high-throughput PS↔PL communication** in video processing and AI inference pipelines. AHB multiplexes address/data, capping bandwidth.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are the **most frequently tested quantitative aspects** of SoC design:

| Formula | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $$P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat}$$ | Total power = dynamic + static (leakage) | Watts (W) |
| $$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ | Dynamic power: switching activity × load capacitance × V<sub>DD</sub>² × frequency | W; **α** = switching activity factor (0 to 1) |
| $$P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$$ | Static leakage power | W; **I<sub>leak</sub>** scales exponentially with temperature |
| $$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}, \quad T_{clk} \geq t_{pd,combinational} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}$$ | Clock period must exceed critical path + setup time + clock skew | seconds (s) |
| $$A_{die} = \sum A_{IP} + A_{interconnect} + A_{memory} + A_{I/O}$$ | Total die area summation (Mead-Conway approximation) | mm² |
| $$S = \frac{T_{sw}}{T_{hw}}, \quad E = \frac{S}{N_{proc}}$$ | Speedup (S) and Efficiency (E) for hardware acceleration | **N<sub>proc</sub>** = number of parallel hardware units |
| $$\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Data bits processed}}{T_{clk}}$$ | Data throughput per clock cycle | bits/second (bps) |
| $$\text{Bandwidth}_{AXI} = f_{clk,AXI} \times W_{data}$$ | AXI bus bandwidth | bps; **W<sub>data</sub>** = bus width (typically 32/64/128 bits) |
| $$\eta_{Amdahl} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{N}}$$ | **Amdahl's Law:** speedup limited by sequential fraction **f** parallelized over **N** processors | Dimensionless ratio |
| $$\text{Cost} \propto A_{die}^{\beta}, \quad \beta \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2$$ | Die cost scales super-linearly with area (Rentz's model) | USD |
| $$N_{Gates} = \frac{A_{die} \cdot \rho}{A_{gate,2NAND}}$$ | Gate count: die area × density / unit gate area | Gates (GE) |
| $$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}, \quad \lambda = \sum \lambda_i$$ | Reliability: Mean Time Between Failures | hours; **λ** = failure rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Amdahl's Law** is **extremely high-yield** for KTU exams — questions frequently ask to compute the maximum speedup achievable when only a fraction of code is parallelized.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **5G base stations**, the baseband signal processing chain uses SoC-FPGAs where:
- The **ARM cores** handle MAC-layer scheduling and protocol stack (software).
- The **FPGA fabric** implements **FFT/iFFT, channel coding (LDPC), and MIMO precoding** (hardware).
- The **AXI HP ports** stream digitized RF samples at **multi-Gbps** between PS and PL.

This partitioning achieves a **10× to 100× speedup** over pure software implementations while consuming **< 10 W** — a level of efficiency impossible with discrete DSP + MCU architectures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Achievable Speedup Using Amdahl's Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A KTU-style problem frequently asks: *"If 80% of a computation can be parallelized on FPGA hardware and 20% must remain sequential on the ARM processor, find the maximum speedup when 4 hardware accelerators are used. Also compute the efficiency."*

### Step 1: Identify the Sequential and Parallelizable Fractions

Let the **fraction of code that is parallelized** be **f**, and the **fraction that remains sequential** be **(1 − f)**.

Given:
- **f = 0.80** (parallelizable portion)
- **(1 − f) = 0.20** (sequential portion)
- **N = 4** (number of parallel hardware accelerators)

### Step 2: Apply Amdahl's Law

Amdahl's Law states that the **overall speedup S(N)** of a system when a fraction **f** of the work is accelerated by a factor of **N** is:

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{N}}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Given Values

Substituting **f = 0.80** and **N = 4** into the formula:

$$S(4) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \frac{0.80}{4}}$$

$$S(4) = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.20}$$

$$S(4) = \frac{1}{0.40} = 2.5$$

### Step 4: Compute the Parallel Efficiency

Efficiency is defined as the **speedup per unit of hardware resource**:

$$E = \frac{S(N)}{N}$$

$$E = \frac{2.5}{4} = 0.625 = 62.5\%$$

### Step 5: Compute the Theoretical Maximum Speedup (N → ∞)

The **fundamental limit** of parallelization is reached when the entire parallel portion is accelerated infinitely:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.0$$

This means **even with infinite hardware**, the system can never achieve more than a **5× speedup** because 20% of the work is inherently sequential.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Amdahl's Law demonstrates why **reducing the sequential portion (1−f)** through better algorithms is often more impactful than adding more hardware. Going from f=0.80 to f=0.95 raises the speedup ceiling from 5× to 20× — a **4× improvement with no additional hardware**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: AXI Bus Bandwidth Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** *"A Zynq-7000 SoC uses an AXI HP (High Performance) port with a 64-bit data width, operating at 150 MHz. The bus supports 2 data phases per clock (DDR-like behavior). Calculate the peak bandwidth."*

### Step 1: Identify Bus Parameters

- **W<sub>data</sub> = 64 bits** (bus width)
- **f<sub>clk</sub> = 150 MHz** (clock frequency)
- **N<sub>phases</sub> = 2** (data transfers per clock cycle — rising and falling edge)

### Step 2: Apply the Bandwidth Formula

The peak bandwidth of a bus is:

$$\text{Bandwidth} = f_{clk} \times W_{data} \times N_{phases}$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Compute

$$\text{Bandwidth} = 150 \times 10^6 \times 64 \times 2$$

$$\text{Bandwidth} = 150 \times 10^6 \times 128$$

$$\text{Bandwidth} = 19{,}200 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/sec}$$

$$\text{Bandwidth} = 19.2 \text{ Gbps} = 2.4 \text{ GB/s}$$

### Step 4: Practical Bandwith Adjustment

In practice, the achievable bandwidth is **60–80% of peak** due to protocol overhead (address phases, acknowledgments, idle cycles). The **effective bandwidth** is:

$$\text{BW}_{eff} = \eta_{proto} \times \text{BW}_{peak} = 0.7 \times 2.4 = 1.68 \text{ GB/s}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 1.68 GB/s is the **sustained data rate** available for streaming video frames from a camera sensor (via FPGA fabric) into DDR memory (via PS) in a typical Zynq-based **machine vision** application.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Dynamic Power Reduction by Voltage Scaling

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** *"A processor core in an SoC operates at V<sub>DD</sub> = 1.2 V, f = 1 GHz, with switching activity α = 0.15 and load capacitance C<sub>L</sub> = 10 nF. The system designer wants to reduce power by lowering V<sub>DD</sub> to 0.8 V (assuming frequency drops to 600 MHz). Compute the power savings."*

### Step 1: Original Dynamic Power

$$P_{dyn,1} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD,1}^2 \cdot f_{clk,1}$$

$$P_{dyn,1} = 0.15 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times (1.2)^2 \times 1 \times 10^9$$

$$P_{dyn,1} = 0.15 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.44 \times 10^9$$

$$P_{dyn,1} = 2.16 \text{ W}$$

### Step 2: New Dynamic Power (After Voltage Scaling)

$$P_{dyn,2} = 0.15 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times (0.8)^2 \times 600 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{dyn,2} = 0.15 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.64 \times 6 \times 10^8$$

$$P_{dyn,2} = 0.576 \text{ W}$$

### Step 3: Power Savings Ratio

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{P_{dyn,1} - P_{dyn,2}}{P_{dyn,1}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{2.16 - 0.576}{2.16} \times 100\% = \frac{1.584}{2.16} \times 100\% \approx 73.3\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Reduction Insight:** Reducing **V<sub>DD</sub> by 33% (1.2 V → 0.8 V)** combined with frequency reduction yielded a **73% power reduction**. This is the foundation of **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** used in mobile SoCs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: SoC Design Trade-off Table

| Design Parameter | Increase Impact (↑) | Decrease Impact (↓) | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **V<sub>DD</sub>** | ↑ P<sub>dyn</sub> (quadratically), ↑ f<sub>max</sub> | ↓ Performance, ↓ Power | Speed vs. Battery life |
| **f<sub>clk</sub>** | ↑ Throughput, ↑ P<sub>dyn</sub> | ↓ Power, ↓ Latency constraint | Real-time vs. Energy budget |
| **N<sub>proc</sub> (HW accel.)** | ↑ S(N) (diminishing per Amdahl), ↑ Area, ↑ Cost | ↓ Cost, ↓ Area | Performance vs. Silicon cost |
| **Process node (nm)** | ↓ Power, ↑ Density, ↑ Cost/mask | ↑ Power, ↑ Per-gate cost | PPA vs. Mask cost (>$10M at 7nm) |
| **Bus width (W)** | ↑ BW<sub>peak</sub>, ↑ Pin count, ↑ Area | ↓ Pin cost, ↓ BW | Bandwidth vs. Packaging cost |
| **Cache size** | ↓ Memory latency, ↑ Area, ↑ Leakage | ↑ Miss rate, ↓ Area | Performance vs. Die area |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SoC-FPGA Top-Level Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **canonical architecture of a Xilinx Zynq-7000 SoC**, showing the Processing System (PS), Programmable Logic (PL), and the **AXI interconnect** that bridges them.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PS["PROCESSING SYSTEM PS"]
        APU["ARM Cortex A9 Dual Core"]
        SCTRL["System Control Registers"]
        DDRC["DDR Memory Controller"]
        OCM["On Chip Memory 256 KB"]
        UART1["UART 0"]
        SPI1["SPI Controller"]
        I2C1["I2C Controller"]
        ENET["Ethernet MAC"]
        USB1["USB 2.0 OTG"]
    end

    subgraph INTERCONNECT["AXI INTERCONNECT FABRIC"]
        AXIGP["AXI GP Ports General Purpose 32 bit"]
        AXIHP["AXI HP Ports High Performance 64 bit"]
        AXIAC["AXI ACP Accelerator Coherency Port"]
    end

    subgraph PL["PROGRAMMABLE LOGIC PL"]
        LUTs["Configurable Logic Blocks LUTs"]
        BRAMs["Block RAM 36 Kb each"]
        DSP48["DSP48E Slices"]
        FFC["Flip Flops Registers"]
        CLKPLL["PLL MMCM Clock Tiles"]
        IOB["SelectIO Pins"]
        PCIE["PCIe Gen2 Hard Core"]
        GTX["GTX Transceivers 6.25 Gbps"]
    end

    APU --> AXIGP
    APU --> AXIHP
    APU --> AXIAC
    APU --> SCTRL
    APU --> DDRC
    APU --> OCM

    AXIGP --> LUTs
    AXIHP --> LUTs
    AXIHP --> BRAMs
    AXIAC --> LUTs

    LUTs --> BRAMs
    LUTs --> DSP48
    LUTs --> FFC
    LUTs --> CLKPLL
    LUTs --> IOB
    LUTs --> PCIE
    LUTs --> GTX
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from the Diagram:** Notice the **three classes of AXI ports** — **GP (General Purpose)** for low-bandwidth control register access, **HP (High Performance)** for streaming data (e.g., video frames) to DDR, and **ACP (Accelerator Coherency Port)** for cache-coherent hardware accelerators. This tri-port architecture is **the defining feature of Zynq SoC-FPGAs** and is a high-yield KTU topic.

---

## 4.2 SoC Design Flow Diagram (Top-Down Methodology)

```mermaid
graph TB
    S1["1 SYSTEM SPECIFICATION Use SystemC or MATLAB to define functionality and PPA targets"]
    S2["2 HARDWARE SOFTWARE PARTITIONING Decide which functions run on ARM software vs FPGA hardware"]
    S3["3 IP CORE SELECTION AND INTEGRATION Choose soft firm or hard IP blocks from library"]
    S4["4 BUS ARCHITECTURE DESIGN Define AMBA AXI AHB APB interconnect topology"]
    S5["5 RTL DESIGN AND SYNTHESIS Write VHDL Verilog HDL and synthesize to FPGA primitives"]
    S6["6 VERIFICATION AND VALIDATION Simulation ModelSim emulation and FPGA prototyping"]
    S7["7 PHYSICAL DESIGN ASIC or BITSTREAM GENERATION FPGA"]
    S8["8 SOFTWARE STACK DEVELOPMENT BSP device drivers OS porting application code"]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8
    S8 -.-> S1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Note the feedback loop:** The design flow is **iterative** — verification failures at step 6 may require re-partitioning at step 2. Real-world SoC projects often cycle through these 8 steps **multiple times** before tape-out (ASIC) or bitstream freeze (FPGA).

---

## 4.3 AMBA Bus Hierarchy — AHB/APB Bridge Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    CPU["ARM Processor AHB Master"]
    DMA["DMA Controller AHB Master"]
    MEM["External Memory SRAM SDRAM AHB Slave"]

    subgraph AHB_DOMAIN["AHB HIGH PERFORMANCE DOMAIN 100 MHz"]
        BRIDGE["AHB to APB Bridge"]
        H1["High Speed Peripheral 1 AHB Slave"]
        H2["High Speed Peripheral 2 AHB Slave"]
    end

    subgraph APB_DOMAIN["APB LOW POWER DOMAIN 50 MHz"]
        P1["UART APB Slave"]
        P2["GPIO APB Slave"]
        P3["Timer APB Slave"]
        P4["I2C APB Slave"]
    end

    CPU --> AHB_DOMAIN
    DMA --> AHB_DOMAIN
    AHB_DOMAIN --> MEM
    BRIDGE --> APB_DOMAIN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Rationale:** The AHB/APB bridge isolates the **high-speed, pipelined AHB bus** (for processor, DMA, memory) from the **low-speed, simple APB bus** (for peripherals). This **two-level hierarchy** minimizes power consumption because APB peripherals operate at lower clock rates and use a **non-pipelined protocol** — saving dynamic power in I/O-heavy systems.

---

## 4.4 Hardware-Software Co-Design Partitioning Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT APPLICATION"]
        APP["Image Processing Algorithm e.g. Sobel Edge Detection"]
    end

    subgraph HW["HARDWARE PARTITION FPGA FABRIC"]
        H1["Sobel Kernel Engine 3x3 convolution"]
        H2["Gaussian Filter Preprocessing"]
        H3["Threshold Binarization"]
    end

    subgraph SW["SOFTWARE PARTITION ARM PROCESSOR"]
        S1["Main Control Loop"]
        S2["Image Capture Camera Driver"]
        S3["Display Driver HDMI LCD"]
        S4["Communication Stack TCP IP USB"]
    end

    APP --> H1
    APP --> H2
    APP --> H3
    APP --> S1
    H1 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S1 --> S4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Partitioning Heuristic:** **Compute-bound, parallelizable kernels** (filtering, convolution) → **Hardware (FPGA)**. **Control-bound, sequential tasks** (drivers, protocol stacks, OS) → **Software (ARM)**. KTU often tests this **fundamental partitioning strategy**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: SoC Definition and Components `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define System-on-Chip (SoC). List any four major components of a typical SoC.**

**Model Answer:**

**System-on-Chip (SoC)** is an integrated circuit that consolidates **all functional blocks of a complete electronic system** onto a single silicon die, typically fabricated using sub-micron CMOS technology.

**Four major components of a typical SoC:**

1. **Processor Core(s):** Central processing unit (e.g., ARM Cortex-A9, RISC-V) — executes control software and OS.
2. **Memory Subsystem:** On-chip SRAM cache (L1/L2), external memory controller (DDR/Flash interface).
3. **Interconnect Fabric:** Bus architecture (AMBA AXI/AHB/APB) that connects IP blocks.
4. **Peripheral Interfaces:** Communication controllers (UART, SPI, I2C, USB, Ethernet, PCIe).
5. *(Optional fifth)* **Custom Hardware Accelerators:** DSP, encryption engines, ML accelerators.
6. *(Optional sixth)* **Clock and Power Management Unit (PMU):** PLLs, voltage regulators, DVFS controllers.

---

### Question 2: Bus Architecture Comparison `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between AMBA AHB and AMBA APB bus architectures. State one typical use case for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)** | **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Protocol Complexity** | Pipelined, burst transfers | Non-pipelined, simple |
| **Clock Speed** | High (100–200 MHz typical) | Low (50 MHz typical) |
| **Data Width** | 32 / 64 / 128 bits | 8 / 16 / 32 bits |
| **Master Support** | Multiple masters (CPU, DMA) | Single master (bridge only) |
| **Power** | Higher dynamic power | Optimized for low power |
| **Use Case** | Processor-to-memory, DMA transfers | Connecting low-speed peripherals like UART, GPIO, Timers |

> **Typical Use Case of AHB:** Connecting ARM processor to external DDR memory controller.
>
> **Typical Use Case of APB:** Connecting the AHB-to-APB bridge to a UART or GPIO peripheral for low-bandwidth I/O.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In SoC architecture diagrams, students commonly **forget to label the bus types** (AXI/HP/ACP/GP) on interconnect lines. **Always annotate bus protocols** on every connection.
> - In Amdahl's Law problems, students often **invert the fractions** (computing f/N as the denominator). Re-read: **denominator = (1−f) + (f/N)**.
> - For hardware-software partitioning questions, **always justify your choice** with quantitative reasoning (speedup, latency, energy). Generic statements like "hardware is faster" earn only partial credit.
> - When asked to "list IP types," students frequently omit **firm IP** — the three-tier classification (soft/firm/hard) is a **must-know**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1: Full SoC Architecture Analysis

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a modern SoC implemented on an FPGA platform** (e.g., Xilinx Zynq-7000). Clearly identify the Processing System (PS), Programmable Logic (PL), and the AXI interconnect fabric. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **AMBA bus architecture** (AHB, APB, AXI). Explain the role of the **AHB-to-APB bridge** and justify why AXI is preferred over AHB for modern SoC-FPGA designs. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) SoC-FPGA Architecture (7 Marks):**

The **Xilinx Zynq-7000 SoC** consists of two major domains:

**1. Processing System (PS) — (3 marks)**
- **ARM Cortex-A9 MPCore:** Dual-core hard processor running at up to **866 MHz** (Zynq-7010) or **1 GHz** (Zynq-7020).
- **L1 Cache:** 32 KB I-cache + 32 KB D-cache per core.
- **L2 Cache:** 512 KB unified.
- **On-Chip Memory (OCM):** 256 KB of low-latency RAM.
- **DDR Controller:** Supports DDR3/LPDDR2 up to **533 MHz**.
- **Peripherals:** UART, SPI, I2C, USB 2.0 OTG, Ethernet MAC, SD/SDIO, GPIO.

**2. Programmable Logic (PL) — (2 marks)**
- **Logic resources:** Lookup Tables (LUTs), Flip-Flops, Multiplexers.
- **Storage:** Block RAM (BRAM, 36 Kb each), Distributed RAM.
- **DSP:** DSP48E1 slices for fixed-point arithmetic.
- **Transceivers:** GTX transceivers (up to 6.25 Gbps) for high-speed serial I/O.
- **Clock Tiles:** Mixed-Mode Clock Managers (MMCMs) and PLLs.

**3. AXI Interconnect — (2 marks)**
- **AXI GP (General Purpose) ports:** 2× 32-bit master + 2× 32-bit slave ports for low-bandwidth control register access.
- **AXI HP (High Performance) ports:** 4× 32/64-bit slave ports for high-throughput streaming data (e.g., video, ADC samples) to DDR.
- **AXI ACP (Accelerator Coherency Port):** 64-bit port for cache-coherent accelerators sharing L2 cache with CPU.

**(b) AMBA Bus Architecture (7 Marks):**

**1. AMBA AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus) — (2 marks)**
- Pipelined, burst-capable, multi-master bus.
- Suitable for **high-bandwidth, high-clock-frequency** transfers.
- Used to connect **processor, DMA, and high-speed memory**.
- *Limitation:* Single shared address/data bus causes arbitration overhead.

**2. AMBA APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus) — (2 marks)**
- Non-pipelined, simple protocol, **single master (bridge only)**.
- Optimized for **minimum power and area**.
- Used for **low-bandwidth peripherals** (UART, GPIO, Timers, I2C).
- Operates at lower clock frequencies (typically half of AHB).

**3. AMBA AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface) — (2 marks)**
- **Separate address/control and data channels** (5 independent channels: AW, W, B, AR, R).
- Supports **out-of-order transaction completion**.
- **Burst transfers** with byte-level strobes.
- **AXI4, AXI4-Lite, AXI4-Stream** variants for different use cases.

**4. Why AXI > AHB in Modern SoCs — (1 mark)**
AXI's **independent read/write channels** enable **simultaneous bidirectional data transfer**, achieving higher effective bandwidth. AHB multiplexes address and data on a shared bus, capping throughput. In Zynq's video pipeline, AXI HP ports sustain **> 1.6 GB/s** to DDR — impossible with legacy AHB.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2: Quantitative Hardware-Software Co-Design

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** A real-time **FIR filter** on a software-only implementation on ARM Cortex-A9 takes **80 ms** per input frame. The filter is ported to FPGA hardware, where it executes in **8 ms**. The software overhead (data transfer, control) consumes **12 ms** per frame. **(7 Marks)**

- (i) Compute the **speedup factor** of the hardware-accelerated system.
- (ii) If the hardware cost is **5× the software cost**, determine whether the hardware acceleration is **cost-effective**, assuming the system processes **10,000 frames/hour** with a software cost of **₹0.001/frame** for processor time.

**(b)** Explain the **hardware-software co-design methodology** for SoC on FPGA. Use Amdahl's Law to justify the limits of parallelization with an example where 85% of a task is parallelizable. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) FIR Filter Speedup & Cost Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- T<sub>sw</sub> = 80 ms (pure software execution time per frame)
- T<sub>hw,core</sub> = 8 ms (pure hardware FIR execution time)
- T<sub>overhead</sub> = 12 ms (data transfer + control overhead)
- HW cost = 5× SW cost
- Frame rate = 10,000 frames/hour
- SW cost = ₹0.001/frame

**(i) Speedup Factor — (3 marks)**

The **total time per frame with HW acceleration** is:

$$T_{hw,total} = T_{hw,core} + T_{overhead}$$

$$T_{hw,total} = 8 + 12 = 20 \text{ ms}$$

The **speedup factor** is the ratio of software time to hardware-accelerated time:

$$S = \frac{T_{sw}}{T_{hw,total}} = \frac{80}{20} = 4.0$$

**Answer: Speedup = 4×**

**(ii) Cost-Effectiveness Analysis — (4 marks)**

**Software cost per frame:** C<sub>sw</sub> = ₹0.001
**Hardware cost per frame:** C<sub>hw</sub> = 5 × 0.001 = ₹0.005

**Total cost per frame:**
- Software-only: ₹0.001/frame
- HW-accelerated: ₹0.005/frame (5× more expensive per frame)

**Per-hour cost comparison (10,000 frames/hour):**
- Software-only: 10,000 × 0.001 = **₹10/hour**
- HW-accelerated: 10,000 × 0.005 = **₹50/hour**

**Time savings per hour:**
- Software-only: 10,000 × 80 ms = 800,000 ms = **800 seconds**
- HW-accelerated: 10,000 × 20 ms = 200,000 ms = **200 seconds**
- **Time saved: 600 seconds (10 minutes) per hour**

**Decision:** The hardware acceleration is **NOT cost-effective** on a pure ₹-per-frame metric, since it costs **5× more per frame** while providing only a **4× speedup**. However, in real-time systems, the 4× speedup may be **necessary** (e.g., to meet a 25 ms latency deadline) regardless of cost. **Cost-effectiveness depends on the real-time deadline constraint**, not just raw economics.

**(b) Hardware-Software Co-Design Methodology (7 Marks):**

**1. Co-Design Process — (3 marks)**

The co-design flow comprises:
1. **System Specification:** Define functionality, performance, and cost targets in a **language-neutral model** (SystemC, C/C++).
2. **Profiling:** Identify **hot-spots** in the algorithm — functions consuming > 5% of execution time are candidates for hardware.
3. **Partitioning:** Map compute-intensive, parallel kernels → **FPGA fabric**; map control/sequential code → **ARM software**.
4. **Co-Simulation:** Verify the partitioned system in a **hardware-software co-simulation environment** (e.g., Xilinx Vivado + QEMU).
5. **Implementation:** Generate HDL for hardware, C/C++ for software, integrate via AXI/AXI4-Stream.

**2. Amdahl's Law Application — (4 marks)**

Given **f = 0.85** (85% parallelizable), the maximum speedup with **N = 2, 4, 8, ∞** processors:

For **N = 2:**
$$S(2) = \frac{1}{(1-0.85) + \frac{0.85}{2}} = \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.425} = \frac{1}{0.575} \approx 1.74$$

For **N = 4:**
$$S(4) = \frac{1}{0.15 + \frac{0.85}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.2125} = \frac{1}{0.3625} \approx 2.76$$

For **N = 8:**
$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.15 + \frac{0.85}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.10625} = \frac{1}{0.25625} \approx 3.90$$

For **N → ∞** (theoretical maximum):
$$S(\infty) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.85} = \frac{1}{0.15} \approx 6.67$$

**Conclusion:** Even with 85% parallelization, the **maximum theoretical speedup is only 6.67×** because the 15% sequential portion acts as a hard ceiling. This is why **algorithm optimization to reduce the sequential fraction** is more valuable than simply adding more hardware.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: SoC Design on FPGA**

- ✅ **SoC Definition:** Single IC integrating processor + memory + peripherals + interconnect on one die.
- ✅ **FPGA-SoC Examples:** Xilinx Zynq-7000/UltraScale+, Intel Cyclone V SoC, Microchip PolarFire SoC.
- ✅ **Zynq Architecture:** PS (ARM Cortex-A9) + PL (FPGA fabric) + AXI interconnect.
- ✅ **AXI Port Types:** **GP** (control), **HP** (high-bandwidth streaming), **ACP** (cache-coherent accelerators).
- ✅ **AMBA Bus Suite:** **AHB** (high-speed, pipelined, burst), **APB** (low-power, simple, non-pipelined), **AXI** (modern, 5-channel, out-of-order).
- ✅ **IP Core Types:** **Soft** (synthesizable HDL), **Firm** (gate-level netlist), **Hard** (layout/GDSII).
- ✅ **HW-SW Partitioning Heuristic:** Parallel + compute-bound → **FPGA**; sequential + control-bound → **ARM software**.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** S(N) = 1 / [(1−f) + (f/N)]; theoretical max S<sub>∞</sub> = 1/(1−f).
- ✅ **Dynamic Power:** P<sub>dyn</sub> = α·C<sub>L</sub>·V<sub>DD</sub>²·f<sub>clk</sub> — scales **quadratically with V<sub>DD</sub>**.
- ✅ **AXI Bandwidth:** BW = f<sub>clk</sub> × W<sub>data</sub> × N<sub>phases</sub>; effective ≈ 70% of peak.
- ✅ **DVFS:** Dynamic Voltage-Frequency Scaling reduces power **> 70%** in many cases.
- ✅ **SoC Design Flow:** Specification → Partitioning → IP Selection → Bus Design → RTL → Verification → Implementation → Software Stack.
- ✅ **Common Pitfalls:** Forgetting to label bus types on diagrams; inverting Amdahl's Law fractions; omitting firmware from "software partition."
- ✅ **Industrial Applications:** 5G baseband, ADAS, AI edge inference, IoT, aerospace radar.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks for "a neat block diagram," **draw with proper labels in boxes**, **use arrows to indicate data flow direction**, and **label all bus widths and protocols** (e.g., "AXI HP, 64-bit, 150 MHz"). A diagram without these annotations is considered **incomplete** by KTU examiners and attracts partial marking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Project (FPGA / Semi-Custom Flow)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST415 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Micro Project (FPGA / Semi-Custom Flow)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Micro Project in VLSI Design (KTU 2024 Context)

In the KTU 2024 scheme for **PECST415 – VLSI Design**, the *Micro Project* listed under Module 4 is not a single schematic. It is a **practical, end-to-end mini-implementation** of a small digital sub-system (typically a 4-bit / 8-bit ALU, counter, UART transmitter, FIR filter, traffic-light controller, etc.) carried out using one of the two industry-standard design methodologies:

1. **FPGA (Full-Custom-like) Implementation Flow** — target device: Xilinx Spartan-6/7, Artix-7, Intel Cyclone, etc.
2. **Semi-Custom (Standard-Cell ASIC) Implementation Flow** — using a **standard-cell library** on a CAD tool such as **Cadence Genus / Innovus, Synopsys Design Compiler / IC Compiler, or open-source alternatives like OpenLane + Sky130.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> A *Micro Project* in VLSI is the application of the **Top-Down Design Methodology** to a small digital block, taking it from **RTL (Verilog/VHDL) → Functional Simulation → Logic Synthesis → Place & Route → Bitstream / GDS-II**, depending on whether the target is an **FPGA** or a **Semi-Custom ASIC**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pre-Fabricated House vs. The Brick-by-Brick House"

Imagine you want a small digital circuit (say, a 4-bit calculator):

- **FPGA Flow = "IKEA Furniture"**: The silicon is **pre-fabricated** with thousands of **Look-Up Tables (LUTs), Flip-Flops, Block RAMs, and programmable routing switches**. You do not manufacture silicon. You simply **write a configuration bitstream** (`.bit` or `.sof`) that tells each LUT what boolean function to perform and how the wires should connect. Fast, cheap, **re-programmable** like a software upgrade.

- **Semi-Custom Flow = "Custom-Built House"**: You start with a **blank silicon die** and use a library of pre-designed, pre-characterised **standard cells** (NAND, NOR, DFF, MUX — all with fixed height, fixed drive strength, fixed delay). You place and route these cells to build your circuit. The final output is a **GDS-II layout file** sent to a foundry (e.g., TSMC, SkyWater). **Not re-programmable** — the silicon itself is permanently wired.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Insight:**
> - **FPGA** = NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) cost is **near zero**; per-unit cost is high. Used for prototyping, low-volume, aerospace, defence.
> - **Semi-Custom ASIC** = NRE cost is **high (lakh-to-crore range)**; per-unit cost is low. Used for high-volume consumer chips (smartphones, IoT).

### 1.3 Standard KTU 2024 Metrics (in Bold)

The micro-project is evaluated against these **standard performance metrics** every KTU examiner expects:

- **Area** — measured in *gate equivalents* (GE) or *µm²* (for ASIC) or *LUTs / Slices / FFs* (for FPGA).
- **Timing / Delay** — measured in *nanoseconds (ns)* or *picoseconds (ps)*, typically the **critical path delay** $t_{pd}$.
- **Power** — **Dynamic Power** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$ and **Static Power** $P_{stat} = V_{DD} I_{leak}$.
- **Operating Frequency** $f_{max} = 1 / t_{pd}$ in **MHz / GHz**.
- **Design Rule Check (DRC)** & **Layout Versus Schematic (LVS)** — must be **clean** for the ASIC flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The FPGA Implementation Flow (Detailed Walkthrough)

The **FPGA flow** is what KTU students most commonly execute, since it requires only a free tool like **Xilinx Vivado / ISE WebPack** or **Intel Quartus Prime Lite**. The steps are:

1. **Spec Capture & RTL Design** — Write **Verilog** (or VHDL) describing the circuit. This is the **Register Transfer Level** description.
2. **Functional / Behavioral Simulation** — Use a testbench + ISim/QuestaSim/Vivado Simulator. No timing yet; only logical correctness is verified.
3. **Synthesis** — Tool converts RTL into a **gate-level netlist** mapped to FPGA primitives (LUTs, FFs, DSP slices, BRAMs).
4. **Implementation (Place & Route)** — Tool places primitives on the FPGA fabric and routes interconnects. Produces timing report.
5. **Bitstream Generation** — Tool produces a binary `.bit` (Xilinx) or `.sof` (Intel) file.
6. **Device Programming** — Bitstream is downloaded onto the FPGA via **JTAG**. The FPGA now acts as the custom hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the KTU Examiner Loves This Topic:**
> The micro-project tests the student's ability to take a design **across all 5 levels of design abstraction** — System, RTL, Logic, Circuit, and (in ASIC flow) Layout.

### 2.2 The Semi-Custom (Standard-Cell) ASIC Flow

The **semi-custom flow** is identical to FPGA up to synthesis, but **diverges** at the technology-mapping stage:

1. **RTL Design** (Verilog / VHDL)
2. **Functional Simulation**
3. **Logic Synthesis** using a **standard-cell library** (e.g., *Synopsys SAED 32nm, Cadence 45nm GSCLib, Sky130*). Output = **gate-level netlist** in **`.v` / `.vhd`** mapped to standard cells.
4. **Floorplanning** — defines core area, IO pad placement, macro placement.
5. **Placement** — standard cells placed in rows.
6. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)** — distributes the clock with low skew.
7. **Routing** — global + detailed routing using metal layers.
8. **Physical Verification** — **DRC, LVS, Antenna Checks**.
9. **GDS-II Generation** — final mask layout for foundry fabrication.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mandate:** Even if the student does not have access to foundry-grade EDA tools, they must **logically walk through each step** in the report and present a **sample standard-cell layout, area report, and timing report**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **cheat-sheet** for every numerical question in the ESE (End Semester Examination) on this module.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|-----------|---------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | Critical Path Delay | $t_{pd} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{d,i}$ | $t_{d,i}$ = delay of $i^{th}$ gate; $N$ = number of gates on path |
| 2 | Max Operating Frequency | $f_{max} = 1 \div t_{pd}$ | Hz; typically expressed in MHz or GHz |
| 3 | Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f$ | Watts; $\alpha$ = switching activity (0 to 1) |
| 4 | Static (Leakage) Power | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \, I_{leak}$ | Watts; $I_{leak}$ in Amperes |
| 5 | Total Power | $P_{tot} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat}$ | Watts |
| 6 | Energy per Operation | $E = P_{tot} \div f = C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{2}$ | Joules; $\alpha = 1$ assumed |
| 7 | Power-Delay Product (PDP) | $PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_{pd}$ | Joules — Figure of Merit |
| 8 | Energy-Delay Product (EDP) | $EDP = PDP \cdot t_{pd}$ | Joules-seconds |
| 9 | FPGA LUT Size Equation | $N_{inputs} \le \log_{2}(\text{LUT size})$ | e.g., a 6-input LUT can realise any boolean function of up to 6 variables |
| 10 | Area Equivalence | $1 \text{ GE} = 2 \text{ NAND2 gates} = 4 \text{ transistors (MOS)}$ | Used to normalise ASIC area |
| 11 | Wire Load Delay (Elmore) | $t_{wire} \approx 0.5 \, R_{wire} \, C_{wire}$ | Elmore approximation for RC ladder |
| 12 | Rent's Rule (for FPGA / ASIC) | $T = k \, N^{p}$ | $T$ = external terminals, $N$ = blocks, $k$, $p$ are empirical constants |
| 13 | Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = f \div \text{pipeline stages}$ | Operations per second |
| 14 | Slack (Static Timing Analysis) | $\text{Slack} = T_{required} - T_{arrival}$ | $+$ = meet timing; $-$ = violation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule (From KTU Valuation Key):** When asked to *calculate the maximum operating frequency*, students often forget to convert the delay units. **Always write:** $f_{max}\,[\text{MHz}] = 1000 \div t_{pd}\,[\mu s]$. Examiners award the conversion step **1 mark** separately.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FPGA flows** are the backbone of **5G base-station prototyping**, **aerospace FPGAs (Microsemi RTG4)**, and **AI inference accelerators (Xilinx Versal AI Edge)**.
- **Semi-custom ASIC flows** power **every smartphone SoC** (Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple M-series, MediaTek Dimensity). The RTL is written in Verilog/SystemVerilog, synthesised with a standard-cell library, and fabricated at 3 nm / 5 nm nodes.
- The micro-project builds the **exact skill set** required for an entry-level **RTL Design Engineer** or **ASIC Verification Engineer** role in Bangalore's semiconductor industry.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Dynamic Power Dissipation

This derivation is a **favourite KTU 14-marker** and is universally expected in any VLSI module touching CMOS circuits. The micro-project typically uses this formula in the power-report analysis section.

**Step 1 — Identify the load capacitor.**
A CMOS gate drives a load capacitance $C_{L}$. The energy required to charge this capacitor from **0 V to $V_{DD}$** is:

$$
E_{charge} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V \, dq = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V \, C_{L} \, dV
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
E_{charge} = C_{L} \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V \, dV = C_{L} \left[ \frac{V^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{V_{DD}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
E_{charge} = \frac{1}{2} \, C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the discharge energy.**
When the capacitor discharges from $V_{DD}$ to **0 V**, the energy stored in it is dissipated as heat in the **PMOS pull-up network** during charging, and in the **NMOS pull-down network** during discharging. Total energy per **complete switching cycle** is:

$$
E_{cycle} = E_{charge} + E_{discharge} = \frac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} = C_{L} V_{DD}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Account for switching activity.**
Only a fraction $\alpha$ of all clock cycles cause a transition at a given node. Hence, the **average dynamic power** is:

$$
P_{dyn} = E_{cycle} \cdot \alpha \cdot f = \alpha \, C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f
$$

**Step 6 — Add static power (if sub-90nm process).**

$$
P_{tot} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat} = \alpha \, C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f + V_{DD} \, I_{leak}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Power is **quadratically dependent on $V_{DD}$**. This is why low-power designs aggressively scale voltage (e.g., $V_{DD}$ from 1.2 V → 0.8 V gives 55% power reduction).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Path Delay of an N-bit Ripple Carry Adder

The micro-project often uses an **N-bit adder or counter**. The critical path must be derived. Consider a **Ripple Carry Adder (RCA)** of $N$ bits.

**Step 1 — Each full-adder has two gate-delays** in carry propagation: one from input to carry-out, one from carry-in to carry-out. Total: $2 t_{gate}$ per stage.

**Step 2 — Sum generation** requires an additional XOR delay $t_{XOR}$ after the carry arrives.

**Step 3 — Total critical-path delay:**

$$
t_{pd} = 2 (N - 1) \, t_{gate} + t_{XOR}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum operating frequency:**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 (N - 1) \, t_{gate} + t_{XOR}}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
For a 4-bit RCA with $t_{gate} = 0.5\,\text{ns}$ and $t_{XOR} = 0.7\,\text{ns}$:

$$
t_{pd} = 2(4 - 1)(0.5) + 0.7 = 2(3)(0.5) + 0.7 = 3.0 + 0.7 = 3.7\,\text{ns}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{3.7\,\text{ns}} = \frac{1000}{3.7}\,\text{MHz} \approx 270.27\,\text{MHz}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-Offs (FPGA vs. Semi-Custom)

| Comparison Axis | FPGA Flow | Semi-Custom ASIC Flow |
|-----------------|-----------|------------------------|
| Time-to-market | **Very short** (hours) | **Long** (months) |
| NRE Cost | **Near zero** | **High** (₹10 L – ₹10 Cr+) |
| Per-unit cost (volume) | High | **Very low** |
| Performance (speed) | Slower (programmable routing adds delay) | **Faster** (optimised cells + routing) |
| Power consumption | Higher (static + dynamic) | **Lower** (especially dynamic) |
| Re-programmability | **Yes** (in-system) | **No** (mask change needed) |
| Density (gates/mm²) | Lower (routing overhead) | **Higher** |
| Tool cost | Free (Vivado WebPack) | **Very expensive** (₹10 L+ licence) |
| Target application | Prototyping, low-volume, military, FPGA-SoC | Consumer electronics, smartphones, IoT |
| Design risk | Low (can re-program) | High (re-spin costs money) |
| Yield issue? | **None** (pre-tested silicon) | **Yes** (yield < 100% on first wafer) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** In viva voce, if the examiner asks *"Why not always use ASIC?"*, the correct one-line answer is: *"Because of NRE cost and time-to-market, FPGAs dominate the prototyping and low-volume segments; ASICs dominate the high-volume consumer market."*

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Micro-Project VLSI Design Flow

This diagram is the **mandatory** flow-chart every micro-project report must contain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Problem Specification] --> A2[Algorithm Design and RTL Modelling in Verilog or VHDL]
    A2 --> A3[Functional Simulation using Testbench]
    A3 --> A4{Is Functional Behaviour Correct?}
    A4 -- No --> A2
    A4 -- Yes --> A5[Logic Synthesis]
    A5 --> A6[Gate Level Netlist Generation]
    A6 --> A7{Target Platform Decision}
    A7 -- FPGA --> B1[FPGA Implementation Flow]
    A7 -- ASIC --> C1[Semi Custom ASIC Flow]
    B1 --> B2[Place and Route on FPGA Fabric]
    B2 --> B3[Timing Simulation]
    B3 --> B4[Bitstream Generation]
    B4 --> B5[Download Bitstream to FPGA via JTAG]
    B5 --> B6[On Board Hardware Validation]
    C1 --> C2[Floorplanning]
    C2 --> C3[Placement of Standard Cells]
    C3 --> C4[Clock Tree Synthesis]
    C4 --> C5[Global and Detailed Routing]
    C5 --> C6[Physical Verification DRC and LVS]
    C6 --> C7[GDS II File Output to Foundry]
    C7 --> C8[Post Layout Timing Analysis]
    B6 --> D1[Area Power Delay Report Generation]
    C8 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Final Project Report and Documentation]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — FPGA Internal Architecture (Simplified)

This is what the examiner expects to see for a 2-mark "Draw the FPGA architecture" question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[IO Buffers and PADs] --> P2[Configurable Logic Block CLB containing LUTs and Flip Flops]
    P2 --> P3[Programmable Interconnect Switch Matrix]
    P3 --> P4[Block RAM BRAM]
    P3 --> P5[DSP Slices for Multiply Accumulate]
    P3 --> P6[Clock Management Tiles CMT and PLL]
    P3 --> P7[Hard IP Cores PCIe Ethernet DDR Controller]
    P2 --> P1
    P4 --> P1
    P5 --> P1
    P6 --> P1
    P7 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping to Real Hardware:**
> - **CLB** = **Logic Block** (Xilinx terminology); in Intel (Altera) it is called a **LAB — Logic Array Block**.
> - **LUT** = Look-Up Table; a 6-input LUT is a 64-bit SRAM that stores the truth table of any 6-variable boolean function.
> - **BRAM** = dedicated 36 Kb / 18 Kb dual-port memory blocks.
> - **DSP Slices** = hardware multipliers + accumulators.
> - **PLL/CMT** = generates internal clocks from an external reference.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Semi-Custom Standard-Cell Row Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1[Substrate P type Silicon Wafer] --> R2[Deep N Well and P Well Formation]
    R2 --> R3[Standard Cell Rows with Fixed Height]
    R3 --> R4[Channel between Rows for Routing M1 and M2]
    R4 --> R5[Over the Cell OTC Routing Layers M3 M4 M5]
    R5 --> R6[Top Metal M6 for Power Straps and Clock Distribution]
    R6 --> R7[IO Pads at Chip Periphery]
    R7 --> R8[Final GDS II Stream Out to Foundry for Tape Out]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Terminology:**
> Standard cells in a library all have **the same height** but **variable width** — this is called the **"fixed-height, variable-width"** rule. It allows cells to be abutted in rows with shared power/ground rails (M1 horizontal).

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — RTL-to-GDS-II Tool Chain (Industry Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Verilog or VHDL RTL] --> S2[Simulation Tool Modelsim QuestaSim]
    S1 --> S3[Synthesis Tool Design Compiler or Genus]
    S3 --> S4[Gate Level Netlist in Verilog]
    S4 --> S5[Place and Route Tool Innovus or IC Compiler2]
    S5 --> S6[Defects in Design?]
    S6 -- Yes --> S5
    S6 -- No --> S7[Physical Verification Calibre or IC Validator]
    S7 --> S8[GDS II File]
    S8 --> S9[Foundry Tape Out and Fabrication]
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a Micro Project in the context of VLSI Design. List the typical steps involved in a semi-custom ASIC design flow.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Micro Project in VLSI** is a small-scale, end-to-end implementation of a digital sub-system, taken from **RTL specification** down to either an **FPGA bitstream** or a **GDS-II layout**, demonstrating the **top-down design methodology**.

Typical steps in the **semi-custom (standard-cell) ASIC flow** are:

1. **RTL design** in Verilog / VHDL.
2. **Functional simulation** to verify logic correctness.
3. **Logic synthesis** with a standard-cell library to produce a gate-level netlist.
4. **Floorplanning** of the die area.
5. **Placement** of standard cells.
6. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)**.
7. **Routing** of signal nets.
8. **Physical verification** — DRC and LVS.
9. **GDS-II generation** for fabrication.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the 9-step list — typically examiners award 0.5 mark per 2–3 steps.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Compare the FPGA implementation flow and the semi-custom ASIC implementation flow in terms of (a) NRE cost, (b) re-programmability, and (c) per-unit cost.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Attribute | FPGA Flow | Semi-Custom ASIC Flow |
|-----------|-----------|------------------------|
| (a) NRE cost | **Near zero** (only tool licence, often free) | **High** (mask + tool licence, ₹10 L to ₹10 Cr) |
| (b) Re-programmability | **Yes**, in-system via JTAG | **No**, requires re-fabrication |
| (c) Per-unit cost | **High** (each FPGA chip costs more) | **Low** at high volume |

*(1 mark per row.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete semi-custom ASIC design flow** from RTL to GDS-II. Mention the tool used at each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 4-bit ripple carry adder, the following parameters are given: gate delay $t_{gate} = 0.6\,\text{ns}$, XOR delay $t_{XOR} = 0.8\,\text{ns}$, load capacitance $C_{L} = 50\,\text{fF}$, supply voltage $V_{DD} = 1.2\,\text{V}$, switching activity $\alpha = 0.25$, operating frequency $f = 200\,\text{MHz}$.
Calculate: (i) the **critical path delay** $t_{pd}$, (ii) the **maximum operating frequency** $f_{max}$, and (iii) the **average dynamic power dissipation** $P_{dyn}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Semi-Custom ASIC Design Flow (7 marks):**

| Step | Operation | Tool Used (Example) |
|------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | RTL Design in Verilog / VHDL | Any text editor + VCS / Modelsim |
| 2 | Functional Simulation | Modelsim, QuestaSim, VCS |
| 3 | Logic Synthesis to standard cells | Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus |
| 4 | Floorplanning | Cadence Innovus, Synopsys IC Compiler II |
| 5 | Placement | Innovus, IC Compiler II |
| 6 | Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS) | Innovus, IC Compiler II |
| 7 | Global + Detailed Routing | Innovus, IC Compiler II |
| 8 | Physical Verification (DRC, LVS, Antenna) | Mentor Calibre, Synopsys IC Validator |
| 9 | GDS-II Stream-Out | Innovus / IC Validator / Magic |

*(Each step: ~0.7 marks; an additional 1 mark for the block diagram showing RTL → Synthesis → Floorplan → Place → CTS → Route → DRC/LVS → GDS-II.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Critical Path Delay of 4-bit RCA:**

$$
t_{pd} = 2(N-1)\,t_{gate} + t_{XOR}
$$

$$
t_{pd} = 2(4-1)(0.6\,\text{ns}) + 0.8\,\text{ns}
$$

$$
t_{pd} = 2 \times 3 \times 0.6 + 0.8 = 3.6 + 0.8 = 4.4\,\text{ns}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Operating Frequency:**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{pd}} = \frac{1}{4.4\,\text{ns}} = \frac{1000}{4.4}\,\text{MHz} \approx 227.27\,\text{MHz}
$$

**(iii) Average Dynamic Power:**

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.25 \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \times (1.2)^{2} \times (200 \times 10^{6})
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.25 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 \times 200 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.25 \times 50 \times 1.44 \times 200 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.25 \times 14400 \times 10^{-9} = 3600 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{W}
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 3.6\,\mu\text{W}
$$

*(Step allocation: 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final answer for (i); 1 mark formula + 1 mark answer for (ii); 1 mark formula + 1 mark answer for (iii) — total 7 marks.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Draw the **internal architecture of a typical FPGA** and explain the function of each major block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A combinational block in a 32 nm CMOS ASIC has a measured **static (leakage) current of $I_{leak} = 1.5\,\mu\text{A}$** at **$V_{DD} = 0.9\,\text{V}$**, and the dynamic power is measured as $P_{dyn} = 12\,\mu\text{W}$ at an activity factor of $\alpha = 0.3$, clock frequency $f = 500\,\text{MHz}$, and load capacitance $C_{L} = 80\,\text{fF}$.
(i) Verify whether the given $C_{L}$ is consistent with $P_{dyn}$.
(ii) Compute the **static power** and the **total power**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) FPGA Architecture (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.2** above. The architecture consists of:

- **Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs)** — each CLB contains **LUTs** (6-input SRAM-based truth tables) and **flip-flops** for sequential logic. (2 marks)
- **Programmable Interconnect** — a switch matrix of pass transistors / muxes that routes signals between CLBs. (2 marks)
- **I/O Blocks (IOBs)** — bidirectional buffers with slew-rate and pull-up/pull-down control. (1 mark)
- **Block RAM (BRAM)** — dedicated dual-port memory. (1 mark)
- **DSP Slices + Clock Management (PLL/CMT)** — for arithmetic and clock generation. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Verify $C_{L}$ consistency:**

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f
$$

Solving for $C_{L}$:

$$
C_{L} = \frac{P_{dyn}}{\alpha \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f}
$$

$$
C_{L} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-6}}{0.3 \times (0.9)^{2} \times 500 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
C_{L} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-6}}{0.3 \times 0.81 \times 500 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
C_{L} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-6}}{121.5 \times 10^{6}} = 98.76 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F} \approx 98.8\,\text{fF}
$$

The given $C_{L} = 80\,\text{fF}$ is **not consistent** — the measured dynamic power corresponds to a load of $\approx 98.8\,\text{fF}$, indicating either an additional parasitic capacitance of $\approx 18.8\,\text{fF}$ not accounted for, or a slight measurement tolerance. The student should report this in the micro-project power-analysis section. *(3 marks)*

**(ii) Static Power and Total Power:**

$$
P_{stat} = V_{DD} \, I_{leak} = 0.9 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.35 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{W} = 1.35\,\mu\text{W}
$$

$$
P_{tot} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat} = 12\,\mu\text{W} + 1.35\,\mu\text{W} = 13.35\,\mu\text{W}
$$

*(2 marks for formula + answer for static; 2 marks for total power.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit-Conversion Blunder:** The single most common mistake in this module is mixing ns with µs with ms. Always normalise everything to a **single base unit (seconds)** before substituting into the formula. A wrong unit conversion costs **2 full marks** outright.
> 2. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** A 7-mark "Explain the flow" question without a labelled block diagram is automatically capped at **5 marks**. Always include the diagram.
> 3. **Forgetting $\alpha$:** In the power formula, students often write $C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ instead of $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$. This is a **1-mark deduction** per occurrence.
> 4. **Confusing CTS with Routing:** CTS is **only** for the clock network. Data-path routing happens in the "Routing" step. Examiners do check this distinction.
> 5. **Writing "GDS2" or "GDS file":** The correct KTU terminology is **"GDS-II"** (with Roman numeral II, written in capitals). Stylistic but evaluated.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Micro Project**

- A **Micro Project in VLSI** is a **top-down implementation** of a small digital block, using either the **FPGA flow** (bitstream output) or the **semi-custom ASIC flow** (GDS-II output).

- **FPGA flow** = RTL → Functional Sim → Synthesis → Place & Route → Bitstream → JTAG download. Fast, low cost, **re-programmable**.

- **Semi-Custom flow** = RTL → Functional Sim → Synthesis with **standard-cell library** → Floorplan → Placement → **CTS** → Routing → **DRC / LVS** → **GDS-II**. Slow, high NRE, **not re-programmable**.

- **Standard cells** = pre-designed, pre-characterised logic gates (NAND, NOR, DFF, MUX) with **fixed height, variable width**, sharing power/ground rails.

- **Dynamic Power** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — **quadratic in $V_{DD}$**.

- **Static Power** $P_{stat} = V_{DD} I_{leak}$ — dominates at **sub-90 nm** nodes.

- **Critical-path delay** of an N-bit **RCA** = $2(N-1) t_{gate} + t_{XOR}$.

- **Max operating frequency** $f_{max} = 1 / t_{pd}$.

- **FPGA fabric** = CLBs (LUTs + FFs) + Programmable Interconnect + BRAM + DSP + PLL + Hard IP.

- **GDS-II** = Graphical Database Stream format, the **final industry-standard** mask-layout file sent to the foundry.

- **DRC (Design Rule Check)** ensures layout obeys foundry rules (min width, min spacing). **LVS (Layout vs. Schematic)** ensures layout matches the netlist.

- **NRE cost** = one-time cost (mask, tool licence). **Per-unit cost** = cost of every individual chip.

- KTU viva-voce **favourite question**: *"If both FPGA and ASIC can implement the same logic, why do companies still spend crores on ASICs?"* — **Answer:** At **high volume**, the low per-unit cost of ASICs amortises the NRE; ASICs also deliver **better speed, area, and power**.

- Always **label units** in every numerical answer. Always **state assumptions** ($\alpha = 1$ for clock, etc.). Always include the **block diagram** for flow-type questions.
